bullets.point: Authentication proves who a caller is (identity), distinct from authorization which decides what they can do. HTTP is stateless, so after a successful login the server must issue a token/credential the client resends on every request; the whole topic is about how that credential is stored and transmitted safely. Interview angle: be able to cleanly separate authn vs authz and explain why statelessness forces per-request credentials.

On login the server signs a JWT (header.payload.signature, HMAC or RSA) containing claims like userId/exp; the client stores it in localStorage and manually attaches it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on each request. The server verifies the signature and expiry without a DB/session lookup, making it stateless and horizontally scalable. Interview angle: localStorage is readable by any JS on the page, so this pattern is vulnerable to XSS token theft, and stateless JWTs are hard to revoke before expiry.

Alternative to manual token handling: the server sends `Set-Cookie` on login and the browser automatically returns the cookie on every same-origin request, so the client code never touches the credential. The cookie can hold a session id (server-side session store) or a JWT. Interview angle: automatic transmission is the key win over localStorage, but it introduces CSRF risk precisely because the browser sends it without app involvement.

Covers the mechanics of reading cookies server-side (e.g. cookie-parser), how the browser scopes cookies by domain/path, and CORS with credentials — `credentials: 'include'` on fetch plus `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` and a specific (non-wildcard) origin on the server. Interview angle: the classic bug is cookies silently not being sent cross-origin because the credentials flags or SameSite settings weren't configured.

Key attributes: HttpOnly (blocks JS access, mitigates XSS theft), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (Lax/Strict/None — CSRF mitigation, None requires Secure), Domain/Path (scope), Expires/Max-Age (lifetime). Interview angle: HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite together are the standard hardening; know that SameSite=Lax is the modern browser default and that SameSite=None;Secure is required for legitimate cross-site cookies.

A minimal backend exposes /signup and /signin that verify credentials and respond with either a JWT (Bearer flow) or `res.cookie(name, token, {httpOnly, secure, sameSite})` (cookie flow), plus auth middleware that reads the token, verifies it, and attaches req.userId. Interview angle: passwords must be hashed (bcrypt/argon2, never plaintext), the JWT secret comes from env config, and the verify step belongs in reusable middleware, not per-route.

A separate React app (different origin/port) calls the backend via fetch/axios; with JWT it stores and manually sends the Bearer header, with cookies it must set `withCredentials`/`credentials:'include'` and the backend must whitelist that origin with credentials enabled. Interview angle: this is where CORS-with-credentials and cookie SameSite issues surface, and where you decide token storage strategy (memory vs localStorage vs HttpOnly cookie).

Serving the React build as static assets from the same Express server (express.static) makes frontend and API same-origin, which eliminates CORS entirely and lets HttpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies work seamlessly. Interview angle: same-origin deployment is the simplest and most secure setup for cookie auth; know the trade-off against a separately deployed/CDN-hosted SPA.

example.title: Session auth for a dashboard SPA behind a shared API
example.body: You are building the login flow for an internal analytics dashboard: a React SPA and an Express API. In dev, React runs on :5173 and the API on :3000, so you hit CORS — cookies aren't sent until you set `credentials:'include'` on fetch and, on the server, `cors({origin:'http://localhost:5173', credentials:true})` (wildcard origin is illegal with credentials). On signin, Express hashes-compares the password with bcrypt, signs a JWT, and sends it via `res.cookie('token', jwt, {httpOnly:true, secure:true, sameSite:'lax'})` so JS can't read it (XSS-resistant) and the browser auto-attaches it. Auth middleware reads the cookie, verifies the JWT, and sets req.userId. For production you drop the React build into express.static on the same origin, which makes CORS moot and lets you tighten to sameSite:'strict'. The debugging story an SDE-2 lives through: 'why is my cookie set in the response but never sent back?' — usually a missing credentials flag, a SameSite/Secure mismatch on http, or a cross-origin/domain scope problem.

qa.q: JWT in localStorage vs a session/JWT in an HttpOnly cookie — what's the security and operational trade-off?
qa.a: localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript on the page, so a single XSS flaw leaks the token; you also send it manually via the Authorization header, which sidesteps CSRF. HttpOnly cookies can't be read by JS (XSS-resistant) and are sent automatically, but that auto-send is exactly what exposes you to CSRF, mitigated with SameSite and/or CSRF tokens. Cookies also give you easy server-controlled expiry/rotation, while stateless JWTs are hard to revoke early. The common recommendation is HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite cookies for browser apps.

qa.q: How do you revoke a JWT before it expires?
qa.a: You can't invalidate a self-contained signed JWT directly because verification is stateless — the token is valid until exp regardless. Practical options: keep tokens short-lived (minutes) with refresh tokens, maintain a server-side denylist/allowlist (which reintroduces state and a lookup), or bump a per-user token version/`tokenVersion` claim that you compare against the DB. This is the core trade-off: pure statelessness costs you revocation, so most real systems add some server state back.

qa.q: What is CSRF, why do cookies enable it, and how do you defend against it?
qa.a: CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into making an unwanted authenticated request; it works because the browser auto-attaches cookies to requests regardless of which site initiated them. Defenses: SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies (browser won't send on cross-site navigations/requests), anti-CSRF tokens (synchronizer/double-submit), and verifying Origin/Referer. Token-in-header auth (Bearer from localStorage) is inherently CSRF-safe because the attacker's site can't read or set that header, which is one reason people accept the XSS trade-off.

qa.q: Walk through configuring CORS so cookies work between a React app and an Express API on different origins.
qa.a: On the client, set `credentials:'include'` (fetch) or `withCredentials:true` (axios). On the server, set `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` and reflect a specific origin — the wildcard `*` is rejected when credentials are included. You must also whitelist the needed methods/headers for preflight, and the cookie needs `SameSite=None; Secure` to be sent cross-site at all. Missing any one of these produces the classic 'cookie is set but never sent back' symptom.

qa.q: Explain the JWT structure and what the signature actually guarantees.
qa.a: A JWT is three base64url parts: header (alg/typ), payload (claims like sub, exp, iat), and signature. The signature is computed over header+payload with a secret (HMAC) or private key (RSA/ECDSA) and guarantees integrity and authenticity — that the claims weren't tampered with and came from someone holding the key. It does NOT provide confidentiality: the payload is only encoded, not encrypted, so never put secrets in it. Also validate `alg` server-side to avoid the `alg:none` and algorithm-confusion attacks.

qa.q: What do HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite each protect against?
qa.a: HttpOnly blocks document.cookie access from JavaScript, so XSS can't exfiltrate the cookie. Secure ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS, preventing interception on plaintext connections. SameSite controls cross-site sending: Strict never sends on cross-site requests, Lax (the modern default) sends only on top-level GET navigations, and None sends always but requires Secure — SameSite is your primary in-cookie CSRF mitigation.

qa.q: Why does serving the React build from the same Express server simplify auth?
qa.a: Same-origin means the browser treats the SPA and API as one origin, so CORS disappears entirely and you avoid the credentials/preflight configuration. Cookies are first-party, letting you use the strictest SameSite=Strict without breaking requests, and there's no cross-site cookie concern. The trade-off is deployment coupling — you give up independently scaling/CDN-hosting the frontend, so large teams often still split origins and pay the CORS cost.

qa.q: Where should an SPA store an access token, and why not localStorage by default?
qa.a: The most XSS-resistant place is an HttpOnly cookie, so JS never touches the token; if you must use header-based auth, keeping the token in memory (a JS variable/closure) is safer than localStorage because it dies on tab close and isn't persisted for theft. localStorage persists and is fully readable by any script, so one XSS gives long-lived token access. A common pattern is a short-lived access token in memory plus a refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie.

bullets.point: The set of mechanisms by which a client and server (or server-to-server) exchange data — request/response, push, and streaming — over transports like HTTP, TCP, and WebSockets. It matters because choosing the wrong pattern (e.g., polling where you need push) is the root cause of latency, cost, and scaling problems; interviewers probe whether you can map a product requirement (chat, notifications, dashboards) to the right communication model.

The main models are synchronous request/response (HTTP REST, gRPC), short/long polling, Server-Sent Events (unidirectional server→client push over HTTP), and WebSockets (full-duplex bidirectional). The interview angle is trade-offs: latency vs overhead, directionality, firewall/proxy friendliness, and reconnection/backpressure — e.g., SSE is simpler and auto-reconnects but is one-way, while WebSockets are bidirectional but need custom heartbeat/reconnect logic.

A protocol (RFC 6455) that starts as an HTTP request with an `Upgrade: websocket` header, then switches the same TCP connection to a persistent full-duplex channel using a lightweight framing protocol. It matters for low-latency, high-frequency bidirectional workloads (chat, live trading, multiplayer, collaborative editing); expect questions on the handshake, framing, `ping/pong` heartbeats, and how it differs from HTTP/2 streams and SSE.

Node's single-threaded event loop and non-blocking I/O make it well-suited to holding thousands of concurrent socket connections cheaply, which is why `ws` and Socket.IO are common choices. The angle: understand that each connection is an in-memory object tied to one process, so connection state doesn't automatically share across instances — a fact that dictates the scaling story later.

The `ws` library gives a bare `WebSocketServer` where you handle `connection`, `message`, `close`, and `error` events and call `socket.send()`; you own message parsing (typically JSON), heartbeats via `ping`/`pong` + `terminate` on dead sockets, and any rooms/broadcast logic. Socket.IO layers reconnection, rooms, and fallbacks on top — knowing when the raw `ws` primitive suffices vs when Socket.IO's abstractions earn their overhead is the practical judgment tested.

The browser `WebSocket` API (`new WebSocket(url)`, `onopen/onmessage/onclose/onerror`, `send()`) plus a client-owned reconnect-with-backoff loop and a message/event protocol agreed with the server. The interview point is resilience: browsers don't auto-reconnect raw WebSockets, so you must handle exponential backoff, resubscribing after reconnect, and buffering or dropping messages sent while disconnected.

Next.js is oriented around serverless/edge request-response and does not host long-lived WebSocket connections in its standard API routes or on typical serverless platforms; you run WebSockets on a separate long-running Node server (or use a managed service like Pusher/Ably) and connect from the client. The angle is architectural: recognizing that stateful persistent connections don't fit a stateless serverless deployment model and separating the real-time tier from the SSR/API tier.

Because connections and room state live in a single process's memory, scaling horizontally requires sticky sessions at the load balancer plus a shared backplane (usually Redis pub/sub) so a message published on one instance reaches subscribers connected to another. Expect deep questions on the Redis pub/sub adapter, sticky routing (or a stateless token approach), handling reconnect storms, and observability of connection counts — this is the highest-signal part of the topic for an SDE-2.

example.title: Live order-tracking + support chat for a food-delivery app
example.body: You're building the customer app's real-time layer: a map that updates the courier's position every few seconds and a support chat, both needing sub-second server→client push. You reject long-polling (too much HTTP overhead at scale) and pick WebSockets for the bidirectional chat while noting SSE would suffice for one-way courier updates. The app is deployed on Next.js/Vercel for SSR and REST APIs, but since serverless can't hold persistent connections, you stand up a dedicated Node `ws` server behind a load balancer. The browser client opens a WebSocket, authenticates with a JWT on connect, subscribes to its order channel, and runs exponential-backoff reconnect that re-subscribes on reopen. As traffic grows you run multiple `ws` instances with sticky sessions and a Redis pub/sub backplane, so a courier-location event ingested on instance A is fanned out to the customer connected to instance B. You add `ping/pong` heartbeats to reap dead connections and export a live connection-count metric to catch reconnect storms after a deploy.

qa.q: When would you choose Server-Sent Events over WebSockets, and vice versa?
qa.a: Choose SSE when you only need server→client push (live feeds, notifications, progress) because it runs over plain HTTP, auto-reconnects with `Last-Event-ID`, and is proxy/firewall friendly with almost no client code. Choose WebSockets when you need low-latency bidirectional traffic (chat, multiplayer, collaborative editing) or binary frames. SSE is limited by browser per-domain HTTP/1.1 connection caps (mitigated under HTTP/2) and is text-only, while WebSockets require you to build heartbeats and reconnection yourself.

qa.q: Walk me through the WebSocket handshake.
qa.a: The client sends a normal HTTP GET with `Connection: Upgrade`, `Upgrade: websocket`, a `Sec-WebSocket-Key` (random base64), and `Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13`. The server responds `101 Switching Protocols` with `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`, which is the SHA-1 of the client key concatenated with a fixed GUID, base64-encoded — proving it understood the protocol. After that the same TCP connection is reused for full-duplex, length-prefixed frames rather than HTTP messages. Because it begins as HTTP, it can share port 443 and pass through most proxies.

qa.q: Why can't you just run WebSockets inside Next.js API routes on Vercel?
qa.a: Next.js API routes on serverless/edge platforms are request-scoped: the function spins up, handles one HTTP request, and is torn down, so it can't hold a long-lived, stateful socket. WebSockets need a persistent process that owns connection state in memory for the connection's lifetime. The standard pattern is to keep Next.js for SSR/REST and run a separate long-running Node WebSocket server (or a managed real-time service like Ably/Pusher), with the client connecting to that endpoint directly.

qa.q: How do you scale a WebSocket service horizontally across multiple instances?
qa.a: Connections are pinned to whichever process accepted them and their room state is in that process's memory, so you need two things: sticky sessions (or connection-level routing) at the load balancer so a client's traffic keeps hitting the same instance, and a shared backplane — typically Redis pub/sub — so an event produced on one instance is published and delivered to subscribers connected to any other instance. Socket.IO's Redis adapter implements exactly this fan-out. You also plan for reconnect storms on deploy and monitor per-instance connection counts.

qa.q: How do you detect and clean up dead WebSocket connections?
qa.a: TCP can keep a socket 'open' long after the peer vanished (network drop, sleeping laptop), so you implement application-level heartbeats: the server periodically sends a `ping`, marks the socket alive on the corresponding `pong`, and on the next interval `terminate()`s any socket that didn't respond. In the `ws` library this is the classic `isAlive` flag pattern on an interval. Without this you leak memory and file descriptors and may broadcast to sockets that will never receive the data.

qa.q: How should the client handle reconnection, and what are the pitfalls?
qa.a: The browser `WebSocket` does not auto-reconnect, so the client must reopen on `close`/`error` using exponential backoff with jitter to avoid a thundering-herd reconnect storm after a server restart. On reconnect it must re-authenticate and re-subscribe to any channels/rooms, and decide how to handle messages produced while offline — either buffer-and-flush with server-side resumption (sequence numbers / last-event-id) or accept a gap and resync state. Assuming the socket is always up is the most common bug.

qa.q: What are the trade-offs between the raw `ws` library and Socket.IO?
qa.a: `ws` is a thin, fast, standards-compliant WebSocket implementation — you get raw frames and must build reconnection, heartbeats, rooms, and any fallback yourself, which is ideal when you want control and minimal overhead. Socket.IO is a higher-level framework adding automatic reconnection, rooms/namespaces, acknowledgements, a Redis adapter for scaling, and an HTTP long-polling fallback, at the cost of a custom (non-WebSocket) wire protocol requiring its client. Pick `ws` for lean, custom protocols or non-browser clients; pick Socket.IO when its batteries-included features would otherwise be reinvented.

qa.q: How do you authenticate and authorize a WebSocket connection?
qa.a: Authenticate at connect time — pass a token (e.g., JWT) via a query param, subprotocol header, or an initial message, and validate it in the server's `connection`/`upgrade` handler before accepting; reject with a close code if invalid. Since the connection is long-lived, also handle token expiry by re-validating periodically or forcing reconnect, and enforce per-message authorization for room joins/actions rather than trusting the initial auth forever. Never rely on cookies alone cross-origin, and validate origin to mitigate cross-site WebSocket hijacking.

bullets.point: The landscape splits into relational (Postgres, MySQL), document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis, DynamoDB), wide-column (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j), and search (Elasticsearch) engines. The interview angle is knowing that the choice is driven by access patterns and consistency needs, not popularity — you pick the store whose data model matches how you read/write, then justify the trade-off.

NoSQL trades ACID guarantees and ad-hoc joins for horizontal scale and flexible schemas, which becomes a liability when your data is highly relational and you need strong consistency and cross-entity queries. The angle: NoSQL isn't 'schemaless,' it just pushes the schema into application code and denormalizes for a fixed query pattern, so unplanned queries and integrity enforcement get expensive.

SQL databases give you a declarative query language, enforced schemas, foreign-key integrity, and ACID transactions — invaluable when correctness and complex relationships matter (payments, inventory, users). Modern Postgres/MySQL also scale vertically and via read replicas far further than most teams need, so 'SQL doesn't scale' is usually premature.

A database is the top-level container/namespace holding tables, indexes, and other objects, created with CREATE DATABASE and typically owned by a role with its own connection/permissions. Practically it matters for isolation and access control — separate services or environments should not share one database, and connection strings target a specific db.

Applications talk to the DB through a client library (e.g. node-postgres `pg`, `mysql2`) that opens TCP connections, handles the wire protocol, and — critically — manages a connection pool so you reuse a bounded set of connections instead of opening one per request. The interview point is pooling: opening connections is expensive and databases cap max connections, so an unpooled or leaking app will exhaust the server under load.

CREATE TABLE declares columns with types, plus constraints — PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, DEFAULT, CHECK, and FOREIGN KEY. The schema is your first and cheapest line of defense for data integrity; pushing invariants into constraints (rather than trusting app code) prevents whole classes of corrupt-data bugs.

The four DML operations — INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE — plus WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT form the core of interacting with data. The SDE-2 nuance is that UPDATE/DELETE without a WHERE clause hit every row, and reads should lean on indexes and pagination rather than pulling full tables.

From Node you send SQL through the driver using parameterized queries (`pool.query('... WHERE id = $1', [id])`) and await the result set. Always parameterize — string-concatenating user input is the classic SQL-injection vector, and prepared statements also let the DB cache the query plan.

A minimal service wires an HTTP layer (Express/Fastify) to a shared connection pool created once at startup, with route handlers issuing queries and mapping rows to JSON. Key concerns: initialize the pool once (not per request), handle async errors so a failed query doesn't crash the process, and close the pool on graceful shutdown.

Relational modeling expresses one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many links via foreign keys — the many-to-many case needs a join/junction table holding both foreign keys. This is where normalization lives: model entities once and reference them, so an interviewer expects you to design a schema (e.g. users, orders, products) and place the FKs correctly.

JOINs combine rows across tables on a matching condition — INNER (only matches), LEFT/RIGHT (keep all rows from one side, NULLs for the other), and FULL. The depth question is performance: joins should run on indexed keys, and you should be able to reason about why an unindexed join or a join fanning out a many-to-many can explode row counts and latency.

example.title: An order-management API for a small e-commerce backend
example.body: You're building a Node.js/Express service backed by Postgres for a store. You model users, products, orders, and order_items, where an order belongs to one user (one-to-many) and links to many products through order_items (many-to-many with quantity and price-at-purchase). At startup you create a single `pg` connection pool from an env-based connection string. A `POST /orders` handler wraps an INSERT into orders plus multiple INSERTs into order_items inside a transaction (BEGIN/COMMIT), so a partial failure rolls back and never leaves an order with no items. A `GET /users/:id/orders` handler runs a parameterized query joining orders, order_items, and products to return each order with its line items. When it gets slow in production, you EXPLAIN ANALYZE the query, discover a sequential scan on order_items.order_id, add an index there, and watch the join drop from hundreds of milliseconds to single digits — the concrete payoff of schema design, foreign keys, transactions, parameterization, and indexed joins all in one flow.

qa.q: When would you choose a NoSQL document store over a relational database, and what do you give up?
qa.a: Choose a document store when your access pattern is a single aggregate fetched/written by key (e.g. a user's whole profile document), the schema varies per record, and you need easy horizontal sharding for write scale. You give up cross-entity joins, foreign-key integrity, and multi-document ACID transactions (or get weaker/limited versions), so any query the data wasn't denormalized for becomes an expensive application-side join. The rule of thumb: relational when data is highly connected and correctness-critical; document when it's self-contained and scale/flexibility dominate.

qa.q: Why must application queries be parameterized, and what does the driver actually do?
qa.a: Parameterization separates the SQL text from the data values, so user input can never alter the query structure — this is the primary defense against SQL injection. The driver sends the query with placeholders and the arguments separately; the database treats arguments strictly as values, and can also cache the prepared plan for reuse. String-concatenating input is unsafe even with escaping because edge cases and encoding issues slip through.

qa.q: Explain the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN with a concrete failure case.
qa.a: INNER JOIN returns only rows with a match on both sides; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, filling NULLs where the right has no match. A classic bug: querying users and their orders with INNER JOIN silently drops users who have zero orders, so a 'customers report' undercounts. If you also put a right-table condition in the WHERE clause of a LEFT JOIN, you accidentally turn it back into an inner join because NULLs fail the predicate — the condition belongs in the ON clause instead.

qa.q: How do you model a many-to-many relationship, and why not just use an array column?
qa.a: You introduce a junction (join) table holding a foreign key to each side, often with a composite primary key on the pair plus any relationship attributes (e.g. quantity, added_at). This keeps referential integrity in both directions, lets you index and join efficiently, and supports querying from either entity. An array/JSON column can't enforce that referenced IDs exist, can't be joined or indexed as cleanly, and forces read-modify-write updates that race under concurrency.

qa.q: What is a connection pool and why does it matter in a Node.js service?
qa.a: A pool maintains a fixed set of open database connections that request handlers borrow and return, instead of opening a new TCP connection and re-authenticating per query. It matters because connection setup is expensive and databases enforce a hard max_connections limit, so an unpooled or connection-leaking app exhausts the server and stalls under load. You size the pool to the DB's capacity divided across app instances, and you must release clients back (especially after errors) to avoid leaks.

qa.q: How do ACID transactions help, and when would you reach for one?
qa.a: A transaction groups statements so they commit all-or-nothing (atomicity), leave the DB in a valid state (consistency), don't interfere via isolation, and survive crashes once committed (durability). You reach for one whenever a logical operation spans multiple writes that must not partially apply — creating an order plus its line items, or transferring a balance between two accounts. Without it, a mid-operation failure or concurrent writer can leave orphaned or double-counted data.

qa.q: A query joining two large tables is slow. How do you diagnose and fix it?
qa.a: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see the actual plan and row counts; a sequential scan on the join key or a huge estimated-vs-actual row gap points at the problem. The most common fix is adding an index on the foreign-key/join column (and on WHERE-filter columns) so the planner can use an index or hash join instead of scanning. Also check for accidental row fan-out from a one-to-many join inflating results, stale table statistics, and whether you're selecting more columns/rows than needed — add pagination and select only what you use.

qa.q: What does normalization buy you, and when would you deliberately denormalize?
qa.a: Normalization stores each fact once and references it, which eliminates update anomalies and keeps writes consistent — change a product name in one row, not thousands. You deliberately denormalize for read-heavy paths where joins are too costly, precomputing or duplicating data (e.g. a cached order_total or a materialized view) and accepting the burden of keeping copies in sync. It's a conscious read-vs-write and consistency trade-off, not a default.

bullets.point: Sets up the two distinct Redis capabilities covered: durable-ish list-based queues (producer/worker fan-in) and fire-and-forget Pub/Sub (fan-out messaging). Interview angle: know which primitive fits which problem — queues for work distribution, Pub/Sub for real-time notifications — they are not interchangeable.

Redis is an in-memory, single-threaded key-value data store that also ships higher-level structures (lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, streams) and messaging primitives. It matters because sub-millisecond latency and the single-threaded command loop mean every command is atomic, which is why it doubles as a cache, queue, and coordination layer.

Spin up Redis via Docker (`docker run -p 6379:6379 redis`) or a native install, and inspect it with `redis-cli`. The interview-relevant detail is the default port 6379 and that redis-cli lets you verify queue/pubsub behavior manually before wiring code.

bullets.step: Redis as a database / KV store
bullets.point: Basic `SET`/`GET`, exp_iry with `EXPIRE`/`SET ... EX`, and the caveat that data lives in RAM. It matters because you must reason about persistence (RDB snapshots vs AOF log) and eviction policies — Redis is durable only if configured for it, otherwise a restart loses data.

Use a list as a FIFO queue: producers `LPUSH` jobs, workers `RPOP` (or the blocking `BRPOP`) to pull them. This matters for offloading slow work (e.g. code execution, email sending) to background workers; the blocking pop avoids busy-polling and lets multiple workers compete for jobs with each job delivered to exactly one worker.

Connect with a client (`redis` or `ioredis`), `await client.connect()`, then call the same commands (`lPush`, `brPop`, `set`, `get`) as promises. Key interview point: a blocking `BRPOP` needs its own dedicated connection, since a connection blocked on a pop cannot issue other commands concurrently.

Publishers `PUBLISH channel message`; subscribers `SUBSCRIBE channel` and receive pushed messages. Critically it is fire-and-forget with no persistence or acknowledgement — a message is delivered only to clients connected at publish time, and offline subscribers miss it entirely. That trade-off (broadcast fan-out vs no durability) is the single most common interview question here.

A subscriber connection must be duplicated/dedicated (`client.duplicate()`) because once subscribed it enters subscriber mode and cannot run normal commands. You then register a callback per channel to react to incoming messages — the pattern for pushing real-time updates back to a WebSocket/SSE layer.

Consolidates the pieces into an end-to-end system: a primary server enqueues work, a worker pool consumes the queue, processes jobs, and publishes results back over Pub/Sub. The exercise cements when to reach for a queue (work distribution, one consumer per job) versus Pub/Sub (broadcast, many consumers).

example.title: A LeetCode-style code-submission pipeline
example.body: A user submits code on your judge platform. The primary API server shouldn't run untrusted code inline (slow, unsafe), so it `LPUSH`es a submission job {submissionId, language, code} onto a Redis list `problems` and immediately returns 202 Accepted. A pool of stateless worker processes each block on `BRPOP problems`, so exactly one worker grabs each job, runs it in a sandbox, and computes the verdict. When done, the worker `PUBLISH`es the result to a channel like `result:<submissionId>`. The WebSocket/SSE gateway that the browser is connected to has `SUBSCRIBE`d to that channel and pushes the verdict back to the user in real time. This cleanly separates the two primitives: the list is a durable-ish work queue giving load-balanced, exactly-one-consumer delivery, while Pub/Sub gives low-latency fan-out to whichever gateway currently holds the user's live connection. An SDE-2 debugging this must know that if the gateway subscribes after the worker publishes, the result is lost — which is why the verdict is also persisted (e.g. `SET submission:<id> <verdict>`) so a reconnecting client can fetch it, and for stronger delivery guarantees you'd graduate the queue to Redis Streams with consumer groups.

qa.q: When would you use Redis Pub/Sub versus a Redis list-based queue?
qa.a: Use a queue (LPUSH/BRPOP) for work distribution where each job must be processed by exactly one consumer and shouldn't be lost if no worker is momentarily free — the job waits in the list. Use Pub/Sub for broadcast/fan-out where every currently-connected subscriber should get the message and staleness is acceptable, e.g. real-time notifications or cache-invalidation signals. The decisive difference is delivery semantics: queues buffer and deliver once to one consumer; Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget to all connected subscribers with no buffering.

qa.q: What are the delivery guarantees of Redis Pub/Sub, and what breaks because of them?
qa.a: Redis Pub/Sub is at-most-once with no persistence: a published message is delivered only to clients subscribed at that instant, and anyone offline or reconnecting misses it — there's no replay. It also has no acknowledgement, so a slow subscriber can have messages dropped once its output buffer overflows. This makes plain Pub/Sub unsuitable as a reliable message bus; for durability and replay you use Redis Streams with consumer groups (XADD/XREADGROUP/XACK) or a real broker like Kafka/RabbitMQ.

qa.q: Why is BRPOP preferred over a polling loop of RPOP, and what's the connection caveat?
qa.a: BRPOP blocks server-side until an item is available (or a timeout), so workers get jobs with near-zero latency and without hammering Redis with empty RPOP calls that waste CPU and network. The caveat is that a blocked connection can't do anything else — so in Node you must give the blocking consumer its own dedicated connection and keep your normal request-serving connection separate. Multiple workers can BRPOP the same key and Redis hands each pushed item to exactly one of them.

qa.q: Redis is single-threaded — how does that affect using it as a queue and why isn't it a bottleneck?
qa.a: The single command-execution thread makes every operation atomic, so LPUSH/BRPOP need no external locking and there are no partial-read race conditions on the list. It's rarely a bottleneck because commands are in-memory and O(1), so a single instance handles well over 100k ops/sec; the real limits are network round-trips and large payloads, mitigated with pipelining and keeping values small. If you do saturate one core, you scale out with sharding/Redis Cluster rather than threads.

qa.q: How do you make Redis-based messaging survive a restart or crash?
qa.a: Plain Pub/Sub can't — messages are never persisted. For queues, list contents are persisted according to your durability config: RDB gives point-in-time snapshots (fast, but you can lose the seconds since the last snapshot) while AOF logs every write and replays it on restart (more durable, larger/slower). For messaging that needs replay and consumer offsets you use Redis Streams, which retain entries and track per-consumer-group acknowledgements, so a crashed worker's un-ACKed messages can be reclaimed via XPENDING/XCLAIM.

qa.q: A subscriber connection can't run normal commands — why, and how do you handle it in Node?
qa.a: Once a connection issues SUBSCRIBE it enters 'subscriber mode' and the protocol only permits (un)subscribe commands plus receiving pushed messages, so you can't GET/SET on it. In Node with node-redis or ioredis you call `client.duplicate()` (or open a second client) to get a dedicated subscriber connection and keep the original for regular commands. You then attach a message callback per channel; forgetting the separate connection is a classic bug that surfaces as 'commands not allowed in subscriber mode' errors.

qa.q: How would you scale Pub/Sub across multiple backend instances behind a load balancer?
qa.a: The problem is that a user's WebSocket lives on only one app instance, but the worker that finishes their job may publish from anywhere — so every app instance subscribes to Redis, and Redis fans the published message out to all of them; the instance holding that user's socket forwards it, the rest ignore it. This is exactly the model Socket.IO's Redis adapter uses. Note that in Redis Cluster, standard PUBLISH only propagates within shard boundaries unless you use sharded Pub/Sub (SPUBLISH/SSUBSCRIBE) or route pub/sub through a single logical channel, which is a common scaling gotcha.

qa.q: What are the risks of using a Redis list as your only queue, and when do you outgrow it?
qa.a: A list gives you FIFO and one-consumer-per-job, but no built-in acknowledgement, retry, dead-letter, or visibility timeout — if a worker BRPOPs a job and then crashes mid-processing, that job is silently gone. You also can't inspect in-flight work or guarantee at-least-once without extra bookkeeping (e.g. RPOPLPUSH into a processing list, then removing on completion). You outgrow it when you need reliable redelivery, ordering guarantees per key, consumer groups, or high throughput with replay — at which point you move to Redis Streams or a dedicated broker like RabbitMQ/Kafka/SQS.

bullets.point: NextAuth (now Auth.js) is an authentication library for Next.js that manages the full auth lifecycle: OAuth/OIDC flows, sessions, CSRF protection, and provider callbacks. It matters because it removes the need to hand-roll cookie handling, token rotation, and provider redirect dances — the parts of auth most likely to be built insecurely. Interview angle: know that it's provider-agnostic and can persist sessions either as a stateless JWT or as DB-backed sessions via an adapter.

Storing a JWT in localStorage exposes it to any injected script, so a single XSS turns into full account takeover with no way to revoke the leaked token before expiry. The correct pattern is an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie that JS cannot read, which is exactly what NextAuth sets by default. Interview angle: articulate the XSS-vs-CSRF trade-off — httpOnly cookies neutralize token theft via XSS but need CSRF defenses (SameSite + CSRF token), which NextAuth handles.

The library is configured through an options object (providers, session strategy, callbacks, custom pages) that you export from the auth handler. This central config is where session strategy (`jwt` vs `database`), the `jwt`/`session` callbacks for enriching tokens, and secret signing are wired. Interview angle: the `session` and `jwt` callbacks are the standard place to attach role/userId claims onto the session the client sees.

Next.js dynamic routes with `[...slug]` (catch-all) or `[[...slug]]` (optional catch-all) match an arbitrary number of path segments and expose them as an array. This matters because auth needs many sub-endpoints (`/signin`, `/callback/:provider`, `/session`, `/signout`, `/csrf`) served from one file. Interview angle: know the difference between `[param]` (one segment), `[...param]` (one or more), and `[[...param]]` (zero or more).

You register the handler at `pages/api/auth/[...nextauth].js` (or `app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts` in the App Router) so every `/api/auth/*` request is routed into NextAuth. This single mount point is what lets the library own signin, OAuth callbacks, session reads, and signout without you defining each route. Interview angle: the callback/redirect URLs you register with OAuth providers must point at `/api/auth/callback/<provider>` under this path.

CredentialsProvider lets you authenticate against your own store (email/password, LDAP, etc.) via an `authorize(credentials)` function that returns a user object or null. It matters when you can't or won't outsource identity to an OAuth provider. Interview angle: using Credentials forces the `jwt` session strategy (DB sessions aren't supported for it), and you are responsible for password hashing (bcrypt/argon2) and rate limiting — NextAuth does not do that for you.

GoogleProvider wires up OAuth2/OIDC with Google using a `clientId`/`clientSecret` from the Google Cloud console and an authorized redirect URI of `/api/auth/callback/google`. It matters for frictionless social login and because Google returns verified email/profile claims. Interview angle: be ready to explain the OAuth authorization-code flow, why the client secret must stay server-side, and how `allowDangerousEmailAccountLinking` / account-linking behaves when the same email arrives from two providers.

GitHubProvider is the same OAuth pattern registered via a GitHub OAuth App, with callback `/api/auth/callback/github`. The reuse here is the point: adding providers is largely declarative config, and NextAuth normalizes each provider's profile into a common user shape. Interview angle: note that GitHub may not return a public email unless you request the `user:email` scope, a common real-world gotcha.

NextAuth ships default auth pages but you override them via the `pages` option (`signIn`, `error`, etc.) to render your own branded UI, then call `signIn('credentials', {...})` from it. Signup specifically is not a NextAuth concept — you build a separate registration endpoint that creates+hashes the user, then sign them in. Interview angle: distinguish sign-IN (NextAuth's job) from sign-UP (your job); the custom page just replaces the default UI and hands credentials to `signIn`.

example.title: Adding social + password login to a B2B SaaS dashboard
example.body: You're an SDE-2 shipping auth for an internal analytics dashboard. Marketing users want 'Sign in with Google', engineers prefer GitHub, and a few service accounts need email/password. You mount NextAuth at `app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts`, register GoogleProvider and GitHubProvider with credentials pulled from env vars (redirect URIs `/api/auth/callback/google` and `/api/auth/callback/github`), and add a CredentialsProvider whose `authorize` looks up the user and verifies an argon2 hash. You switch the session strategy to `jwt`, and in the `jwt`/`session` callbacks you attach `userId` and `role` so route guards can authorize without a DB round-trip. You override the `pages.signIn` option to render your branded login screen, and build a separate `/signup` API route that creates the user (hashing the password) before calling `signIn('credentials')`. During testing you hit the classic bug: GitHub logins arrive with `email: null` until you add the `user:email` scope, and you confirm the session cookie is httpOnly+Secure so a reflected-XSS attempt can't exfiltrate the token.

qa.q: Why are httpOnly cookies preferred over localStorage for session tokens, and what new risk do they introduce?
qa.a: localStorage is readable by any JavaScript on the page, so an XSS injection can steal the token and impersonate the user with no revocation possible. An httpOnly cookie is invisible to JS, closing that exfiltration path, and adding Secure ensures it's only sent over HTTPS. The trade-off is CSRF: because cookies are sent automatically on cross-site requests, you need SameSite=Lax/Strict plus a CSRF token — both of which NextAuth applies by default.

qa.q: What is the difference between NextAuth's `jwt` and `database` session strategies, and when do you pick each?
qa.a: With the `jwt` strategy the session lives entirely in a signed, encrypted cookie — stateless, fast, and scales horizontally with no session store, but you can't easily revoke a session before it expires. The `database` strategy stores sessions server-side via an adapter, giving instant revocation and server-side introspection at the cost of a DB read per request. Choose `jwt` for stateless/edge-friendly apps or when using CredentialsProvider (which requires it); choose `database` when you need immediate logout/revocation or centralized session control.

qa.q: Why does NextAuth use a catch-all route, and what would break without it?
qa.a: Authentication requires many sibling endpoints — signin, per-provider OAuth callbacks, session, signout, csrf — and the catch-all `[...nextauth]` lets a single handler own all of `/api/auth/*`. Without it you'd have to define each endpoint manually and keep them in sync with the library's expectations. The provider redirect URIs (`/api/auth/callback/<provider>`) specifically resolve into this catch-all, so OAuth callbacks would 404 without it.

qa.q: What are your responsibilities when using CredentialsProvider that NextAuth does not handle?
qa.a: NextAuth only calls your `authorize` function and manages the resulting session; it does not store users, hash passwords, or throttle attempts. You must hash with bcrypt/argon2 and compare in constant time, enforce rate limiting and lockout to blunt brute force, and validate input yourself. You're also locked into the `jwt` session strategy, since database sessions aren't supported for credentials.

qa.q: How do you get custom data like a user role or ID into the session the client reads?
qa.a: Use the `jwt` and `session` callbacks. In the `jwt` callback you enrich the token (e.g. `token.role = user.role`) on initial sign-in, and in the `session` callback you copy it onto `session.user` so it's exposed to the client. This avoids extra DB lookups on every request when using the JWT strategy, and keeps authorization claims flowing through to middleware and route guards.

qa.q: Walk through the OAuth authorization-code flow that fires when a user clicks 'Sign in with Google'.
qa.a: NextAuth redirects the browser to Google's authorization endpoint with the client ID, requested scopes, redirect URI, and a state parameter for CSRF protection. The user consents, and Google redirects back to `/api/auth/callback/google` with a short-lived authorization code. NextAuth exchanges that code (plus the client secret, server-side) for access/ID tokens, verifies them, normalizes the profile into a user, and establishes the session. The client secret never touches the browser, which is why the exchange happens on the server.

qa.q: A user signs in with Google and later with GitHub using the same email. What happens, and what's the concern?
qa.a: By default NextAuth treats them as distinct accounts (keyed by provider + provider account id) to avoid silently linking identities. You can enable automatic linking, but only when the provider verifies the email — `allowDangerousEmailAccountLinking` is named that way because linking on an unverified email lets an attacker who controls an OAuth app claim someone else's account. The safe pattern is to link only verified emails, or require the user to confirm the link while already authenticated.

qa.q: How is 'sign up' different from 'sign in' in NextAuth, and how do you implement a custom signup page?
qa.a: NextAuth handles sign-IN — verifying an identity and creating a session — but has no built-in registration concept. You override `pages.signIn` to render your own UI, but for signup you build a separate endpoint that creates the user record and hashes the password, then call `signIn('credentials', ...)` to log them in immediately after. For OAuth providers there's effectively no separate signup: first successful login provisions the account (often via an adapter's `createUser`).

bullets.point: Languages sit on axes of static vs dynamic typing (when types are checked: compile-time vs runtime) and strong vs weak typing (how strictly type rules are enforced). TS adds a static, structural type layer over JS, which is dynamically and weakly typed; the interview angle is knowing TS types are erased at compile time and provide zero runtime guarantees — validation at trust boundaries (API responses, user input) still needs runtime checks like zod.

TS is a strict syntactic superset of JavaScript that adds optional static typing and compiles down to plain JS — all valid JS is valid TS. It matters because it catches type errors, enables safe refactoring, and powers editor tooling (autocomplete, go-to-def) without changing runtime behavior; the key nuance is that its type system is structural (duck-typed), not nominal.

tsc transpiles TS to JS for a chosen target (ES version) and performs type checking; the two jobs are separable — you can emit JS even with type errors (unless noEmitOnError) or type-check without emitting (--noEmit for CI). Interview angle: in real builds tsc is often used only for type-checking while esbuild/swc/Babel do the actual (type-stripping) transpile, because they're far faster but do NOT type-check.

Core primitives (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol) plus TS-specific any, unknown, never, and void. The discriminating knowledge is any (opts out of checking — avoid) vs unknown (type-safe top type requiring narrowing before use) vs never (bottom type for unreachable/exhaustiveness), and leaning on inference rather than annotating everything.

tsconfig.json defines compiler options, target/module, and file includes for a project. The interview-relevant flags are the strict family — especially strictNullChecks (separates null/undefined from other types) and noImplicitAny — plus target, module/moduleResolution, and paths; knowing why strict: true is the sane default and what each strict sub-flag buys you signals maturity.

Interfaces describe the shape of objects and classes and support declaration merging and extends-based inheritance. They matter for defining contracts (API DTOs, class implements); the classic interview point is interface vs type — interfaces are open (mergeable, better for public API surfaces and OOP), types are closed but more expressive (unions, intersections, mapped/conditional types).

type aliases name any type: primitives, unions, intersections, tuples, and computed/mapped/conditional types that interfaces can't express. They're the tool for union modeling (e.g. discriminated unions) and type-level programming; unlike interfaces they cannot be reopened/merged, which is often a feature (prevents accidental augmentation).

Arrays typed as T[] or Array<T>, with readonly T[] for immutability and fixed-length tuples [string, number] for positional data. The nuance is tuples model heterogeneous positional structures (like React useState returns) and support labels and rest elements; also knowing that TS arrays are not bounds-checked so arr[i] is typed T even when the index is out of range (unless noUncheckedIndexedAccess).

Enums define named constant sets; numeric enums are bidirectional (reverse-mapped) while string enums are not. The important trade-off: enums emit real runtime objects (increasing bundle size and breaking type-only erasure), so many teams prefer union string literal types or `as const` objects — const enums inline values but interact poorly with isolatedModules/bundlers.

Generics parameterize types over other types for reusable, type-safe abstractions (containers, functions, higher-order utilities) without resorting to any. Depth points: generic constraints (T extends X), default type params, and inference from arguments; mastery shows in reading/writing utility types (Partial, Pick, Record) which are generics plus mapped/conditional types.

TS uses ES module syntax (import/export, named and default) and resolves modules per moduleResolution. Interview-relevant: import type / export type for type-only imports (elided at compile so no runtime cost and safe under isolatedModules), the CommonJS vs ESM interop story (esModuleInterop), and how module vs the older namespace differs.

example.title: Typing a payments API client end-to-end
example.body: You're building a shared TypeScript SDK that other teams import to call your payments service. You model the wire contracts with interfaces for DTOs (PaymentRequest, PaymentResponse) and a discriminated union type for results — type Result = { status: 'succeeded'; id: string } | { status: 'failed'; error: string } | { status: 'pending' } — so a switch on status gives compile-time exhaustiveness (the default branch assigns to a never to catch newly added states). A string-literal union (or `as const` object) replaces an enum for currency codes to keep the bundle lean and erasable. The core request function is generic — request<TReq, TRes>(path, body: TReq): Promise<TRes> — reusing built-ins like Pick and Partial for update payloads. Crucially, because tsc types are erased at runtime, you validate the fetched JSON with a runtime schema (zod) at the network boundary and treat parsed data as unknown until narrowed, rather than trusting an as cast. tsconfig runs with strict: true and noUncheckedIndexedAccess; CI runs tsc --noEmit for type-checking while esbuild does the fast transpile, and all cross-file DTO imports use import type so nothing leaks into the emitted JS.

qa.q: interface vs type — when do you reach for each?
qa.a: Both describe object shapes and are largely interchangeable there, but interfaces support declaration merging and are conventionally better for public/extensible API surfaces and class contracts (implements/extends). type aliases are closed (no merging) and strictly more expressive: unions, intersections, tuples, and mapped/conditional types can only be written as types. A common rule is interfaces for object/class contracts, types for unions and type-level computation; the merging behavior of interfaces is the deciding factor when you need — or want to forbid — later augmentation.

qa.q: any vs unknown vs never — explain the difference.
qa.a: any disables type checking entirely and is assignable both ways, so it silently propagates unsafety — avoid it. unknown is the type-safe top type: anything is assignable to it, but you can't use it until you narrow (typeof/instanceof/guards), making it the correct type for untrusted input like JSON. never is the bottom type — no value inhabits it — used for functions that never return (throw/infinite loop) and for compile-time exhaustiveness checks in discriminated unions.

qa.q: Are TypeScript types available at runtime? What's the implication?
qa.a: No — types are fully erased during compilation; the emitted JS has no type information (interfaces, type aliases, generics all vanish). The implication is you cannot make runtime decisions based on types and must not trust `as` casts on external data. At trust boundaries (API responses, form input, message queues) you need runtime validation (zod, io-ts, manual guards) to actually enforce shapes; TS only guarantees consistency of your own code, not the shape of data crossing the boundary.

qa.q: Why might a team avoid enums in favor of union literal types or `as const`?
qa.a: Enums emit a real runtime object, which adds bundle size and breaks the otherwise clean type-erasure model, and numeric enums add reverse mappings you rarely want. const enums inline values but are disallowed/awkward under isolatedModules and many bundlers (swc/esbuild/Babel transpile files in isolation). String-literal unions ('a' | 'b') or `as const` objects give the same type safety with zero or minimal runtime footprint and full erasability, which is why they're often preferred; enums still shine when you want a single named namespace of related constants with runtime iteration.

qa.q: What does strictNullChecks change, and why is it important?
qa.a: With it off, null and undefined are assignable to every type, so the compiler can't catch null-dereference bugs — the biggest source of runtime errors. With it on, null and undefined are their own types that must be explicitly included in a union (string | null) and narrowed before use, forcing you to handle the empty case. It's the single most valuable strict flag; enabling it on a legacy codebase is the hard part of adopting strict mode and typically surfaces many latent bugs.

qa.q: Explain generic constraints and give a case where you need one.
qa.a: A constraint (T extends SomeShape) restricts what types can be substituted for a type parameter, letting you access members of T safely inside the generic. For example, function getProp<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] — the K extends keyof T constraint guarantees key is a valid property and lets the return type be precisely T[K]. Without the constraint you'd only have an opaque T and couldn't index into it or would fall back to any, losing the type link between input and output.

qa.q: What is a discriminated union and why is it powerful?
qa.a: It's a union of object types that share a common literal-typed field (the discriminant), e.g. { kind: 'circle'; r: number } | { kind: 'square'; side: number }. Switching on the discriminant narrows the type in each branch so you get the correct members with no casting, and adding a `const _: never = x` in the default branch makes the compiler error whenever a new variant is added but not handled. It's the idiomatic TS pattern for modeling state machines, API results, and events with compile-time exhaustiveness.

qa.q: How do you type-check and transpile in a modern build, and why separate them?
qa.a: tsc does two jobs — type-checking and emitting JS — but they can be decoupled. Fast transpilers (esbuild, swc, Babel) strip types per-file without any type-checking, so they're used for the actual build/bundle, while tsc --noEmit runs in CI/editor purely for type safety. The separation matters because per-file transpilers can't understand cross-file type info (hence isolatedModules constraints like requiring `export type`), so you rely on them for speed and on tsc as the source of truth for correctness.

bullets.point: An HTTP API that takes a git repo URL, clones it, generates a unique deployment id, uploads every source file to object storage (S3/Cloudflare R2) under that id, then LPUSHes the id onto a Redis queue and writes an initial status. It matters because it decouples the fast client-facing request from the slow, resource-heavy build — the API returns immediately while work is queued for async processing. Interview angle: producer side of a queue, unique/non-enumerable id generation, and treating storage as the source of truth.

A worker that blocks on the Redis queue (BRPOP / RPOP loop), pulls the popped id's source from storage, runs the build (npm install && npm run build), then uploads the build output (dist/) back to storage and flips status to deployed. It matters because it is the consumer half of the queue and the place untrusted code actually executes. Interview angle: worker/consumer pattern, at-least-once delivery, idempotency, and sandboxing untrusted builds.

An HTTP server that reads the Host header, extracts the subdomain as the deployment id, maps the request path to the corresponding storage key (defaulting to index.html), fetches the artifact, sets the right Content-Type, and streams it back. It matters because it is how a built deployment becomes a live URL. Interview angle: wildcard-DNS subdomain routing, static serving, MIME handling, and where a CDN/cache belongs.

A React app where the user pastes a repo URL, calls the upload service, polls deploy status, and surfaces the final live URL (id.yourdomain.com). It matters as the glue that wires the three backend pieces into a usable flow. Interview angle: async status UX (polling vs SSE/WebSockets), optimistic/loading states, and end-to-end integration.

example.title: Building a mini-Vercel that deploys a Vite React repo
example.body: A developer pastes https://github.com/user/my-vite-app into your dashboard. The upload service clones it, mints id `a1b2c3`, uploads the source to `s3://builds/a1b2c3/*`, LPUSHes `a1b2c3` to Redis, and returns the id. A deploy worker BRPOPs the id, downloads the source, runs `npm install && npm run build` inside an ephemeral container, uploads `dist/` to `s3://dist/a1b2c3/*`, and sets status=deployed. Wildcard DNS routes `a1b2c3.yourdomain.com` to the request handler, which resolves the subdomain to the id, fetches `dist/a1b2c3/index.html`, and serves the live site. Realistic debugging you'd hit: the build fails because a required env var is missing (surface exit code + stderr as status=failed), the site loads but assets 404 because the handler returns the wrong Content-Type or doesn't fall back to index.html for SPA routes, and a worker crash mid-build re-runs the job — which is fine only because the build is idempotent and keyed by id.

qa.q: Why put a Redis queue between the upload service and the deploy service instead of building synchronously in the upload request?
qa.a: Builds are slow and CPU/memory-heavy, so running them inline would block the request and couple your API's throughput to build capacity. A queue lets the upload API return instantly, absorbs bursts, and lets you scale build workers horizontally and retry failures independently. Trade-off: a naive LPUSH/RPOP loop gives at-least-once semantics but loses in-flight jobs if a worker crashes — for durability you'd want a broker with visibility timeouts and a dead-letter queue (SQS/RabbitMQ) or Redis Streams with consumer groups.

qa.q: How do you safely run untrusted user code during the build?
qa.a: Never run npm install/build directly on the host — a malicious postinstall script could exfiltrate secrets or attack your infra. Run each build in an ephemeral, throwaway sandbox: a Docker container or Firecracker microVM with no host mounts, dropped capabilities, CPU/memory/pid limits, restricted network egress, and a hard timeout, destroyed after every build. This contains blast radius and guarantees a clean environment per deployment.

qa.q: How does the request handler map a URL like a1b2c3.yourdomain.com to the right files?
qa.a: A wildcard DNS record (*.yourdomain.com) points every subdomain at the request handler. The handler reads the Host header, takes the subdomain as the deployment id, maps the request path to a storage key such as dist/<id>/<path> (defaulting to index.html), fetches from S3/R2, sets Content-Type from the extension, and streams the body. In production you front this with a CDN keyed on host+path so most requests never hit your origin or storage.

qa.q: The queue is at-least-once — how do you keep builds correct when a job can be processed twice?
qa.a: Make builds idempotent and keyed by deployment id so reprocessing overwrites the same deterministic artifact path rather than creating duplicates. Track status (queued/building/deployed/failed) with atomic transitions, or use a broker with a visibility timeout so only one worker owns a job at a time. Then a worker crash simply makes the job visible again and it re-runs cleanly, without corrupting state or serving a half-built artifact.

qa.q: Polling vs WebSockets/SSE for showing deploy status on the frontend — what would you choose?
qa.a: Polling every few seconds is stateless, trivially survives reconnects, and is perfectly fine for short-lived builds — it just wastes a few requests. WebSockets/SSE give lower-latency updates but add connection state and scaling cost (sticky sessions or a Redis pub/sub fan-out) plus reconnection logic. For an MVP I'd poll; at scale, SSE backed by Redis pub/sub is the pragmatic middle ground and streams build logs well.

qa.q: Why store both the raw source and the built output in object storage instead of just building on upload?
qa.a: Separation lets the deploy worker — a different machine from the uploader — fetch and build independently, and lets you rebuild without re-uploading. The source is the reproducible input; the built dist is what the request handler serves. Keeping artifacts per deployment id also gives you versioning, caching, and instant rollback by simply pointing serving at a previous id.

qa.q: How do you generate deployment ids, and why not use an auto-increment integer?
qa.a: Use a short random id (nanoid, or base62 of a UUID) rather than a sequential int. Sequential ids let anyone enumerate and access other users' deployments by guessing subdomains, and can collide under concurrent uploads. A random id doubles as both the subdomain and the storage key prefix; if you want human-friendly URLs, combine a slug with a random suffix.

qa.q: What are the main failure modes across these services and how do you surface them?
qa.a: Build failures (missing deps or wrong build command), storage failures (partial/failed uploads), and serving 404s (missing file or SPA route). Capture the build's stdout/stderr and exit code, set status=failed, and stream the logs to the frontend so the user can self-diagnose. For serving, fall back to index.html for client-side routes but return a real 404 otherwise; add health checks, timeouts, and a dead-letter queue with alerting so stuck jobs don't hang silently.

bullets.point: Orient yourself in the Turborepo monorepo before writing code: apps/user-app (Next.js, App Router, server actions + NextAuth), apps/bank-webhook (a small Express server), and shared packages/db (the Prisma client + schema) and packages/ui. The interview angle is understanding why a payments product uses a shared DB package (one Prisma client, one schema, imported by every app) and how workspace boundaries keep the bank simulator decoupled from the wallet UI.

Complete the money-in flow: a server action creates an OnRampTransaction row with status Processing plus a unique token, the user is sent to a mock bank, and the bank calls the bank-webhook server, which credits the user's Balance and flips the row to Success. The point is that crediting is asynchronous and webhook-driven (you never trust the client to say 'I paid') — the key discussion is idempotency and webhook authenticity.

Implement a sendMoney server action that atomically debits the sender and credits the receiver inside a single Prisma $transaction, using a row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) on the sender's balance so the sufficient-funds check and the debit can't be interleaved by a concurrent request. This is the crux of the topic: preventing double-spend/lost-update race conditions, and the choice between pessimistic locking and optimistic (version-column) locking.

Add a p2pTransfer model (fromUserId, toUserId, amount, timestamp) and write that ledger row inside the same transaction that moves the balances, so the money movement and its audit record commit or roll back together. Interview angle: this is the beginning of a double-entry/ledger design — the transactions table is the source of truth for reconciliation and history, while Balance is a derived cache.

Build the Next.js page (ideally a server component that queries Prisma directly, or a server action) that lists a user's p2pTransfers, distinguishing sent vs received by comparing fromUserId/toUserId against the session user id. The takeaway is scoping every query to the authenticated user (no client-supplied user id), correct amount formatting, and ordering by timestamp.

example.title: Concurrency-safe wallet transfers under load
example.body: You own the wallet service. A user with a 100 rupee balance fires two 100 rupee transfers nearly simultaneously (double-tap, retry, or a scripted attack). With a naive read-check-write server action, both requests read balance=100, both pass the 'balance >= 100' check, and both debit — the account goes to -100 and money is created out of thin air (lost update / double-spend). The SDE-2 fix is to wrap debit+credit+ledger-insert in a single Prisma $transaction and take a pessimistic row lock via a raw `SELECT balance FROM "Balance" WHERE "userId" = $1 FOR UPDATE` so the second request blocks until the first commits, then re-reads 100→0 and correctly fails the check. In the same transaction you insert the p2pTransfer ledger row, so history and balances stay consistent. Separately, the onramp webhook must be idempotent: the bank retries on timeout, and without deduping on the transaction token you'd credit the deposit twice — so the webhook updates only rows still in Processing (a conditional update) and returns 200 on the already-Success case.

qa.q: Why is 'Add Money' (onramp) implemented via a webhook from the bank instead of just crediting the balance when the user clicks pay?
qa.a: Because the wallet never actually holds the funds until the bank confirms settlement, and settlement is asynchronous — it can succeed, fail, or be delayed after the user leaves your page. The webhook is the bank's authoritative, out-of-band callback saying money really moved, so you credit only on that signal. Crediting on the client click would let anyone fake a deposit; the OnRampTransaction sits in Processing until the trusted server-to-server webhook flips it to Success.

qa.q: How do you prevent a double-spend when two transfers hit the same account concurrently?
qa.a: Do the whole operation in one DB transaction and serialize access to the sender's balance row with a pessimistic lock — in this repo a raw `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` inside Prisma's $transaction. The lock forces the second request to wait until the first commits, so the balance check reads the post-debit value and correctly rejects the overdraft. Without the lock, both requests read the stale balance, both pass the check, and you get a lost update. The alternative is optimistic locking (a version/updatedAt column with a conditional update and retry), better under low contention.

qa.q: What's the difference between Prisma's array-form $transaction and the interactive (callback) form, and why does this project need the interactive one?
qa.a: The array form (`prisma.$transaction([...])`) just batches independent queries into one atomic commit but gives you no intermediate results or branching. The interactive form (`prisma.$transaction(async tx => {...})`) lets you run a query, read its result, make decisions (like the balance check), and issue further writes — all on the same connection/transaction. Transfers need the interactive form because you must lock and read the balance, branch on sufficiency, and then conditionally debit/credit; you also drop to `tx.$queryRaw` there because Prisma has no first-class SELECT FOR UPDATE.

qa.q: The bank webhook can be called more than once for the same deposit. How do you make it safe?
qa.a: Make it idempotent: key on the unique transaction token and only apply the credit if the OnRampTransaction is still in Processing, ideally via a single conditional/atomic update (update ... where status = Processing) rather than read-then-write, and return 200 on an already-processed token so the bank stops retrying. Wrap the balance increment and the status flip in one transaction so they can't partially apply. Separately, the endpoint must be authenticated — a signature/HMAC or shared secret from the bank — because the reference implementation trusting an unauthenticated JSON body is a real vulnerability.

qa.q: Why write the p2pTransfer ledger row inside the same transaction as the balance updates instead of after?
qa.a: Atomicity: the money movement and its record must both commit or both roll back. If you insert the ledger row after the transaction commits and that insert fails (crash, deploy, DB blip), balances change with no history — you can't reconcile or show the user what happened; the reverse leaves a phantom transaction that never moved money. Putting debit, credit, and the ledger insert in one transaction guarantees the audit trail always matches the balances.

qa.q: How should the balance/amount be stored — float, decimal, or integer?
qa.a: Never floating point — binary floats can't represent decimal currency exactly and accumulate rounding errors. Store money as an integer in the smallest unit (paise/cents), so 100.00 rupees is 10000, which makes arithmetic exact and comparisons safe; the 100xDevs schema uses an Int amount for exactly this reason. If you need fractional units or large ranges, use a fixed-precision DECIMAL/NUMERIC type, not double.

qa.q: How do you correctly scope the P2P transactions page so a user only sees their own history?
qa.a: Derive the user id from the server-side session (NextAuth's getServerSession), never from a client-supplied parameter, and query `p2pTransfer where fromUserId = me OR toUserId = me` on the server (server component or server action) ordered by timestamp desc. You then label each row sent vs received by comparing fromUserId to the session id. Doing the query on the client with a user id from props/query-string would be an IDOR — anyone could read others' transactions.

qa.q: What database isolation level matters here, and what anomaly are you defending against?
qa.a: The core anomaly is the lost update (and a read-then-write race on the balance). At the default Read Committed level a plain read-check-write is still vulnerable, which is why you add explicit row locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) rather than relying on the isolation level alone. You could instead raise the transaction to Serializable and handle serialization-failure retries, but explicit pessimistic locking on the single hot row is simpler and more predictable for this write pattern.

bullets.step: How to approach building any project
bullets.point: A meta-lesson: don't jump to code. Move top-down — features -> UX flows -> architecture -> stack -> implementation — so ambiguous requirements get scoped before you commit to a design. Interview angle: this is exactly the structured decomposition an SDE-2 is expected to show in a system-design or take-home round.

Define the concrete feature set: P2P transfers, on-ramp (add money from a bank), merchant receive-payments, and transaction history, then cut to an MVP. The signal being tested is prioritization and saying no — shipping wallet balance + on-ramp + P2P before KYC, refunds, or notifications.

Map the consumer screens and flows: view balance, add money, send to another user, see transactions. Doing this first surfaces the data you must persist (balances, transactions) and the states you must render (processing/success/failure), which de-risks the schema and API design later.

Design the merchant-side surface separately: receiving payments, revenue/settlement view, and a different auth identity. The point is that merchant and consumer are distinct personas with different data and permissions — a classic reason to split into two apps rather than role-flagging one.

A Turborepo monorepo with two Next.js apps (user-app, merchant-app), shared packages (db/Prisma, ui, store), and a separate bank-webhook server for on-ramp callbacks. Interview angle: justify the split — shared domain code with independently deployable frontends, and an external webhook boundary you don't control the timing of.

Next.js + TypeScript (App Router), Prisma + PostgreSQL, NextAuth, Recoil, Tailwind, Turborepo, and an Express webhook service. Be able to defend each choice: Postgres for ACID money operations, Prisma for typed schema/migrations, NextAuth to avoid rolling your own session layer.

Scaffold the Turborepo, create the app and package workspaces, and wire the shared tsconfig/eslint/build pipeline. This is where you set up workspace dependencies (apps import @repo/db, @repo/ui, @repo/store) so code is shared, not copy-pasted.

bullets.step: Add Prisma as a shared db package
bullets.point: Model User, Merchant, Balance, OnRampTransaction, and p2pTransfer; run migrations; export a singleton PrismaClient. Two interview-critical details: store money as integers (paise), never floats, and reuse one client instance to avoid exhausting the connection pool (especially in serverless).

bullets.step: Create a shared Recoil store package
bullets.point: Put atoms/selectors (e.g. balance state) in a @repo/store package so both apps share client state logic. The reason for a separate package rather than per-app state is a single source of truth for derived values like locked vs. unlocked balance.

bullets.step: Wire Recoil into the Next.js apps
bullets.point: Wrap each app in a RecoilRoot. The App Router gotcha: RecoilRoot uses React context so it must live in a 'use client' provider component imported into the server layout — you cannot make the root layout itself a client component.

Add the [...nextauth] route handler and a SessionProvider, establishing the auth plumbing. The point of this step is infrastructure: a single place that issues/validates sessions so every protected route and server action can call getServerSession.

Configure a Credentials provider (phone + password) with bcrypt-hashed passwords, JWT session strategy, and a callback that injects the user id into the token/session. Interview angle: know why you add userId in the jwt/session callbacks — without it the session has an email but no stable key to scope DB queries.

A first shared UI component rendering the signed-in user and sign-in/out actions. It's the vehicle for wiring auth state into the UI and proving the shared @repo/ui package is consumable from both apps.

A consolidation pause to verify auth, Prisma, and the monorepo wiring all work end-to-end before building features on top. Practically it's a smoke test — sign up, sign in, session persists, DB reads work — so later bugs aren't ambiguous.

The core money flow: create an OnRampTransaction in Processing state with a token, then a bank webhook hits your Express server to confirm, which credits the balance. This must be atomic and idempotent — update balance and mark the txn Success inside one DB transaction with a row lock, and reject duplicate webhook deliveries. This is the highest-signal step in the whole project.

Extract the appbar into a presentational component in @repo/ui that takes props (user, onSignin, onSignout) instead of reaching into auth directly. The lesson is dependency inversion: shared UI stays framework/auth-agnostic and each app injects behavior.
Build the navigation sidebar (Home, Transfer, Transactions, P2P) with active-route highlighting. Minor but it establishes the authenticated app-shell layout and client-side navigation pattern.

The page where a user picks a bank and amount and kicks off the on-ramp: it inserts the OnRampTransaction and redirects to the (simulated) bank. This is the client entry point to the webhook flow built earlier and where you validate input server-side.

A Prisma seed script that creates test users, balances, and merchants so the app is demoable and testable without manual setup. Interview angle: seeds make local dev and CI deterministic and give integration tests a known fixture.

bullets.step: Landing page redirect based on auth
bullets.point: Make / redirect to /dashboard when authenticated (and to sign-in otherwise) using a server-side redirect in the layout/page. Doing it server-side via getServerSession avoids a flash of the wrong page that a client-side check would cause.

example.title: Debugging a double-credited wallet during on-ramp
example.body: You built the add-money flow: the user-app creates an OnRampTransaction (status Processing, unique token), the user is sent to a mock bank, and the bank calls your Express webhook to confirm payment. In production you notice some wallets get credited twice. The cause is the bank retrying the webhook (network timeout) plus your handler reading the balance, adding the amount, and writing it back in separate queries — a classic read-modify-write race.
