qa.a: Prisma throws typed errors — PrismaClientKnownRequestError with codes like P2002 (unique constraint violation), P2025 (record not found for update/delete), and P2003 (foreign key constraint). In an Express handler you wrap calls in try/catch, inspect error.code, and map them to appropriate HTTP statuses (P2002 to 409, P2025 to 404, validation issues to 400) rather than leaking a 500. This keeps the DB layer's semantics translated into a clean API contract.

qa.q: When would you drop to raw SQL, and how does Prisma support it?
qa.a: You go raw for queries the client can't express efficiently — complex aggregations, window functions, recursive CTEs, or hand-tuned performance-critical paths. Prisma offers $queryRaw (returns rows, use the tagged-template form so parameters are safely bound) and $executeRaw (returns affected row count), plus $queryRawUnsafe when you must build dynamic SQL (with your own injection safeguards). The trade-off is you lose the generated type safety, so you type the result manually and confine raw usage to a small, well-tested layer.

bullets.point: A middleware is a function that sits in the request/response pipeline and runs before the request reaches its final handler, so it can inspect, mutate, short-circuit, or pass the request along. It is the canonical place for cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, rate limiting, header injection) so that logic isn't duplicated across every route. Interview angle: describe it as a composable chain where each layer decides whether to call `next()` or terminate the request early.

Next.js middleware is a single `middleware.ts` at the project (or `src/`) root that runs on the Edge runtime before a request hits pages, route handlers, or even cached content. Because it executes at the CDN edge close to the user, it is ideal for fast redirects, rewrites, A/B routing, geolocation, and optimistic auth gates. Interview angle: it runs on the Edge runtime, so no native Node APIs (no `fs`, no raw DB drivers) and no heavy CPU/DB work — keep it lightweight.

You export a function named `middleware(request: NextRequest)` that returns a `NextResponse` — typically `NextResponse.next()` to continue, `NextResponse.redirect(url)` to send the client elsewhere, or `NextResponse.rewrite(url)` to serve a different path transparently. Interview angle: know the difference — redirect changes the URL the browser sees (3xx), rewrite keeps the URL but serves different content server-side.

Middleware reads cookies and headers via `request.cookies` / `request.headers` (e.g. verify a JWT) and forwards enriched data to the route using `NextResponse.next({ request: { headers } })`, since you cannot directly mutate the downstream handler's arguments. You can also respond immediately with `NextResponse.json(...)` to block a request entirely. Interview angle: the standard pattern for propagating derived values (like a decoded user id) is setting request headers, not global/context state, because each request is isolated at the edge.

By default middleware runs on every request, so you scope it with the `config.matcher` array or with early-return conditionals inside the function. Matchers support path globs, negative lookaheads to exclude static assets (`/((?!_next/static|favicon.ico).*)`), and `has`/`missing` conditions on headers/cookies/query. Interview angle: the matcher must be a statically-analyzable constant (evaluated at build time) and scoping it well matters for performance since middleware fires on matching static files too.

example.title: Edge auth gate for a multi-tenant dashboard
example.body: You own a Next.js app where `/dashboard/*` is private and `/login`, `/`, and marketing pages are public. You add a root `middleware.ts` with `config.matcher = ['/dashboard/:path*']` so it only fires on protected routes. On each matched request it reads the `session` cookie and verifies the JWT signature using a Web Crypto (edge-compatible) library — an optimistic check, not a DB lookup. If the token is missing or invalid it returns `NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url))`; if valid it decodes the tenant/user id and forwards it downstream via `NextResponse.next({ request: { headers } })` so server components read it without re-parsing the cookie. Because this runs at the edge, unauthenticated users are bounced before any page render or data fetch, cutting latency and load. The debugging gotcha you hit: full authorization (is this user allowed in THIS tenant?) still needs a DB check in the data layer, because doing it in middleware would mean a slow database round-trip on every request from the edge — middleware only does the cheap, stateless gate.

qa.q: Why does Next.js run middleware on the Edge runtime, and what does that constrain?
qa.a: The Edge runtime executes middleware in a lightweight V8 isolate deployed close to the user (CDN edge), which minimizes latency for redirects, rewrites, and auth gates that must run on every request. The trade-off is a restricted API surface: no native Node.js modules (`fs`, `net`), no raw TCP database drivers, and tight CPU/time budgets. As a result you use Web-standard APIs (fetch, Web Crypto) and keep logic stateless and fast; anything requiring the full Node runtime or heavy I/O belongs in a route handler or server component.

qa.q: What is the difference between NextResponse.redirect, rewrite, and next()?
qa.a: `next()` passes the request through to the normal handler (optionally with modified request headers). `redirect()` returns a 3xx and changes the URL the browser navigates to — the client sees the new URL. `rewrite()` keeps the original URL in the address bar but internally serves content from a different path, which is how you implement transparent proxying, A/B tests, or localized routing without exposing internal paths.

qa.q: How do you pass data computed in middleware (like a decoded user id) to a route handler or server component?
qa.a: You cannot mutate the handler's arguments or share in-memory context, so you set request headers via `NextResponse.next({ request: { headers: newHeaders } })` and read them downstream with `headers()`. Each edge request is isolated, so header propagation is the reliable channel. Avoid trying to stash state in module-level variables — isolates are reused unpredictably and it leaks across requests.

qa.q: How does the matcher work and why not just check the path inside the function?
qa.a: `config.matcher` declares which paths invoke middleware at all, and it must be a static, build-time-analyzable constant (you can't compute it at runtime). Using it means the middleware bundle never even runs for excluded paths, which is more efficient than always invoking the function and returning early — important because without a matcher, middleware fires on every request including `_next/static` assets and images. In practice you combine both: a matcher for coarse scoping plus conditionals for fine-grained logic.

qa.q: Can you have multiple middleware files, and how do you compose logic?
qa.a: No — Next.js supports exactly one middleware entry point (`middleware.ts` at root or `src/`). To run multiple concerns you compose them manually inside that single function, typically as a chain of helper functions each taking the request and returning either a response (short-circuit) or null (continue). Libraries like `next-connect` or hand-rolled composition patterns are used to keep this readable.

qa.q: Why is doing full authentication/authorization purely in middleware considered an anti-pattern?
qa.a: Middleware runs at the edge with no cheap access to your database, so doing full session or permission verification there forces a slow round-trip on every request and can't be trusted as the only gate. The recommended pattern is optimistic checks in middleware — verify a signed JWT's signature/expiry to bounce obviously-unauthenticated users fast — while the authoritative authorization (does this user own this resource?) happens in the data access layer close to the DB. This keeps the edge fast and keeps security enforced where the data actually lives.

qa.q: In what order does middleware run relative to caching, routing, and static files?
qa.a: Middleware runs before routing resolution and before cached responses are served, on every request that matches the matcher — including matched static assets unless you exclude them. This is why a poorly-scoped matcher hurts performance and why middleware can invalidate CDN caching benefits if it forces dynamic behavior. It executes first, decides the fate of the request, and only then does Next.js resolve the route or serve cached content.

qa.q: How would you rate-limit or geo-restrict traffic using Next.js middleware, and what are the limits?
qa.a: You read `request.geo` (country/region, populated on supporting hosts like Vercel) or the client IP and apply logic — e.g. redirect EU users to a localized path or reject requests from a blocked region. For rate limiting you need shared state, which the edge lacks, so you call an external edge-compatible store (Upstash Redis, Vercel KV) over fetch and return `NextResponse.json({error}, {status:429})` when exceeded. The limitation is that any per-request external call adds latency and cost, so you cache aggressively and keep the store geographically close.

bullets.point: Overview of the four array-preprocessing patterns covered: prefix sums, frequency arrays, difference arrays (prefix + freq for ranges), and sliding window. Matters because the majority of 'count subarrays/substrings' interview problems collapse into one of these four, so recognizing which one applies is the real skill.

Precompute P[i] = sum of the first i elements so any range sum [l,r] = P[r+1] - P[l] in O(1) after an O(n) build. This is the foundation for O(1) range queries and, combined with a hashmap, for counting subarrays that satisfy a sum condition.

Given per-leg altitude gains, the highest point is just the max over the running prefix sum starting at 0. Trivial application, but the interview signal is instantly reframing 'cumulative/highest-so-far' as a prefix scan rather than a nested loop.

When the value domain is small and bounded (26 letters, 128 ASCII, 0..K-1), index an array by value to count occurrences in O(1) with no hashing overhead and better cache behavior than a hashmap. It is effectively a perfect hash for dense small-integer keys.

Build a size-26 (or 128) count array in one pass, then argmax. The nuance interviewers probe is the tie-break rule (first-seen vs lexicographically smallest) and whether input is guaranteed lowercase ASCII vs arbitrary Unicode, which would break the fixed-size array assumption.

To add v across [l,r], do diff[l]+=v and diff[r+1]-=v, then a single prefix sum materializes the final array — turning m range updates from O(m·n) into O(m+n). This is the write-heavy dual of prefix sums and the basis of line-sweep counting.

Minimum platforms = maximum number of trains present at any instant. Either +1 at each arrival / -1 at each departure+1 on a time-indexed difference array then prefix-sum for the running max, or sort arrivals and departures and two-pointer merge (O(n log n)), which wins when timestamps are large/sparse. Same shape as Meeting Rooms II.

The meta-technique: the number of subarrays meeting a sum condition equals the number of earlier prefix-states that pair with the current one. Maintain a map of prefix-state -> count and, at each index, add the count of the matching state before recording the current — the engine behind divisible-by-K, sum=k, and equal-0/1 problems.

A subarray sum is divisible by K iff two prefix sums share the same remainder mod K, so count prefixes by remainder and add C(count,2) pairs. Initialize remainder 0 with count 1 for the empty prefix, and normalize negatives with ((sum % K) + K) % K to avoid language-specific negative modulo. O(n) time, O(K) space.

Maintain a window [left,right] with an invariant, expanding right and shrinking left, for overall O(n). Applies only when contribution is monotone (typically non-negative), so growing the window can only push a metric one way — that monotonicity is exactly what lets you move pointers greedily instead of re-scanning.

Slide a length-k window across the digit string of the number and count windows whose value is non-zero and divides the original number. Classic fixed-window: maintain the current window value incrementally rather than reparsing k digits each step.

bullets.step: Variable window + contribution counting (LC 1358, all 3 chars)
bullets.point: For each right endpoint, track the last-seen index of a, b, and c; every substring ending at right that starts at or before min(last_a,last_b,last_c) contains all three, so add min(...)+1. This counts valid substrings ending at each position in O(1), giving O(n) instead of O(n^2) enumeration.

Map odd->1, even->0, reducing 'exactly k odds' to 'subarrays with sum exactly k'. Solve as atMost(k) - atMost(k-1) with a monotone sliding window, or with the prefix-count hashmap. The atMost trick generalizes to any 'exactly k' counting problem where 'at most k' is a clean window.

example.title: Capacity planning and abuse detection for an API gateway
example.body: You own an API gateway and build an analytics-plus-guardrails pipeline over per-second traffic. Prefix sums answer 'how many requests hit /checkout between 09:00 and 09:15' in O(1) after one pass over the second-buckets. A difference array over each connection's [start,end] interval yields the peak concurrent long-lived connections per second — the exact 'maximum platforms' computation that sizes your worker pool. A frequency array over HTTP status codes (bounded 100-599) tallies the error mix cheaply for a dashboard. Sliding windows enforce per-client rate limits ('at most k requests in any rolling 60s') and flag bursts. When you need to count how many 5-minute buckets had a volume divisible by an autoscaling step, or had exactly k spike-seconds, you reach for the prefix-sum-plus-hashmap counting pattern. The two bugs that actually bite in review: taking a modulo of a negative delta without normalizing, and an off-by-one at the difference array's r+1 decrement boundary.

qa.q: How does prefix-sum plus hashmap count subarrays with sum divisible by K, and how do you handle negative numbers?
qa.a: A subarray (l,r] has sum divisible by K iff prefix[r] and prefix[l] have the same remainder mod K, so group prefixes by remainder and every pair within a group is a valid subarray. Iterate once, and at each index add count[rem] to the answer before incrementing count[rem]; seed remainder 0 with count 1 for the empty prefix. For negatives, normalize with ((sum % K) + K) % K because in most languages % of a negative is negative, which would split one true remainder class into two buckets.

qa.q: When can you NOT use a sliding window for 'subarray sum equals k', and what do you use instead?
qa.a: When the array contains negative numbers. Sliding window relies on monotonicity — extending right only increases the sum and shrinking left only decreases it — so you can move pointers greedily. Negatives destroy that invariant, so you fall back to prefix sum plus a hashmap of prefix values, which is O(n) and sign-agnostic. Sliding window is valid only when the window metric changes monotonically with window size.

qa.q: Explain the difference array technique and how it solves the maximum-platforms problem.
qa.a: To apply +v over [l,r], set diff[l]+=v and diff[r+1]-=v; a single prefix sum over diff reconstructs the final array, turning m range updates from O(m·n) into O(m+n). For platforms, each train occupies [arrival,departure]: do +1 at arrival and -1 at departure+1 on a time-indexed diff array, prefix-sum to get the concurrent-train count at each instant, and take the max. If timestamps are large or sparse, instead sort arrivals and departures and two-pointer merge, counting overlaps in O(n log n).

qa.q: How do you count subarrays with 'exactly k' of something using sliding windows?
qa.a: Direct 'exactly k' is awkward, but 'at most k' is a clean monotone window, so exactly(k) = atMost(k) - atMost(k-1). For Nice Subarrays, map odd->1 and even->0; atMost(k) counts subarrays with at most k ones by expanding right and shrinking left when the count exceeds k, adding (right-left+1) each step. Subtracting atMost(k-1) leaves exactly k, all in O(n) with O(1) extra space, and the trick generalizes to any countable window property.

qa.q: When do you choose a fixed-size frequency array over a hashmap?
qa.a: Use a fixed array when keys are small, dense, and bounded — 26 lowercase letters, 128 ASCII, digits, or remainders 0..K-1 — because you get O(1) access with no hashing constant factor, contiguous cache-friendly memory, and naturally ordered iteration. Use a hashmap when the domain is large, sparse, or unbounded (arbitrary 32/64-bit ints, strings) to avoid allocating a huge mostly-empty array. The array is essentially a perfect hash whenever the key is a dense small integer.

qa.q: Walk through the contribution-counting trick in 'number of substrings containing all three characters' (LC 1358).
qa.a: Fix the right endpoint and maintain the most recent index at which each of a, b, c appeared. Any substring ending at right whose start is at or before min(last_a,last_b,last_c) must contain all three characters, so exactly min(last_a,last_b,last_c)+1 substrings ending here are valid, and you add that to the answer. This counts valid substrings ending at each position in O(1), giving O(n) overall instead of the O(n^2) of enumerating every substring.

qa.q: When do prefix sums stop being the right tool, and what replaces them?
qa.a: Static prefix sums give O(1) range queries but O(n) point updates, since changing one element forces rebuilding the whole suffix. If the array is mutated between queries, switch to a Fenwick/BIT or segment tree for O(log n) update and query. Prefix sums are the read-heavy, immutable-data choice; the difference array is the write-heavy dual — batch many range updates cheaply, then materialize with one final prefix pass.

bullets.point: Server ships a near-empty HTML shell plus a JS bundle; the browser downloads, parses, and executes JS to build the DOM and fetch data at runtime (the classic SPA model, e.g. plain React/Vite). It matters because it gives cheap-to-host, highly interactive apps but pays with slow first paint (blank screen until JS hydrates), weak SEO for crawlers that don't run JS, and TTI that degrades with bundle size — the interview angle is recognizing when a heavy client bundle is the root cause of poor Core Web Vitals.

For each request the server runs the app, fetches data, and returns fully-formed HTML, which the client then hydrates to attach interactivity (Next.js getServerSideProps / React Server Components with dynamic rendering). It matters for fast, SEO-friendly first paint on personalized or frequently-changing pages; the trade-offs interviewers probe are per-request server cost/latency (TTFB), the need for caching/CDN, and hydration mismatches or the 'hydration is slow' problem.

HTML is rendered once at build time and served as static files from a CDN, optionally refreshed via revalidation/ISR (Next getStaticProps, Gatsby, Astro). It matters for the best possible TTFB and scalability on content that changes infrequently (docs, blogs, marketing, catalogs); the key interview nuance is handling dynamic-ish data with Incremental Static Regeneration or on-demand revalidation, and knowing that build times explode as page count grows.

example.title: Choosing a rendering strategy for an e-commerce site
example.body: You're building a storefront on Next.js with three page types and must pick a rendering mode per route rather than one for the whole app. Product listing and detail pages use SSG with ISR: they're SEO-critical and mostly static, so you pre-render them at build time and set revalidate (or on-demand revalidation triggered by a webhook when inventory/price changes) to keep them fresh without a full rebuild. The logged-in cart, checkout, and 'recommended for you' pages use SSR because they're per-user and can't be cached publicly, so you render on request and lean on edge caching only where safe. The interactive parts inside an already-rendered page — image zoom, add-to-cart, filters — are CSR/hydration on top of the server HTML. A common real debugging task here is a hydration mismatch warning on product pages caused by rendering a locale-dependent price or Date.now() on the server that differs from the client, which you fix by making that value deterministic or deferring it to a client-only effect.

qa.q: When would you choose SSR over SSG, and vice versa?
qa.a: Use SSG when content is the same for all users and changes infrequently (blogs, docs, product catalogs) — you get CDN-level TTFB and near-infinite scalability. Use SSR when the page is personalized per user or depends on data that must be fresh on every request (dashboards, authenticated views, live pricing). The decision hinges on data freshness requirements and cacheability, not on whether the page is 'dynamic' in a UI sense. If content changes often but isn't per-user, ISR/on-demand revalidation is the middle ground that keeps SSG's performance.

qa.q: What is hydration, and why can it hurt performance?
qa.a: Hydration is the client-side process where React (or another framework) attaches event listeners and reconstructs component state on top of server-rendered HTML so the static markup becomes interactive. It hurts because the browser must download and execute the full JS bundle and walk the entire tree before the page is interactive, so a page can look ready (fast FCP) but have a slow TTI — the 'uncanny valley' where clicks don't work yet. Mitigations include reducing bundle size, code-splitting, streaming SSR with Suspense, partial/selective hydration, islands architecture, and React Server Components which ship zero JS for non-interactive parts.

qa.q: How does CSR affect SEO, and how do you mitigate it?
qa.a: With pure CSR the initial HTML is an empty shell, so crawlers that don't execute JavaScript (or crawl with a limited render budget) may index a blank page, and social/link-preview scrapers that never run JS see no metadata. Modern Googlebot does render JS but on a deferred, best-effort second pass, which delays and can degrade indexing. Mitigations: use SSR or SSG for crawlable content, add dynamic rendering/prerendering for bots, or at minimum server-render the meta tags. For most content-driven sites, SSG/SSR is the correct answer rather than trying to patch CSR.

qa.q: What is Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) and what problem does it solve?
qa.a: ISR lets you keep SSG's static, CDN-served output while updating individual pages after deployment, either on a time interval (revalidate: N seconds) or on-demand via a revalidation API/webhook. It solves SSG's core weakness — stale content and long full rebuilds — so a 100k-page catalog can update a single changed product without rebuilding everything. The typical model is stale-while-revalidate: the first request after expiry serves the old page, triggers a background regeneration, and subsequent requests get the fresh version. The trade-off is eventual consistency and a small window of stale data, which is fine for most content but not for strongly consistent needs.

qa.q: How do these rendering modes map to Core Web Vitals like FCP, LCP, and TTI?
qa.a: SSG generally wins FCP/LCP because HTML arrives instantly from a CDN with no server compute; SSR has good FCP/LCP too but adds TTFB from per-request rendering and data fetching. CSR has the worst FCP/LCP since content only appears after JS downloads, parses, and fetches data. TTI is dominated by JS bundle size and hydration cost, so a large CSR or heavily-hydrated SSR app can have poor TTI even when the paint metrics look acceptable — which is why bundle size and hydration strategy matter as much as the rendering mode.

qa.q: Can you mix rendering strategies in one application? How?
qa.a: Yes, and it's the recommended approach — modern frameworks let you choose per route or even per component. In Next.js you can make marketing pages SSG, dashboards SSR, and docs SSG with ISR, all in one app; with the App Router and React Server Components you mix server components (no client JS) with client components ('use client') on the same page. The mental model is: pick the strategy per route based on that route's data freshness, personalization, and SEO needs, then use client components/islands only for the interactive slices. This avoids the false premise that an app must commit to a single rendering mode.

qa.q: What are the trade-offs of SSR at scale, and how do you handle them?
qa.a: SSR runs application code and data fetching on every request, so it increases server CPU, latency (TTFB), and infrastructure cost, and makes you vulnerable to slow upstream data sources or traffic spikes. You handle it with layered caching: full-page or fragment caching with stale-while-revalidate at a CDN/edge, per-route cache-control headers, request-level data caching/deduplication, and moving compute to the edge to cut round-trips. For pages that don't truly need per-request freshness, converting them to SSG/ISR removes the load entirely — the biggest SSR win is often deciding a route doesn't need SSR.

bullets.point: A single version-controlled repo that holds multiple projects (apps + libraries) rather than one repo per project (polyrepo). Interview angle: a monorepo is a repository topology, not a deployment topology — it has nothing to do with being a monolith; you can ship many independently deployed services from one monorepo.

They give you atomic cross-package commits, a single source of truth for dependency versions, trivial code sharing, and unified tooling/CI, which makes wide refactors (rename an API across 20 consumers in one PR) safe. The trade-offs an SDE-2 must name: slower/heavier CI, VCS scaling pain at large size, weaker access control granularity, and the need for a task orchestrator to keep builds fast.

Two distinct layers: package-manager workspaces (npm/yarn/pnpm workspaces, or pnpm-workspace.yaml) that link internal packages and hoist deps, and task orchestrators/build tools (Turborepo, Nx, Lerna, Rush, Bazel) that schedule and cache tasks across the graph. Interview angle: workspaces solve linking; they do NOT solve build ordering or caching — that's why you add Turborepo on top.

Created by Jared Palmer, acquired by Vercel in late 2021; originally written in Go and later rewritten in Rust. Its design goal was Bazel-class incremental caching with near-zero configuration, positioned as far simpler to adopt than Bazel or (at the time) Nx.

A build system actually compiles/bundles code (tsc, esbuild, webpack, vite, next build). An orchestrator does not compile anything itself — it reads the task dependency graph, decides run order and parallelism, and caches results. Getting this distinction right is the crux of most Turborepo interview questions.

Turbo wraps your existing package.json scripts; it builds a task graph from turbo.json, runs independent tasks in parallel, enforces topological order for dependent ones, and caches each task's outputs. It is a thin coordination + caching layer, which is why adopting it rarely requires changing your actual build commands.

`npx create-turbo@latest` scaffolds a workspace with apps/ and packages/, a root package.json declaring workspaces, a turbo.json, and shared config packages. Convention-over-configuration means you get a working incremental pipeline immediately.

By convention apps/ holds deployable units (web, docs) and packages/ holds shared internal libraries (ui, eslint-config, typescript-config). This split communicates intent — apps consume packages, packages don't consume apps — and keeps the dependency graph acyclic.

`turbo run dev` (or `turbo dev`) fans out to each workspace's matching script in parallel; `turbo run build` respects graph order. Know `--filter=<pkg>` to scope to one package and its deps, and that dev servers run as long-lived persistent tasks that stream interleaved output.

Declares `workspaces` globs (or defers to pnpm-workspace.yaml), a `packageManager` field that pins the tool via Corepack, shared dev tooling, and root scripts that delegate to turbo. Pinning packageManager is what makes builds reproducible across dev machines and CI.

An internal library consumed via the workspace protocol ("@repo/ui": "workspace:*"), never published to npm. Interview nuance: it can be a 'just-in-time' package (raw TS transpiled by the consuming app) or a compiled package (pre-built with its own build step and outputs) — the choice affects caching and build ordering.

A Next.js app that imports from @repo/ui, wired through workspace linking and (for JIT packages) Next's transpilePackages. It demonstrates how an app resolves an internal dependency to local source instead of a registry download.

A hands-on exercise: add a route that renders a component from the shared UI package, proving the end-to-end sharing loop. The takeaway is that editing packages/ui is instantly reflected in the app with no publish step.

The heart of the setup: the `tasks` map (formerly `pipeline`) defines each task's `dependsOn` (`^build` means run upstream dependencies' build first), `outputs` (what to cache), `inputs`, `env`/`globalEnv`, `cache`, and `persistent` for servers. Misconfiguring outputs or env here is the most common source of stale/incorrect cache bugs.

Add a second app or package and wire it to the shared eslint/tsconfig and ui packages so tooling and components are reused rather than copied. This shows the marginal cost of a new project in a well-structured monorepo is small.

Turbo hashes each task's inputs — source files, dependency graph, relevant env vars, and config — into a cache key, then stores outputs plus captured logs. On a hit it restores outputs and replays logs instead of re-running ("never do the same work twice"). Local cache lives under node_modules/.cache/turbo; remote cache (Vercel or self-hosted) shares hits across teammates and CI, which is the biggest real-world CI speedup.

Add a backend (e.g., Express) app so frontend and backend live in one graph and can share code. This is where monorepos pay off: shared types and validation between client and server in a single atomic PR.

Create a shared package (types, utils, validation schemas) consumed by both the web app and the Node app, demonstrating cross-stack reuse. The payoff is a single definition — change the type once and every consumer is type-checked against it in the same commit.

example.title: Full-stack product monorepo with shared types and fast CI
example.body: You own a product with a Next.js web app (apps/web), an Express API (apps/api), and shared packages: @repo/types (Zod schemas + inferred TS types), @repo/ui (React components), and @repo/config (eslint/tsconfig). All are linked via pnpm workspaces with "workspace:*". You define API request/response shapes once in @repo/types; both the API handlers and the web fetch layer import them, so a breaking field change surfaces as a type error in the same PR across client and server. turbo.json declares build.dependsOn = ["^build"] so @repo/types builds before apps that consume it, marks dev as persistent + uncached, and lists .next/ and dist/ as outputs. Locally you run `turbo dev --filter=web...` to boot only the web app and its deps. The real win is CI: with Vercel Remote Caching enabled, a PR that only touches apps/web restores cached build/lint/test outputs for @repo/types, @repo/ui, and apps/api instead of rebuilding them, cutting a ~9-minute pipeline to under 2. The classic bug you debug: the web build reads NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL but you forgot to list it under the build task's env, so Turbo served a stale cached bundle pointing at the old URL across environments — fixed by adding it to the task's env/globalEnv so the cache key busts when it changes.

qa.q: How does Turborepo's caching work, and what determines a cache hit?
qa.a: For each task Turbo computes a hash over its inputs: the package's source files, its resolved dependency graph, declared env vars, and relevant config. That hash is the cache key; the first run stores the task's outputs (the paths you list under `outputs`) plus its captured stdout/stderr. On a later run with the same key, Turbo restores the outputs and replays the logs instead of executing the task. Remote caching (Vercel or self-hosted) uploads these artifacts so a teammate or CI job can get a hit for work someone else already did.

qa.q: What does `dependsOn: ["^build"]` mean, and how is it different from `"build"`?
qa.a: The `^` prefix means 'run this task in all of the package's topological dependencies first' — so before building app X, Turbo builds every internal package X depends on. Without the caret, a bare `"build"` would refer to another task within the same package (e.g., build depending on the same package's codegen). This is how Turbo enforces correct build order across the workspace graph while still parallelizing independent branches.

qa.q: Do you still need workspaces if you use Turborepo, or does Turbo replace them?
qa.a: You need both; they solve different problems. Package-manager workspaces (pnpm/npm/yarn) handle installing and symlinking internal packages via the workspace protocol so imports resolve to local source. Turborepo sits on top and handles task scheduling, graph ordering, parallelism, and caching. Turbo reads the dependency graph that workspaces establish — it does not link packages or install dependencies itself.

qa.q: What's the most common way to get a stale or wrong cache in Turborepo, and how do you prevent it?
qa.a: Env-var cache poisoning: a task's behavior depends on an environment variable (like an API URL or feature flag) that isn't part of the cache key, so Turbo serves a cached artifact built with a different value. You prevent it by declaring those variables in the task's `env` (or `globalEnv` for repo-wide ones) so changing them busts the hash. The same class of bug happens when `outputs` is incomplete — Turbo caches a 'success' but restores nothing, leaving you with missing build artifacts.

qa.q: What is a `persistent` task and why can't other tasks depend on it?
qa.a: A persistent task is a long-running process that never exits on its own — typically a dev server or watcher. Turbo marks it persistent so it won't try to cache it or wait for it to 'finish,' and it forbids other tasks from listing it in `dependsOn`, because a task that never completes would deadlock the graph. Practically this is why `dev` is persistent and uncached while `build` is cached.

qa.q: Turborepo vs Nx — when would you pick each?
qa.a: Turborepo is minimal-config, wraps your existing npm scripts, and its core value is task orchestration plus local/remote caching, so it's fast to adopt on an existing JS/TS monorepo. Nx is a heavier platform: it offers computation caching too but adds code generators, project graph visualization, module-boundary lint rules, and richer plugin/executor abstractions, at the cost of more buy-in. Choose Turbo when you want caching and ordering with low ceremony; choose Nx when you want an opinionated framework with enforced architecture and generators across a large org.

qa.q: When would you advise against a monorepo?
qa.a: When teams need hard access-control boundaries (a monorepo makes fine-grained per-project permissions awkward), when the repo would grow large enough to strain Git/CI without investment in tooling like sparse checkout and remote caching, or when projects are genuinely unrelated with no shared code or release coordination. Monorepos also concentrate blast radius — a bad root-level change or dependency bump can break everyone at once — so without disciplined CI and ownership (CODEOWNERS), the coordination cost can outweigh the sharing benefits.

qa.q: How do you run a task for just one package and its dependencies?
qa.a: Use `--filter`. `turbo run build --filter=web` scopes to the web package; `--filter=web...` includes its dependencies, and `--filter=...web` includes its dependents. Combined with Turbo's caching and change detection (e.g., `--filter=[origin/main]` to target packages changed since a git ref), this is how you keep local dev and CI from rebuilding the entire graph on every change.

bullets.point: These are all built-in generic utility types (Pick, Partial, Readonly, Record, Exclude) that transform one type into another at compile time only — zero runtime cost. The prereq is being comfortable with generics (<T>), keyof, indexed access (T[K]), union types, and mapped/conditional types, because every utility type is just a thin wrapper over those primitives. Interview angle: expect to be asked to re-implement one from scratch, which tests whether you understand mapped types rather than memorizing names.

Pick constructs a new type by selecting a subset of keys K (a union of literal keys) from T, e.g. Pick<User, 'id' | 'name'>. It matters for building narrow DTOs/view models from a large domain type without duplicating fields, so the derived type stays in sync when the source changes. Interview angle: contrast with Omit (which is the complement, Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>) and know K is constrained by keyof T so invalid keys are a compile error.

Partial makes every property of T optional (adds `?` to each key via a mapped type with the `?` modifier). It matters for PATCH/update payloads, config objects with defaults, and builder patterns where not all fields are supplied. Interview angle: it's shallow (nested objects stay required) so a DeepPartial needs a recursive conditional type; also know its inverse is Required<T> which strips optionality with `-?`.

Readonly marks every property immutable at the type level (`readonly` modifier via mapped type), preventing reassignment after construction. It matters for enforcing immutability contracts on shared state, Redux-style stores, and function params you don't want mutated. Interview angle: stress it is compile-time only — nothing freezes at runtime (use Object.freeze for that) — it's shallow, and `as const` produces deep readonly plus literal narrowing which Readonly<T> does not.

Record<K, V> builds an object type whose keys are K (typically a union of string/number literals or a broad string/number) and whose values are all V, e.g. Record<'admin'|'user', Permissions>. It matters for typing dictionaries, lookup maps, and exhaustive config keyed by a union. Interview angle: when K is a finite literal union, Record enforces every key is present (exhaustiveness); when K is `string`, it's an index signature with no such guarantee — know that trade-off.

Exclude is a conditional type that removes from union T all members assignable to U (T extends U ? never : T, distributed over the union). It matters for narrowing unions — e.g. dropping 'loading' from a status union or removing null. Interview angle: it operates on union members not object keys (that's Omit), its counterpart is Extract<T, U> which keeps the matching members, and the distributive `never` collapse is what makes it work.

z.infer<typeof schema> extracts the static TypeScript type from a zod runtime schema, giving you a single source of truth: define the schema once and derive the type instead of hand-writing a parallel interface that can drift. It matters because zod validates at runtime (API boundaries, form input, env vars) while z.infer keeps the compile-time type in lockstep. Interview angle: know z.infer vs z.input/z.output (they differ when transforms/defaults/coercion change the parsed shape), and that this is the reverse direction of the utility types — deriving a type from a value rather than a type from a type.

example.title: Building a typed user-settings PATCH endpoint
example.body: You own a User service. The canonical domain type is a large `User` interface (id, email, passwordHash, role, profile, createdAt...). For the public API you derive a `PublicUser = Omit<User, 'passwordHash'>` and a settings view with `Pick<User, 'email' | 'role' | 'profile'>` so the DTO can never accidentally leak the hash. The PATCH handler accepts `Partial<Pick<User, 'email' | 'profile'>>` so clients send only the fields they're changing. Role-based access is a `Record<Role, Permission[]>` map, where `Role` is a literal union — so adding a new role produces a compile error until you fill in its permissions (built-in exhaustiveness). The request status union is narrowed with `Exclude<Status, 'internal-error'>` before it's serialized to the client. Crucially, the request body isn't trusted, so you define a zod schema `updateUserSchema` and use `type UpdateUserBody = z.infer<typeof updateUserSchema>` as the handler's param type: zod `.parse()` validates at runtime and z.infer guarantees the compile-time type matches the validator exactly, so the two can't drift. Once frozen, the resolved settings object is passed around as `Readonly<Settings>` to guarantee downstream code doesn't mutate shared state.

qa.q: How would you implement Pick<T, K> and Partial<T> from scratch?
qa.a: Both are one-line mapped types. `type MyPick<T, K extends keyof T> = { [P in K]: T[P] }` iterates the selected keys and copies their value types via indexed access. `type MyPartial<T> = { [P in keyof T]?: T[P] }` maps over all keys adding the optional `?` modifier. The K constraint `extends keyof T` is what makes invalid keys a compile error, and being able to write these shows you understand mapped types rather than memorizing utility names.

qa.q: Pick/Omit vs Exclude/Extract — what's the difference and when do you use each?
qa.a: Pick and Omit operate on the *keys* of an object type (Omit<T,K> = Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>). Exclude and Extract operate on the *members of a union*. So you Omit a field from an interface, but you Exclude a variant from a status union like `Exclude<'idle'|'loading'|'error', 'error'>`. Mixing them up is a common mistake; the giveaway is whether you're reshaping an object or filtering a union.

qa.q: Readonly<T> is compile-time only — what does that mean in practice and how do you get real immutability?
qa.a: Readonly<T> and the `readonly` modifier are erased at compile time; the emitted JavaScript has no protection, so a value cast or `any` can still mutate the object at runtime. It's also shallow — nested objects remain mutable. For runtime immutability use Object.freeze (also shallow), and for deep compile-time immutability plus literal narrowing use `as const`, which is strictly stronger than Readonly<T>.

qa.q: When does Record<K, V> guarantee all keys exist and when doesn't it?
qa.a: When K is a finite literal union (e.g. `Record<'a'|'b', number>`), TypeScript requires every member key to be present, giving you free exhaustiveness — add a union member and the object errors until updated. When K is a wide type like `string` or `number`, Record degrades to an index signature with no presence guarantee, and accessing a key returns V even if it's actually undefined (unless noUncheckedIndexedAccess is on). Prefer literal-union keys when you want the compiler to enforce completeness.

qa.q: What is z.infer and why prefer it over writing a matching interface by hand?
qa.a: z.infer<typeof schema> statically extracts the type a zod schema produces after parsing, making the schema the single source of truth. Hand-writing a parallel interface means two definitions that silently drift when one changes; z.infer eliminates that class of bug because the type is derived from the validator. It also bridges runtime and compile time: zod validates untrusted input at the boundary, and z.infer ensures the value you get back is typed exactly as validated.

qa.q: When do z.input and z.output differ from each other?
qa.a: They differ whenever the schema transforms the data — via .transform(), .default(), coercion (z.coerce), or .pipe(). z.input is the type accepted *before* parsing; z.output (what z.infer aliases) is the type produced *after*. For example a field with `.default('x')` is optional on input but required on output, and `z.coerce.number()` accepts unknown/string on input but yields number on output. Using z.infer for a function's input parameter is a bug in those cases — you'd want z.input there.

qa.q: How does Exclude actually work under the hood?
qa.a: Exclude<T, U> is defined as `T extends U ? never : T`. Because conditional types distribute over naked union type parameters, this is evaluated per-member of T: each member that's assignable to U becomes `never`, and `never` is absorbed when the union is reassembled, so those members drop out. That distributive behavior over unions plus the `never` collapse is the whole mechanism — the same pattern underpins NonNullable = Exclude<T, null | undefined>.

qa.q: How would you compose these utilities to type a safe update payload that excludes immutable fields?
qa.a: Layer them: `Partial<Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'passwordHash'>>`. Omit (built on Pick + Exclude) strips the fields clients must never change, then Partial makes the remainder optional so a PATCH can send any subset. This is fully derived from the source User type, so adding a field to User automatically flows through, and dropping an immutable field is a one-line change. It demonstrates that utility types compose cleanly because each just returns another type.

bullets.point: Next.js is a React meta-framework that bakes in routing, server rendering, API routes and bundling. You reach for it over CRA/Vite when you need SEO, server data-fetching or a single codebase for front + back. Prereq is solid React + JS fundamentals.

A client-only React app ships an empty <div id=root> — crawlers and link-preview bots see nothing. Next server-renders real HTML with correct <title>/<meta>, so pages are indexable and shareable. Use the Metadata API for per-route SEO.

In client React, a parent renders → fetches → then the child renders → fetches, creating sequential round-trips (waterfalls). Server Components fetch on the server, close to the DB, and can parallelise — eliminating client waterfalls and spinner chains.
File-based routing, SSR/SSG/ISR, API routes, image/font optimisation, middleware, and zero-config bundling. Know these as the 'why Next' checklist in interviews.
create-next-app scaffolds the App Router: app/ holds routes, page.tsx is the UI for a route, layout.tsx wraps it. Understand the app/ vs pages/ router distinction.
Routing is the filesystem: a folder = a URL segment, page.tsx = that route's UI, nested folders = nested routes, [id] = dynamic segments. No react-router config.
Composing UI from components (often + Tailwind). Reinforces that presentational structure is just React; interactivity is where the server/client split starts to matter.
In the App Router components are Server Components by default — they render to HTML on the server, get streamed to the browser, then hydrate. Faster first paint, better SEO, secrets stay server-side.
layout.tsx wraps its route subtree and PERSISTS across navigation — shared nav/sidebar/footer don't unmount or re-fetch when you move between child pages. Key for perf and UX.
Layouts nest: each segment can have its own layout, composing top-down (root → section → page). Lets you scope shared UI to just part of the app.
Route groups (folder) organise files and share layouts WITHOUT adding a URL segment — useful for grouping (marketing) vs (app) sections cleanly.
Colocating reusable, non-route components outside the routing tree keeps app/ focused on routes. A structure/organisation concern interviewers probe for maintainability.
The moment you add an event handler or a hook, that component must be a Client Component ('use client') — Server Components can't have interactivity or browser APIs.

The core mental model: Server Components (default) ship zero JS, can fetch and touch secrets; Client Components ('use client') run in the browser for state/effects/handlers. You can import client into server and pass server-rendered children down, but not the reverse.

example.title: Real-world: the signin + dashboard of a SaaS app
example.body: Picture the cohort's own app. The /dashboard route is a Server Component: it fetches the user's data directly from the DB on the server (no client waterfall, DB creds never reach the browser) and streams ready HTML — so it's fast and SEO-clean. The persistent sidebar lives in layout.tsx, so navigating between dashboard tabs never re-renders or re-fetches it. The signin FORM, however, needs state and an onClick submit, so it's a Client Component ('use client'). This split — server for data + shell, client for islands of interactivity — is the pattern you defend in an interview.

qa.q: When do you use a Server Component vs a Client Component, and what happens if you fetch data in a Client Component?
qa.a: Default to Server Components for data-fetching, static content and anything touching secrets/DB — they ship no JS. Use Client Components only for interactivity (state, effects, event handlers, browser APIs). Fetching in a Client Component works but moves the request to the browser (extra round-trip, exposes endpoints, re-introduces waterfalls and loading states) — usually a smell unless the data is user-triggered/real-time.

qa.q: How does Next.js eliminate the request waterfall that plain React suffers from?
qa.a: By moving fetches to the server. Server Components run on the server next to the data source and can fire fetches in parallel before any HTML is sent, so the client receives a fully-populated page instead of rendering, discovering it needs data, fetching, then re-rendering down the tree.

qa.q: What is hydration and what's its cost?
qa.a: Hydration is React attaching event listeners and reconstructing component state on top of the server-rendered HTML in the browser. Cost: the JS bundle for client components must download and execute before the page is interactive (TTI lag), and a server/client markup mismatch throws hydration errors. Minimising client components keeps hydration cheap.

qa.q: SSR vs SSG vs ISR — how do you choose?
qa.a: SSG (build-time HTML) for content that rarely changes — fastest, cacheable at the edge (docs, marketing). SSR (per-request) for personalised or always-fresh data (dashboards). ISR (static + background revalidation) for mostly-static content that updates periodically (product pages, blogs) — you get SSG speed with a freshness window.

qa.q: Does 'use client' mean the component only runs in the browser?
qa.a: No — a common misconception. Client Components are still server-rendered once to HTML for the initial load (good for SEO/first paint); 'use client' marks them as also needing to run in the browser (hydrate) so they can be interactive. They run in both places, not client-only.

qa.q: How do nested layouts render, and why do they help performance?
qa.a: Layouts compose from the root down and preserve state across navigation within their subtree — a parent layout doesn't unmount when a child page changes. So shared chrome (nav, sidebar) and its data survive route transitions, avoiding needless re-renders/re-fetches and giving instant-feeling navigation.

qa.q: Why is SEO fundamentally better in Next than in a CRA/Vite SPA?
qa.a: A SPA serves an empty HTML shell and builds the DOM in JS after load — crawlers/preview bots that don't execute JS (or execute it partially) see nothing, and meta tags aren't present at request time. Next sends complete HTML with route-specific metadata (via the Metadata API) on the first response, so content and social/OG tags are there immediately.

bullets.point: The thesis is that keeping your hands on the home row and driving everything (editing, navigation, window/session management, browsing) via keyboard cuts context-switch latency and RSI, and compounds over thousands of small actions per day. The interview angle isn't the tools themselves but demonstrating you optimize your own workflow and understand where your time actually goes.

vi (and modern Vim) is a modal editor: Normal mode for movement/operators, Insert for typing, Visual for selection, plus operator+motion composition (e.g. d2w, ci"). It matters because it's on virtually every Unix box (bastion hosts, containers, CI shells) where you have no GUI, so `vimtutor`-level fluency is a baseline ops skill, and its grammar (verb+noun) is the mental model every other tool on this list reuses.

bullets.step: Vimium — Vim keys in the browser
bullets.point: Vimium is a browser extension giving Vim-style keybindings: `f` to link-hint and follow, `j/k` scroll, `H/L` history, `/` find, `d/u` half-page. It matters as a low-cost way to keep the keyboard-first mental model consistent across your editor and browser during research/docs/PR review, eliminating the reach-for-mouse tax.

Neovim is a Vim fork with a first-class embedded Lua runtime, async job control, built-in LSP client, Treesitter parsing, and a stable RPC/msgpack API for external UIs. It matters because it turns the editor into a real IDE (completion, go-to-definition, diagnostics) via LSP without leaving the terminal, and Lua config (vs Vimscript) makes plugins faster and maintainable — this is what people mean today by 'daily-driving Vim'.

The VSCodeVim / vscode-neovim extensions bring modal editing into VS Code, letting you keep muscle memory while retaining VS Code's debugger, extension ecosystem, and remote/dev-container tooling. The trade-off worth articulating: VSCodeVim reimplements Vim in JS (some fidelity gaps, occasional lag on large files) whereas vscode-neovim embeds a real nvim process for near-perfect semantics — pick based on how much of your config you need.

A tiling window manager (i3/sway on Linux, yabai/Rectangle on macOS, PowerToys FancyZones/komorebi on Windows) auto-arranges windows into non-overlapping tiles addressed by keyboard, with workspaces/tags for grouping. It matters because it removes manual window dragging/alt-tab hunting and gives deterministic, reproducible layouts — the OS-level analog of the same keyboard-driven philosophy.

tmux multiplexes one terminal into multiple sessions/windows/panes and, crucially, detaches: your processes keep running server-side after you disconnect and you reattach later. It matters for remote work — survive SSH drops, resume long builds/REPLs, and share a session for pairing — making it the terminal-side complement to a tiling WM and a common reason `screen`/`tmux` shows up in ops interviews.

Shell rc files (.bashrc/.zshrc, .zprofile) plus tool configs (.vimrc/init.lua, .tmux.conf, git config) define aliases, functions, PATH, prompt, and completions; version-controlling them as 'dotfiles' makes any machine reproducible in minutes. Know the login vs interactive distinction (which files run when, e.g. .zprofile once at login vs .zshrc per interactive shell) — a frequent source of 'my alias/PATH isn't loading' bugs.

example.title: Debugging a prod incident on a bastion host, keyboard-only
example.body: You're paged for elevated 5xx on a service reachable only through a jump host with no GUI. On your laptop, a tiling WM snaps a browser (driven with Vimium to jump through the runbook and Grafana) beside your terminal. In the terminal you SSH to the bastion and attach a persistent tmux session so a flaky VPN won't kill your work; you split panes to tail logs in one, run the app in a REPL in another, and edit the config in the third. The remote box only has vi, so you fix a bad timeout value with `ci"` and `:wq` — no mouse, no GUI needed. Back on your workstation you reproduce locally in Neovim, where the LSP flags the type error your patch introduced before you even save, and you commit using the git aliases from your version-controlled dotfiles. The whole loop — browse, connect, edit, verify, commit — never left the keyboard, and the tmux session is still alive when you reattach the next morning to confirm the fix held.

qa.q: Explain Vim's modal editing model and why operator+motion composition is powerful.
qa.a: Vim separates modes: Normal (navigate/command), Insert (type), Visual (select), plus command-line. The power is a composable grammar where operators (d delete, c change, y yank) combine with motions or text objects (w word, } paragraph, i" inside quotes), so `daw`, `ci(`, `y2j` are all learned from a small set of primitives rather than memorized shortcuts. This means your editing vocabulary scales multiplicatively instead of additively, and the same verbs work across counts, motions, and text objects.

qa.q: What does Neovim add over classic Vim, and why does it matter for daily use?
qa.a: Neovim adds an embedded Lua interpreter (faster, saner config than Vimscript), async job/event handling, a built-in LSP client, Treesitter for accurate syntax/highlighting/structural edits, and a stable RPC API so external GUIs and tools can drive it. Practically, LSP + Treesitter give you IDE features — completion, diagnostics, go-to-def, semantic highlighting — inside a terminal editor. It matters because you get modern tooling without abandoning the modal workflow or the terminal.

qa.q: VSCodeVim vs vscode-neovim — how do they differ and when would you choose each?
qa.a: VSCodeVim reimplements Vim semantics in TypeScript inside the extension, so it's easy to install and integrates with VS Code settings, but it can diverge from real Vim on complex commands, macros, and large-file performance. vscode-neovim embeds an actual Neovim process and delegates Normal-mode editing to it, giving near-perfect fidelity and letting you reuse your init.lua/plugins, at the cost of setup complexity and needing nvim installed. Choose VSCodeVim for light modal editing; choose vscode-neovim if you rely on real Vim macros, custom mappings, or an existing config.

qa.q: Why is tmux valuable for remote work, and how is detach/attach implemented conceptually?
qa.a: tmux runs a persistent server process that owns your sessions, windows, and panes; your client terminal merely attaches to it. When your SSH connection drops or you detach with the prefix + d, the server and all child processes keep running, and you reattach later with `tmux attach` to the exact same state. This is what lets long builds, REPLs, and monitoring survive disconnects, and enables session sharing for pair debugging.

qa.q: How do login vs interactive shells affect where you put configuration, and what's a common bug?
qa.a: A login shell (e.g. first SSH login) sources profile files like .zprofile/.bash_profile once; interactive non-login shells source .zshrc/.bashrc every time a new shell opens. PATH and one-time environment exports belong in the profile, while aliases, functions, and prompt setup belong in the rc file. The classic bug is putting an alias in .zprofile (so it doesn't show up in new terminal tabs) or setting PATH in .zshrc so it gets appended repeatedly or doesn't apply to non-interactive contexts like cron.

qa.q: What problem does a tiling window manager solve, and what's the trade-off versus a stacking WM?
qa.a: A tiling WM automatically arranges windows into non-overlapping regions addressed by keyboard, with workspaces/tags for grouping, eliminating manual dragging, resizing, and alt-tab hunting and giving reproducible layouts. The trade-off is a steeper learning curve and friction with apps that assume floating windows (dialogs, some GUIs), which is why most tiling WMs support float exceptions and per-app rules. It's the desktop-level expression of the same keyboard-first, deterministic philosophy as tmux and Vim.

qa.q: You edit config on a remote container that only has vi — what's your minimal survival kit?
qa.a: Enter insert mode with `i`/`a`, escape with Esc, save-and-quit with `:wq`, quit-without-saving `:q!`, delete a line `dd`, undo `u`. For targeted edits, `/pattern` to search, `ciw`/`ci"` to replace a word or quoted value, and `:%s/old/new/g` for a global substitution. Knowing these means you're never blocked on a bastion host or minimal image that lacks nano or a GUI editor.

qa.q: Why version-control your dotfiles, and how would you make them portable across machines?
qa.a: Version-controlling dotfiles (rc files, editor/tmux/git configs) makes any new machine reproducible in minutes, gives you history/rollback, and documents your environment. Portability techniques include a bare git repo or a symlink manager (GNU stow, chezmoi), guarding OS- or host-specific blocks behind conditionals (e.g. `case $(uname)`), keeping secrets out via templating or a separate untracked include, and bootstrapping tool installs from a single script. The goal is idempotent setup: clone, run one command, and the environment is identical.

bullets.point: Next.js is not just a React renderer; it ships a backend runtime so a single deployable serves UI, API endpoints, and server-rendered pages. The interview angle is knowing the three backend surfaces (Server Components for reads, Route Handlers for public HTTP APIs, Server Actions for mutations) and when each is appropriate versus reaching for a separate Express/Nest service.

Classic CSR fetches data in useEffect after mount, forcing loading/error state machines, request waterfalls, and exposing API keys to the browser. This is the baseline problem Next's server model solves; be ready to articulate why useEffect fetching hurts SEO, TTFB, and creates client-server round-trips.

App-router Server Components are async functions that await data (fetch or a DB call) on the server before HTML is produced, so there is no client loading spinner and no exposed credentials. Interview points: fetch is deduped/cached per request, secrets stay server-side, and you push the fetch to where the data lives instead of round-tripping through the browser.

bullets.step: Loading UI with loading.tsx and Suspense
bullets.point: A loading.tsx (or explicit <Suspense>) wraps a route segment so Next streams a fallback instantly while the async Server Component resolves, then swaps in the real content. Know that this is React Suspense + streaming SSR under the hood, letting the shell paint fast while slow data streams in, avoiding an all-or-nothing blocking render.

app/api/*/route.ts exports named functions (GET, POST, etc.) that receive a Web Request and return a Response/NextResponse — the way to build actual HTTP endpoints for third-party clients, webhooks, or mobile apps. Contrast with the old pages/api handler signature and know these run on the server (Node or Edge runtime) with full access to secrets and DB.

bullets.step: Consolidating the backend into the app
bullets.point: Instead of a standalone API server, you move endpoints into Next Route Handlers so frontend and backend deploy together, share types, and skip CORS. The trade-off worth stating: simpler DX and colocated types, but coupled scaling/deploy and a serverless execution model that complicates long-lived connections and background jobs.

A Client Component ('use client') holds form state and POSTs credentials to the signup endpoint, handling validation feedback and pending state. The angle is the client/server boundary: only interactive, stateful pieces need 'use client', and you should never trust client-side validation alone.

The POST handler validates input (e.g. zod), checks for existing users, hashes the password with bcrypt/argon2, and persists — never storing plaintext. Interview depth: why hashing (not encryption) with a salt and adaptive cost, returning generic errors to avoid user enumeration, and doing all validation server-side.

Prisma provides a typed schema, migrations, and a generated client so DB access is type-safe end-to-end. Be ready on migrate dev vs migrate deploy, how the schema maps to SQL, and that Prisma is an ORM/query builder you call directly from Server Components and handlers.

bullets.step: Better fetches: skip the HTTP hop
bullets.point: From a Server Component you should call the DB (or a shared service function) directly rather than fetching your own /api route, because self-fetching adds a needless HTTP round-trip and serialization overhead. The principle: Route Handlers are for external callers; internal server code should invoke the data layer directly.

In dev, hot-reload re-imports modules and instantiates a new PrismaClient each time, exhausting DB connections; the fix is caching the instance on globalThis in non-production. Interview relevance: this generalizes to any expensive connection-holding client under serverless/HMR, and to connection pooling limits (pgBouncer/Data Proxy) in production.

Server Actions ('use server' functions) let you invoke server code straight from a form or event without hand-writing an API route, and pair with revalidatePath/revalidateTag to refresh cached data. Know the internals: Next generates a hidden POST endpoint per action, so treat inputs as untrusted and enforce auth/validation just like any endpoint.

example.title: Building auth + a dashboard for a SaaS app in a single Next.js app
example.body: You are building the onboarding and dashboard for a B2B SaaS. The marketing pages and the app live in one Next.js (app router) deployment backed by Postgres via Prisma. The signup page is a Client Component that POSTs to a POST Route Handler (app/api/auth/signup/route.ts), which validates with zod, checks for an existing email, hashes the password with bcrypt, and inserts the user. After login, the dashboard route is an async Server Component that reads the user's projects by calling Prisma directly (no self-fetch to /api), so the HTML arrives populated with no client spinner and no exposed DB URL. Slow widgets (usage charts) are wrapped in Suspense with a loading.tsx so the shell streams instantly. 'Create project' and 'rename' are handled by Server Actions that mutate via Prisma and call revalidatePath to refresh the list. In dev you hit 'too many connections' from Postgres because HMR keeps making new PrismaClients — you fix it with the globalThis singleton pattern, and before production you front Postgres with a pooler since each serverless invocation opens its own connection. The mobile app team later needs the same data, so you keep the JSON Route Handlers as the public, versioned API while the web app keeps calling the data layer directly.

qa.q: When do you use a Server Component, a Route Handler, and a Server Action?
qa.a: Use Server Components for reads that render into a page — await the DB/fetch directly so data is server-rendered with no client waterfall. Use Route Handlers for public HTTP endpoints consumed by external clients (mobile, webhooks, third parties) or when you need fine control over the Request/Response. Use Server Actions for mutations triggered from the UI (form submit, button click) where you want to skip authoring an endpoint and get progressive enhancement plus cache revalidation. The rule of thumb: reads in components, mutations in actions, and explicit HTTP contracts in handlers.

qa.q: Why is fetching your own /api route from a Server Component considered an anti-pattern?
qa.a: The Server Component already runs on the server with direct access to your DB and service functions, so calling your own API means an extra HTTP round-trip, JSON serialization/deserialization, and a second execution context for no benefit. It adds latency and can even deadlock in constrained serverless setups. Instead, extract a plain async function (the data layer) and call it from both the Server Component and any Route Handler that external clients need.

qa.q: Explain the Prisma singleton problem in Next.js and how you fix it.
qa.a: In development, Next's hot module replacement re-evaluates modules on each change, and if you `new PrismaClient()` at import time you spawn a new client — and a new connection pool — every reload, quickly hitting Postgres's connection limit. The fix is to store the client on `globalThis` and reuse it, only creating a fresh instance in production where modules aren't reloaded. In production serverless, the deeper issue is that each function instance opens its own pool, so you add an external pooler (pgBouncer, Prisma Accelerate/Data Proxy) to cap total connections.

qa.q: How does loading.tsx / Suspense streaming actually work?
qa.a: Next uses React's streaming SSR: the route shell renders immediately and any subtree wrapped in Suspense (which loading.tsx creates automatically for a segment) emits a fallback in the initial HTML stream. As each async Server Component resolves on the server, Next streams the finished HTML chunk plus a small script that swaps out the fallback in place. This gives fast first paint and avoids blocking the whole page on the slowest query, without shipping a client-side loading state machine.

qa.q: What are the security considerations with Server Actions?
qa.a: A Server Action compiles to a hidden POST endpoint that anyone can invoke with crafted arguments, so you must treat its inputs as fully untrusted — validate with something like zod and enforce authentication/authorization inside the action, never relying on the fact that it's 'only' called from your UI. Next includes origin/CSRF protections for actions, but that doesn't replace authz checks. Also avoid closing over sensitive data in the action's bound arguments, since those are serialized to the client.

qa.q: How does caching and revalidation work for data fetching in the app router?
qa.a: Next extends fetch with caching semantics: by default many fetches are cached (force-cache) and you opt into freshness with `{ next: { revalidate: N } }` for time-based ISR or `{ cache: 'no-store' }` for always-dynamic. Tag-based invalidation (`next: { tags: [...] }` plus `revalidateTag`) and `revalidatePath` let mutations surgically bust the cache after a Server Action writes data. The mental model is a request-scoped dedupe layer plus a persistent data cache, and you should be explicit rather than relying on defaults, which have shifted across Next versions.

qa.q: What are the trade-offs of running your backend inside Next.js versus a separate service?
qa.a: Colocating gives shared TypeScript types, no CORS, one deploy, and simpler local dev, which is ideal for product teams shipping a web app. The costs: you inherit the serverless/edge execution model (short-lived, stateless), which is awkward for long-running jobs, websockets, or holding DB connections, and you couple frontend and backend scaling and release cadence. For heavy async workloads, streaming pipelines, or multiple consumers, a dedicated service (or offloading to queues/workers) is usually the better boundary.

qa.q: How should password signup be implemented securely in a Route Handler?
qa.a: Validate and normalize input server-side (never trust the client), then check for an existing account and hash the password with an adaptive algorithm like bcrypt or argon2 using a per-user salt and a sensible cost factor — hashing, not reversible encryption. Return generic, uniform responses and timing to avoid leaking whether an email is registered (user enumeration). Store only the hash, issue a session/JWT on success, and put the handler behind rate limiting to blunt credential-stuffing.

bullets.point: Sets the study arc for DSA: master how to measure cost (complexity) before touching data structures, so every later choice (array vs. hashmap, sort vs. scan) is justified by asymptotics rather than intuition. Interview angle: interviewers expect you to state and defend the time/space complexity of every solution unprompted — this module is the vocabulary for that.

Quantifies how an algorithm's running time and memory scale with input size n, independent of hardware or language, by counting dominant operations. It matters because the same problem has solutions differing by orders of magnitude at scale (n=10^6 makes O(n^2) ~10^12 ops, infeasible); interviewers judge whether you can reason about scalability, not just correctness.

Big-O is the asymptotic upper bound (worst case), Big-Omega the lower bound (best case), and Big-Theta the tight bound when upper and lower match; drop constants and lower-order terms. The common interview trap is conflating O with worst case and average case — O(n^2) quicksort is worst case while its Theta on random input is n log n, so state which case you mean.

Hands-on derivation: count loop iterations, nested loops (multiply), sequential blocks (add, keep max), and log factors from halving. The skill tested is spotting hidden costs — e.g., string concatenation in a loop is O(n^2), or a .contains() on a list inside a loop turns O(n) into O(n^2).

Memorize the ladder: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!), and map each to a pattern — hashmap lookup, binary search, single scan, comparison sort, nested loop, subset enumeration, permutations. Interviewers want you to instantly recognize which class a candidate solution falls into and whether it clears the input constraints.

A formula for recurrences of the form T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n) (divide-and-conquer), comparing f(n) against n^(log_b a) across three cases to get the closed-form bound without unrolling. It's the fast way to answer 'why is merge sort O(n log n)?' (a=2,b=2,f=n → case 2) or binary search O(log n) (a=1,b=2,f=1), a frequent follow-up when you propose a recursive solution.

example.title: Choosing a lookup strategy for a 50M-row user-permissions service
example.body: You own an authorization service that checks, per request, whether a user has a given permission across ~50M user-role rows. The v1 implementation loads roles into a List and does a linear .contains() scan per check — O(n) per lookup, and under load with n in the millions this dominates p99 latency. Profiling confirms the hot path scales linearly with tenant size. You migrate the membership check to a HashSet (O(1) average lookup, O(n) build amortized at load), and for range-style queries ('all permissions granted before time T') you keep a sorted array plus binary search (O(log n)). When a teammate proposes a recursive tree-merge to reconcile role hierarchies, you use the Master Theorem to show the merge-based approach is O(n log n) versus the O(n^2) pairwise reconciliation, justifying the design in the review. Every decision here — the data structure swap, the sort-then-binary-search, the recurrence analysis — is the complexity vocabulary from this module applied to a real latency bug.

qa.q: What's the difference between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega, and why does saying 'O(n^2)' alone underspecify an algorithm?
qa.a: Big-O is an asymptotic upper bound, Big-Omega a lower bound, and Big-Theta a tight bound that holds only when the upper and lower bounds coincide. Big-O says the algorithm is no worse than the bound but doesn't claim it's achieved — O(n^2) is technically a correct (if loose) bound for an O(n) algorithm. It also doesn't specify which input distribution you mean; you should pair the notation with a case, e.g. 'O(n^2) worst case, Theta(n log n) on average,' as with quicksort.

qa.q: Why do we drop constants and lower-order terms, and when does that abstraction bite you in practice?
qa.a: Asymptotic analysis describes growth as n approaches infinity, where constants and lower-order terms become negligible relative to the dominant term, giving a hardware-independent comparison. It bites you at small or bounded n and in constant-factor-sensitive hot paths: an O(n) algorithm with a huge constant (cache misses, syscalls) can lose to an O(n log n) one for realistic sizes, and two O(n log n) sorts can differ 3x in wall-clock. Big-O guides algorithm selection; profiling settles constant factors.

qa.q: State the Master Theorem and use it to explain merge sort's complexity.
qa.a: For T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n) with a>=1, b>1, compare f(n) to n^(log_b a): case 1, if f is polynomially smaller, T=Theta(n^(log_b a)); case 2, if f=Theta(n^(log_b a)), T=Theta(n^(log_b a) * log n); case 3, if f is polynomially larger (and regularity holds), T=Theta(f(n)). Merge sort is T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n), so a=2,b=2, n^(log_2 2)=n, and f(n)=n matches — case 2 — giving Theta(n log n).

qa.q: Give an example of code that looks O(n) but is actually O(n^2), and how to fix it.
qa.a: Building a string by repeated concatenation in a loop (s += token) is O(n^2) in languages with immutable strings because each concatenation copies the whole accumulated string; the fix is a StringBuilder/list-join for O(n). Similarly, calling list.contains(x) inside a loop over the list is O(n^2) — replace the inner membership test with a HashSet for O(1) average, making the whole thing O(n). The tell is a hidden linear operation nested inside another linear loop.

qa.q: How does amortized complexity differ from worst-case, using a dynamic array as the example?
qa.a: Amortized complexity is the average cost per operation over a sequence, guaranteeing the total, even though individual operations may spike. A dynamic array's push is O(n) worst case on the resize that copies all elements, but because capacity doubles, resizes happen geometrically rarely, so the total cost of n pushes is O(n) — amortized O(1) per push. It differs from average-case analysis: amortized makes no probabilistic assumption about inputs, it's a guaranteed bound over the worst sequence.

qa.q: Given constraints like n <= 10^5 or n <= 20 in a problem statement, how do you back out the intended complexity?
qa.a: Assume roughly 10^8 simple operations per second as the feasibility budget. n <= 10^5 permits about O(n log n) or O(n sqrt n) (~10^6–10^7 ops) but rules out O(n^2) at 10^10; n <= 10^3 tolerates O(n^2); n <= 20 signals exponential O(2^n) subset enumeration or O(n! ) permutations is expected. Reading constraints backwards is a fast way to eliminate solution classes before you start coding — interviewers give constraints deliberately.

qa.q: When is O(log n) better than O(1) in real systems, or when would you accept worse asymptotics on purpose?
qa.a: Asymptotically O(1) always wins at scale, but you accept worse asymptotics when constant factors, memory, or simplicity dominate for your actual n. A balanced BST's O(log n) ordered lookup can beat a hash table's O(1) when you also need range queries, sorted iteration, or predictable (non-amortized, no rehash-spike) latency. Similarly a linear scan over a small contiguous array often beats a hashmap due to cache locality, so 'better Big-O' is a starting heuristic, not the final answer.

bullets.point: Start from the boilerplate mono-structure (separate backend Express app and frontend React app, plus package.json per side). The interview angle is recognizing a full-stack project layout and being able to bootstrap dependencies, env config, and the MongoDB connection string quickly.

Define the User model (firstName, lastName, username/email, password) with validation, unique index on username, and trim/lowercase normalization. Matters because schema design (indexes, required fields, field constraints) is your first line of data integrity and query performance in MongoDB.

Modularize routes with express.Router() instead of hanging everything off the app, e.g. a user router file exporting a router object. The point is separation of concerns and scalability — routers keep route definitions, middleware, and handlers grouped by domain.

Mount routers under a versioned prefix like app.use('/api/v1/user', userRouter) in the entry file. API versioning and a consistent base path matter for backward compatibility and clean client integration.
