Pull in the core stack: express, mongoose, jsonwebtoken (JWT), bcrypt (hashing), zod (input validation), and cors. Knowing what each solves — and that you never hand-roll auth/crypto — is a common interview signal of production maturity.

Implement /signup (validate with zod, hash password with bcrypt, create user, issue JWT) and /signin (verify credentials, return JWT). The interview depth is on password hashing (salt + cost factor), never storing plaintext, and returning a signed token rather than a session.

Write middleware that reads the Authorization: Bearer <token> header, verifies the JWT with the secret, and attaches userId to req before protected handlers run. Middleware is the reusable gate for every authenticated route — expect questions on where it sits in the chain and how it short-circuits with 401/403.

Add authenticated endpoints: update profile (PUT, zod-validated partial fields, re-hash password if changed) and a bulk user search using a filter with $regex/$or on first/last name. Tests your grasp of query building and protecting mutations behind auth.

Introduce a separate Account collection holding userId (ObjectId ref to User) and balance. Splitting balance out of User is a deliberate normalization choice — it isolates the frequently-mutated, transactionally-sensitive money field from mostly-static profile data.

Implement money transfer inside a Mongoose session/transaction: debit sender and credit receiver as one atomic unit, aborting on insufficient funds or error. This is the marquee interview topic — MongoDB multi-document transactions require a replica set, and the transaction prevents partial transfers under failure.

Seed each new user with a random starting balance on signup (create the Account doc alongside the User). Simple, but it teaches you to keep the User and Account creation consistent — ideally the pair should also be atomic.
Add a dedicated account router mounted at /api/v1/account, separate from the user router. Reinforces domain-based routing — money operations live apart from identity operations.

Expose GET /balance (return the authenticated user's balance) and POST /transfer (amount + recipient, runs the transaction). These are the business-critical endpoints; expect questions on validating amount > 0, checking sufficient funds, and idempotency.

Consolidate and smoke-test the backend end to end: signup, signin, protected balance read, and a transfer. The value is verifying the auth-to-transaction flow works as a whole before pivoting to the frontend.

Set up react-router-dom with BrowserRouter and a Routes tree for /signup, /signin, and /dashboard. Client-side routing matters for SPA navigation without full reloads and sets up where protected/redirect logic will live.

Build primitives — Button, InputBox, Heading, SubHeading, BottomWarning — as props-driven, composable pieces. The interview angle is component reuse and a lightweight design system rather than duplicated markup per page.

Compose the Signin page from the primitives, manage controlled inputs with useState, and call the backend signin on submit. Demonstrates assembling a screen from components plus local form state handling.
Build the post-login Dashboard: an AppBar, a Balance display fed by GET /balance, and a Users list with search. This is the app's home screen and shows how you fetch and render authenticated data.
Group the shared building blocks used by both Signup and Signin (input boxes, headings, warning links). Keeps the two auth screens visually and behaviorally consistent through shared components.
Build the components only visible after auth — Users list, Send-Money view, balance card. These consume the token and hit protected endpoints, tying UI state to authentication.

Connect frontend to backend with axios: send the JWT in the Authorization header, persist the token (localStorage), redirect on auth, and execute a real end-to-end transfer. The integration step is where CORS, token storage trade-offs, and error handling all surface.

example.title: Building and debugging the money-transfer flow in a Paytm-style wallet
example.body: You're an SDE-2 shipping the peer-to-peer transfer feature. A user signs in, the React app stores the JWT and calls GET /api/v1/account/balance with a Bearer header; the auth middleware verifies the token and sets req.userId. On the Send Money screen the user picks a recipient and an amount, and the client POSTs to /transfer. On the server you open a Mongoose session, start a transaction, re-read the sender's account inside the session, reject if balance < amount, then $inc the sender down and the recipient up and commit — aborting on any error so you never leave money debited-but-not-credited. During load testing you notice two concurrent transfers from the same account can both pass the balance check and overdraw it; you fix it by moving the check-and-decrement into the atomic update (a conditional $inc guarded by balance >= amount) inside the transaction, and confirm your MongoDB is a replica set so multi-document transactions are even supported. This one feature exercises the whole stack: Mongoose schemas, JWT auth middleware, zod validation of the amount, transactional writes, and the axios-wired React UI.

qa.q: Why store the balance in a separate Account collection instead of a field on the User document?
qa.a: It separates a hot, transactionally-sensitive, frequently-mutated value (money) from mostly-static profile data, which keeps write contention and document churn off the User document. It also lets the account/money domain evolve independently — you can add fields like currency, frozen status, or per-account indexes without bloating User. In interviews, frame it as normalization plus isolating the field that participates in transactions.

qa.q: MongoDB is often called schemaless — how do multi-document transactions work here and what do they require?
qa.a: Mongoose transactions use a client session: you startSession, startTransaction, run all reads/writes with { session }, then commitTransaction or abortTransaction on error. They give ACID semantics across multiple documents, so a debit and credit either both apply or neither does. Critically, multi-document transactions require MongoDB to run as a replica set (even a single-node replica set locally) — they do not work on a standalone mongod.

qa.q: A naive transfer reads the balance, checks it, then decrements. What's the race condition and how do you fix it?
qa.a: Two concurrent transfers can both read the same balance, both pass the check, and both decrement — overdrawing the account (a classic check-then-act TOCTOU bug). The fix is to make the check and decrement atomic: use a conditional update like updateOne({ _id, balance: { $gte: amount } }, { $inc: { balance: -amount } }) and treat a modifiedCount of 0 as insufficient funds. Running this inside the transaction gives both atomicity of the single account update and all-or-nothing across both accounts.

qa.q: Why bcrypt for passwords, and what's the trade-off in the cost factor?
qa.a: bcrypt is a deliberately slow, salted, adaptive hash designed to resist brute-force and rainbow-table attacks — unlike fast hashes like SHA-256 which are unsuitable for passwords. The per-password salt defeats precomputation, and the cost factor (rounds) lets you scale difficulty as hardware improves. The trade-off: higher cost hardens against attackers but adds latency to every signin, so you tune it to a few hundred milliseconds rather than maxing it out.

qa.q: JWT vs server-side sessions — why does this project use JWT and what are the downsides?
qa.a: JWTs are stateless: the server signs a token, the client sends it on each request, and the server just verifies the signature without a session store — which scales horizontally with no shared session state. The downsides are revocation and expiry: you can't easily invalidate a valid unexpired token, so logout/ban needs short TTLs plus refresh tokens or a denylist. You also can't store sensitive data in the payload since it's only signed, not encrypted, and readable by anyone.

qa.q: The frontend stores the JWT in localStorage. What's the security trade-off versus httpOnly cookies?
qa.a: localStorage is simple and immune to CSRF (nothing is auto-sent), but it's readable by any JavaScript, so an XSS vulnerability leaks the token directly. httpOnly cookies are invisible to JS (mitigating XSS token theft) but are sent automatically, so they need CSRF protection (SameSite, CSRF tokens). For a real wallet you'd lean toward httpOnly cookies with SameSite plus strict XSS hygiene; localStorage is acceptable for a learning project but worth calling out as a weakness.

qa.q: Why validate inputs with zod on the backend when the React form already validates?
qa.a: Client-side validation is purely UX — it can be bypassed by hitting the API directly with curl or Postman, so it is never a security boundary. Zod on the server enforces the real contract: types, required fields, string lengths, and amount > 0, rejecting malformed requests with a 400 before they touch business logic or the database. The rule is validate on the client for feedback, validate on the server for trust.

qa.q: Walk through what the auth middleware does and where it sits in the request lifecycle.
qa.a: It runs before the protected route handlers: it reads the Authorization header, extracts the Bearer token, and calls jwt.verify with the secret. On success it attaches the decoded userId to req and calls next() to pass control down the chain; on a missing, malformed, or invalid/expired token it short-circuits with a 401/403 and never reaches the handler. Mounting it selectively (e.g., on the account router but not on signup/signin) is what distinguishes public from protected endpoints.

bullets.point: A modern SPA (React/Vue) compiles to static HTML/CSS/JS, so it needs no application server — the canonical AWS pattern is S3 (storage) + CloudFront (CDN/TLS) + Route 53 (DNS). Interview angle: articulate why you don't put a React app on EC2/nginx — no runtime, no autoscaling, near-zero cost, global low-latency delivery, and the whole thing is cache-friendly and immutable.

`npm run build` (Vite/CRA) runs bundling, minification, tree-shaking and code-splitting to emit a `dist/`/`build/` folder with content-hashed filenames (e.g. `app.4f8a2.js`). The content hash is the key detail: it enables aggressive far-future caching because any code change produces a new filename, so cache invalidation is implicit for assets — only `index.html` needs to change.

A CDN caches your assets at globally distributed edge PoPs so users are served from a nearby location instead of a single origin region, cutting latency and offloading the origin. Interview angle: understand cache key, TTL, cache hit ratio, and that the CDN also gives you TLS termination, HTTP/2/3, and DDoS absorption at the edge — not just speed.

S3 is a flat key-value object store (not a filesystem) — each file is an object with a key, metadata, and content-type. You create a bucket to hold the build output; keep it private and treat it purely as the CloudFront origin rather than exposing it publicly.

Use `aws s3 sync ./dist s3://bucket --delete` to push the build; the `--delete` flag prunes stale objects. Set `Content-Type` correctly and differentiated `Cache-Control` headers — long `max-age=31536000, immutable` for hashed assets, `no-cache`/short TTL for `index.html` — because S3 metadata drives how CloudFront and browsers cache each object.

bullets.step: Access via the S3 endpoint (and its limits)
bullets.point: S3 offers a website endpoint (`*.s3-website-<region>.amazonaws.com`) supporting index/error documents and redirects but HTTP-only and no custom TLS, versus the REST endpoint (`*.s3.amazonaws.com`) which supports HTTPS and private access. Interview angle: the website endpoint alone can't give you HTTPS on a custom domain — that's the motivation for putting CloudFront in front.

bullets.step: Put CloudFront in front of S3
bullets.point: CloudFront becomes the public entry point: it fronts the private bucket, terminates TLS with an ACM cert, caches at the edge, and locks down the origin via Origin Access Control (OAC) so the bucket stays private and is only readable by that distribution. Interview angle: know OAC (current, SigV4) vs the deprecated OAI, and that you configure a default root object of `index.html`.

Request an ACM certificate for your domain — it MUST be in us-east-1 for CloudFront regardless of bucket region because CloudFront is a global service — add the domain as an Alternate Domain Name (CNAME) on the distribution, then create a Route 53 alias A/AAAA record pointing at the distribution. Alias records (vs plain CNAME) let you point the zone apex at CloudFront for free.

Client-side routers own paths like `/dashboard`, but S3 has no such object, so a hard refresh returns 403 (OAC/REST origin) or 404. Fix it with CloudFront custom error responses mapping 403/404 to `/index.html` with a 200 so the router can take over — but scope this carefully so genuine 404s for missing assets still surface, and use a real error page where you don't want SPA fallback.

example.title: Shipping an internal analytics dashboard to production on AWS
example.body: You own the React dashboard for an internal analytics tool. You run `vite build`, producing content-hashed assets plus `index.html`. You create a private S3 bucket `dash-prod-assets`, then `aws s3 sync ./dist s3://dash-prod-assets --delete`, tagging hashed JS/CSS with `Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable` and `index.html` with `no-cache`. You put a CloudFront distribution in front with OAC so the bucket is never public, attach an ACM cert issued in us-east-1, and add `dash.company.com` as a Route 53 alias to the distribution. Everything works until a colleague reports that refreshing on `dash.company.com/reports/42` returns an access-denied error — because that key doesn't exist in S3 and OAC returns 403. You add a CloudFront custom error response mapping 403 -> `/index.html` (200), and the SPA router resolves the deep link. On the next deploy you notice users still see the old UI: the hashed assets updated but browsers cached the old `index.html`, so you fix the `Cache-Control` on `index.html` and issue a one-off `create-invalidation --paths '/index.html'` to flush the edge.

qa.q: Why deploy a React app to S3 + CloudFront instead of running it on EC2 or a container?
qa.a: A built SPA is just static files with no server-side runtime, so there's nothing to execute on a box — S3 + CloudFront gives you global edge delivery, automatic scaling, and near-zero cost without patching servers or managing load balancers. You'd only need a server for SSR/SSG-at-request-time (Next.js server rendering), API endpoints, or logic that can't live in the browser. For a pure client-rendered SPA, a server is wasted operational overhead and a worse latency profile.

qa.q: You have a private S3 bucket. How do you serve it over HTTPS on a custom domain while keeping the bucket private?
qa.a: Put CloudFront in front using Origin Access Control (OAC), which signs origin requests with SigV4 so only that distribution can read the bucket, and keep 'Block Public Access' on. Attach an ACM certificate to the distribution for the domain, add the domain as an alternate name, and point a Route 53 alias record at the distribution. The bucket is never publicly reachable; all traffic flows through CloudFront which terminates TLS at the edge.

qa.q: Why must the ACM certificate for CloudFront be created in us-east-1, and what breaks if it isn't?
qa.a: CloudFront is a global service whose control plane lives in us-east-1, so it only reads ACM certs from that region regardless of where your S3 bucket or users are. If you issue the cert in another region, it simply won't appear in the distribution's certificate dropdown and you can't attach it. Certs for regional services (ALB, API Gateway) follow normal per-region rules — this us-east-1 requirement is specific to CloudFront.

qa.q: A user refreshes the page on /orders/123 and gets an error, but navigating there in-app works. What's happening and how do you fix it?
qa.a: Client-side routes exist only in the JS router; S3 has no object at key `orders/123`, so the origin returns 404 (website endpoint) or 403 (REST endpoint with OAC), which the browser shows on a hard load. The fix is a CloudFront custom error response mapping 403/404 to `/index.html` with a 200 status so the SPA boots and the router resolves the path. Scope it thoughtfully — blanket-mapping all 404s to index.html hides genuinely missing assets and can hurt SEO for public sites.

qa.q: Describe your cache-control and invalidation strategy for deploys. Why not just invalidate everything each time?
qa.a: Give content-hashed assets `max-age=31536000, immutable` since a code change yields a new filename — cache busting is automatic and you never need to invalidate them. Give `index.html` `no-cache` or a short TTL so it revalidates and picks up references to the new hashed files. Blanket `/*` invalidations are slow, cost money beyond the first 1,000/month, and momentarily tank your cache hit ratio; with hashing you at most invalidate just `/index.html`, or skip invalidation entirely by letting its short TTL expire.

qa.q: What's the difference between the S3 website endpoint and the REST endpoint, and which should CloudFront use?
qa.a: The website endpoint (`s3-website-<region>`) supports index/error documents and redirect rules but is HTTP-only, public, and doesn't support OAC. The REST endpoint (`s3.amazonaws.com`) supports HTTPS and private access via OAC/bucket policy but has no website-style routing. Best practice is CloudFront -> REST endpoint with OAC for a private, secure origin, and you replicate the index/error-document behavior with CloudFront's default root object and custom error responses. One gotcha: with the REST origin, missing keys come back as 403, so your SPA fallback should handle 403, not just 404.

qa.q: How does CloudFront edge caching actually decide a cache hit, and what levers control it?
qa.a: CloudFront builds a cache key from the request (path plus whatever headers, query strings, and cookies your cache policy includes) and serves from the edge if a fresh entry exists, otherwise it fetches from the origin and stores it per the TTL. TTL comes from origin `Cache-Control`/`Expires` bounded by the distribution's min/default/max TTL settings. Keep the cache key minimal (don't forward unnecessary query strings/cookies) to maximize hit ratio, and use a cache policy rather than the legacy forwarded-values config.

qa.q: How would you set up automated frontend deployments to this stack in CI?
qa.a: In CI: build the app, `aws s3 sync ./dist s3://bucket --delete` with per-file `Cache-Control` (sync hashed assets first with long TTL, then upload `index.html` last with no-cache to avoid a window where index references not-yet-uploaded assets), then optionally `cloudfront create-invalidation --paths '/index.html'`. Use an OIDC-federated IAM role scoped to that bucket and distribution rather than long-lived keys. For zero-downtime and rollback, keep versioning on the bucket or deploy to a prefix and flip the CloudFront origin path.

bullets.point: Two worlds coexist: C-style null-terminated char arrays (raw pointer, manual sizing, O(n) strlen, easy to overflow) and std::string (owns a heap buffer, tracks size + capacity, RAII cleanup, SSO for small strings). Interview angle: know that std::string uses Small String Optimization to store short strings inline without heap allocation, that .size() is O(1) while strlen is O(n), and when to pass std::string_view to avoid copies.

A pointer holds a memory address; dereferencing (*p) reads/writes the pointee and p->m accesses members. They enable indirection, aliasing, polymorphism (base* to derived), and building linked structures. Interview angle: nullptr vs uninitialized/dangling pointers, pointer-vs-reference (references can't rebind or be null), pointer arithmetic scaling by sizeof(T), and why raw owning pointers are discouraged in favor of smart pointers.

new allocates on the heap and returns a pointer whose lifetime you manage manually; you must delete (or delete[] for arrays) exactly once or you leak/double-free. Interview angle: pair new with delete and new[] with delete[], understand that the heap outlives the current scope (unlike stack/automatic storage), and know that modern C++ replaces raw new/delete with std::make_unique / std::make_shared so RAII guarantees cleanup even on exceptions.

C++ passes by value by default (copies the argument), so use pass-by-reference (T&) to mutate the caller's object, const T& to avoid copying large objects without allowing mutation, and T* when the argument is optional/nullable. Interview angle: const-ref for read-only heavy types, the cost of unnecessary copies, and move semantics (T&&) / passing by value + std::move for sink parameters that take ownership.

A class bundles data (members) with behavior (methods) plus access control (public/private/protected) and lifetime hooks: constructor, destructor, copy/move constructor and assignment. Interview angle: RAII (acquire in ctor, release in dtor), the Rule of 0/3/5, when the compiler synthesizes special members, and the difference between stack objects (deterministic destruction at scope end) and heap objects (destroyed on delete).

example.title: A parser that reads a large log file into custom record objects
example.body: Imagine building a log-ingestion utility that reads a multi-GB access log and turns each line into a LogRecord object. You store parsed fields in std::string members (letting SSO keep short tokens like HTTP verbs off the heap) and pass each raw line into your parser as `const std::string&` to avoid copying millions of lines. For a batch of records too large to size at compile time you originally used `new LogRecord[n]` and had to remember `delete[]` on every early-return path — a classic leak source, so you refactor to `std::vector<LogRecord>` / `std::make_unique`, letting RAII destructors free memory even when parsing throws. The LogRecord class owns its buffers, so you define (or =default/=delete) its copy and move members to control whether records are cheaply moved into the vector or accidentally deep-copied. Pointers appear when you hand out non-owning `LogRecord*` into an index map for O(1) lookup, and you must ensure those pointers don't dangle when the vector reallocates and moves its elements.

qa.q: What is the difference between a C-style string and std::string, and when would you still use a C-string?
qa.a: A C-string is a raw `char*`/`char[]` terminated by '\0' with no stored length, manual memory management, and O(n) length lookup; std::string owns a resizable heap buffer, tracks size and capacity, and cleans itself up via RAII. std::string is the default for safety and convenience. You still reach for C-strings at C API boundaries (POSIX/Win32 calls), for compile-time string literals, or when interfacing with libraries expecting `const char*` — you get one via `.c_str()`.

qa.q: Explain Small String Optimization (SSO).
qa.a: SSO lets std::string store short strings (typically up to ~15 chars on 64-bit libstdc++/libc++) directly inside the string object's own storage instead of allocating on the heap. This avoids a malloc/free for the common case of short strings, improving performance and locality. The trade-off is a larger sizeof(std::string) (usually 24-32 bytes) and a branch on every access to check whether the data is inline or heap-allocated.

qa.q: Pointer vs reference — what are the concrete differences?
qa.a: A reference must be bound at initialization, can never be null, and cannot be rebound to another object — it's an alias. A pointer can be null, can be reseated, supports arithmetic, and requires explicit dereferencing. Use references for mandatory, non-null parameters (especially const& to pass large objects cheaply); use pointers when the value is optional/nullable, when you need to represent 'no object', or when you need to iterate/rebind.

qa.q: What goes wrong with raw new/delete, and how do smart pointers fix it?
qa.a: Raw new/delete require you to free exactly once on every code path; exceptions, early returns, and mismatched new[]/delete cause leaks, double-frees, or undefined behavior. std::unique_ptr and std::shared_ptr apply RAII: the destructor frees automatically when the owner goes out of scope, even during stack unwinding. unique_ptr is zero-overhead single ownership; shared_ptr adds a reference count (atomic, thus not free) for shared ownership. Prefer make_unique/make_shared for exception safety and a single allocation.

qa.q: When should a function take a parameter by value, by const reference, or by rvalue reference?
qa.a: Use const T& for read-only access to objects that are expensive to copy — no copy, no mutation. Use by-value for cheap/trivially-copyable types (int, pointers) or when the function needs its own copy anyway (a 'sink' where you then std::move it into storage). Use T& when you must mutate the caller's object, and T&& (or the by-value-then-move idiom) for perfect forwarding and taking ownership of temporaries efficiently.

qa.q: What is the Rule of Three/Five, and when does it apply?
qa.a: If a class manually manages a resource and needs a custom destructor, it almost certainly also needs a custom copy constructor and copy assignment (Rule of Three) to avoid shallow-copy double-frees; in C++11+ add the move constructor and move assignment (Rule of Five) for efficiency. The Rule of Zero is the modern preference: don't manage resources manually — wrap them in std::string/vector/unique_ptr so all five special members are correctly compiler-generated and you write none of them.

qa.q: Explain RAII and why it's central to C++ resource management.
qa.a: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties a resource's lifetime to an object's lifetime: acquire in the constructor, release in the destructor. Because C++ guarantees destructors run deterministically at scope exit — including during exception stack unwinding — resources like memory, file handles, locks, and sockets are released automatically and exactly once. This is why std::lock_guard, std::unique_ptr, and std::fstream are leak-safe without try/finally, which C++ doesn't even have.

qa.q: A pointer into a std::vector suddenly points to garbage after inserting elements. Why?
qa.a: When a vector exceeds its capacity it allocates a larger buffer, moves/copies its elements there, and frees the old buffer — invalidating all pointers, references, and iterators into the old storage. Any raw LogRecord* you cached now dangles. Fixes: reserve() enough capacity up front so no reallocation occurs, store indices instead of pointers, or use a container with stable addresses (std::deque, std::list, or a node-based map). This is a frequent source of subtle heap-use-after-free bugs caught by AddressSanitizer.

bullets.point: Installing a compiler (g++/clang++/MSVC), invoking it with a standard flag (e.g. -std=c++17), and understanding the four-stage pipeline: preprocess → compile → assemble → link. Interview angle: know why an 'undefined reference' is a linker error (not a compile error) and why the chosen -std flag changes which language features and overload resolutions are available.

main() is the entry point returning int (0 = success), #include pulls in declarations via the preprocessor, and `using namespace std` / `std::` controls name lookup. Interview angle: static/global objects are constructed before main runs and destroyed after it returns, and dynamic initialization order across translation units is unspecified (the 'static init order fiasco').

std::cin/std::cout are buffered stream objects; >> and << are overloaded operators, and >> skips whitespace and does type-checked extraction. Interview angle: `std::endl` flushes the buffer (expensive in loops — prefer '\n'), and `std::ios::sync_with_stdio(false)` + `cin.tie(nullptr)` is the standard trick to make cin/cout competitive with scanf/printf in hot input loops.

Fixed set of primitives (int, long, char, bool, float, double) whose sizes are only minimum-guaranteed, plus signed/unsigned variants and fixed-width aliases (int32_t, size_t). Interview angle: signed integer overflow is undefined behavior while unsigned wraps modulo 2^n; implicit signed↔unsigned conversions and float equality/precision are classic bug sources — prefer fixed-width types when layout or range matters.

if/else, the ternary ?:, and switch for dispatching on an integral/enum value. Interview angle: switch cases fall through unless you break (intentional fallthrough is a bug magnet), a dense switch can compile to a jump table (O(1)) vs an if-else chain's sequential O(n) comparisons, and && / || short-circuit — relied on for guards like `if (p && p->x)`.

for, while, do-while (runs at least once), range-based for over containers, plus break/continue. Interview angle: reason about loop invariants and off-by-one boundaries, know that range-based for uses iterators under the hood so mutating the container during iteration can invalidate them, and prefer range-for when you don't need the index.

Declaration vs definition, parameter passing by value / reference (&) / const-reference / pointer, overloading, default arguments, and recursion with its stack-frame cost. Interview angle: pass large objects by const& to avoid copies, use non-const& or pointer to signal mutation/optional, and lean on const-correctness; understand recursion depth risks (stack overflow) and when to convert to iteration.

Fixed-size, contiguous, zero-indexed blocks with no bounds checking; a C-array decays to a pointer to its first element when passed to a function, losing its size. Interview angle: out-of-bounds access is undefined behavior, sizeof(arr) gives the byte length only in the declaring scope (not after decay), and production code should prefer std::array (stack, size in type) or std::vector (heap, dynamic) which carry their size and support .at() bounds checking.

example.title: A CLI sensor-log summarizer
example.body: You're asked to write a small command-line tool that reads temperature readings from stdin (piped from a sensor log) and prints min, max, and average. You set up the build with `g++ -std=c++17 -O2 summarize.cpp`. In main() you read a count then loop with a range/index loop pulling values via std::cin into a fixed std::array<double, N> (or a std::vector when N is unknown at compile time). You factor the statistics into small functions — `double average(const std::vector<double>&)` taking a const-reference to avoid copying the buffer — and use conditional checks to skip malformed lines and guard against an empty input (which would divide by zero). This one tool exercises every step in the topic: toolchain setup, the main() lifecycle, buffered stream I/O (turning off sync and using '\n' instead of endl for speed), correct numeric data types (double for the mean, size_t for the count to avoid signed/unsigned mismatch), conditionals, loops, functions with const-ref passing, and array/contiguous storage — and the classic bug you'll debug is either an off-by-one on the read loop or integer overflow when summing counts into the wrong type.

qa.q: Walk me through what happens from source file to running executable in C++.
qa.a: Four stages: the preprocessor expands #includes and macros into a single translation unit; the compiler turns that into assembly for the target; the assembler produces an object file (.o/.obj) with possibly unresolved symbols; the linker resolves those symbols across object files and libraries into an executable. This is why a missing function definition surfaces as an 'undefined reference' at link time rather than a compile error, and why each .cpp compiles independently — headers provide declarations so units can be compiled separately and linked together.

qa.q: Why is `std::endl` often a performance problem, and how do you speed up cin/cout?
qa.a: `std::endl` writes '\n' and then flushes the stream buffer to the OS; doing that inside a tight loop forces a syscall per line and kills throughput — use '\n' and let the buffer flush naturally (or on program exit). To speed up standard streams further, call `std::ios::sync_with_stdio(false)` to decouple C++ streams from C stdio, and `std::cin.tie(nullptr)` so cout isn't flushed before every cin read. Together these make iostream competitive with scanf/printf in I/O-heavy problems.

qa.q: What is array decay, and how does it affect sizeof?
qa.a: When a C-style array is passed to a function (or assigned to a pointer), it 'decays' to a pointer to its first element, losing the length information carried by its type. So `sizeof(arr)` returns the full array size in bytes only in the scope where it's declared; inside a function receiving it as a parameter, sizeof yields the pointer size (e.g. 8), not the array size. That's why you must pass the length separately for C-arrays, or use std::array/std::vector/std::span which retain their size.

qa.q: How do you decide between pass-by-value, const reference, non-const reference, and pointer?
qa.a: Pass by value for small, cheap-to-copy types (int, pointers) or when you need an independent copy anyway. Pass by const reference for large read-only objects to avoid the copy while guaranteeing you won't mutate. Use a non-const reference when the function must modify the caller's object and the argument is always required. Use a pointer when the argument is optional (can be nullptr) or when you want to make 'this may be reassigned/null' explicit at the call site. Const-correctness on references also lets the compiler bind temporaries and communicates intent.

qa.q: Explain the difference between signed and unsigned overflow in C++ and why it matters.
qa.a: Signed integer overflow is undefined behavior — the compiler may assume it never happens and optimize accordingly, so it's not safe to rely on wraparound. Unsigned overflow is well-defined and wraps modulo 2^n. The practical trap is mixed signed/unsigned arithmetic and comparisons: expressions like `for (int i = 0; i < vec.size(); ++i)` compare a signed int to an unsigned size_t, and a subtraction like `a - b` on unsigned types where b > a yields a huge positive number instead of a negative. Prefer consistent types and fixed-width integers when range and behavior must be predictable.

qa.q: When is a switch statement better than an if-else chain, and what's the fallthrough gotcha?
qa.a: A switch over a dense set of integral/enum values can be compiled into a jump table giving effectively O(1) dispatch, whereas an if-else chain evaluates conditions sequentially (O(n)). switch is also clearer for exhaustive enum handling and lets the compiler warn on unhandled cases. The gotcha is that cases fall through to the next unless you break (or return), so a missing break silently executes subsequent cases — modern C++ offers [[fallthrough]] to mark deliberate fallthrough and silence warnings.

qa.q: Give concrete examples of undefined behavior a developer can hit with just these basics.
qa.a: Reading or writing past the end of an array (no bounds checking); signed integer overflow; using an uninitialized local variable's value; dividing by zero; returning a reference/pointer to a local that goes out of scope; and reading an object as the wrong type. UB means the program may crash, produce garbage, or appear to work — and optimizers can transform code assuming UB never occurs, so bugs can vanish in debug builds and manifest only under -O2. This is the core reason to prefer bounds-checked containers (.at()) and initialize variables at declaration.

qa.q: Compare C-style arrays, std::array, and std::vector — when would you use each?
qa.a: A C-array is a raw fixed-size block with no size tracking, decays to a pointer, and offers no bounds checking — avoid in modern code except for interop or tiny local buffers. std::array<T,N> is a zero-overhead stack-allocated fixed-size array that keeps its size in the type, supports .size()/.at(), and is copyable/assignable — use it when the size is a compile-time constant. std::vector<T> is a heap-backed dynamic array that can grow/shrink (amortized O(1) push_back) and owns its memory — the default choice when the size is runtime-determined, at the cost of a heap allocation and a level of indirection.

bullets.point: It provides a single machine-readable contract for an HTTP API so docs, server, and clients never drift apart. Without it teams hand-write fetch code and types, docs go stale, and integration bugs surface only at runtime. Interview angle: enables contract-first workflows that decouple frontend/backend teams and let both build in parallel against a stable interface.

A language-agnostic JSON/YAML standard (formerly Swagger) that describes REST endpoints, parameters, request/response schemas, auth, and servers. It is a specification, not a tool: a large ecosystem (docs UIs, validators, code/client generators) consumes it. Current major version is 3.x; know that 3.1 realigned with full JSON Schema Draft 2020-12 while 3.0 used a modified subset.

Top-level keys: openapi (version), info (title/version), servers, paths (each path -> HTTP methods -> operation with parameters/requestBody/responses), and components (reusable schemas, securitySchemes, parameters, responses referenced via $ref). security applies auth globally or per-operation, and tags group operations for docs. Understanding $ref-based reuse and where auth is declared is the common interview probe.

Two approaches: design-first (author YAML/JSON by hand or in an editor, then implement against it) vs code-first (derive the spec from annotated code or typed schemas). Design-first gives a stable contract for parallel work but risks implementation drift; code-first guarantees the spec matches running code but couples the contract to your framework. Mature setups add contract tests so neither approach silently diverges.

Using @hono/zod-openapi you define routes with Zod schemas that do double duty: runtime request/response validation AND automatic OpenAPI generation. This makes one Zod schema the single source of truth for static TS types, runtime checks, and the published spec, eliminating the classic drift between validation and docs. Interview angle: this is code-first done well because the validating schema is the contract.

Serve interactive API docs by pointing a renderer (Swagger UI, Scalar, or Redoc) at your generated /openapi.json. It gives a browsable, always-current reference with a try-it-out console that fires real requests against the running server. In Hono, Scalar (@scalar/hono-api-reference) or Swagger UI is mounted as a route that reads the same spec the framework emits.

Tools like openapi-typescript, orval, or openapi-generator turn the spec into typed client SDKs, replacing hand-written fetch calls and keeping request/response types in sync with the backend. Regeneration on spec change surfaces breaking changes at compile time in the consumer. Caveat: generated clients are only as correct as the spec, so a wrong or under-specified spec produces confidently-typed but wrong code.

example.title: Shipping a typed payments API for a web + mobile frontend
example.body: You are building a payments service in Hono and two teams (a React web app and a React Native app) need to call it. You define each route with @hono/zod-openapi: a Zod schema like CreatePaymentBody validates the incoming request at runtime, doubles as the TS type in your handler, and auto-generates the OpenAPI 3.1 document served at /openapi.json. You mount Scalar at /docs so QA and the frontend devs get a live, always-current reference with a try-it-out console. The frontend teams run openapi-typescript (or orval) against your spec in CI to generate a fully typed client, so when you add a required idempotencyKey field, their build fails immediately instead of erroring in production. Later, when debugging a 422, you can see the exact Zod-derived schema in the spec, confirm the client sent a malformed amount, and know the validation, docs, and generated client all came from one source of truth.

qa.q: What changed between OpenAPI 3.0 and 3.1, and why does it matter?
qa.a: 3.1 is a full superset of JSON Schema Draft 2020-12, while 3.0 used a modified, incompatible subset. Practical consequences: nullability moves from nullable: true to type: ["string", "null"], you gain features like proper type arrays and top-level webhooks, and you can reuse standard JSON Schema tooling directly. It matters because tool compatibility differs by version, so generators and validators must target the version you emit.

qa.q: Design-first vs code-first: when would you choose each?
qa.a: Design-first (author the spec, then implement) is best when multiple teams need a stable contract to build against in parallel or when the API is a public product; the risk is the server drifting from the spec. Code-first (generate the spec from typed schemas like Zod) guarantees the spec reflects running code and reduces duplication, but couples the contract to your framework and can leak implementation detail. Strong teams pick one and add contract testing (e.g., schemathesis or a validation middleware) so drift is caught mechanically rather than by review.

qa.q: In the Hono + Zod setup, how does one schema become a single source of truth?
qa.a: The Zod schema is compiled to a runtime validator (rejecting bad requests with structured errors), inferred into a static TypeScript type used inside the handler, and converted to an OpenAPI schema object embedded in the generated document. Because all three derive from the same definition, you cannot validate one shape while documenting another. This closes the classic gap where hand-written validation, TS types, and docs each say something slightly different.

qa.q: Why is $ref / components important, and what breaks without it?
qa.a: components lets you define a schema, parameter, response, or securityScheme once and reference it with $ref everywhere it is used. Without it you duplicate the same object across dozens of operations, so a field change must be edited in many places and generated clients emit many near-identical types instead of one shared model. It also keeps the document readable and makes diffs meaningful when the contract evolves.

qa.q: How do required, optional, and nullable map from schema to spec, and why do people confuse them?
qa.a: Required means the key must be present (controlled by the object's required array, not the field itself); optional means the key may be absent; nullable means the value may be null when present. They are orthogonal: a field can be required-and-nullable (must be sent, may be null) or optional-and-non-nullable. Confusion arises because 3.0 expressed null via nullable: true while 3.1 uses type: ["...", "null"], and because Zod's .optional() vs .nullable() vs .nullish() each map differently.

qa.q: What are the risks of auto-generated clients and how do you mitigate them?
qa.a: A generated client is only as correct as the spec, so an under-specified or wrong schema yields confidently-typed but incorrect code, and different generators produce different ergonomics and error handling. Mitigations: treat the spec as reviewed source, pin the generator version and commit generated output (or regenerate in CI) so diffs are visible, and add a smoke/contract test that hits the real API through the generated client. Also validate the spec itself in CI to catch drift before clients are built from it.

qa.q: How would you handle a breaking API change with an OpenAPI-driven workflow?
qa.a: First distinguish breaking (removing/renaming a field, making an optional field required, tightening types) from additive changes. Use a spec-diff tool (e.g. oasdiff) in CI to classify the change and fail the pipeline on unintended breaks. For real breaks, version the API (path or header based), keep the old operations in the spec during a deprecation window using the deprecated flag, and let consumers regenerate clients against the new version on their own schedule.

qa.q: How do you ensure the running implementation actually matches the published spec?
qa.a: In code-first setups (Zod-generated), the spec is emitted from the same schemas that validate requests, so drift is structurally limited but response bodies can still lie if handlers return unvalidated data. Add response validation in non-prod, and run contract/property-based tests such as schemathesis or Dredd that exercise every operation against the spec. In design-first setups this testing is mandatory because the server is written independently of the contract.

bullets.point: Capping how many requests a client can make in a window protects you from brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, scraping, accidental client retry storms, and runaway third-party costs (SMS/email/LLM calls). The interview angle: rate limiting is about fairness and abuse prevention at the application layer, which is distinct from DDoS mitigation (volumetric, network layer) and from simple throttling for capacity.

Limits can live at the CDN/edge (Cloudflare), the load balancer / API gateway (nginx, Kong), or in application middleware. Earlier layers are cheaper because they reject traffic before it consumes your compute, but only the app layer has enough context for per-user or per-endpoint keys; production systems usually layer both.

A typical forgot-password / send-OTP endpoint accepts an email or phone and triggers an out-of-band message (token link or numeric OTP). It matters because it is unauthenticated, side-effecting (sends real messages, costs money), and a common target, so it is the canonical example for why input endpoints need protection.

With no limit, an attacker scripts thousands of requests: OTP/email bombing a victim, brute-forcing a short numeric OTP, enumerating which emails exist, and amplifying your SMS/email bill. This shows that an endpoint being functionally correct is not the same as being safe under adversarial load.

Demonstrating the attack against a live but consenting/owned system (e.g. a simple loop of curl/fetch calls) makes the blast radius concrete: cost spikes, degraded service, and a real DoS on the messaging provider. The takeaway is that these are not theoretical bugs and must be caught in design review, not after the bill arrives.

Fix it by keying limits on IP and on the target email/phone, using an algorithm like token bucket or sliding window (in-memory map for a demo, Redis in reality). Per-target keys matter because IP-only limits let an attacker bomb a single victim from many IPs while staying under any per-IP cap.

An in-process counter breaks the moment you run more than one instance behind a load balancer, since each replica sees only its own slice of traffic, so the effective limit is N times too loose. The fix is a shared store (Redis with atomic INCR/Lua or a sliding-window structure); also note IP keys are unreliable behind NAT/CGNAT and trivially rotated.

A CAPTCHA forces a challenge (image, invisible risk score, or proof-of-work) to distinguish humans from bots, complementing rate limits when the abuse comes from distributed or low-volume-per-IP bots. It matters because rate limiting alone can't stop a botnet spreading load thinly across many IPs; the trade-off is added user friction, so it's usually gated to risky flows.

Turnstile renders a widget that returns a token to the client, which your backend must verify server-side by POSTing the token + secret to the siteverify endpoint before honoring the request. The critical detail is that the client-side token is meaningless without server-side validation, and tokens are single-use and short-lived.

Volumetric attacks are absorbed at the edge via an anycast CDN/scrubbing network (Cloudflare, AWS Shield) that handles L3/L4 floods, plus a WAF and edge rate rules for L7 (HTTP) floods. Interview angle: know the L3/L4 (SYN floods, UDP amplification) vs L7 (HTTP request floods that look legitimate) distinction, because they need different mitigations and origin IPs must be hidden behind the edge.

example.title: The password-reset endpoint that ran up a $40k Twilio bill
example.body: You ship an unauthenticated POST /auth/forgot-password that takes an email or phone and sends a 6-digit OTP via Twilio. It works in the demo. A week later an attacker (or a buggy client) discovers it and loops it: they bomb one user with hundreds of SMS, brute-force the short OTP (10^6 space, guessable if you allow unlimited attempts), and drive the SMS bill through the roof while degrading delivery for real users. As the SDE-2 on call, you (1) add a Redis-backed sliding-window limiter keyed on BOTH source IP and the target email/phone (e.g. 3 requests / 15 min per target, plus a coarser per-IP cap), because your service runs 4 replicas so an in-memory counter would have been 4x too loose; (2) cap OTP verification attempts and lengthen/expire the code; (3) put Cloudflare Turnstile on the form and verify the token server-side via siteverify before sending anything; and (4) move the origin behind Cloudflare so edge WAF + rate rules absorb L7 floods and the origin IP is hidden from direct volumetric attacks. The layers matter: rate limits stop the cheap script, captcha stops the distributed bot that rotates IPs, and the edge stops the volumetric flood.

qa.q: Compare fixed-window, sliding-window, token-bucket, and leaky-bucket rate limiting. When would you pick each?
qa.a: Fixed window (count per calendar minute) is simplest but allows a 2x burst at the window boundary. Sliding window (log or weighted counter) smooths that boundary at higher memory/CPU cost. Token bucket lets you allow controlled bursts (tokens accumulate up to a cap, refilled at a steady rate) and is the common choice for APIs that want burst tolerance. Leaky bucket enforces a strictly smooth output rate (good for shaping traffic to a downstream), but drops or queues bursts. For most web APIs, token bucket or sliding window in Redis is the practical pick.

qa.q: How do you implement rate limiting correctly across a horizontally scaled service?
qa.a: You cannot use in-process counters because each replica sees only its own traffic, making the effective limit multiply by the number of instances. Use a shared store like Redis with an atomic operation: INCR + EXPIRE for fixed window, a sorted set (ZADD/ZREMRANGEBYSCORE) for sliding window, or a Lua script for token bucket to keep the read-modify-write atomic and avoid race conditions. The trade-off is a network hop per request and Redis becoming a hot dependency, so you make failures fail-open or fail-closed deliberately and consider local pre-checks to shed obvious abuse.

qa.q: What should you key your rate limits on, and why is IP alone a bad choice?
qa.a: For an abuse-sensitive endpoint you want multiple keys: per-IP, per-account/target (email, phone, userId), and sometimes per-API-key. IP alone is weak because attackers rotate through cloud IPs or botnets to stay under a per-IP cap, and conversely CGNAT/corporate NAT puts thousands of legitimate users behind one IP, so a tight per-IP limit causes false positives. Keying on the target resource (e.g. the email being reset) stops victim-focused bombing that an IP limit would miss.

qa.q: Rate limiting and CAPTCHA both fight abuse. Why do you often need both?
qa.a: Rate limiting caps request volume per key, but a distributed botnet can spread load across thousands of IPs so each stays under the limit while the aggregate is still an attack. A CAPTCHA raises the per-request cost for automation regardless of source distribution, catching bots that rate limits let through. The trade-off is user friction, so the usual pattern is: allow normal traffic freely, apply rate limits as a baseline, and trigger CAPTCHA only on risk signals or sensitive flows.

qa.q: How does server-side CAPTCHA verification (e.g. Cloudflare Turnstile / reCAPTCHA) actually work, and what's the common mistake?
qa.a: The widget runs in the browser and returns an opaque token to your frontend, which sends it with the form submission. Your backend must POST that token plus your secret key to the provider's siteverify endpoint and only proceed if it returns success. The classic mistake is trusting the presence of a token client-side without server verification, which is trivially bypassed; also remember tokens are single-use and expire quickly, so verify once and don't cache them.

qa.q: Distinguish L3/L4 from L7 DDoS attacks and their mitigations.
qa.a: L3/L4 attacks are volumetric/protocol floods (SYN floods, UDP/DNS/NTP amplification) that try to saturate bandwidth or connection tables; they're mitigated by anycast scrubbing networks and providers like Cloudflare or AWS Shield that absorb and filter traffic before it reaches you. L7 attacks are HTTP request floods that look like legitimate requests (e.g. hammering an expensive search endpoint), so they need application-aware defenses: WAF rules, edge rate limiting, JS/CAPTCHA challenges, and caching. A key operational point is hiding your origin IP behind the edge so attackers can't bypass mitigation by hitting the origin directly.

qa.q: What's the difference between failing open and failing closed in a rate limiter, and how do you choose?
qa.a: Fail-open means if the limiter's backing store (Redis) is unavailable, you allow the request through; fail-closed means you reject it. Fail-open preserves availability but removes your protection exactly when things are already stressed, which is dangerous for abuse-critical endpoints like login or OTP. Fail-closed protects the endpoint but can turn a Redis blip into a full outage. The usual answer is to fail-open on best-effort throttling of normal endpoints and fail-closed (or degrade to a stricter local limit) on security-critical ones, and to make Redis itself highly available.

qa.q: How would you prevent OTP brute-force even with rate limiting in place?
qa.a: Rate limiting the send endpoint doesn't protect the verify endpoint, so you separately cap verification attempts per code/account and lock or invalidate the OTP after a small number of failures. Use a sufficiently large code space and short TTL so guessing is impractical within the validity window, and never reveal whether the email exists (return a generic response) to avoid enumeration. Adding exponential backoff and a CAPTCHA after a few failures further raises the cost of automated guessing.

bullets.point: Hooks are functions that let function components subscribe to React features (useState, useEffect, useContext, useRef, useReducer) without classes. The Rules of Hooks — call only at the top level and only from React functions — exist because React has no names for hooks; it tracks them positionally as an ordered linked list on the component's fiber, so a conditional or looped call would shift indices and corrupt state. Interview angle: be able to explain the by-call-order mechanism, not just recite the rule.

A custom hook is just a function whose name starts with `use` and which composes other hooks to extract reusable stateful logic. The key nuance: it shares logic, not state — every component that calls it gets its own isolated state/effects instance. Interview angle: contrast with HOCs/render props (no wrapper hell, better typing, composable) and stress the `use` prefix is what lets the linter enforce the Rules of Hooks.

A useFetch/useQuery-style hook encapsulates loading/error/data state plus a dependency-driven request in useEffect, with cancellation via AbortController and cleanup on unmount or dep change. It matters because it centralizes race-condition handling (ignoring stale in-flight responses), retries, and caching in one place. Interview angle: explain the fast-typing race where an earlier request resolves last, and why this is exactly the problem React Query/SWR solve with request dedup + cache.

Hooks like useLocalStorage, useMediaQuery, useOnlineStatus, useEventListener, and useWindowSize wrap imperative browser APIs and subscriptions, owning the add/removeEventListener lifecycle so callers don't leak listeners. Interview angle: prefer useSyncExternalStore for subscribing to external mutable sources (avoids tearing in concurrent React), and handle SSR — guard `window`/`localStorage` access and avoid hydration mismatches by not reading client-only values during the first render.

useDebounce, useThrottle, useInterval, and useTimeout manage timers with correct clearTimeout/clearInterval cleanup and stable references. The classic trap is the stale closure: an interval captures the callback from the render it was created in; Dan Abramov's pattern stores the latest callback in a ref and reads ref.current inside the tick so the interval isn't torn down and recreated every render. Interview angle: know when to debounce vs throttle and why useRef beats putting the callback in the effect deps.

example.title: A responsive product-search dashboard
example.body: You're building the search view of an admin dashboard. A useDebounce hook holds back the query ~300ms so you don't fire a request per keystroke. The debounced value feeds a useFetch hook that issues the API call inside useEffect keyed on the query, aborting the previous request with AbortController and setting an `ignore` flag in cleanup so a slow earlier response can't overwrite the latest results (the race you'll actually get a bug report for). A useLocalStorage hook persists recent searches across reloads, and a useMediaQuery hook swaps the results grid to a single column on mobile. Each hook is independently testable and reusable, the components stay declarative, and all timer/listener/request cleanup lives inside the hooks rather than being duplicated across pages — which is precisely the maintainability win an SDE-2 is expected to deliver.

qa.q: Why must hooks be called at the top level, never inside conditions or loops?
qa.a: React doesn't identify hooks by name; it stores them as an ordered list on the component's fiber and matches each call to its slot by call order across renders. If you call a hook conditionally, the order changes between renders and the indices misalign, so state from one hook gets read by another. The top-level rule guarantees a stable call sequence every render, which is what makes positional tracking safe.

qa.q: What's the difference between a custom hook and a plain utility function?
qa.a: A custom hook is a function that itself calls React hooks, so it's bound by the Rules of Hooks and gets linted for them via the `use` prefix; a utility function is pure JS and can be called anywhere. Because it can use useState/useEffect/useRef, a custom hook carries component-scoped state and lifecycle behavior, whereas a util just transforms inputs to outputs. The `use` naming is not cosmetic — it's the signal the linter and React tooling rely on.

qa.q: If two components call the same custom hook, do they share state?
qa.a: No. A custom hook shares logic, not state — each call site instantiates its own independent state and effects, just as if you'd inlined the hook body into that component. If you actually need shared state across components, you need a shared store: Context, a state library, or useSyncExternalStore over an external source. This 'logic not state' distinction is a very common interview probe.

qa.q: How do you prevent race conditions in a data-fetching hook?
qa.a: Inside the fetching useEffect, either abort the prior request with an AbortController or keep a local `let ignore = false` and set `ignore = true` in the cleanup function, then only commit results when not ignored/aborted. This handles the case where the query changes rapidly and an older request resolves after a newer one — without it, stale data overwrites fresh data. In production you'd usually delegate this to React Query/SWR, which add dedup, caching, and revalidation on top.

qa.q: Explain the stale-closure problem in a setInterval hook and how to fix it.
qa.a: If you pass a callback to setInterval inside an effect that runs once, the interval permanently closes over the state/props from that first render, so it always sees stale values. Adding the callback to the effect deps 'fixes' correctness but tears down and recreates the timer on every render, breaking the cadence. The idiomatic fix (Dan Abramov's useInterval) stores the latest callback in a ref, updates ref.current in a separate effect each render, and calls ref.current() inside a single long-lived interval — fresh values without resetting the timer.

qa.q: When would you debounce versus throttle, and how do you implement debounce as a hook?
qa.a: Debounce waits until activity stops (fire once after N ms of silence) — ideal for search-as-you-type; throttle guarantees at most one call per interval — ideal for scroll/resize/mousemove where you want steady sampling. A useDebounce hook typically keeps the debounced value in state and runs a useEffect that setTimeout-s an update keyed on the raw value, clearing the timer in cleanup so each new keystroke cancels the pending update. The clearTimeout in the effect's cleanup is what makes it actually debounce rather than schedule N updates.

qa.q: What are the SSR and concurrency pitfalls in browser-API hooks?
qa.a: On the server there's no `window`, `document`, `matchMedia`, or `localStorage`, so unguarded access throws; and if the first client render produces a different value than the server HTML, you get a hydration mismatch warning and visual flicker. Use lazy state initializers guarded by `typeof window`, defer client-only reads to an effect, and for subscribing to external mutable sources use useSyncExternalStore, which is tear-safe under concurrent rendering and provides a getServerSnapshot for SSR. Rolling your own subscribe+useState can tear (show inconsistent values) during concurrent renders.

qa.q: Why did the community move from HOCs and render props to custom hooks?
qa.a: HOCs and render props both share logic by nesting components, which produces deeply nested 'wrapper hell', clashing prop names, and awkward typing. Custom hooks share logic through plain function composition — no extra tree nodes, explicit inputs/outputs, and clean TypeScript inference. The trade-off is that hooks can't be used conditionally and only work in function components, whereas HOCs can still be useful for cross-cutting concerns that wrap rendering itself (e.g., error boundaries, which must remain class-based).

bullets.point: Sets up the two axes of scaling a stateless API tier plus its stateful database, and why read/write latency and throughput eventually force a scaling decision. Interview angle: know that app servers are usually easy to scale (stateless), while the database is the real bottleneck, which is why indexing and data modeling dominate this topic.

Scaling up: give one machine more CPU, RAM, IOPS, or faster disk. It's the simplest lever (no code change, no distributed-systems complexity) but hits hard ceilings, gets exponentially expensive, and leaves a single point of failure. Interview angle: it buys time and is often the right first move, but it cannot give you HA on its own.

bullets.step: Horizontal scaling in a Node.js app
bullets.point: Run multiple identical stateless instances behind a load balancer (or use the Node `cluster`/PM2 to fork workers per core, since a single Node process is single-threaded for JS). The key constraint: instances must share no in-process state — sessions, rate-limit counters, and caches move to Redis/DB so any instance can serve any request. Interview angle: sticky sessions vs. stateless JWT, and why `cluster` only scales one box while a load balancer scales many.

Back-of-envelope sizing: estimate DAU, RPS (peak ≈ several× average), payload sizes, storage growth/day, and memory per connection to decide instance count and DB size. Interview angle: system-design rounds expect you to derive RPS from users and reason about read:write ratio, connection limits, and headroom rather than guess.

Scaling out across many commodity machines gives near-linear capacity and fault tolerance, but introduces load balancing, data partitioning/sharding, replication, and consistency concerns. Interview angle: stateless tiers scale trivially; the hard part is the data layer — read replicas for read-heavy loads, sharding for write-heavy/large datasets, and the CAP trade-offs that follow.

An index (default B-tree) is a separate sorted structure that turns an O(n) sequential scan into an O(log n) lookup for equality and range queries on the indexed column(s). The trade-off: every index adds write/update cost and storage, so you index columns used in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY, not everything. Interview angle: use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm the planner actually uses the index, and know B-tree vs. Hash vs. GIN/GiST use cases.

Multi-column indexes serve queries that filter on a prefix of the columns; column order matters (leftmost-prefix rule), so `(a,b)` helps `WHERE a` and `WHERE a AND b` but not `WHERE b` alone. Covering indexes (INCLUDE) and partial indexes (`WHERE` clause) further optimize hot queries. Interview angle: be able to explain why `(user_id, created_at)` beats two single-column indexes for a paginated per-user feed.

Declare indexes in schema.prisma with `@@index([...])`, `@unique`/`@@unique`, and `@id`; Prisma emits the DDL via migrations so indexes are version-controlled with the schema. Interview angle: ORMs don't auto-create indexes on foreign keys or frequent filters — you must add them explicitly, and unique constraints double as indexes.

Organizing data into related tables to eliminate redundancy and update/insert/delete anomalies (1NF→2NF→3NF removes repeating groups, partial, and transitive dependencies). Trade-off: normalized schemas need JOINs (read cost) while denormalization trades storage/consistency for read speed. Interview angle: normalize for write-heavy/OLTP integrity, denormalize selectively for read-heavy paths and analytics.

One-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many, modeled with foreign keys — the many-to-many case requires a join/junction table holding both FKs. Interview angle: know where the FK lives (on the 'many' side), and that a junction table can carry its own attributes (e.g., role/joined_at on a user↔team link).

Practical walkthrough: take a flat/denormalized table, split repeating and dependent attributes into their own tables, and wire them with FKs and the right relationship type. Interview angle: recognize a normalization smell (e.g., comma-separated values or duplicated customer data per order row) and refactor it into properly keyed tables.

example.title: Scaling a course platform's video-progress and comments service
example.body: You run a Node.js/Postgres API for an online course platform. It starts on one box; as signups climb you first vertically scale the DB (more RAM so the working set and indexes stay in memory). When the API tier saturates CPU, you go horizontal: run the API as several stateless instances behind a load balancer, move sessions/rate-limits to Redis so any instance can serve any request, and add a read replica for the read-heavy 'watch progress' and 'course comments' endpoints. A capacity estimate (say 500k DAU, ~5k peak RPS, 90:10 read:write) drives instance count and connection-pool sizing. The comments feed query WHERE course_id = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20 is slow on a sequential scan, so you add a composite index (course_id, created_at) — declared in Prisma as @@index([courseId, createdAt]) — and confirm with EXPLAIN ANALYZE that the planner uses it. Finally you normalize the schema: instead of storing course title and instructor name on every progress row, you split users, courses, and enrollments into their own tables with FKs, modeling user-to-course as many-to-many via an enrollments junction table that also carries progress_pct and enrolled_at.

qa.q: When would you choose vertical scaling over horizontal scaling, and vice versa?
qa.a: Vertical scaling is the right first move when the bottleneck fits on one bigger machine and you want zero code/architecture change — it's fast and avoids distributed-systems complexity, which is why it's common for the primary database early on. You switch to horizontal scaling when you hit the ceiling of a single machine, need high availability (removing the single point of failure), or need near-linear capacity growth. In practice you scale the stateless app tier horizontally early and defer horizontal DB scaling (replicas/sharding) as long as possible because it's the hardest part.

qa.q: What does it take to make a Node.js service horizontally scalable?
qa.a: The instances must be stateless — no session data, counters, or caches held in process memory — so any request can hit any instance. You externalize that state to Redis or the database, use stateless auth like signed JWTs (or a shared session store), and put a load balancer in front. Within a single box you can also use the Node `cluster` module or PM2 to fork one worker per core (since JS execution is single-threaded), but that only scales one machine; a load balancer across boxes is what gives you true horizontal scale and fault tolerance.

qa.q: How does a B-tree index speed up queries, and what does it cost?
qa.a: A B-tree keeps the indexed column(s) in sorted order, so equality and range lookups become O(log n) tree traversals instead of an O(n) sequential scan, and it can also satisfy ORDER BY without a separate sort. The cost is on writes: every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE that touches an indexed column must also update the index, plus extra storage. So you index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY on hot paths, and avoid indexing everything — especially low-selectivity columns where a scan is cheaper than an index traversal.

qa.q: Explain composite index column ordering with a concrete example.
qa.a: A composite index follows the leftmost-prefix rule: `(course_id, created_at)` can serve `WHERE course_id = ?`, `WHERE course_id = ? AND created_at > ?`, and `WHERE course_id = ? ORDER BY created_at`, but it cannot efficiently serve `WHERE created_at = ?` alone because created_at isn't a leading column. So you put the equality/most-selective filter first and the range/sort column second. For a per-course comment feed this single composite index beats two separate single-column indexes because Postgres can walk one structure to both filter and return rows in sorted order.

qa.q: Prisma manages the schema — do you still need to think about indexes?
qa.a: Yes. Prisma only creates indexes you declare: `@id`, `@unique`/`@@unique`, and `@@index([...])`. It does not automatically index foreign-key columns or your frequent filter/sort columns, so those queries will do sequential scans until you add explicit `@@index` entries. The upside is that once declared they live in schema.prisma and ship via migrations, so indexes are code-reviewed and version-controlled rather than applied ad hoc in production.

qa.q: What problems does normalization solve, and when would you denormalize?
qa.a: Normalization (up to 3NF) removes redundancy and the resulting update/insert/delete anomalies — e.g., storing a customer's address on every order means an address change must update many rows and risks inconsistency. Splitting into keyed tables linked by FKs makes each fact live in exactly one place. You denormalize deliberately on read-heavy paths where JOIN cost hurts (e.g., a precomputed feed, analytics/OLAP tables, or a cached aggregate), accepting extra storage and the burden of keeping copies consistent in exchange for faster reads.

qa.q: How do you model a many-to-many relationship, and why can't a plain FK do it?
qa.a: A foreign key on one row can only point to a single parent, so it natively expresses one-to-many (the FK lives on the 'many' side). For many-to-many — e.g., students enrolled in many courses and courses having many students — you introduce a junction table holding both foreign keys (student_id, course_id), typically with a composite unique/primary key on the pair. That table can also carry relationship attributes like enrolled_at or progress_pct, which is exactly why you can't collapse it into either parent.

qa.q: How would you approach capacity estimation for a new read-heavy API?
qa.a: Start from users: derive average RPS from DAU and requests-per-user-per-day, then assume peak is several times average (traffic isn't uniform). Combine that with the read:write ratio, average payload size, and per-request DB work to size instance count, DB connections (mind Postgres's connection limit — use a pooler like PgBouncer), and read replicas for reads. Add storage growth per day to plan disk, and always leave headroom (target well under 100% utilization) so you can absorb spikes and node failures without cascading.

bullets.step: Topic framing: where backend state lives
bullets.point: Sets up the core question — should application state live inside the server process or be externalized, and how do the Singleton pattern plus in-process pub/sub let you share and broadcast that state cleanly within one Node process. The interview thread is knowing when in-memory state is fine and exactly where it breaks under horizontal scale.

Stateless servers keep no per-client memory between requests (context is carried in the request or fetched from a shared store), so any instance serves any request — trivial load balancing, safe restarts, easy autoscaling. Stateful servers hold session/connection/game state in memory for lower latency and real-time use, but need sticky sessions and complicate failover. Interview angle: articulate the trade-off and know that the standard fix is to keep only the connection stateful and externalize shared/durable state to Redis or a DB.

Node runs a single-threaded event loop over one shared process heap, so any module-scoped variable persists across requests for the process lifetime — fast, but lost on crash/restart, unbounded (memory-leak risk), and NOT shared across workers. Interview angle: under PM2 cluster or k8s replicas each process has its own heap, so process memory silently diverges — the classic 'works in dev, breaks in prod' bug.

Singleton guarantees one instance with a single access point; in Node you get it almost free by exporting one instance (the require/import cache dedupes per process), or explicitly via a static getInstance() with lazy init. Used for shared resources: DB pools, config, in-memory stores, the pub/sub hub. Interview angle: it's a controlled global — downsides are hidden coupling, hard testing/mocking, and it's only 'single' per process, so DI is often cleaner.

Pub/Sub decouples publishers from subscribers through named topics/channels so components communicate without direct references (fan-out, no tight coupling). The event manager is made a Singleton so every part of the app publishes and subscribes against the same registry. Interview angle: an in-memory manager only reaches subscribers in the same process — cross-instance fan-out requires an external broker like Redis pub/sub, Kafka, or NATS.

Concretely: a class holding a Map of topic -> Set of callbacks, with subscribe/publish/unsubscribe, exported as one instance; often it also bridges an external Redis subscription to in-process WebSocket connections. Interview angle: the load-bearing details are unsubscribe-on-disconnect to avoid leaking dead sockets/closures, try/catch around each callback so one bad subscriber doesn't break the fan-out, and choosing sync vs async dispatch.

example.title: Real-time stock price WebSocket fan-out service
example.body: You build a WebSocket service where clients subscribe to stock symbols (AAPL, TSLA...). A SubscriptionManager singleton holds a Map of symbol -> Set of client sockets, and on startup opens one Redis connection subscribed to the price channels. When a Redis message arrives, the singleton looks up subscribers for that symbol and pushes to each socket; on client disconnect the 'close' handler unsubscribes to avoid leaking dead sockets. The singleton is the right call because the connection handler, the Redis message handler, and the disconnect handler all need one authoritative registry and one Redis connection without threading references everywhere. The key SDE-2 insight surfaces when you scale out: each WS instance keeps its own in-memory socket registry (stateful, process-local), but the shared truth lives in Redis — Redis pub/sub fans every price tick out to all instances, and each instance delivers only to its locally connected clients. If you had relied on an in-process EventEmitter instead of Redis, a publish on instance A would never reach a client connected to instance B — the exact bug that passes in dev with one process and fails in production behind a load balancer.

qa.q: Why are stateless backends preferred for horizontal scaling, and when is statefulness unavoidable?
qa.a: Stateless servers hold no per-client memory between requests, so any instance can serve any request — that gives trivial load balancing, safe restarts, and easy autoscaling. Statefulness becomes unavoidable for long-lived WebSocket connections, real-time games, or matchmaking where round-tripping to a store on every event is too slow. The standard pattern is to keep only the connection itself stateful and externalize any shared or durable state to Redis/DB so the servers stay effectively stateless for routing.

qa.q: How does Node's module system give you a 'free' singleton, and what are the limits?
qa.a: require/import caches a module after first evaluation, so every importer receives the same exported object — export an instance and you have a process-wide singleton with no extra code. The limits: it's per-process only (a cluster/PM2/k8s deployment gets one per worker), the cache can be defeated by different resolved module paths or by clearing require.cache, and it makes the dependency implicit and global, which hurts testability and hides coupling.

qa.q: Compare a module-level singleton with a getInstance() singleton.
qa.a: A module-level export creates the instance eagerly at import time — simplest and enough for most cases. getInstance() with a static field enables lazy initialization (created on first use), can take init parameters, and gives an explicit access point, at the cost of boilerplate. Both are single-per-process; pick the lazy form when construction is expensive or must wait until config/env is ready.

qa.q: What are the real downsides of the Singleton pattern?
qa.a: It is effectively a controlled global, so it introduces hidden shared state and coupling that make code paths depend on each other invisibly. It complicates unit testing because you must reset or mock the shared instance between tests, and it can cause initialization-order and circular-dependency problems. It also gives a false sense of 'exactly one' since it is only one per process — dependency injection is often a cleaner, more testable alternative.

qa.q: Why is in-memory pub/sub insufficient at scale, and how do you fix it?
qa.a: An in-process event manager or EventEmitter only reaches subscribers living in the same process, so with multiple instances behind a load balancer a publish on instance A never reaches subscribers connected to instance B. You fix it with an external broker — Redis pub/sub, Kafka, or NATS — where every instance subscribes to the channel and then re-fans-out to its own local in-memory subscribers.
