The in-memory singleton becomes the last-hop delivery mechanism, while the broker handles cross-instance distribution.

qa.q: How do you implement a basic pub/sub manager without leaking memory?
qa.a: Keep a Map of topic -> Set of callbacks: subscribe adds a callback, publish iterates the set and invokes each, unsubscribe removes it. The critical hygiene is removing subscriptions when a client disconnects (for WebSockets, on the 'close' event), deleting topic entries once their set is empty, and wrapping each callback in try/catch so one throwing subscriber doesn't abort the whole fan-out. Skipping unsubscribe-on-disconnect leaks closures and references to dead sockets, which grows the heap until the process falls over.

qa.q: In a WebSocket fan-out service, why keep the socket registry in a singleton rather than passing it around?
qa.a: The registry is shared mutable state that several independent code paths — the connection handler, the broker/Redis message handler, and the disconnect handler — all must reach. A singleton provides one authoritative registry and a single shared broker connection without threading references through every function or middleware. The trade-off is the usual singleton coupling, but for a single logical resource per process it's a pragmatic and common choice.

qa.q: How does process-level state behave under PM2 cluster mode or Kubernetes with multiple replicas?
qa.a: Each worker or replica is a separate OS process with its own heap, so module-level variables and singletons are duplicated and not shared — a counter incremented on one instance is invisible to the others. Sticky sessions can pin a given client to one instance, but any truth that must be consistent across clients has to live in an external store like Redis or a database. This is precisely why relying on in-process state passes in single-process dev and breaks once you scale to many instances in production.

bullets.point: A function that solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller input, defined by a base case (stops the recursion) and a recursive case (reduces toward the base case). Each call pushes a frame onto the call stack, so understanding the stack is essential — it's where stack-overflow bugs, space cost (O(depth)), and execution order all come from. Interview angle: interviewers check that you always state the base case first and prove the input strictly shrinks.

An early hands-on problem that forces you to express a concrete computation (e.g. a value derived from a number/series) purely in terms of a smaller subproblem plus a base case. The point is mechanical fluency: mapping an iterative loop into f(n) = combine(f(n-1)), and tracing the unwinding of the stack. This builds the habit of trusting the recursive call rather than mentally simulating every frame.

Split the input into independent subproblems, solve each recursively, then combine the results — merge sort, quicksort, binary search, and closest-pair are the canonical examples. Runtime follows a recurrence you analyze with the Master Theorem (e.g. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n)). Interview angle: be ready to derive the recurrence and to explain why the 'combine' step's cost dominates or doesn't.

The mental framework: define the problem in terms of a strictly smaller version of itself, assume the recursive call already returns the correct answer for that smaller input (the 'recursive leap of faith'), then only write the combine step and the base case. Correctness reduces to three checks: base case is right, each call makes progress toward it, and the combine logic is right assuming the subcall is. This is what lets you write and reason about recursion without tracing the full call tree.

A refinement of recursion for exploring a decision tree: at each step make a choice, recurse, then undo the choice (restore state) before trying the next option — used for permutations, subsets, N-Queens, Sudoku, and maze/path problems. The key lever is pruning: abandon a branch the moment it can't lead to a valid solution, which is what separates it from naive brute force. Interview angle: articulate the choose/explore/unchoose template and how constraints prune the search space.

Count the ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2), i.e. Fibonacci. Naive recursion is O(2^n) because subproblems overlap; memoization (top-down) or a bottom-up DP collapses it to O(n) time, and rolling two variables brings space to O(1). This is the standard bridge from recursion to dynamic programming — recognizing overlapping subproblems is the whole lesson.

example.title: Recursively computing sizes over a nested file/asset tree
example.body: You're building a storage dashboard that shows, for each folder, the total size of everything beneath it. The natural implementation is recursive: size(node) = own size if it's a file (base case), otherwise the sum of size(child) over all children (the divide-and-conquer combine step). Two production issues surface immediately. First, some deployments have very deep directory trees, and a naive recursive crawl blows the call stack — so you rewrite it with an explicit stack (iterative DFS), which is the same recursion with your own heap-allocated stack instead of the language's. Second, the storage graph is actually a DAG because symlinks and shared assets are referenced by multiple folders, so the same subtree gets recomputed repeatedly — the same overlapping-subproblem trap as the Staircase problem, fixed by memoizing size(node) in a map keyed by node id. When a user then asks 'which combination of top-level folders fits under a 10 GB export budget,' that's a backtracking search over subsets with pruning (abandon a branch once the running total exceeds the budget). One feature exercises base-case/recursive-case discipline, divide-and-conquer aggregation, memoization, stack-depth limits, and backtracking.

qa.q: When would you choose iteration over recursion, and vice versa?
qa.a: Prefer recursion when the problem is naturally self-similar — trees, divide-and-conquer, backtracking — because it maps cleanly to the structure and is far more readable. Prefer iteration when depth can be large and unbounded (risking stack overflow) or when the language lacks tail-call optimization, since each recursive frame costs O(depth) stack space. Any recursion can be converted to iteration with an explicit stack; you'd do that specifically to control depth or memory, trading readability for safety.

qa.q: What is tail recursion, and can you rely on the runtime optimizing it away?
qa.a: A call is tail-recursive when the recursive call is the very last operation, so nothing needs to be done with its result — the current frame can be reused instead of pushing a new one. Languages/compilers with tail-call optimization (Scheme, Scala, some C/C++ setups) turn it into a loop with O(1) stack. But you cannot rely on it in Java, Python, or JavaScript engines generally — the JVM and CPython deliberately do not perform TCO — so deep tail recursion there still overflows and you must rewrite it iteratively.

qa.q: How do you analyze the time complexity of a recursive function?
qa.a: Write the recurrence relation from the code: how many recursive calls, on what fraction/size of input, plus the non-recursive work per call. For divide-and-conquer of the form T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n), apply the Master Theorem by comparing f(n) against n^(log_b a). For tree-shaped recursion, count nodes in the recursion tree times work per node — e.g. naive Fibonacci is O(2^n) because the tree roughly doubles each level, while memoization prunes it to O(n) distinct nodes.

qa.q: Explain the difference between backtracking and plain brute force.
qa.a: Both explore the space of candidate solutions, but backtracking builds solutions incrementally and abandons a partial candidate the instant it violates a constraint (pruning), whereas brute force generates complete candidates and only then checks validity. Pruning can cut the effective search space by orders of magnitude — N-Queens is intractable by brute force but fast with backtracking because most placements are rejected early. The mechanical signature of backtracking is the 'undo' step: after recursing on a choice you restore state so the next choice starts clean.

qa.q: Why is naive recursion for the staircase/Fibonacci problem slow, and how do you fix it?
qa.a: Because subproblems overlap: ways(n-1) and ways(n-2) both recompute ways(n-3) and below, producing an exponential O(2^n) call tree with massive redundancy. Memoization (top-down) caches each result the first time it's computed, so every distinct subproblem is solved once — O(n) time, O(n) space. Bottom-up DP does the same iteratively, and since each state only depends on the previous two, you can roll two variables and drop space to O(1).

qa.q: How do you argue that a recursive function is correct?
qa.a: Three checks. First, the base case returns the right answer directly. Second, every recursive call operates on a strictly smaller input that provably moves toward the base case (guaranteeing termination). Third, assuming the recursive call returns the correct answer for the smaller input — the 'recursive leap of faith' — the combine step produces the correct answer for the current input. This is essentially proof by induction, and it lets you reason locally without tracing the entire call tree.

qa.q: What are the space costs of recursion that engineers often overlook?
qa.a: Even when a recursive algorithm's 'obvious' space looks O(1), the call stack itself consumes O(depth) memory because every unreturned frame is live simultaneously — for unbalanced recursion (like a degenerate quicksort partition or a linked-list-shaped tree) depth can be O(n), risking overflow. This is why balanced divide-and-conquer recurses on the smaller partition first or caps depth, and why deep production traversals often use an explicit heap-allocated stack, which moves the frames off the limited call stack onto the much larger heap.

bullets.point: A linear structure of nodes where each node holds a value plus a pointer to the next node, so elements live at scattered heap addresses rather than one contiguous block. The trade-off vs arrays is the whole interview: O(1) insert/delete at a known position vs O(n) random access, and poor cache locality (each hop is a potential cache miss) that makes them slower in practice than Big-O suggests.

Traverse, search (O(n)), insert, and delete — all reduce to careful pointer rewiring while handling the head, tail, and empty-list edge cases. Insert/delete at the head is O(1); at an arbitrary position it's O(n) to reach the node but O(1) to splice. A dummy/sentinel head node is the standard trick to avoid special-casing head mutations.

The canonical answer is the iterative three-pointer walk (prev, curr, next) that flips each next pointer in place — O(n) time, O(1) space. Recursion is elegant but costs O(n) stack. Interviewers extend this to reverse-in-groups-of-k or reverse a sublist [m,n], which test whether you actually understand the pointer bookkeeping.

Floyd's tortoise-and-hare: advance one pointer by 1 and another by 2; if they ever meet there is a cycle, in O(n) time and O(1) space (beats the O(n)-space hash-set approach). Follow-up: reset one pointer to head and advance both by 1 — they meet at the cycle's entry node, which is the real gotcha to be able to prove.

Two numbers stored as linked lists of digits (typically least-significant first); walk both lists simultaneously summing digit+carry and building the result node by node. It matters because it drills carry propagation, unequal lengths, and a trailing carry that creates a final node — the exact edge cases interviewers watch for. Same pattern powers big-integer arithmetic beyond native int width.

Each node adds a prev pointer, enabling O(1) deletion given only a node reference and bidirectional traversal, at the cost of extra memory and two pointers to keep consistent on every mutation. This is the backbone of an LRU cache and of many language runtimes' ordered collections — the O(1) 'unlink a known node' property is exactly what a singly linked list can't give you.

example.title: Building an LRU cache for a service layer
example.body: You're adding an in-memory LRU cache in front of a slow datastore, needing O(1) get and put with eviction of the least-recently-used entry. The standard design is a hash map (key -> node) combined with a doubly linked list ordered by recency: the map gives O(1) lookup, and the doubly linked list lets you unlink a touched node and move it to the front in O(1) — impossible with a singly linked list because you'd need O(n) to find the predecessor. On every get/put you splice the node to the head; on overflow you drop the tail. A sentinel head and tail node eliminate null-checks at the boundaries. This single scenario exercises node structure, O(1) insert/delete, pointer rewiring, and precisely why 'doubly' beats 'singly' — and debugging it usually means chasing a mis-updated prev/next pointer that corrupts the list ordering.

qa.q: When would you pick a linked list over a dynamic array, given arrays usually win in practice?
qa.a: Choose a linked list when you need frequent O(1) insertion/deletion at positions you already hold a reference to (e.g., splicing nodes in an LRU cache or intrusive OS queues), or when you can't tolerate the amortized O(n) copy of an array resize. Otherwise arrays almost always win: contiguous memory means cache-friendly iteration and no per-node pointer overhead. The honest SDE-2 answer is that linked lists are rarely the right default and their O(1) claims are dominated by cache misses on real hardware.

qa.q: Explain Floyd's cycle detection and how to find the node where the cycle begins.
qa.a: Move a slow pointer 1 step and a fast pointer 2 steps per iteration; if they meet, a cycle exists, else fast reaches null. It works because once both are in the cycle the fast pointer gains one step per iteration on the slow one, so it must eventually land on it. To find the entry, keep one pointer at the meeting point and reset the other to head, then advance both by 1 — the distance math (head-to-entry equals meeting-point-to-entry around the loop) guarantees they meet exactly at the cycle start. It's O(n) time, O(1) space.

qa.q: Walk through reversing a singly linked list iteratively and its complexity.
qa.a: Maintain prev=null and curr=head; each step save next=curr.next, point curr.next=prev, then advance prev=curr and curr=next. When curr is null, prev is the new head. It's O(n) time and O(1) space, which is why it's preferred over the recursive version that consumes O(n) stack and can overflow on long lists. The one-liner insight: you're flipping each pointer exactly once while carrying three references so you never lose the rest of the list.

qa.q: Why does an LRU cache need a doubly linked list rather than a singly linked one?
qa.a: On a cache hit you must move the touched node to the most-recently-used end in O(1), which requires unlinking it from its current position — and unlinking needs the predecessor. A doubly linked list stores prev, so given the node (found via the hash map) you rewire node.prev.next and node.next.prev in constant time. A singly linked list would force an O(n) scan to find the predecessor, breaking the O(1) guarantee that's the entire point of the structure.

qa.q: What are the edge cases in the 'add two numbers as linked lists' problem?
qa.a: Unequal list lengths (keep consuming the longer one), a final carry that must create a new most-significant node (e.g., 5+5 -> 0->1), and one list being empty. If digits are stored most-significant-first instead of least-significant-first you must reverse both first or use a stack. Using a dummy head for the result list avoids special-casing the first appended node.

qa.q: How do you remove the nth node from the end in a single pass?
qa.a: Use two pointers off a dummy head: advance a lead pointer n+1 steps ahead, then move both until lead hits null; the trailing pointer now sits just before the target, so you splice it out with trailing.next = trailing.next.next. The dummy head cleanly handles removing the actual head node. It's O(n) time, O(1) space, and one pass instead of the naive length-then-index two-pass approach.

qa.q: How would you detect whether two singly linked lists intersect, and find the intersection node?
qa.a: Two-pointer trick: pA walks list A then continues onto B, pB walks B then A; after at most len(A)+len(B) steps they either meet at the intersection node or both reach null (no intersection). It equalizes the path lengths so the pointers align, giving O(m+n) time and O(1) space. Note intersection means shared nodes by identity, not equal values — after the shared node the tails are identical objects.

qa.q: What's the memory trade-off of doubly vs singly linked lists, and what's an XOR linked list?
qa.a: A doubly linked list costs an extra pointer per node (16 bytes on 64-bit) to gain O(1) deletion-by-reference and backward traversal. An XOR linked list stores a single field holding prev XOR next, recovering both neighbors during a walk using the previously visited address, halving pointer memory. It's a clever trick but rarely used: it breaks garbage-collected languages, defeats debuggers and memory-safety tooling, and the savings almost never justify the fragility.

bullets.point: Remote Procedure Call is a paradigm where calling a function on a remote server looks like a local call — the client stub serializes args, ships them over the network, and the server executes and returns a result. It matters because it abstracts away transport/serialization so you think in terms of methods and messages, not HTTP verbs and URLs; the interview angle is contrasting RPC (action/verb-oriented, tightly coupled contracts) with REST (resource/noun-oriented, HATEOAS).

A hand-rolled RPC: agree on a wire format (e.g. JSON {method, params}), have the client POST it, and dispatch on the method name server-side to invoke the real function. The point is to expose what a framework hides — manual serialization, method routing, error propagation, and the lack of a typed contract — which is exactly the pain gRPC/protobuf solve.

Know the landscape: JSON-RPC and XML-RPC (simple, text-based, HTTP), SOAP (heavy, WSDL-defined, enterprise), Thrift (Facebook), and gRPC (Google, HTTP/2 + protobuf). Interview angle: pick based on schema/typing needs, streaming support, cross-language codegen, and performance — gRPC wins on binary efficiency and streaming, JSON-RPC on human-readability and browser reach.

Protobuf is Google's language-neutral IDL and binary serialization format: you define messages/services in a .proto file and compile it to typed code in many languages. It matters because it gives you a single source-of-truth contract, compact tag-based binary encoding (much smaller/faster than JSON), and forward/backward compatibility when fields are added — key for evolving microservice APIs.

Covers scalar types (int32/int64, sint/fixed variants, bool, string, bytes, double), enums, nested messages, repeated (lists), map<k,v>, and oneof. The nuance to know: every field has a unique tag number (the wire identity — never reuse/renumber), all fields are optional in proto3 with type defaults, and choosing sint/varint vs fixed affects encoding size for negative/large numbers.

In protobuf you declare a `service` with `rpc Method(Request) returns (Response)` entries, defining the RPC surface purely from the contract. This matters because the service block is what the compiler turns into client stubs and server interfaces — the contract drives codegen, so API design happens in the .proto, not in handwritten glue.

gRPC takes the compiled .proto and generates a server base class you implement and a client stub you call; it runs over HTTP/2 giving multiplexing, and supports four call types: unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional streaming. Interview angle: know why HTTP/2 matters (multiplexed streams over one connection, header compression), that gRPC needs a proxy (grpc-web/Envoy) for browsers, and how deadlines, cancellation, and metadata work.

Extending a live API means adding new fields/messages/RPCs while preserving compatibility: give new fields fresh tag numbers, never change or reuse existing ones, and use `reserved` for removed fields. This is the crux of schema evolution in interviews — old clients ignore unknown fields and new clients see defaults for missing ones, so additive changes stay backward/forward compatible.

example.title: Order + Payment services in an e-commerce backend
example.body: You own an Order service that must call a Payment service to charge a card. Instead of a REST call with hand-written JSON DTOs on both sides, you define a payment.proto with a `PaymentService` exposing `rpc Charge(ChargeRequest) returns (ChargeResponse)`, where ChargeRequest has typed fields (int64 amount_cents, string currency, string idempotency_key with fixed tag numbers). Both teams compile the same .proto to generate the Go server skeleton and the Java client stub, so the contract is shared and type-safe across languages. Payment runs over HTTP/2, letting Order set a 2s deadline and propagate cancellation if the checkout request is abandoned. When Payments later needs to return a fraud score, they add `float fraud_score = 7;` — older Order builds simply ignore the new field, so the rollout is non-breaking. If you ever needed a stream of settlement events, you'd switch that RPC to server-streaming rather than polling.

qa.q: When would you choose gRPC over REST, and when not?
qa.a: Choose gRPC for internal service-to-service traffic where you want strong typed contracts, cross-language codegen, low latency, compact binary payloads, and streaming (e.g. high-throughput microservices, real-time feeds). Avoid it for public/browser-facing APIs where human-readable JSON, caching, and broad tooling matter — browsers can't speak raw gRPC without grpc-web plus a proxy like Envoy. REST is also better when loose coupling and easy debuggability outweigh performance.

qa.q: Why does gRPC use HTTP/2, and what does that buy you?
qa.a: HTTP/2 provides request/response multiplexing over a single TCP connection, so many concurrent RPCs share one connection without head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer, plus header compression (HPACK) and binary framing. This is what makes long-lived bidirectional streaming and high concurrency efficient. It also enables server push semantics for streaming RPCs, which HTTP/1.1 could only fake with long-polling or chunked hacks.

qa.q: How do protobuf field tag numbers work and why can't you reuse them?
qa.a: Each field's tag number is its identity on the wire — encoded values are keyed by (tag << 3 | wire_type), not by field name, so names are purely for code generation. If you reuse a retired tag for a new field of a different type, an old serialized message could be misinterpreted by a new client, silently corrupting data. That's why you mark removed tags/names as `reserved` to prevent accidental reuse.

qa.q: Explain protobuf's backward/forward compatibility model.
qa.a: In proto3 all fields are optional and additive changes are safe: unknown fields encountered by an older reader are preserved/ignored, and fields missing for a newer reader take type defaults (0, empty string, etc.). This means new clients can read old data (backward compat) and old clients can read new data (forward compat) as long as you only add fields with new tags and never change existing types or renumber. Removing a field is fine if you reserve its tag.

qa.q: What are the four gRPC call types and give a use case for each?
qa.a: Unary (one request, one response) for a normal call like Charge. Server-streaming (one request, stream of responses) for pushing a feed of updates or paginated results. Client-streaming (stream of requests, one response) for uploading chunks/metrics that aggregate into one result. Bidirectional streaming (both stream independently) for chat, live telemetry, or interactive sessions — over a single HTTP/2 connection.

qa.q: How do deadlines/timeouts and cancellation work in gRPC, and why prefer deadlines over timeouts?
qa.a: A client sets a deadline (an absolute point in time) that propagates through the call as metadata; the server sees remaining time and can abort work, and cancellation flows downstream to nested RPCs. Deadlines are preferred over per-hop timeouts because they compose across a call chain — each service subtracts elapsed time rather than each restarting its own clock, preventing the total latency budget from ballooning. Without deadlines, hung dependencies leak resources and cause cascading failures.

qa.q: Compared to a hand-rolled JSON-RPC, what concrete problems does gRPC + protobuf solve?
qa.a: It removes hand-written serialization and method-dispatch glue by generating typed stubs from one contract, so client and server can't drift out of sync and you get compile-time type checking across languages. Binary protobuf is smaller and faster to encode/decode than JSON, and the schema gives you disciplined evolution via tag numbers and reserved fields. You also get built-in streaming, deadlines, metadata, and status codes instead of reinventing them.

qa.q: What does `oneof` do and how does it differ from just having multiple optional fields?
qa.a: `oneof` declares a group of fields where at most one can be set at a time; setting one clears the others, and it saves memory since only the active field is stored. It's ideal for tagged-union / variant payloads (e.g. a result that is either a Success or an Error). Unlike several independent optional fields, it enforces mutual exclusivity in the generated code and lets you switch on which case is set, but note oneof fields can't be `repeated`.

bullets.point: WebRTC gives browsers/native apps sub-500ms, peer-to-peer real-time audio, video, and arbitrary data with no plugins and built-in encryption. The interview angle is knowing when to reach for it: use it for interactive/bidirectional media (calls, screen share, gaming, live collaboration) and NOT for one-to-many broadcast at scale where HLS/DASH's multi-second latency but CDN-friendly cacheability wins.

Core pieces: RTCPeerConnection (the connection), MediaStreamTrack (audio/video), RTCDataChannel (arbitrary data over SCTP), SDP (capability description), ICE/STUN/TURN (NAT traversal), and a signaling server you provide. Interviewers probe whether you can name each component's job — especially that STUN discovers your public reflexive address and TURN relays media when a direct path is impossible.

Connection setup is an out-of-band SDP offer/answer exchange plus ICE candidate trickling, both relayed through your signaling channel (usually WebSocket). Key insight: WebRTC deliberately does NOT specify signaling, so the app owns identity, rooms, and message transport; ICE then runs connectivity checks over gathered candidate pairs and promotes the best working path.

The canonical sequence: getUserMedia -> new RTCPeerConnection(config) -> addTrack -> createOffer -> setLocalDescription -> signal offer; the remote sets it, createAnswer, and signals back, while both fire onicecandidate to exchange candidates and ontrack to render incoming media. Getting the ordering and event wiring right (and handling renegotiation) is what separates a demo from a robust integration.

The backend is primarily a stateless-ish signaling relay (WebSocket) that manages rooms and forwards offers/answers/ICE candidates between peers, plus a coturn TURN/STUN deployment for NAT traversal. Media typically does NOT flow through this server in P2P mode — except through TURN — so scaling signaling and scaling media are separate concerns to reason about.

The frontend uses navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia/getDisplayMedia to capture tracks, wires RTCPeerConnection events, and attaches inbound MediaStreams to <video> srcObject. Practical gotchas interviewers like: HTTPS/secure-context requirement, autoplay + muted policies, permission handling, and cleaning up tracks/connections to avoid leaked camera indicators.

pc.getStats() returns an RTCStatsReport of live metrics — RTT, jitter, packetsLost, bytes/framesPerSecond, resolution, bitrate, and candidate-pair state. This is the primary tool for diagnosing bad calls and driving adaptive decisions; being able to say 'high jitter + rising packet loss on the inbound-rtp report points to congestion, not a NAT problem' demonstrates real operational depth.

Libraries like simple-peer and PeerJS wrap the offer/answer/ICE boilerplate into a few callbacks, speeding up 1:1 builds; server-side, mediasoup/Janus/Pion/LiveKit provide SFU infrastructure. The trade-off interviewers want: convenience and fewer bugs vs. losing fine control over renegotiation, codecs, and simulcast — and the risk of an unmaintained abstraction hiding critical behavior.

Mesh connects every peer to every other (O(n^2) uplinks) and collapses beyond ~4 participants; an SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) has each peer send one upstream and forwards/selects streams (with simulcast) to others; an MCU mixes everything into one composited stream — light on client bandwidth but CPU-heavy on the server. The expected answer is choosing SFU for most group-call products and knowing the bandwidth/CPU/latency trade-offs of each.

example.title: Telehealth video consultation: 1:1 launch, a NAT failure, then group visits
example.body: You build a doctor-patient video consult feature. v1 is 1:1 P2P: a Node WebSocket signaling server pairs the two participants in a room and relays SDP offers/answers and trickled ICE candidates; the browser captures the camera via getUserMedia and renders the remote track via ontrack. It works on your laptop, but QA reports calls that connect then show frozen video for users on hospital corporate networks. getStats() shows ICE stuck with no succeeded candidate pair — the culprit is symmetric NAT/firewalls, so plain STUN can't punch through and you must deploy a coturn TURN server so media can relay. Once calls are stable, product asks for 3-4 person case reviews. You correctly reject a mesh (uplink and CPU blow up per added participant) and introduce an SFU (e.g., mediasoup/LiveKit): each client sends one upstream with simulcast layers, and the SFU forwards the appropriate resolution to each viewer. You wire getStats-based monitoring to flag high jitter/packet loss and downshift resolution on congested links. This single feature exercises the entire topic: why WebRTC, the SDP/ICE/STUN/TURN jargon, signaling, browser APIs, stats-driven debugging, and the mesh-vs-SFU architecture decision.

qa.q: STUN vs TURN — what does each do, and why is TURN a last resort?
qa.a: STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT) lets a peer discover its public IP:port (server-reflexive candidate) so two peers can attempt a direct hole-punched connection; the STUN server sees no media. TURN (Traversal Using Relays around NAT) is a media relay used when a direct path is impossible — typically symmetric NATs or restrictive firewalls. TURN is the fallback because ALL media flows through your server, consuming real bandwidth and cost, so ICE only promotes a relay candidate pair when direct/reflexive pairs fail.

qa.q: Walk through the SDP offer/answer handshake and why signaling isn't part of WebRTC.
qa.a: The offerer calls createOffer(), setLocalDescription(offer), and sends the SDP; the answerer setRemoteDescription(offer), createAnswer(), setLocalDescription(answer), and sends it back, after which both sides know each other's codecs, ICE parameters, and media lines. SDP is the negotiated description of capabilities. WebRTC intentionally leaves signaling transport unspecified so apps can reuse existing auth, presence, and infrastructure — most people use WebSocket, but it could be HTTP long-poll, SIP, or even copy-paste.

qa.q: Mesh vs SFU vs MCU — how do you choose for a group video product?
qa.a: Mesh has every participant send a stream directly to every other (O(n^2) uplinks and decode load), so it only survives tiny groups (~3-4). An SFU has each client send one upstream and the server selectively forwards streams — with simulcast it can send different resolutions to different receivers — giving the best balance of client cost and server scalability, so it's the default for most products. An MCU decodes and mixes everyone into a single composite stream, minimizing client bandwidth/decoders but incurring heavy server CPU and added latency; it's used for legacy interop or very constrained clients.

qa.q: Why does WebRTC prefer UDP, and how is media secured and made resilient to loss?
qa.a: Real-time media favors UDP because retransmitting late packets is worse than dropping them; head-of-line blocking from TCP would spike latency. WebRTC handles loss with codec-level concealment, NACK/retransmission for select frames, FEC, and jitter buffering rather than strict reliability. Media is always encrypted — DTLS handshakes to exchange keys and RTP is protected as SRTP — so encryption is mandatory, not optional; data channels run over SCTP-over-DTLS and can be configured ordered/reliable or unordered/unreliable.

qa.q: How would you debug a call that connects but has terrible video quality?
qa.a: Pull pc.getStats() and inspect the inbound/outbound-rtp and candidate-pair reports: rising packetsLost and jitter with adequate bandwidth points to network congestion or Wi-Fi loss; low framesPerSecond or frameHeight with high qualityLimitationReason='cpu' points to encode/decode pressure; a high currentRoundTripTime indicates a long/relayed path. I'd correlate whether the selected candidate pair is a TURN relay (added latency), check if bitrate is being capped, and then act — enable/tune simulcast, lower target resolution, or adjust bandwidth constraints — instead of guessing.

qa.q: What is trickle ICE and why does it matter for connection time?
qa.a: Without trickle, a peer gathers ALL ICE candidates (including slow-to-resolve relay candidates) before sending its SDP, adding seconds of setup delay. Trickle ICE sends each candidate via signaling as soon as it's discovered (onicecandidate) so connectivity checks start immediately in parallel with gathering. It's the standard approach and dramatically cuts time-to-first-frame; the remote side just adds candidates with addIceCandidate as they arrive.

qa.q: What is the glare problem and how does 'perfect negotiation' solve it?
qa.a: Glare happens when both peers try to create/send an offer simultaneously (e.g., both add a track at once), leaving the state machine in conflict. Perfect negotiation resolves it by designating one peer 'polite' and one 'impolite': on a colliding offer the polite peer rolls back its own local offer and accepts the remote one, while the impolite peer ignores the incoming offer and proceeds. This pattern, driven by onnegotiationneeded plus signalingState checks, gives robust, symmetric renegotiation logic without race conditions.

bullets.point: Orientation for the bootcamp: sets expectations that Rust's learning curve is front-loaded (the borrow checker) but pays off in runtime safety and performance. Interview framing: articulate Rust's core promise — memory safety without a garbage collector, enforced at compile time.

Roadmap from tooling and syntax basics to the ownership model, then type modeling (structs/enums), error handling, collections, and advanced topics. The mental model to carry through: everything downstream is a consequence of the ownership/borrowing rules.

Rust targets CPU-bound, latency-sensitive, and systems work where Node's single-threaded event loop and GC pauses fall short — think proxies, parsers, WASM, embedded, and high-throughput services. Interview angle: Node wins on iteration speed and I/O-bound glue; Rust wins when you need predictable memory, real multithreading, and zero-cost abstractions. It's not either/or — Rust often ships as native addons (napi-rs) or CLIs alongside Node.

Install via rustup (the toolchain manager), which manages rustc, cargo, and toolchains (stable/beta/nightly) and targets. Know that rustup lets you pin per-project toolchains and cross-compile targets, unlike a single global compiler.

`cargo new` scaffolds a project with Cargo.toml (manifest: metadata, dependencies, edition) and src/main.rs (binary) or src/lib.rs (library). Cargo is build system + package manager + test runner in one — the reason Rust's project ergonomics feel cohesive versus the fragmented npm/webpack/jest chain.

rust-analyzer is the LSP that provides inline types, borrow-checker diagnostics, and refactors — non-negotiable for productivity. `fn main()` is the entrypoint and `println!` is a macro (the `!` matters: macros are compile-time code generation, not functions, which is how format strings get type-checked).

`let` bindings are immutable by default; types are inferred but statically checked. Shadowing (re-declaring `let x`) lets you reuse a name with a new type/value without mutation. Scalar types are fixed-width (i32/u64/f64/bool/char) — no implicit numeric coercion, so casts are explicit with `as`.

`if`, `match`, and `loop` are expressions that return values (`let y = if cond { 1 } else { 2 }`). Three loop forms: `loop` (infinite, can `break` with a value), `while`, and `for` over iterators. Prefer iterator `for` over index loops — it sidesteps bounds bugs and is idiomatic.

Declared with `fn`, explicit typed parameters and return type after `->`. Distinguish statements (no value) from expressions (the trailing expression without a semicolon is the return value). This expression-oriented design is why omitting a semicolon meaningfully changes behavior.

No garbage collector and no manual malloc/free — memory is freed deterministically when its owner goes out of scope (RAII / the `Drop` trait). This gives predictable, pause-free reclamation; the compiler statically guarantees no use-after-free, double-free, or leaks-by-dangling. The whole ownership system exists to make this safe at zero runtime cost.

Bindings are immutable unless declared `let mut`. Mutability is a property of the binding/reference, not just the data, and it composes with borrowing (you can only take a `&mut` if the binding is `mut`). Immutable-by-default is a correctness lever: it makes shared state and aliasing bugs visible at compile time.

Fixed-size, `Copy` types (integers, bools, arrays of known size) live on the stack and are cheap to copy; dynamically-sized or owned data (String, Vec, Box<T>) stores its buffer on the heap with a stack-side pointer/len/capacity. Understanding this predicts what moves vs copies and where allocation cost lives — a common interview probe.

Every value has exactly one owner; assigning or passing a non-Copy value moves ownership (the source is invalidated), and when the owner drops, the value is freed. This single-owner rule is what makes deterministic cleanup and data-race freedom decidable at compile time. Expect to explain move-vs-clone and why `let b = a;` can invalidate `a`.

Instead of moving, you can borrow via references: any number of shared `&T` immutable borrows, XOR exactly one exclusive `&mut T` — never both simultaneously. This aliasing-XOR-mutability rule is the borrow checker's core invariant and is precisely what prevents data races and iterator invalidation without a runtime. Lifetimes (later) exist to prove references never outlive their data.

Product types for modeling domain data: named-field structs, tuple structs, and unit structs. Derive traits (`#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]`) to opt into printing/cloning/equality. They're the primary building block; combined with enums they give you algebraic data types.

`impl` blocks attach behavior: methods take `self`/`&self`/`&mut self` (borrowing rules apply to the receiver), and associated functions (no self, e.g. `Struct::new`) act as constructors/factories. The `&self` vs `&mut self` vs `self` choice encodes whether a method reads, mutates, or consumes the value.

Sum types where each variant can carry different data (`enum Msg { Quit, Move{x:i32,y:i32}, Write(String) }`) — far richer than C enums or TS string unions. They make illegal states unrepresentable and pair with `match` for exhaustive handling. `Option` and `Result` are just enums from the standard library.

`match` destructures values and is exhaustive — the compiler forces you to handle every variant (or add `_`), so adding an enum variant surfaces every site that must change. `if let`/`let else`/`while let` are ergonomic single-pattern forms. Exhaustiveness is a major reliability feature versus switch-with-fallthrough languages.

Recoverable errors use `Result<T, E>`; unrecoverable bugs use `panic!` (unwinds/aborts). The `?` operator propagates errors up by early-returning the `Err`, replacing verbose try/catch nesting. Idiomatic APIs return `Result`; libraries often use `thiserror`/`anyhow` — reserve `unwrap()`/`expect()` for cases you can prove can't fail.

`Option<T>` (`Some`/`None`) is Rust's null replacement — absence is encoded in the type, so you can't dereference a 'null' by accident. Combinators (`map`, `and_then`, `unwrap_or`, `?`) let you chain without unwrapping. This is the language-level fix for the billion-dollar null mistake.

Heap-backed growable containers: `Vec<T>` (dynamic array), `HashMap<K,V>`, and `String` (UTF-8 growable); plus slices (`&[T]`, `&str`) as borrowed views. Ownership and borrowing govern them — e.g. you can't push to a Vec while holding a reference into it (that's iterator invalidation, caught at compile time).

Dependencies (crates) are declared in Cargo.toml with semver ranges and resolved via Cargo.lock; crates.io is the registry. Feature flags enable conditional compilation to keep builds lean, and workspaces group multiple crates. Know the crate-vs-module distinction and how `cargo add`/features affect compile time and binary size.

bullets.step: Leftovers — Traits, Generics, Lifetimes, Concurrency, Macros, Async
bullets.point: Traits = shared behavior/interfaces enabling static (generics, monomorphized) or dynamic (`dyn Trait`) dispatch; generics + trait bounds give zero-cost polymorphism. Lifetimes are compile-time annotations proving references stay valid. `Send`/`Sync` make 'fearless concurrency' — the borrow checker prevents data races across threads — and async/`Future`s (with runtimes like Tokio) provide cooperative, poll-based concurrency for I/O. These are the topics an SDE-2 must eventually master to write real Rust.

example.title: Building a concurrent web scraper / ingestion service in Rust
example.body: You're replacing a Node.js scraper that pegs one CPU and stalls under GC pressure. In Rust you model each scraped record as a struct with derived Debug/Clone, and represent a page's outcome as an enum (Fetched(String), Redirected(Url), Failed(StatusCode)) matched exhaustively. Network and parse errors return Result<Page, ScrapeError> and propagate with `?`; missing optional fields (e.g. a page's canonical URL) are Option<String> so you never dereference a null. Results accumulate into a Vec and are deduped via a HashMap keyed by URL — and when you try to hold a reference into that Vec while also pushing, the borrow checker stops you at compile time, killing a whole class of iterator-invalidation bugs. For throughput you go async with Tokio: an HTTP client trait lets you swap real vs mock fetchers (generics for tests, dyn for a plugin registry), and you fan out with tasks. Because shared state (the dedup map) must cross threads, you wrap it in Arc<Mutex<..>> — Send/Sync bounds force you to make sharing explicit, so there are no data races. Ownership guarantees each response buffer is freed the moment its task finishes, giving flat, GC-pause-free memory instead of Node's sawtooth. The result ships as a single static binary via `cargo build --release`.

qa.q: Explain the borrow checker's core rule and why it prevents data races.
qa.a: At any point a value can have either any number of shared immutable references (&T) or exactly one exclusive mutable reference (&mut T), never both. Since a data race requires two threads accessing the same memory where at least one writes, and the rule forbids simultaneous aliasing-plus-mutation, races become impossible to express in safe code. The check is fully static, so it costs nothing at runtime, and it also incidentally prevents single-threaded bugs like iterator invalidation.

qa.q: What's the difference between move, Copy, and Clone?
qa.a: Move is the default for heap-owning types: assigning or passing the value transfers ownership and invalidates the source, preventing double-frees. Copy is an opt-in marker for cheap, fixed-size stack types (integers, bool, char) where a bitwise duplicate is made and the original stays valid. Clone is an explicit, potentially expensive deep copy you call with `.clone()` for types like String or Vec — the language never inserts it implicitly, so allocation cost is always visible in the source.

qa.q: When do you reach for lifetimes, and what problem do they solve?
qa.a: Lifetimes are compile-time annotations that let the borrow checker prove a reference never outlives the data it points to, eliminating dangling pointers without a runtime. You need explicit annotations when a function returns a reference derived from its inputs or when a struct stores a reference — the compiler can't always infer which input the output borrows from. They generate no code; they're purely a proof obligation, which is why elision rules handle most cases automatically.

qa.q: Result vs Option, and what does the `?` operator actually do?
qa.a: Option<T> models presence/absence (a value that may be missing), while Result<T, E> models success/failure with an error payload. The `?` operator unwraps the Ok/Some case or, on Err/None, early-returns that variant from the enclosing function — so it only compiles where the return type is compatible. This replaces nested match/try-catch with linear code while keeping error handling explicit in the type signature, unlike exceptions which are invisible in signatures.

qa.q: Static vs dynamic dispatch with traits — trade-offs?
qa.a: Generics with trait bounds (`fn f<T: Trait>`) are monomorphized: the compiler generates a specialized copy per concrete type, giving inlinable, zero-cost calls at the price of larger binaries and longer compiles. `dyn Trait` (trait objects) uses a vtable for runtime dispatch — one indirection per call, smaller code, and it lets you store heterogeneous types in one collection (e.g. Vec<Box<dyn Handler>>). Reach for generics on hot paths and dyn when you need runtime polymorphism or to break monomorphization bloat.

qa.q: How does Rust achieve 'fearless concurrency' without a GC?
qa.a: Two auto-derived marker traits gate sharing: Send means a type can be moved to another thread, Sync means &T can be shared across threads. The borrow checker plus these bounds mean the compiler rejects code that would share mutable state without synchronization — e.g. you must wrap shared mutable data in Arc<Mutex<T>>, and a plain Rc won't compile across threads because it isn't Send. So data races are caught at compile time rather than debugged at 3am in production.

qa.q: Box vs Rc vs Arc vs RefCell — when do you use each?
qa.a: Box<T> is single-owner heap allocation (for recursive types, trait objects, or large values). Rc<T> is single-threaded shared ownership via reference counting; Arc<T> is its atomic, thread-safe counterpart (more expensive due to atomic ops). RefCell<T> moves borrow checking to runtime, enabling interior mutability (mutating through a shared reference) and panicking on violated borrow rules. Common combos: Rc<RefCell<T>> for shared mutable single-threaded graphs, Arc<Mutex<T>> for shared mutable state across threads.

qa.q: Why choose Rust over Node.js for a service, and when would you not?
qa.a: Choose Rust for CPU-bound work, low and predictable latency (no GC pauses), true multithreading, tight memory footprints, or when memory-safety bugs are unacceptable — parsers, proxies, data pipelines, WASM. Stick with Node when the workload is I/O-bound glue, iteration speed and the npm ecosystem matter more than raw performance, or the team lacks Rust expertise and the borrow-checker ramp isn't worth it. A pragmatic middle ground is keeping Node for orchestration and dropping to Rust via native addons (napi-rs) for the hot path.

bullets.point: Halving-search over a sorted or monotonic search space in O(log n) time, O(1) space. The whole game is the loop invariant and boundary handling: pick one template (closed [lo,hi] or half-open [lo,hi)) and stick to it, use mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2 to avoid integer overflow, and make sure the window strictly shrinks so it terminates. Preconditions matter: the array must be sorted, or more generally the predicate you search on must be monotonic.

Applying the template to the canonical case: find a target's index in a sorted array (or report absence). Interviewers watch how you handle the off-by-one and empty-range cases; the reliable move is the half-open interval so hi is always exclusive and lo==hi means 'not found'. This is the muscle memory you reuse for every variant that follows.

Find two indices whose values sum to a target. The optimal answer is a single-pass hash map (value -> index) in O(n) time / O(n) space, checking for target - x before inserting x. Classic because it tests whether you reach for hashing instead of the O(n^2) double loop, and it exposes edge cases like duplicate values and using the same element twice.

bullets.step: Two-Sum Variant on a sorted array (inferred)
bullets.point: The natural follow-up: given a sorted array, find a pair summing to target using the two-pointer technique (lo from left, hi from right, move inward based on sum vs target) in O(n) time / O(1) space. It contrasts with the hash approach and generalizes to 3Sum/4Sum via sort + fix-one + two-pointer. The insight — sortedness lets you prune a whole direction each step — is what bridges into lower_bound thinking.

Boundary searches instead of exact-match: lower_bound returns the first index with a[i] >= x, upper_bound the first with a[i] > x. These power 'search insert position', first/last occurrence, and counting (count = upper_bound - lower_bound). The interview point is that most 'find the smallest/largest index satisfying P' problems reduce to a lower_bound on a monotonic predicate.

A direct use of boundary search — e.g. count occurrences of a value, or find the first and last position of an element in a sorted array — solved as two binary searches in O(log n) rather than a linear scan. It cements that once you can locate boundaries you compose them to answer range/count queries cheaply.

bullets.step: Taking it to the next level: binary search on the answer
bullets.point: When the answer is a number and 'is X feasible?' is a monotonic predicate (false...false,true...true), binary search the answer space and run an O(n) feasibility check per candidate. Canonical problems: Koko eating bananas, capacity to ship packages in D days, minimize the largest subarray sum. This is the single highest-leverage binary-search pattern in interviews because it turns optimization into a log-factor search — total O(n log(range)).

Given a binary string of a's and b's and at most k allowed flips, find the longest substring of one repeated character. Solved with a sliding window: expand right, track the count of the minority char, shrink left when flips-needed exceeds k; run it once assuming target 'a' and once assuming 'b', take the max — O(n). It's a great example of 'longest window satisfying a constraint', and note it can also be phrased as binary search on the window length (O(n log n)), which is a weaker but instructive alternative.

A second string problem extending the window theme — e.g. longest substring without repeating characters or longest substring with at most k distinct characters — using a two-pointer window with a frequency map in O(n). The takeaway is recognizing the 'variable-size window with a validity condition you can incrementally maintain' family, which covers a large share of string interview questions.

example.title: An SRE health-window and capacity service for a service-mesh dashboard
example.body: You are building the backend for a reliability dashboard. Requests arrive with monotonically increasing timestamps stored in a sorted array, so seeking the start of a query window ('errors in the last 15 minutes') is a lower_bound over timestamps, and the count in a range is upper_bound - lower_bound — the exact technique time-series stores use to slice range queries in O(log n) instead of scanning. For an anomaly rule you correlate metrics: given a sorted list of per-endpoint error counts and a target budget, a two-sum/two-pointer pass finds two endpoints whose combined errors exactly blow the budget, so you can flag the pair. Capacity planning is binary search on the answer: 'what is the minimum number of pods so every request finishes within the SLA?' is monotonic (more pods never makes it worse), so you binary-search the pod count and run an O(n) feasibility check that simulates the load per candidate. Finally, health-check streaks map straight onto Vasya and String: encode each probe as 'g' (green) or 'r' (red), and 'the longest stretch we can call healthy if we tolerate up to k transient red checks' is a sliding window over the sequence. One dashboard, and four steps of this topic each show up as the natural, optimal tool.

qa.q: Why write mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2, and how do you guarantee your binary search terminates?
qa.a: lo + hi can overflow a fixed-width integer when both are large (the classic bug in Java's Arrays.binarySearch, fixed in 2006); lo + (hi - lo)/2 computes the same midpoint without ever exceeding hi. Termination requires the search window to strictly shrink every iteration: with a half-open [lo, hi) loop you set lo = mid + 1 or hi = mid, and since mid is always in [lo, hi), one of the bounds always moves. The infinite-loop trap is a closed-interval template where mid rounds toward lo and you set lo = mid without the +1.

qa.q: Explain lower_bound vs upper_bound and how you'd count occurrences of a value in a sorted array.
qa.a: lower_bound returns the index of the first element >= x; upper_bound returns the first element > x. Both are binary searches on a monotonic predicate over a half-open interval: for lower_bound, if a[mid] < x move lo = mid+1 else hi = mid. The count of x is simply upper_bound(x) - lower_bound(x), giving an O(log n) range query, and lower_bound also directly answers 'search insert position'.

qa.q: Compare the hash-map and two-pointer solutions to Two Sum. When do you pick each?
qa.a: On an unsorted array, a single-pass hash map (value -> index) is O(n) time / O(n) space and preserves original indices — the default answer. On an already-sorted array (or when you can sort and indices don't matter), the two-pointer sweep is O(n) time / O(1) space, which is better on memory and extends to 3Sum/4Sum. If sorting is required just to enable two pointers, you pay O(n log n), so for a one-off query on unsorted data the hash map usually wins.

qa.q: What signals that a problem is 'binary search on the answer', and what's the complexity?
qa.a: The answer is a single number within a bounded range, and feasibility is monotonic: if value v works then every v on one side also works (false...false, true...true). You binary-search the value range and run a feasibility predicate at each step, e.g. Koko eating bananas or 'ship packages in D days'. Cost is O(f * log(range)) where f is the predicate cost (often O(n)); the key correctness detail is proving monotonicity and choosing the initial [lo, hi] so the true answer is inside it.

qa.q: How would you solve Vasya and String, and what is the alternative framing?
qa.a: Sliding window: keep a window and the count of the minority character inside it; expand the right edge, and whenever the number of flips needed (the minority count) exceeds k, advance the left edge. Run it once treating the target as 'a' and once as 'b', take the max window length — O(n) time, O(1) extra space. The alternative is binary search on the window length L: for a fixed L you can check in O(n) whether some length-L window needs <= k flips, and feasibility is monotonic in L, giving O(n log n) — correct but strictly slower than the two-pointer sweep.

qa.q: How does binary search adapt to a rotated sorted array?
qa.a: At each step compute mid; because the array is one rotation of a sorted array, at least one of [lo, mid] and [mid, hi] is fully sorted. Determine which half is sorted by comparing a[lo] with a[mid], check whether the target lies within that sorted half's value range, and recurse into the half that can contain it. It stays O(log n); the subtlety is picking consistent inclusive/exclusive comparisons to handle duplicates and the pivot correctly.

qa.q: Binary search usually assumes distinct sorted data — how do you handle duplicates or a floating-point search space?
qa.a: For duplicates, stop doing exact-match search and switch to lower_bound/upper_bound so you deterministically land on the first or last matching index rather than an arbitrary one. For a real-valued answer (e.g. binary-searching a ratio or a physical threshold), there's no integer step, so you either iterate a fixed number of times (~100 iterations is well past double precision) or loop while hi - lo > epsilon, avoiding the exact-equality comparisons that never terminate with floats.

bullets.point: Establishes the testing model for server-side JS: a runner (Jest/Vitest) provides describe/it/expect, lifecycle hooks (beforeEach/afterAll), and assertions, while Node code under test is just imported and invoked. Interview angle: know that Express handlers are plain functions of (req, res, next), so most logic can be tested without a network by injecting fake req/res or by driving the app with an HTTP client like supertest.

bullets.step: Testing a simple app (pure unit tests)
bullets.point: Start with pure functions (validators, formatters, business rules) that have no I/O — deterministic input/output, no mocks needed. Interview angle: pure functions are the cheapest, fastest, most valuable tests; a strong sign of good architecture is that core logic is extracted out of route handlers so it can be unit-tested in isolation.

Use supertest to send real HTTP requests against the Express `app` object (not a bound port) and assert on status codes, headers, and JSON bodies. Interview angle: you export `app` separately from `server.listen()` precisely so tests can `request(app).get('/...')` without occupying a port, enabling parallel test files.

Cover routes with path params, query strings, request bodies, auth middleware, and multiple response branches — asserting both happy paths and error paths (400/401/404/500). Interview angle: interviewers probe whether you test the failure and edge cases (missing fields, invalid IDs, unauthorized) rather than only the 200 case, since that's where real bugs hide.

Vitest offers a near drop-in Jest-compatible API (describe/it/expect, `vi` instead of `jest`) with first-class ESM/TypeScript support, faster startup via Vite's transform pipeline, and shared config with the app's Vite setup. Interview angle: be able to articulate the migration motivation — native ESM without babel/ts-jest gymnastics, watch-mode speed, and unified tooling — plus the main gotcha that `jest.*` calls and some globals config must be swapped to `vi.*`/vitest config.

Wire Vitest into a real project: `vitest.config.ts` (test.environment 'node', globals, setup files), npm scripts for run/watch/coverage, and organizing test files alongside or under a __tests__ folder. Interview angle: know `test.globals: true` vs explicit imports, and that Node vs jsdom environment must match what you're testing (backend = node).

Introduces integration tests that hit a real (test) database — seeding data, isolating state per test (transactions/truncation or a fresh in-memory/containerized DB), and cleaning up in hooks. Interview angle: the key trade-off is realism vs speed/flakiness; discuss test DB isolation strategies (mongodb-memory-server, testcontainers, per-test transaction rollback) and why sharing state across tests causes order-dependent flakiness.

Replace external modules/collaborators (DB client, third-party SDK, mailer, fetch) with `vi.mock('module')` so a unit test exercises only the code under test without real I/O. Interview angle: mock at boundaries you don't own (network, filesystem, payment APIs); over-mocking your own code produces tests that assert implementation details and break on every refactor.

When a dependency exposes nested objects/chained methods (e.g. a Prisma client or an ORM query builder), a shallow mock isn't enough — deep mocking auto-stubs the whole object tree (via `mockDeep` from vitest-mock-extended or recursive auto-mocks). Interview angle: recognize deep mocks are powerful but brittle and can hide contract drift, so they're best for chained fluent APIs where hand-stubbing every level is impractical.

Control what a mock returns per scenario: `mockReturnValue`/`mockResolvedValue`/`mockRejectedValue` for defaults and `mockReturnValueOnce` for sequenced calls, so you can simulate success, empty results, and failures. Interview angle: use `mockResolvedValueOnce` chains to test retry/pagination logic, and always assert both the mocked outcome's effect and that the mock was called with the expected args.

A mock replaces an implementation entirely; a spy (`vi.spyOn(obj,'method')`) wraps a real method to record calls while optionally keeping or overriding behavior via `.mockImplementation`. Interview angle: use a spy when you want the real behavior to run but still assert it was called (e.g. verifying a logger fired); use a full mock when the real behavior is expensive or has side effects — and remember to restore spies (`mockRestore`/`restoreAllMocks`) to avoid leaking state across tests.

Run the test suite automatically on every push/PR (GitHub Actions: install, lint, test with coverage, block merge on failure) and gate deployment on green. Interview angle: discuss required status checks as merge gates, caching node_modules for speed, running tests against a service container (e.g. a Postgres/Mongo service) for integration tests, and separating fast unit tests from slower e2e stages.

bullets.step: Unit vs integration vs e2e tests
bullets.point: Unit tests isolate a single unit with mocked collaborators (fast, many); integration tests verify several real components together, e.g. route + DB (slower, fewer); e2e drive the full deployed stack through the UI/API like a user (slowest, fewest). Interview angle: articulate the test pyramid — push volume to the base for fast feedback, reserve e2e for critical user journeys — and the trade-off between confidence/realism and speed/flakiness/maintenance cost.

example.title: Testing a MERN order-checkout API
example.body: You own the `/api/orders` endpoint in a MERN backend: it validates a cart, calls a Stripe SDK to charge a card, writes an order document to MongoDB, and emails a receipt. You write pure unit tests for the cart-total/validation logic, then supertest-driven tests hitting the Express `app` for the POST route asserting 201 on success and 400/402 on bad input or declined payment. Stripe and the mailer are external boundaries you don't own, so you `vi.mock` them and use `mockResolvedValueOnce` to simulate a successful charge and `mockRejectedValueOnce` to simulate a decline; you use `vi.spyOn` on the logger to assert the failure was logged without silencing it. For the DB you run integration tests against mongodb-memory-server, seeding a product and truncating between tests so runs are order-independent. Finally a GitHub Actions workflow runs lint + unit + integration tests with coverage on every PR as a required merge check, and a single e2e test drives the real checkout flow end-to-end before deploy — giving you the classic pyramid: many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, one high-value e2e.

qa.q: Why do you export the Express `app` separately from `server.listen()`, and how does that help testing?
qa.a: Separating them lets tests import the `app` object and pass it directly to supertest (`request(app)`) without binding to a real port. This avoids port conflicts, lets test files run in parallel, and keeps tests fast since no real network socket is opened. The `listen()` call lives in a thin entrypoint that's only used in production/dev.

qa.q: What's the difference between a spy and a mock in Vitest, and when do you use each?
qa.a: A mock (`vi.fn()` or `vi.mock`) replaces an implementation entirely; a spy (`vi.spyOn(obj,'method')`) wraps an existing real method so you can observe calls while, by default, keeping the original behavior. Use a spy when you want the real code to run but still assert it was invoked (e.g. a logger or analytics call); use a full mock when the real behavior is slow, non-deterministic, or has side effects like network/DB access. Always restore spies with `mockRestore`/`restoreAllMocks` so behavior doesn't leak into other tests.

qa.q: How do you decide what to mock versus what to exercise for real?
qa.a: Mock at boundaries you don't own or that are expensive/non-deterministic: third-party APIs, payment gateways, email, network, clock, filesystem. Exercise your own business logic and, where feasible, your database via integration tests, because mocking things you own tends to assert implementation details and makes tests break on harmless refactors. The rule of thumb is to mock I/O and side effects, not your domain logic.

qa.q: What are the trade-offs of the test pyramid (unit vs integration vs e2e)?
qa.a: Unit tests are fast, isolated, and numerous, giving pinpoint feedback but low confidence that components work together. E2e tests exercise the whole stack like a real user, giving the highest confidence but are slow, flaky, and expensive to maintain. Integration tests sit in between (e.g. route + real DB). The pyramid says keep most tests at the fast base and reserve a small number of e2e tests for critical journeys, balancing confidence against speed and flakiness.

qa.q: How do you keep database-backed tests fast and non-flaky?
qa.a: Isolate state so tests don't depend on each other's order: use a dedicated test DB (mongodb-memory-server or a testcontainer), and reset between tests via truncation, dropping collections, or wrapping each test in a transaction that rolls back. Seed exactly the data each test needs in `beforeEach` and clean up in `afterEach`/`afterAll`. Avoid shared mutable global state, and in CI run the DB as a service container so integration tests have a real engine to hit.

qa.q: Why migrate from Jest to Vitest, and what breaks in the migration?
qa.a: Vitest gives native ESM and TypeScript support without babel or ts-jest configuration, faster startup and watch mode via Vite's transform pipeline, and shared config with a Vite-based frontend. The API is largely Jest-compatible, so the main changes are swapping the `jest` global for `vi` (`vi.fn`, `vi.mock`, `vi.spyOn`), moving config into `vitest.config.ts`, and setting `test.environment` (node for backend). Watch out for Jest-specific globals, timer APIs, and module-mock hoisting semantics that differ subtly.

qa.q: What is deep mocking and when is it justified?
qa.a: Deep mocking auto-stubs an entire nested object graph — every property and chained method returns a mock recursively — typically via `mockDeep` from vitest-mock-extended. It's justified for dependencies with fluent/chained APIs or large surface areas (ORM/query builders, complex SDK clients) where hand-stubbing each level is impractical. The downside is brittleness and that it can hide real contract changes, so prefer shallow, explicit mocks for simple collaborators and reserve deep mocks for genuinely deep interfaces.

qa.q: What should a CI pipeline enforce for a tested MERN service?
qa.a: On every push/PR it should install dependencies (with caching), run lint, type-check, and the test suite with coverage, and be configured as a required status check so failing tests block merge. Integration tests should run against a service container (Mongo/Postgres) rather than mocks, and the slower e2e stage can be separated so unit feedback stays fast. Deployment is gated on a green pipeline, ideally with coverage thresholds and separate fast/slow stages to keep developer feedback quick.

bullets.point: Exercises a single unit (function/module) in isolation with all external dependencies (DB, HTTP, clock, other services) mocked or stubbed. Fast, deterministic, and cheap, so it forms the base of the test pyramid. Interview angle: unit tests catch logic/branch bugs early but prove nothing about whether components actually wire together correctly.

Verify that multiple units work together across a real boundary, e.g. route handler + service + real database, catching serialization, query, migration, and wiring bugs that mocks hide. The core trade-off is fidelity vs. speed: they need real infrastructure and run slower, but give far more confidence than unit tests alone.

You need an isolated, production-like environment: a dedicated test database (never dev/prod), applied migrations, deterministic seed data, and env/config that points the app at the test infra. The governing concern is isolation and repeatability so runs don't leak into real data or contaminate each other.

Spin up a disposable database before the suite (a Docker/docker-compose service or Testcontainers), apply schema/migrations, then tear it down after. Ephemeral, containerized DBs give production-parity behavior (real SQL dialect, constraints, transactions) without the shared-state flakiness of a long-lived shared instance.

Wire Vitest via vitest.config with globalSetup and/or setupFiles to bring up infra, run migrations, and expose a ready test app/client before specs execute. globalSetup runs once per whole run (ideal for starting the container/DB), while setupFiles run per worker/file (connections, globals) — mixing them up causes duplicated or missing setup.

Write tests that drive real endpoints (e.g. supertest against the Express app) and assert both the HTTP response and the resulting DB state, not just the status code. Cover happy path, validation/edge cases, and error/failure handling so the test reflects real client behavior end-to-end within the service.

beforeAll runs once per file for expensive one-time setup (connect DB, boot server); beforeEach runs before every test for cheap idempotent resets (truncate tables, reseed) that guarantee isolation, with afterEach/afterAll for cleanup. Interview angle: putting per-test reset in beforeEach is what prevents order-dependent, flaky failures.

Run the full suite automatically on every push/PR: the pipeline provisions a DB service, applies migrations, runs unit + integration + E2E, and gates merge/deploy on green. Tests are only trustworthy when they run in CI on a clean environment, not just on someone's laptop; this is also where you enforce coverage and prevent 'works on my machine' regressions.

Exercise the entire system through the UI as a real user would — a browser drives the frontend, which calls the real backend and DB. Highest fidelity but slowest and most brittle, so they sit at the top of the pyramid and are kept few, focused on critical user journeys (login, checkout) rather than exhaustive coverage.

A browser-based E2E framework offering real-browser execution, time-travel debugging, automatic waiting/retries, and network stubbing (cy.intercept). Interview angle: contrast with Playwright — Cypress historically ran in-browser with same-origin/single-tab limits and per-test parallelism caveats, while Playwright offers true multi-browser, multi-tab, and faster cross-process parallelism.

example.title: Testing the checkout API of an e-commerce backend
example.body: You own POST /orders in a Node/TypeScript service backed by Postgres. Unit tests cover the pure price/discount/tax logic with mocked inputs so branch bugs surface instantly. Integration tests bring up a real Postgres via Testcontainers (started in Vitest globalSetup), apply migrations, then use supertest to POST a cart and assert both the HTTP 201 body and that an orders row was created and inventory was decremented; beforeAll connects and migrates once, while beforeEach truncates and reseeds so tests are order-independent and non-flaky. GitHub Actions runs unit + integration on every PR with a postgres service container and blocks merge on red. Finally, one Cypress E2E test drives a real browser through add-to-cart to checkout to the confirmation page against a deployed staging build, validating the whole journey — frontend, API, and DB — the way a customer actually experiences it.

qa.q: Explain the test pyramid and where unit, integration, and E2E tests fit.
qa.a: The pyramid prescribes many fast, cheap unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and very few slow, brittle E2E tests at the top. As you move up, fidelity and confidence increase but speed, determinism, and maintainability decrease. The rationale is cost-efficiency: catch most defects cheaply at the bottom and reserve expensive full-system tests for a handful of critical user journeys.

qa.q: Should integration tests use a real database or a mock/in-memory substitute?
qa.a: Prefer a real instance of the same engine (e.g. Postgres via Testcontainers) because the whole point is to catch bugs in real SQL, constraints, transactions, and migrations that mocks and dialect-swapping (like SQLite-for-Postgres) silently hide. In-memory or mocked DBs are faster but give false confidence and let dialect-specific bugs reach production. The trade-off is runtime and setup complexity, which containerization largely neutralizes.

qa.q: How do you keep integration tests isolated and avoid flakiness?
qa.a: Give each run a clean, dedicated database and reset state between tests — typically truncate/reseed or wrap each test in a transaction that rolls back in beforeEach. Avoid shared mutable state, real time (inject a clock), and reliance on test execution order. For parallel workers, isolate by schema/database per worker or serialize DB-touching suites so they don't stomp on each other.

qa.q: In Vitest, what is the difference between globalSetup, setupFiles, beforeAll, and beforeEach?
qa.a: globalSetup runs once for the entire run — ideal for starting a container or DB and exposing teardown. setupFiles run once per test file/worker for things like registering globals or opening a connection. beforeAll runs once per describe/file for expensive per-file setup, and beforeEach runs before every individual test for cheap resets that guarantee isolation. Choosing the wrong scope causes either duplicated expensive setup or leaked state between tests.

qa.q: How do you run integration tests that need a database in CI?
qa.a: Provision the DB as part of the pipeline — a service container (GitHub Actions services:) or Testcontainers started by the test bootstrap — then apply migrations before running the suite against it. Pass connection details via environment variables, run on every PR, and fail the build (block merge) on any failure. Keeping infra ephemeral per run avoids cross-build contamination and matches production behavior.

qa.q: Why are E2E tests flaky, and how do you reduce it?
qa.a: They span network, rendering, async timing, and third-party dependencies, so timing races and environmental noise cause intermittent failures. Reduce it by relying on the framework's auto-waiting/retry and asserting on stable selectors (data-testid) instead of arbitrary sleeps, stubbing unreliable external calls (cy.intercept), seeding state via API rather than clicking through setup, and keeping the suite small and focused on critical paths. Run them against a stable, dedicated environment.

qa.q: Cypress vs Playwright — how would you choose?
qa.a: Cypress has an excellent DX — time-travel debugging, an interactive runner, and auto-retrying assertions — but historically runs inside the browser with same-origin/single-tab constraints and weaker native parallelism. Playwright runs out-of-process, supports true multi-browser (Chromium/Firefox/WebKit), multi-tab/multi-origin, and fast built-in parallelism, making it stronger for broad cross-browser coverage and CI scale. Pick Cypress for polished single-app debugging ergonomics; pick Playwright for cross-browser breadth, parallel throughput, and complex multi-context flows.

qa.q: A feature spans a React form, an API, and a DB write. How do you decide what to test at each layer?
qa.a: Push logic-heavy, branch-rich pieces (validation rules, price calc) into unit tests where they're cheapest to exhaust. Cover the API-plus-DB contract — request in, correct response and persisted state out, including error paths — with integration tests. Reserve one or two E2E tests for the critical end-to-end journey through the UI. The heuristic: test each behavior at the lowest layer that can meaningfully verify it, and don't duplicate the same assertion at multiple layers.

bullets.point: Linting is static analysis: a tool parses source without running it and flags bugs, suspicious constructs (unused vars, unreachable code, floating promises), and anti-patterns. The interview angle is understanding it as a compile-time quality gate distinct from tests (behavior) and formatting (whitespace) — it catches a class of errors before they reach runtime or review.

ESLint is the de-facto pluggable JS/TS linter: it parses code into an AST (espree by default, @typescript-eslint/parser for TS), traverses nodes, and runs configurable rules that register listeners on node types and emit reports, many with autofixers. Know that its power is extensibility — rules, plugins, and shareable configs — and that config comes in legacy `.eslintrc` (extends/overrides) or the newer flat `eslint.config.js` model.

ESLint historically shipped stylistic rules (semi, quotes, indent), but formatting concerns have been deprecated from core and moved to @stylistic; the modern guidance is to NOT format with ESLint and delegate that to Prettier. The interview point is separation of concerns: linting decides code correctness/quality, formatting decides layout, and mixing them causes rule conflicts and slower runs.

The `dbaeumer.vscode-eslint` extension surfaces violations as inline squiggles as you type and can auto-fix on save via `editor.codeActionsOnSave: { source.fixAll.eslint }`. It matters because it shifts feedback left to the editor, but you must still enforce the same rules in CI/pre-commit since not every developer configures their editor identically.

React linting relies on `eslint-plugin-react` (JSX/props correctness), `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` (rules-of-hooks + exhaustive-deps), and often `eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y` for accessibility. The high-value rule to discuss is `exhaustive-deps`, which catches stale-closure bugs in useEffect/useMemo/useCallback dependency arrays — the most common source of subtle React bugs.

Shareable configs bundle opinionated rule sets: Airbnb (strict, comprehensive), StandardJS (zero-config, no semicolons), and framework configs like eslint-config-next; `typescript-eslint` provides the recommended TS presets.
