Know the distinction — a shareable config is consumed via `extends` and sets rules, whereas a plugin ships the rule implementations the config references.

Next.js ships `eslint-config-next` (invoked via `next lint` or its flat-config export), which bundles the React, React-hooks, and a Next-specific plugin plus the `core-web-vitals` ruleset that flags perf/SEO issues like using `` instead of `next/image` or misusing ``. It gives sensible defaults out of the box; the discussion point is layering your own rules on top without fighting the preset.

Prettier is an opinionated formatter that discards your original styling and reprints the AST to a consistent layout, with a deliberately tiny config surface (printWidth, semi, singleQuote, trailingComma). Run it alongside ESLint and use `eslint-config-prettier` to switch off ESLint's conflicting stylistic rules; avoid `eslint-plugin-prettier` in hot paths since running Prettier as a lint rule is markedly slower and noisier.

Git pre-commit hooks run a script before a commit is created; teams manage them with Husky and pair them with lint-staged so ESLint/Prettier run only on staged files rather than the whole repo. The nuance to raise: hooks are a fast local guard but are bypassable (`git commit --no-verify`), so CI must re-run the same checks as the real enforcement boundary.

example.title: Standardizing code quality across a Next.js + TypeScript team monorepo
example.body: You're the SDE-2 setting up tooling for a 12-person Next.js + TypeScript app where PRs are full of formatting noise and recurring useEffect stale-closure bugs. You start from `eslint-config-next` for framework/core-web-vitals rules, layer on `typescript-eslint`'s recommended preset, and enable `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` as an error. You add Prettier for all formatting and wire `eslint-config-prettier` last in the extends chain so ESLint stops fighting Prettier over quotes and semicolons. Developers get instant editor feedback via the VSCode ESLint plugin with fix-on-save. To stop bad code before it's committed, you install Husky + lint-staged so a pre-commit hook runs `eslint --fix` and `prettier --write` only on staged files, keeping commits fast. Finally, because `--no-verify` can skip the hook, your CI pipeline runs `next lint` and `prettier --check` as the authoritative gate that blocks merges — so local hooks are convenience, and CI is enforcement.

qa.q: What's the difference between linting and formatting, and why run both ESLint and Prettier?
qa.a: Linting is static analysis for code correctness and quality — unused variables, floating promises, rules-of-hooks violations — and can be semantically aware. Formatting is purely about layout (indentation, line width, quotes) and doesn't judge correctness. They're separate concerns, so the modern setup lets ESLint handle quality and Prettier own formatting, wiring `eslint-config-prettier` to disable ESLint's stylistic rules that would otherwise conflict with Prettier.

qa.q: How does ESLint work under the hood?
qa.a: ESLint parses source into an AST using a configurable parser (espree by default, @typescript-eslint/parser for TypeScript). It then walks the tree, and each enabled rule subscribes to specific node types (e.g. `CallExpression`, `VariableDeclarator`); when a listener fires and detects a violation it reports a message and optionally provides a fixer that produces text edits. This AST-based design is why rules are precise and why some can autofix while others can't.

qa.q: Explain the react-hooks/exhaustive-deps rule and the risk of disabling it.
qa.a: It statically checks that every reactive value referenced inside useEffect/useMemo/useCallback appears in the dependency array. Missing dependencies cause stale-closure bugs where the callback captures an outdated value and effects don't re-run when they should. Blindly disabling it with an eslint-disable comment usually hides a real bug; the correct fixes are adding the dep, using a functional state updater, or a ref — not silencing the warning.

qa.q: eslint-config-prettier vs eslint-plugin-prettier — which do you use and why?
qa.a: `eslint-config-prettier` is a config that turns OFF ESLint rules that conflict with Prettier; you add it last in the extends chain and run Prettier as a separate step. `eslint-plugin-prettier` runs Prettier as an ESLint rule so formatting issues appear as lint errors. The recommended approach is eslint-config-prettier plus running Prettier separately, because running Prettier through ESLint is slower and floods the lint output with formatting errors.

qa.q: What is the flat config, and how does it differ from the legacy .eslintrc setup?
qa.a: Flat config (`eslint.config.js`) is ESLint's current configuration model where you export an ordered array of config objects, each with `files`, `plugins` (imported as real JS modules), `languageOptions`, and `rules`. It replaces `.eslintrc`'s cascading/`extends` string-resolution and `overrides` with plain JavaScript composition and explicit imports, which is more predictable and removes the magic plugin-name resolution. Flat config is the default in ESLint 9+, so knowing the migration is increasingly expected.

qa.q: How do pre-commit hooks work, why pair them with lint-staged, and why is CI still needed?
qa.a: A pre-commit hook is a script Git runs before creating a commit; tools like Husky make these hooks version-controlled and easy to install. lint-staged runs linters/formatters only against files in the Git staging area, so commits stay fast instead of scanning the whole repo. CI is still the real gate because hooks run locally and can be skipped with `git commit --no-verify` or an unconfigured machine, so the pipeline must re-run the same checks to actually enforce them.

qa.q: What's the difference between a shareable config and a plugin in ESLint?
qa.a: A plugin (e.g. eslint-plugin-react) is a package that implements and exports rules, processors, and sometimes configs. A shareable config (e.g. eslint-config-airbnb) is a package that sets which rules are enabled and at what severity, typically referencing rules from one or more plugins. In short: plugins provide the rules, configs decide how to use them — and many packages ship both.

qa.q: How do you lint TypeScript with ESLint, and what's the performance trade-off?
qa.a: You use `typescript-eslint`, which provides the parser (@typescript-eslint/parser) and rules, plus recommended presets. Basic rules work off the AST alone, but type-aware rules (like no-floating-promises) require `parserOptions.project` so the parser loads the TypeScript type information. That type-checking makes linting significantly slower and more memory-heavy, so the trade-off is enabling type-aware rules only where their bug-catching value justifies the cost, and tuning the tsconfig scope.

bullets.point: Kubernetes (K8s) is an open-source container orchestrator that manages deployment, scaling, networking, and self-healing of containerized workloads across a cluster of machines. It gives you a declarative control plane: you describe desired state, and controllers reconcile actual state toward it. Interview angle: it's an orchestrator, not a container runtime (it drives containerd/CRI-O), and its core value is the reconciliation loop.

Pre-K8s ops meant manually provisioning VMs, SSHing to deploy, hand-rolled bash/Ansible for config, and bespoke scripts for scaling, health checks, and failover. This was error-prone, hard to reproduce, and gave no standard way to bin-pack containers or recover from node failure. Interview angle: understand the pain points (snowflake servers, no self-healing, poor resource utilization) that K8s exists to solve.

With K8s you declare workloads as manifests, and the cluster handles scheduling, rollouts, restarts of dead containers, horizontal scaling, and service discovery automatically. Infrastructure becomes declarative and version-controllable (GitOps). Interview angle: the shift is from imperative 'how' to declarative 'what' plus automatic drift correction.

A cluster = a control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager) plus worker nodes running kubelet + a container runtime. For learning you spin one up with kind, minikube, or k3s; in production you use a managed offering (EKS/GKE/AKS) or kubeadm. Interview angle: know the control-plane vs node split and that etcd is the single source of truth for cluster state.

Everything in K8s is a REST resource exposed by the kube-apiserver; all reads/writes (kubectl, controllers, kubelets) go through it and persist to etcd. Resources are versioned and grouped (e.g. apps/v1, v1) and extensible via CRDs. Interview angle: the API server is the single front door and enforces authn/authz, admission control, and validation before anything is stored.

kubectl is the CLI that translates commands into API-server calls using your kubeconfig context and credentials. Core verbs: apply, get, describe, logs, exec, delete, port-forward. Interview angle: prefer `kubectl apply -f` (declarative) over `kubectl create/run` (imperative) for reproducibility, and know `describe`/`logs` are your first debugging stops.

A Pod is the smallest deployable unit: one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing a network namespace (same IP/localhost) and volumes. You rarely create bare Pods directly because nothing reschedules them if the node dies. Interview angle: Pods are ephemeral and mortal; a bare Pod has no self-healing, which is why you use controllers instead.

Deleting a Pod triggers graceful termination: it's sent SIGTERM, given a terminationGracePeriodSeconds window to clean up, then SIGKILL. A standalone Pod stays gone; a Pod owned by a controller gets recreated. Interview angle: this difference (bare Pod vs controller-owned) demonstrates why self-healing needs a ReplicaSet/Deployment above the Pod.

A manifest is a YAML/JSON declaration of desired state with four key top-level fields: apiVersion, kind, metadata, and spec. Applying it lets the relevant controller reconcile reality to match. Interview angle: manifests are the declarative, Git-versionable contract; `spec` is your intent and `status` (managed by K8s) is observed reality.

A consolidation point to confirm the mental model so far: cluster + API server + kubectl + Pods + manifests. Interview angle: be able to trace a single `kubectl apply` end-to-end from CLI through the API server to etcd and onto a kubelet scheduling a Pod.

A Deployment is a higher-level controller that manages ReplicaSets to provide declarative rollouts, rollbacks, and versioned updates of stateless Pods. Changing the Pod template creates a new ReplicaSet and rolls traffic over gradually (rolling update). Interview angle: this is the object you deploy stateless apps with; it adds rollout strategy and revision history on top of a ReplicaSet.

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running, recreating any that die, using a label selector to track its Pods. Interview angle: you almost never manage ReplicaSets directly - Deployments own them - but understanding it explains where self-healing and replica-count reconciliation actually happen.

When a ReplicaSet is created or a Pod dies, the ReplicaSet controller compares desired vs actual replica count, creates missing Pods via the API server, and the scheduler binds them to nodes where kubelets start them. Interview angle: this is the reconciliation loop in action - watch, diff, act - the pattern behind every K8s controller.

Written as kind: ReplicaSet with replicas, a selector, and a Pod template; applying it makes the controller drive the cluster to that replica count. Interview angle: know that the selector must match the template labels or the API rejects it, and that scaling is just editing `replicas` and re-applying.

kind: Deployment wraps the same replicas/selector/template plus a rollout strategy (RollingUpdate with maxSurge/maxUnavailable, or Recreate). Applying an updated image triggers a controlled rollout you can monitor with `kubectl rollout status` and reverse with `kubectl rollout undo`. Interview angle: be ready to explain maxSurge/maxUnavailable trade-offs for zero-downtime deploys.

Because bare Pods and even ReplicaSets don't give you versioned, zero-downtime rollouts or one-command rollback. Deployments manage successive ReplicaSets so you can update the image, pause/resume, and revert to a known-good revision safely. Interview angle: the answer is rollout orchestration + revision history + rollback, not just replica management.

Pods get ephemeral IPs that change on every reschedule, so you can't address them directly; you need a stable endpoint in front of the replica set. Interview angle: this motivates Services - the problem is that Pod IPs are non-durable and you need a stable virtual IP + load balancing across healthy Pods.

A Service is a stable virtual IP and DNS name that load-balances across a dynamic set of Pods matched by a label selector, backed by Endpoints/EndpointSlices. Types: ClusterIP (internal, default), NodePort (fixed port on every node), LoadBalancer (external). Interview angle: know how a Service decouples clients from ephemeral Pod IPs and that kube-proxy/iptables (or IPVS) implements the routing.

type: LoadBalancer provisions an external cloud load balancer that routes internet traffic to the Service, which then fans out to Pods; it builds on NodePort/ClusterIP underneath. Interview angle: it's one LB (and often one cloud bill) per Service, which is why an Ingress controller is usually preferred to expose many HTTP services behind a single external LB.

Creating a LoadBalancer Service makes the cloud-controller-manager provision an external LB, the Service controller populates EndpointSlices from selector-matched healthy Pods, and kube-proxy programs node routing rules so traffic reaches Pods. Interview angle: trace external request -> cloud LB -> NodePort -> kube-proxy rules -> Pod, and note Endpoints update automatically as Pods come and go.

example.title: Deploying and exposing a stateless REST API on EKS
example.body: You're an SDE-2 shipping a stateless Node.js order-service to a managed cluster (EKS). You write a Deployment manifest (apiVersion: apps/v1, kind: Deployment) with replicas: 3, a label selector app=order-service, a Pod template pointing at your container image, plus liveness/readiness probes and a RollingUpdate strategy (maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0) for zero-downtime deploys. `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml` hits the API server, which persists to etcd; the Deployment controller creates a ReplicaSet, the scheduler places 3 Pods, and kubelets pull the image and start containers. To make it reachable you add a Service (initially ClusterIP for internal callers, then a LoadBalancer for external traffic), whose selector matches app=order-service so the cloud-controller-manager provisions an ELB and EndpointSlices track the 3 healthy Pods. When you push v2, you bump the image tag and re-apply; the Deployment spins up a new ReplicaSet and shifts Pods over one at a time while readiness probes gate traffic. A bad v2 is a one-liner to fix: `kubectl rollout undo`. When a node dies at 2am, the ReplicaSet notices the missing replica and reschedules it, and the Service's endpoints update automatically - no pager, no manual intervention.

qa.q: What's the difference between a Pod, a ReplicaSet, and a Deployment, and which should you deploy with?
qa.a: A Pod is the smallest unit - one or more co-located containers sharing network/storage - but it has no self-healing on its own. A ReplicaSet sits above Pods and guarantees a desired replica count, recreating dead Pods via its label selector. A Deployment sits above ReplicaSets and adds versioned rollouts, rollback, and rollout strategy. You deploy stateless apps with a Deployment; it owns the ReplicaSet for you, so you rarely touch Pods or ReplicaSets directly.

qa.q: Walk through what happens end-to-end when you run `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml`.
qa.a: kubectl authenticates via kubeconfig and sends the manifest to the kube-apiserver, which runs authn/authz, admission controllers, and validation, then persists the object to etcd. The Deployment controller sees the new/changed object, creates or updates a ReplicaSet; the ReplicaSet controller creates Pod objects to hit the desired count. The scheduler assigns each unbound Pod to a node based on resources/affinity, and the kubelet on that node pulls the image via the container runtime and starts the containers, reporting status back to the API server.

qa.q: Why can't clients just talk to Pod IPs directly, and how does a Service solve it?
qa.a: Pod IPs are ephemeral - they change whenever a Pod is rescheduled, scaled, or replaced during a rollout - so hardcoding them breaks constantly. A Service provides a stable virtual IP and DNS name plus load balancing across a dynamic set of Pods selected by labels, backed by EndpointSlices that update automatically as Pods come and go. kube-proxy (via iptables or IPVS) programs each node to route Service traffic to a healthy backing Pod, decoupling clients from Pod churn.

qa.q: Explain a rolling update and how maxSurge/maxUnavailable control it.
qa.a: On a Pod template change, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet and gradually scales it up while scaling the old one down, so both versions coexist briefly. maxSurge is how many extra Pods above desired count can be created during the rollout (throughput of the roll), and maxUnavailable is how many below desired can be down at once (availability floor). For zero-downtime you typically set maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1+; readiness probes gate when new Pods receive traffic, and `kubectl rollout undo` reverts to the previous ReplicaSet.

qa.q: Compare ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer Service types and when you'd use each.
qa.a: ClusterIP (default) exposes the Service only inside the cluster on a virtual IP - use it for internal service-to-service traffic. NodePort opens a fixed port on every node's IP and forwards to the Service - useful for on-prem or as a building block, but awkward for public traffic. LoadBalancer provisions an external cloud load balancer in front of NodePort/ClusterIP - use for exposing a service externally, but since it's typically one LB per Service, an Ingress controller is usually preferred to route many HTTP services behind a single external LB.

qa.q: What is the reconciliation loop and why is it central to Kubernetes?
qa.a: Controllers continuously watch the API server for the desired state (spec) and compare it against observed state (status), then take actions to close the gap - create, delete, or update resources - and repeat forever. This makes K8s declarative and self-healing: if a Pod dies or a node fails, the loop notices the drift and corrects it without human intervention. It's the shared pattern behind Deployments, ReplicaSets, Services, and every CRD-backed operator.

qa.q: What role does etcd play and why does it matter operationally?
qa.a: etcd is the cluster's single source of truth - a consistent, distributed key-value store holding all API objects and their state, and the API server is the only component that talks to it directly. Its consistency (Raft) is what lets controllers reliably reconcile. Operationally it's critical: losing etcd means losing cluster state, so it needs regular backups, an odd-numbered quorum of members, and low-latency disk; etcd health issues surface as a slow or unresponsive control plane.

qa.q: How does a controller-owned Pod behave differently from a bare Pod when it's deleted or its node fails?
qa.a: A bare Pod is mortal: delete it or lose its node and it's gone permanently, because nothing owns it. A Pod owned by a ReplicaSet/Deployment is tracked by that controller's replica count, so on deletion or node failure the controller recreates it (the scheduler places the replacement on a healthy node). This ownership - expressed via ownerReferences and the label selector - is exactly why you use controllers instead of bare Pods for anything you want to survive failures.

bullets.point: Docker Swarm is Docker's native container orchestrator: it turns a pool of Docker Engines into a single logical cluster with built-in service discovery, load balancing, rolling updates, and desired-state reconciliation. Interview angle: know why it exists (simpler than Kubernetes, ships with Docker, uses the same CLI/Compose files) and its trade-off — lower operational overhead but a smaller ecosystem and less feature depth, which is why most production shops standardize on Kubernetes.

A swarm has manager nodes (maintain cluster state and schedule work) and worker nodes (run tasks); managers use the Raft consensus algorithm to replicate state, so you run an odd number (3 or 5) to tolerate failures while keeping a quorum. Interview angle: quorum math — N managers tolerate (N-1)/2 failures, so 3 managers survive 1 loss, 5 survive 2; losing quorum freezes scheduling (existing containers keep running) until it's restored. Also mention the built-in overlay network, gossip protocol for node membership, and mutual TLS auto-rotation between nodes.

This is the core object model: a service is the declarative desired state (image, replica count, ports, update policy); a task is a single scheduled slot the manager assigns to a node; and a container is the running instance that fulfills that task. Interview angle: tasks are immutable and atomic — a failed task isn't restarted in place, the reconciler kills it and schedules a fresh one to converge back to desired replica count. Know the two service modes: replicated (N copies) vs global (one per node, e.g. for log/monitoring agents).

`docker swarm init --advertise-addr <IP>` bootstraps the first manager and prints a `docker swarm join --token ...` command; run the worker token on the second node to join it. Interview angle: understand join tokens (separate manager vs worker tokens, rotatable via `docker swarm join-token --rotate`), the advertise/listen address distinction for multi-NIC hosts, and required ports — 2377/tcp (cluster management), 7946/tcp+udp (node gossip), 4789/udp (VXLAN overlay data plane).

`docker service create --name web --replicas 3 -p 8080:80 nginx` schedules tasks across nodes; the routing mesh then makes the published port reachable on every node's IP regardless of where the replicas actually run. Interview angle: explain the ingress routing mesh vs host-mode publishing, how `docker service scale`/`docker service update --image` trigger rolling updates (with `--update-parallelism`, `--update-delay`, and rollback on failure), and prefer `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml` for multi-service declarative deploys.

example.title: Rolling out a zero-downtime API update across a small production cluster
example.body: You run a payments API on a 5-node swarm (3 managers spread across availability zones, 2 dedicated workers) defined in a Compose stack deployed via `docker stack deploy`. The `api` service runs 6 replicas published on port 443 through the ingress routing mesh, sitting behind an external load balancer that health-checks every node IP. To ship v2 you run `docker service update --image registry/api:v2 --update-parallelism 2 --update-delay 15s --update-failure-action rollback api`: the reconciler drains and replaces two tasks at a time, waiting for each batch to pass its healthcheck before proceeding, and auto-reverts if a batch fails. Mid-rollout one manager's host dies — because you kept an odd number of managers you still have quorum (2 of 3), so scheduling continues uninterrupted and the lost node's tasks are rescheduled onto healthy nodes. When you later debug an intermittent 502, the usual suspects are a failing container-level HEALTHCHECK marking tasks unhealthy, or a blocked overlay port (4789/udp) breaking the routing mesh between nodes.

qa.q: How does Swarm decide how many manager nodes you should run, and what happens when you lose quorum?
qa.a: Managers replicate cluster state via Raft, which requires a majority (quorum) to commit changes, so you run an odd number to maximize fault tolerance per node — 3 managers tolerate 1 failure, 5 tolerate 2. If you lose quorum (e.g. 2 of 3 managers down), the swarm can no longer schedule, update, or reconcile anything and management API calls fail, but critically the already-running containers on worker nodes keep serving traffic. Recovery means bringing managers back or force-creating a new cluster with `docker swarm init --force-new-cluster` from a surviving manager.

qa.q: Explain the difference between a service, a task, and a container in Swarm.
qa.a: A service is the declarative spec of desired state — which image to run, how many replicas, ports, and update/restart policy. The manager decomposes that into tasks, each of which is a single scheduling unit bound to one node and backed by exactly one container. Tasks are immutable and have a lifecycle that ends terminally; if a container dies, Swarm doesn't restart that task in place — it removes the failed task and creates a new one to reconcile back to the desired replica count.

qa.q: What is the routing mesh and how does ingress publishing differ from host-mode?
qa.a: The routing mesh means a service's published port is exposed on every node in the swarm; a request hitting any node's IP on that port is transparently routed (via IPVS load balancing over the ingress overlay network) to a healthy task, even if that node runs no replica of the service. This is convenient for putting an external LB in front of all nodes, but adds a hop. Host-mode publishing (`--publish mode=host`) instead binds the port only on nodes actually running a task, skipping the mesh — useful for high-throughput or when you want the client IP preserved, at the cost of losing the every-node reachability guarantee.

qa.q: Walk through how a rolling update works and how you'd make it safe.
qa.a: `docker service update` replaces tasks in batches controlled by `--update-parallelism` (how many at once) and `--update-delay` (pause between batches). Combined with a container `HEALTHCHECK`, Swarm waits for new tasks to become healthy before continuing, and `--update-failure-action rollback` (plus `--update-max-failure-ratio`) auto-reverts if too many fail. To make it safe you keep parallelism low, set a realistic delay and healthcheck, run enough replicas that draining a batch doesn't overload the rest, and optionally use `--update-order start-first` so a new task is up before the old one is torn down.

qa.q: Which ports must be open between swarm nodes and what does each carry?
qa.a: Three: 2377/tcp for cluster management (the manager control plane / API), 7946 on both tcp and udp for node-to-node membership and gossip, and 4789/udp for the VXLAN data plane that overlay networks (including the ingress mesh) tunnel over. A very common production bug is that 2377 is open but 4789/udp or 7946 is blocked by a firewall or security group — nodes join fine but cross-node overlay traffic and the routing mesh silently break, producing intermittent connection failures.

qa.q: Replicated vs global services — when do you use each?
qa.a: Replicated (the default) runs a fixed number of tasks that the scheduler spreads across eligible nodes; you use it for stateless app tiers where you scale by count. Global runs exactly one task on every node (present and future) that matches the constraints, which is the right model for per-host agents like log shippers, monitoring/metrics collectors, or a node-level proxy. A key property of global mode is that adding a new node automatically gets a task without you re-scaling.

qa.q: How does Swarm compare to Kubernetes and when would you still choose it?
qa.a: Swarm is built into Docker, uses the same CLI and Compose files, has a gentle learning curve, and gives you TLS, service discovery, load balancing, and rolling updates out of the box with almost no setup. Kubernetes is far more powerful and extensible (CRDs, operators, autoscaling, a huge ecosystem) but much heavier to operate. You'd reasonably pick Swarm for small teams, edge/on-prem deployments, or internal tooling where operational simplicity beats feature depth — though be aware the industry and hiring has consolidated heavily around Kubernetes, and Swarm's future investment is uncertain.

qa.q: How does Swarm handle secrets and configuration, and why is that better than env vars or bind mounts?
qa.a: Swarm has first-class `docker secret` and `docker config` objects: secrets are stored encrypted in the Raft log, sent to only the nodes running tasks that need them over mutual TLS, and mounted into the container as an in-memory tmpfs file at `/run/secrets/<name>` rather than exposed as an environment variable. That's safer than env vars (which leak via `docker inspect`, child processes, and crash dumps) and safer than bind-mounting files (which requires the secret to sit in plaintext on every host's disk). Secrets and configs are also immutable, so rotating one means creating a new object and updating the service to reference it.

bullets.step: Observability pillars: logging, monitoring, alerts, status pages
bullets.point: Logging = discrete timestamped event records, monitoring = continuous metric collection + visualization, alerts = automated notification on threshold breach, status pages = external communication of system health during incidents. Interview angle: articulate the three pillars (logs, metrics, traces), why metrics are cheap-to-aggregate while logs are high-cardinality/expensive, and how status pages cut support load and preserve trust during outages.

bullets.step: Services as the unit of monitoring
bullets.point: A service is a logically deployable unit (microservice/API) that you monitor as a first-class entity with its own golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation). Observability is organized around service boundaries because SLIs/SLOs, ownership, and on-call rotations are defined per service, and cross-service dependencies drive distributed tracing.

A SaaS observability platform that unifies APM, infrastructure, logs, browser, and synthetics on a single telemetry store (NRDB) queried by NRQL. Language agents auto-instrument apps and ship telemetry with minimal code. Interview angle: know agent-based instrumentation vs OpenTelemetry (vendor-neutral), and the consumption pricing model (per-GB ingest + per-user seats) which makes log/metric cardinality a real cost lever.

Everything New Relic observes — application, host, container, DB, queue, Lambda — is an entity with a unique GUID, tags, a health status, and relationships to other entities. The entity-centric model is what lets you pivot from a slow service to the host it runs on to the DB it calls. Interview angle: a consistent tagging strategy (team, env, service) is what makes filtering, grouping, and access control tractable at scale.

Custom, NRQL-backed views combining time-series charts, billboards, and tables across many entities for operational and SLO reporting. They matter for shared situational awareness and for executive/SLO summaries; best practice is dashboards-as-code (Terraform) so they are version-controlled and reproducible rather than hand-built and drift-prone.

An alert condition is a NRQL threshold evaluated over a window; conditions group into policies, and workflows route incidents to notifiers. Key knobs are static vs baseline (anomaly) thresholds, evaluation window, and a for-duration to prevent flapping. Interview angle: prefer symptom-based alerts (user-facing SLO breach) over cause-based ones, and tune thresholds/aggregation to fight alert fatigue.

A SQL-like language over NRDB: SELECT ... FROM EventType WHERE ... FACET ... SINCE ... TIMESERIES. It powers both dashboards and alert conditions. Interview angle: know FACET (group-by), percentile()/rate()/latest(), and that TIMESERIES buckets data into time windows — the same query drives a chart and the alert that watches it.

Per-service transaction traces, throughput, error rate, Apdex, database and external-call timing, and distributed tracing across service hops. It answers 'is this service healthy and where is the time going.' Interview angle: explain Apdex (satisfied/tolerating/frustrated ratio against a target T) and how distributed tracing propagates a trace/span context via headers (e.g. W3C traceparent) to stitch a request across services.

A Node logging library with configurable levels, multiple transports (console, file, HTTP/log-shipper), and JSON output. It matters because machine-parseable structured logs with correlation/trace IDs are what make logs searchable and linkable to traces. Interview angle: use JSON not plaintext, attach request/trace IDs, pick levels deliberately, and avoid synchronous file I/O that blocks the event loop under load.

Log-based metrics aggregate log data into counts/rates (e.g. error-level events per minute via NRQL FACET/rate) to bridge logging and monitoring. Interview angle: it is convenient but ties monitoring cost to log ingest volume and can explode cardinality — for hot, high-frequency signals prefer emitting a purpose-built metric directly rather than reconstructing it from logs.

The value under which 95%/99% of requests complete — the metric that captures worst-case user experience the mean hides. Interview angle: you alert on p99, not average, because a healthy mean can mask a painful tail; and percentiles are NOT averageable across hosts or time buckets, so you must compute them over the raw distribution (e.g. histogram) rather than averaging per-host p99s.

Host, container, and cloud-integration monitoring: CPU, memory, disk, network, and process-level metrics per host. Its value is correlation — linking an app-level slowdown seen in APM to resource saturation (CPU throttling, memory pressure, disk I/O) on the underlying host. Interview angle: this is how you distinguish a code regression from an infra/capacity problem.

Status pages communicate incident state to users/stakeholders (public or internal), while notifiers are the delivery channels — Slack, PagerDuty, email, webhook — wired to alert workflows. Together they close the loop from detection to human response to external communication. Interview angle: route by severity, dedupe/group incidents to avoid paging storms, and keep the status page authoritative during an outage.

bullets.step: Triage workflow: debugging a real problem
bullets.point: The capstone that ties the stack together: alert fires on an SLI breach, you open the dashboard to scope blast radius, drill into an APM distributed trace to find the slow hop, read correlated structured logs by trace ID for the root cause, and check the Infrastructure tab to rule in/out host saturation. Interview angle: be able to narrate this alert-to-root-cause path fluently — it is the practical point of all the preceding tooling.

example.title: Checkout service p99 latency spike during a flash sale
example.body: You own a Node.js checkout API instrumented with New Relic APM and Winston JSON logs (each line carrying the distributed-trace ID). During a flash sale, a baseline-anomaly alert on checkout p99 latency fires and routes to PagerDuty via a notifier workflow. You open the team dashboard (NRQL: SELECT percentile(duration, 95, 99) FROM Transaction WHERE appName = 'checkout' FACET name TIMESERIES) and see p99 jumped from 300ms to 4s while the mean barely moved — a classic tail-latency problem. Drilling into an APM distributed trace shows the time is spent in a downstream inventory service call, and following the trace ID into the correlated logs reveals lock contention on a hot SKU row. The Infrastructure tab confirms the inventory host is CPU-saturated, so it is a capacity + contention issue, not a checkout code regression. You scale the inventory tier, post an update to the public status page, and afterward add a log-derived error-rate metric plus a tighter p99 SLO alert so the next occurrence is caught earlier.

qa.q: What are the three pillars of observability, and when do you reach for each?
qa.a: Metrics are cheap, pre-aggregated numeric time series ideal for dashboards, alerting, and trend/capacity analysis. Logs are high-cardinality discrete events you use for detailed forensic root-cause once you know roughly where to look. Traces stitch a single request across services to localize which hop is slow or failing. In practice you alert on metrics, use a trace to find the culprit service, then read that service's logs (linked by trace ID) for the exact cause.

qa.q: Why alert on p99 rather than average latency, and why can't you average p99 across hosts?
qa.a: The mean is dominated by the bulk of fast requests and hides the painful tail that real users hit, so a healthy average can coexist with many users experiencing multi-second latency; p99 surfaces that worst-case. You cannot average per-host or per-bucket p99 values because percentiles are not linear/additive — the 99th percentile of a combined distribution is not the mean of the parts' 99th percentiles. Correct aggregation requires the underlying distribution, typically merging histograms and then computing the percentile.

qa.q: How does distributed tracing propagate context across services?
qa.a: On the first request a trace ID and a root span ID are generated; each service creates a child span and injects trace context into outbound calls via headers (commonly the W3C traceparent header, or a vendor format). Downstream services extract that context, continue the same trace, and report spans back to the backend, which reassembles them by parent-child relationship into one waterfall. Sampling decisions are also carried in the header so the whole trace is kept or dropped consistently.

qa.q: Static vs baseline (anomaly) alert thresholds — trade-offs, and how do you reduce alert fatigue?
qa.a: Static thresholds are simple and predictable but need constant tuning and fail for workloads with strong daily/weekly seasonality. Baseline/anomaly thresholds adapt to expected patterns and catch relative deviations, but can be noisy during legitimate traffic shifts and are harder to reason about. To cut fatigue, alert on user-facing symptoms (SLO breach) rather than every cause, add a for-duration so transient blips don't page, group related conditions into one incident, and route by severity so only actionable, urgent issues page a human.

qa.q: What is Apdex and how is it computed?
qa.a: Apdex is a normalized 0-1 satisfaction score built around a target response time T. Requests are bucketed as satisfied (<= T), tolerating (> T and <= 4T), or frustrated (> 4T or errored), and the score is (satisfied + tolerating/2) / total. It gives a single user-centric health number that is easier to reason about and alert on than a raw latency distribution, though it is a coarse summary and you still need percentiles for tail detail.

qa.q: Structured logging with Winston: what practices matter, and when should you derive metrics from logs vs emit them directly?
qa.a: Emit JSON not plaintext, attach a correlation/trace ID and consistent fields, choose log levels deliberately, and avoid synchronous file writes that block Node's event loop under load. Deriving metrics from logs (e.g. counting error-level events via NRQL) is convenient and needs no new instrumentation, but it couples monitoring cost to log ingest volume and risks cardinality blowups. For hot, high-frequency signals you want in dashboards/alerts, emit a purpose-built metric directly; reserve log-derived metrics for lower-volume or ad-hoc needs.

qa.q: Agent-based instrumentation vs OpenTelemetry — how do you choose?
qa.a: Vendor agents (like New Relic's) auto-instrument common frameworks with near-zero code and deep out-of-the-box detail, at the cost of vendor lock-in. OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral standard (SDKs + collector) that lets you instrument once and export anywhere, but often needs more setup and may lag agents on some auto-instrumentation. Choose OTel when you want portability, multi-backend flexibility, or already standardize on it; choose the agent for fastest time-to-value and richest single-vendor experience — many teams run OTel and export to New Relic to get both.

qa.q: Walk through how you'd triage a production latency incident using this tooling.
qa.a: Start from the firing alert to confirm which SLI degraded and its blast radius, then open the dashboard to see whether it's global or scoped to certain endpoints/hosts/regions. Pull an APM distributed trace for a slow request to localize the offending hop (DB, external call, or a specific service), then jump to that service's structured logs by trace ID for the concrete error or slow query. Check the Infrastructure tab to decide whether it's code (regression) or capacity (CPU/memory/disk saturation), apply the fix, communicate via the status page, and afterward add or tighten an alert so it's detected earlier next time.

bullets.step: Scope: what scaling in K8s means
bullets.point: Overview of the three scaling axes you get in Kubernetes: horizontal pod scaling (more replicas), vertical scaling (bigger pods), and node/cluster scaling (more machines). The interview angle is knowing these are distinct control loops that interact — HPA adds pods, but pods only schedule if the Cluster Autoscaler has added nodes.

The step title 'Horizontal pod accelerator' means the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — a controller that watches a metric (CPU/memory or custom/external via the metrics API) and adjusts a Deployment/ReplicaSet's replica count. It requires metrics-server (or a custom metrics adapter) to be installed; without a metrics pipeline the HPA reports <unknown> and does nothing.

A deliberately CPU-burnable demo app (e.g. a small HTTP server that does busy work per request, like the classic php-apache image) used to trigger autoscaling under load. Point of choosing this app: it makes CPU utilization move predictably so you can watch the HPA react in real time.

Writing the YAML for a Deployment (pod template, replicas, container image) and a Service to expose it, which are the objects the HPA scales and load-balances across. Interview relevance: the HPA scales the Deployment's replica count, and the Service's endpoints update automatically as pods come and go — you don't manually wire new pods into load balancing.

Driving load (e.g. a busybox wget loop) so measured CPU exceeds the target, then watching replicas increase via `kubectl get hpa` / `kubectl get pods`. Key nuance: scale-up is fast but bounded by stabilization windows and pod readiness — new pods must pass readiness probes before they take traffic, so effective capacity lags the replica count.

desiredReplicas = ceil(currentReplicas * (currentMetricValue / desiredMetricValue)), clamped to [minReplicas, maxReplicas] and with a small tolerance (default 10%) so it doesn't flap on tiny deltas. Interviewers probe this: e.g. 3 replicas at 90% CPU with a 50% target → ceil(3 * 90/50) = ceil(5.4) = 6 replicas. Requests are the denominator for utilization, so wrong requests break the math.

Requests are what the scheduler reserves and what CPU-utilization-based HPA is measured against; limits are the hard ceiling (memory over-limit → OOMKill, CPU over-limit → throttling). The trade-off worth articulating: setting no requests makes HPA CPU% meaningless and lets the scheduler overcommit nodes, while limits set too low cause silent throttling/OOM that looks like an app bug.

A separate controller that adds/removes nodes when pods are Pending due to insufficient capacity (scale-up) or when nodes are underutilized and their pods can be rescheduled (scale-down). It operates on the infra layer via the cloud provider's node groups, and is the necessary counterpart to HPA — HPA can request pods that will sit Pending forever unless the Cluster Autoscaler provisions nodes for them.

End-to-end exercise: deploy the app, install metrics-server, create an HPA, generate load, and observe pods and nodes scale, then scale back down. The takeaway is seeing the full loop — metric → HPA → new pods → (if needed) Cluster Autoscaler → new nodes — and the delays at each hop.

example.title: Autoscaling a checkout service for a flash sale
example.body: You run a stateless checkout API on EKS. Baseline traffic needs 3 pods, but marketing schedules a flash sale expecting a 10x spike. You set CPU requests to 250m and a limit to 500m per pod, then create an HPA targeting 60% CPU utilization with minReplicas=3, maxReplicas=40. When the sale starts, CPU jumps to ~95%; the HPA applies ceil(3 * 95/60) = 5, then keeps stepping up each cycle until pods stall in Pending because the three m5.large nodes are full. The Cluster Autoscaler sees the Pending pods and adds nodes from the node group, the scheduler places the new pods, readiness probes pass, and the Service's endpoints pick them up. The debugging this exercises: why HPA said <unknown> before metrics-server was healthy, why replicas plateaued (node capacity, not the HPA), and why the 500m limit caused throttled tail latency under burst even though average CPU looked fine. After the sale, scale-down is gentler — HPA respects the stabilization window (default 5 min) before removing pods, and the Cluster Autoscaler only drains a node once its pods fit elsewhere.

qa.q: How does the HPA decide how many replicas to run, and what's the exact formula?
qa.a: It uses desiredReplicas = ceil(currentReplicas * (currentMetricValue / desiredMetricValue)), clamped to [minReplicas, maxReplicas]. A tolerance (default 10%) prevents flapping when the ratio is close to 1, and stabilization windows smooth out scale-down. For CPU-utilization targets the denominator is the sum of container CPU requests, which is why the HPA silently misbehaves if requests aren't set.

qa.q: What's the difference between resource requests and limits, and how does each affect scheduling and runtime?
qa.a: Requests are the amount the scheduler reserves on a node and are used for bin-packing and for HPA CPU-utilization math; a pod won't schedule unless a node has its requests free. Limits are the runtime ceiling — exceeding a memory limit triggers an OOMKill, while exceeding a CPU limit causes CFS throttling rather than termination. A common production mistake is a large gap between requests and limits, which lets nodes get overcommitted and causes noisy-neighbor throttling under load.

qa.q: HPA and Cluster Autoscaler — how do they relate, and what happens if you only have one?
qa.a: HPA scales pods (application layer); the Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes (infra layer) by reacting to Pending pods and underutilized nodes. They're complementary control loops: HPA can keep raising the replica count, but those extra pods sit Pending forever if there's no node capacity and no Cluster Autoscaler. Conversely, a Cluster Autoscaler with no HPA just right-sizes nodes for a fixed pod count. You typically want both, plus sane resource requests so the autoscaler can reason about capacity.

qa.q: Your HPA shows TARGET as <unknown> and never scales. How do you debug it?
qa.a: <unknown> means the HPA can't read the metric. First check that metrics-server (or your custom/external metrics adapter) is installed and healthy — `kubectl top pods` should return data. Then confirm the pods have CPU requests defined, since utilization is a percentage of requests and is undefined without them. Also check the HPA's target references the right Deployment and that RBAC allows the controller to read the metrics API.

qa.q: Why can effective capacity lag behind the replica count during a scale-up, and how do you mitigate it?
qa.a: A new pod counts as a replica immediately but doesn't receive traffic until it passes its readiness probe, and the pod itself may wait on image pull, node provisioning (via Cluster Autoscaler), and app warm-up. So there's a multi-stage delay: HPA decision → pod scheduled → node added if needed → readiness. Mitigations include pre-pulling images, tuning readiness probes, keeping a warm buffer via minReplicas, using overprovisioning (pause pods) so nodes are ready ahead of demand, and setting startupProbes for slow-booting apps.

qa.q: What are the trade-offs between scaling on CPU utilization versus custom/external metrics?
qa.a: CPU utilization is easy and built in but is a poor proxy for many workloads — an I/O-bound or queue-consuming service can be saturated while CPU stays low. Custom metrics (e.g. requests-per-second, p99 latency) or external metrics (e.g. SQS queue depth, Kafka lag) scale on what actually reflects load, giving tighter SLO control. The cost is operational complexity: you need a metrics adapter (like Prometheus Adapter or KEDA), and badly chosen or noisy metrics can cause aggressive flapping.

qa.q: How does the HPA avoid thrashing (rapid scale up/down), and what knobs control it?
qa.a: It has a built-in tolerance so small metric deviations don't trigger changes, and behavior policies with stabilization windows — by default scale-down waits 300s and considers the max recommendation over that window, while scale-up is more responsive. In the autoscaling/v2 API you can set `behavior.scaleUp` and `behavior.scaleDown` with policies capping how many pods or what percentage can change per period. This lets you make scale-up aggressive for spikes while keeping scale-down conservative to avoid dropping capacity prematurely.

qa.q: Can HPA and VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) be used together, and what's the conflict?
qa.a: They conflict if both act on the same resource metric: VPA changes pod CPU/memory requests, and HPA scales replicas based on CPU utilization relative to those same requests, so they fight and cause instability. The safe pattern is HPA on CPU/custom metrics with VPA in recommendation-only mode, or HPA on a custom metric (like RPS) while VPA manages CPU/memory requests. VPA also historically required pod restarts to apply new requests, which matters for availability.

bullets.point: The three observability pillars: metrics (cheap, aggregatable numeric time series ideal for alerting/dashboards), logs (high-cardinality event records), and traces (a request's path across services). Interview angle: know RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) for request-driven services and USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for resources, and why metrics are the backbone of alerting while logs/traces are for drill-down.

An open-source, pull-based monitoring system with a multi-dimensional data model (metric name + key/value labels) and its own local TSDB. It scrapes HTTP /metrics endpoints on an interval instead of receiving pushes. Interview angle: pull vs push trade-offs — pull gives target liveness (up==0) and centralized service discovery for free but struggles with short-lived batch jobs (solved via Pushgateway).

Hand-exposing a /metrics endpoint in the Prometheus text exposition format (e.g. http_requests_total 42) before pulling in a client library. It demonstrates the format is just plain text over HTTP — name, optional labels, value, plus # HELP and # TYPE comments. Matters for understanding what client libs generate under the hood and for debugging broken exporters.

Wiring in the official client library (prom-client for Node) which owns a registry, ships default process/runtime metrics, and serializes everything to the exposition format on demand. Why it matters: hand-rolling counters is error-prone under concurrency, and the registry prevents duplicate-metric registration bugs.

A monotonically increasing cumulative value that only goes up or resets to 0 on restart — request totals, errors, bytes processed. You never read the raw number; you apply rate()/increase() in PromQL to derive a per-second rate. Interview angle: counter + rate() is resilient to restarts and scrape gaps because rate() detects and corrects for resets.

Refactoring instrumentation into a dedicated module/middleware so metrics are defined and registered exactly once, not recreated per request (which throws on re-registration). Centralizing also enforces consistent label sets and makes cardinality easier to reason about — scattered ad-hoc metrics are a common source of label drift.

A value that can move both up and down — in-flight requests, queue depth, current memory, active connections. Unlike a counter, you read the instantaneous value directly (often via inc()/dec()). Interview angle: pick a gauge for point-in-time state and a counter for accumulating events; using the wrong type yields meaningless graphs.

Samples observations (e.g. latency) into predefined cumulative buckets, exposing _bucket, _sum, and _count series, enabling server-side quantiles via histogram_quantile(). Interview angle: histogram vs summary — histogram bucket counts are additive so you can aggregate p99 across replicas, whereas summaries compute quantiles client-side and cannot be aggregated. Bucket boundaries should straddle your SLO.

The complete service: a registry, a counter for request totals, a gauge for in-flight requests, and a latency histogram, all served from a single /metrics handler wrapped in middleware. The takeaway is a clean, low-cardinality instrumentation layer that covers the RED signals with minimal per-request overhead.

Running the server against a prometheus.yml whose scrape_configs define job_name, targets, and scrape_interval; Prometheus then discovers and scrapes targets into its TSDB. This is also where relabeling and service discovery live. Matters because misconfigured targets or intervals silently produce no data — the number one 'why is my graph empty' cause.

The query language over instant vectors and range vectors, with label matchers and functions like rate(), sum by (label), and histogram_quantile(). Interview angle: rate() requires a range-vector window (e.g. [5m]), aggregation operators group across labels, and you should be able to write an error-rate or p99-latency query on the spot.

The built-in expression browser/graph tab for ad-hoc PromQL exploration and debugging queries. It is deliberately for investigation, not production dashboards — no persistence, sharing, variables, or rich panels — which is precisely the gap Grafana fills.

A visualization/dashboarding platform that queries data sources (Prometheus, Loki, etc.) and renders shareable, templated dashboards with variables and its own alerting. It decouples visualization from storage. Interview angle: Prometheus stores and queries the data; Grafana only reads and visualizes it — distinct layers of the stack.

Adding a grafana service next to prometheus with ports, a named volume for persistence, and provisioning files for data sources/dashboards. Matters for a reproducible dev stack and for understanding config-as-code provisioning versus fragile manual UI setup that is lost on container recreation.

bullets.step: Add Prometheus as a data source
bullets.point: Pointing Grafana's Prometheus data source at the server URL and then running PromQL from panels. Key gotcha: inside a container, use the compose service name (http://prometheus:9090), not localhost, which resolves to the Grafana container itself. Ideally provision the source as code rather than clicking through the UI.

Defining alert rules on metric expressions that fire only after the condition holds for a duration (for:), routed via Alertmanager (Prometheus) or Grafana alerting to receivers like Slack/PagerDuty. Interview angle: alert on user-facing symptoms (error rate, latency/SLO burn) not causes (high CPU), use for: to prevent flapping, and know Alertmanager's grouping, silencing, and inhibition.

example.title: Instrumenting a checkout API to catch latency and error regressions
example.body: You own a Node checkout service. After a Black Friday incident where p99 latency quietly spiked and nobody noticed until customers complained, you add prom-client instrumentation: a counter http_requests_total{route,method,status}, a gauge http_in_flight_requests, and a histogram http_request_duration_seconds with buckets tuned around your 300ms SLO (crucially normalizing routes to /order/:id so cardinality stays bounded). You expose /metrics, add a Prometheus service in docker-compose scraping it every 15s, and stand up Grafana pointed at Prometheus by its compose hostname (http://prometheus:9090, not localhost). In Grafana you build a RED dashboard: request rate via sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])), an error ratio, and fleet-wide p99 via histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)). Finally you add an alert that fires when the 5-minute error rate exceeds 1% for 10 minutes, routed to Slack via Alertmanager — so the next regression pages you before it pages your customers.

qa.q: Why is Prometheus pull-based, and when do you still need push?
qa.a: Prometheus scrapes targets over HTTP on an interval. Pulling gives target liveness for free (a failed scrape sets up==0), centralizes scrape config and service discovery, and stops clients from overwhelming the server. The weakness is short-lived or batch jobs that exit before being scraped — for those you push to a Pushgateway, which Prometheus then scrapes. Fully push-based systems like StatsD suit ephemeral jobs but lose that built-in health signal.

qa.q: How do you choose between a counter, gauge, and histogram?
qa.a: Use a counter for monotonically increasing event counts (requests, errors) — always consumed via rate(). Use a gauge for values that move both directions (in-flight requests, queue depth, memory). Use a histogram for distributions where you want quantiles or SLO buckets (latency, payload size). Picking a counter where you need current state, or a gauge where you need a rate, produces graphs that are numerically meaningless.

qa.q: Why do you apply rate() to a counter instead of reading its value?
qa.a: A raw counter value is meaningless on its own and resets to zero whenever the process restarts. rate() and increase() compute the per-second change over a range window and automatically treat any decrease as a reset. That makes derived rates robust to restarts and missed scrapes, and lets you sum comparable rates across instances.

qa.q: Histogram vs summary — what's the trade-off?
qa.a: Histograms bucket observations client-side with predefined boundaries and expose cumulative bucket counters; quantiles are computed server-side with histogram_quantile(), and because bucket counts are additive you can aggregate across replicas. Summaries compute quantiles on the client — exact for that instance but impossible to aggregate and more expensive. In distributed systems you almost always want histograms; the price is that quantile accuracy depends on how well your buckets straddle the region of interest.

qa.q: What is label cardinality and why is it dangerous?
qa.a: Cardinality is the number of unique label-value combinations, and each one is a separate time series held in memory and on disk. Putting unbounded values — user IDs, request IDs, raw URLs with query params — into labels causes a cardinality explosion that can OOM Prometheus and slow every query. Keep labels low-cardinality and bounded, e.g. normalize /users/123 to /users/:id.

qa.q: How does histogram_quantile compute a fleet-wide p99?
qa.a: Histograms expose cumulative _bucket{le="..."} counters. To get a p99 across instances you first combine them with sum(rate(..._bucket[5m])) by (le), then wrap that in histogram_quantile(0.99, ...), which linearly interpolates within the bucket where the 99th-percentile rank falls. Accuracy is bounded by the bucket width around that quantile, so you tune bucket boundaries near your SLO target.

qa.q: In docker-compose, why does pointing Grafana at http://localhost:9090 fail?
qa.a: Inside a container, localhost refers to that container's own network namespace, not the host or the Prometheus container, so Grafana connects to itself and fails. Compose services share a network and are addressable by service name, so the data source URL should be http://prometheus:9090. Persist Grafana with a named volume and provision the data source as code so it survives container recreation.

qa.q: What makes a good alerting strategy in Prometheus/Grafana?
qa.a: Alert on user-facing symptoms — error rate, latency/SLO burn, availability — rather than causes like high CPU, which cuts noise and false pages. Use the for: clause so a condition must hold for a duration before firing, preventing flapping on transient spikes. Alertmanager then handles routing, grouping related alerts into one notification, silencing during maintenance, and inhibition (suppressing downstream alerts when a parent alert is already firing).

bullets.point: A walkthrough of the finished product: users browse problems, submit code in multiple languages, the code is compiled and run against hidden test cases in an isolated environment, and a verdict (AC / WA / TLE / MLE / RE / CE) comes back — plus contests and leaderboards. Seeing the end-to-end flow first matters because it frames the whole thing as a distributed compute problem wearing a web-app costume; the hard part is not the CRUD, it's safely running untrusted code at scale.

The load-bearing skills: sandboxed/remote code execution, async job queues (submit → enqueue → worker → verdict), a deterministic judging pipeline, contest and leaderboard modeling, and monorepo structure across platform + setter + worker. Interview angle: name these as the reason the project is resume-worthy — it exercises systems concerns (isolation, backpressure, fairness) that plain full-stack CRUD never touches.

Beyond MVP: real-time verdict streaming (WebSocket/SSE), an Elo/Glicko-style rating system, virtual contests, editorials, plagiarism detection, and partial/subtask scoring. The interview value is scoping discipline — being able to say what is MVP (submit → judge → verdict) versus what is a fast-follow, and defending the cut line under a deadline.

The uncomfortable questions: how do you stop malicious submissions (fork bombs, memory bombs, filesystem/network access, reading other test data), how do you make time limits fair across heterogeneous hardware, and how do you cap compute cost when judging is the expensive part. These adversarial/fairness concerns are exactly what a senior interviewer probes; the MVP demo hides them.

Typical choice: Next.js (React) frontend, a Node/Express (or Next API) backend, Postgres via Prisma for durable state, Redis as the submission queue, and Docker containers as the sandboxed judge/worker. The point is fitting tools to a compute-heavy async workload — a durable relational store for submissions/contests, a fast in-memory broker to decouple the web tier from judging, and containers for per-submission isolation.

The user-facing architecture and the submission lifecycle: the web/API tier writes a Submission row (status=PENDING) and pushes a job to Redis, decoupling the request from execution; judge workers pull jobs, run code in a container, and write back the verdict; the client learns the result via polling or a push channel. Interview angle: the queue is what gives you backpressure, horizontal worker scaling, and a fast HTTP response instead of holding a connection open for seconds.

The problem-authoring side: authors define a problem (statement, constraints, time/memory limits), upload test inputs and expected outputs, and often a boilerplate/driver generator produces language-specific function stubs from a single problem definition. This service is the source of truth the judge trusts — its output format, test-case storage layout, and limit metadata are what the worker reads at judge time, so its correctness directly determines verdict correctness.

Core entities: User, Problem, TestCase (or test data referenced by object store), Submission (code, language, verdict, runtime, memory, timestamps), Contest, ContestParticipant, and a rating/leaderboard model. Discussion points: indexing submissions by (userId, problemId) and (contestId), computing rankings without full-table scans, and whether to store large test data as BLOBs vs in S3-style storage with only pointers in Postgres.

Reading the actual setter implementation: how a problem folder/record is structured, how test cases are parsed and stored, and how the boilerplate generator turns a problem's input/output signature into runnable driver code per language. Understanding this end-to-end matters because a bug here (wrong signature, mismatched I/O parsing) surfaces as bogus WA verdicts that look like platform bugs.

Reading the platform code: the submit endpoint that persists a submission and enqueues the job, the worker that spins up a container with resource limits and captures stdout/exit status/timings, the diff/verdict logic, and how results propagate back to the UI. This is where the async architecture and sandbox config become concrete — trace one submission from button click to rendered verdict.

example.title: Judging a contest submission under a 1-second time limit
example.body: You're an SDE-2 on the judge team. A contestant submits a C++ solution to Problem D during a live round. The Next.js frontend POSTs the code to the API, which writes a Submission row (status=PENDING) and pushes {submissionId, language} onto a Redis queue — the HTTP call returns in ~50ms, and the UI starts polling the submission's status. A judge worker pulls the job, fetches the problem's test cases and limits from the setter's store, wraps the code with the generated boilerplate driver, and runs it inside a fresh Docker container hardened with no network, a read-only filesystem, a memory cgroup cap, a process/pids limit (to kill fork bombs), and a wall-clock timeout slightly above the 1s CPU budget. It streams each test input, diffs stdout against expected output, and records runtime/memory. On the third hidden test the process exceeds 1s, so the worker writes verdict=TLE, and the next poll flips the UI to 'Time Limit Exceeded on test 3.' Later a contestant reports 'my AC solution got TLE' — you debug by checking whether the time limit is measured as CPU time vs wall time, whether a cold container/JIT startup ate into the budget, and whether the judge box was overloaded running too many concurrent containers. That triage — fairness, isolation, and queue saturation — is the real job.

qa.q: How do you safely execute arbitrary untrusted user code, and what specifically stops a malicious submission?
qa.a: Run each submission in a short-lived, isolated sandbox — typically a Docker container (or firecracker/gVisor for stronger isolation) started fresh per submission and destroyed after. Harden it: no network, read-only or ephemeral filesystem, a non-root user, a memory cgroup limit (kills memory bombs → MLE/RE), a pids/process limit (kills fork bombs), dropped Linux capabilities and a seccomp profile to block dangerous syscalls, and a wall-clock timeout as a backstop to the CPU limit. Never run untrusted code in the API process or share a container across submissions, since that leaks state and test data.

qa.q: Why judge submissions through an async queue instead of running the code synchronously in the request handler?
qa.a: Judging takes seconds and is CPU-bound, so doing it inline would tie up a web worker/connection, collapse under a contest's submission spike, and give a terrible client experience. A queue (Redis/SQS/etc.) decouples the tiers: the API returns immediately after persisting PENDING and enqueuing, judge workers scale horizontally and pull at their own pace, and the queue absorbs bursts as backpressure instead of dropping requests. It also makes retries, rate limiting, and priority (e.g., contest vs practice) natural.

qa.q: How do you enforce fair, deterministic time limits across different hardware and languages?
qa.a: Measure CPU time, not just wall-clock, so an overloaded host or scheduler jitter doesn't unfairly TLE a correct solution, and pin/limit concurrency per judge box so containers aren't starved. Normalize by hardware (Codeforces publishes a reference machine) and give slower languages a multiplier or higher limit for the same problem. Warm the runtime where possible to avoid cold-start/JIT costs counting against the budget, and always keep a wall-clock backstop so an infinite loop still gets killed.

qa.q: How would you model submissions and contest leaderboards in the database for efficient ranking?
qa.a: Store each Submission with userId, problemId, contestId, language, verdict, runtimeMs, memoryKb, and createdAt, indexed on (contestId, userId) and (userId, problemId). For rankings, don't scan all submissions on every request — maintain a denormalized standings/score table (per contest: solved count, penalty/time, last-AC timestamp) updated as verdicts land, or use a Redis sorted set keyed by score for live leaderboards. Large test data lives in object storage (S3) with only pointers in Postgres to keep rows small.

qa.q: What does the problem setter's boilerplate/driver generator do, and why decouple it from the languages?
qa.a: The setter defines a problem once — its input/output signature, constraints, and limits — and a generator emits language-specific driver code (stubs plus the I/O harness that reads stdin, calls the user's function, and prints output in the expected format). Decoupling means an author adds a problem or a new language without hand-writing a harness per pair, and the judge trusts one canonical I/O contract. It's a load-bearing correctness surface: a mismatch between the generated parser and the test files shows up as spurious WA verdicts that look like platform bugs.

qa.q: How do you deliver the verdict back to the user in near real-time — polling, SSE, or WebSockets?
qa.a: Polling the submission status endpoint every 1-2s is the simplest and perfectly adequate for MVP — it's stateless and trivially scalable, at the cost of some redundant requests. SSE (server-sent events) is a clean one-way upgrade for pushing status changes without the client hammering the API. WebSockets are warranted when you also want live leaderboard/contest updates and bidirectional traffic; the trade-off is connection state and needing a pub/sub layer (e.g., Redis) so the worker that computed the verdict can notify the web tier holding the socket.

qa.q: How does a rating system like Elo/Glicko work here, and when do you update it?
qa.a: After a rated contest, each participant's rating changes based on their rank versus the field's expected performance: you beat higher-rated users → gain more, lose to lower-rated → drop more, roughly like Elo generalized to a multiplayer ranking (Codeforces uses a custom variant). Update it as a batch job once final standings are frozen (after system tests / anti-cheat), not live during the round, because ratings must reflect the settled result and be recomputable. Store rating history per user so you can display graphs and re-run if a contest is invalidated.

qa.q: A user reports their previously-accepted solution now gets TLE intermittently. How do you triage?
qa.a: First separate submission-level from system-level causes: check whether the limit is measured as CPU vs wall time (wall time makes it sensitive to host load), and whether judge boxes are running too many concurrent containers so submissions starve for CPU. Look at container cold-start/JIT overhead counting toward the budget, and whether the solution is genuinely borderline (near the limit on the largest test). Confirm determinism by re-judging on an idle reference machine; if it passes there, the fix is capacity/concurrency control or moving to CPU-time measurement, not the user's code.

bullets.point: A distributed, append-only commit log used as a durable, high-throughput pub/sub and event-streaming backbone. Unlike a traditional queue, messages are retained (by time/size, not by consumption) and reread by offset, which is why it fits event sourcing, log aggregation, and decoupling services. Interview angle: it's a replicated log, not a message broker that deletes on ack.

Core vocabulary: broker (a Kafka server), topic (named log), partition (the unit of ordering/parallelism), offset (per-partition message index), producer/consumer, consumer group, replica/leader/ISR, and the controller. Being precise here matters because most Kafka trade-offs (ordering, parallelism, delivery guarantees) are stated in terms of partitions and offsets.

Run a broker locally via Docker (or KRaft mode / ZooKeeper-backed in older versions) to develop against a real broker. Practically you expose a bootstrap server (e.g. localhost:9092) and create topics with a chosen partition count. Interview angle: modern Kafka (2.8+/3.x) can run in KRaft mode without ZooKeeper, since the metadata is itself a Raft-managed log.

Use a client like KafkaJS: construct a Kafka instance with the broker list, then create producer/consumer objects, connect them, and send/subscribe. Key point is connections are long-lived and the client handles metadata discovery, leader routing, and retries. Interview angle: producers batch and are async; you must await connect() and handle graceful disconnect on shutdown.

bullets.step: Splitting into producer and consumer scripts
bullets.point: Separate the producing and consuming code into independent processes to mirror real deployments where producers and consumers scale and fail independently. This makes the decoupling concrete: producers don't know or wait for consumers, and consumers can be down and catch up later via retained offsets. It also sets up testing consumer-group behavior with multiple consumer processes.

A consumer group is a set of consumers sharing a groupId; Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one consumer in the group, so parallelism is capped by partition count. Multiple groups each get their own full copy of the stream (independent offsets). Interview angle: to scale a workload you add partitions AND consumers; extra consumers beyond partition count sit idle.

Observing that messages with the same key land on the same partition and are consumed in order, while different partitions are processed concurrently and have no cross-partition ordering. Rebalancing reassigns partitions when consumers join/leave, briefly pausing consumption. Interview angle: ordering is guaranteed only within a partition, never across the topic.

The canonical consumer-to-partition scenarios: (a) consumers < partitions — some consumers own multiple partitions; (b) consumers == partitions — one-to-one, ideal balance; (c) consumers > partitions — surplus consumers are idle. This frames capacity planning and why over-provisioning consumers without enough partitions wastes resources.

How the producer maps a message to a partition: default is hash(key) % partitions, round-robin when no key, or a custom partitioner. The choice trades off ordering (same key => same partition => ordered) against load balance (skewed/hot keys cause partition hotspots). Interview angle: partition count is hard to reduce later and re-keying reshuffles ordering, so choose the key and count deliberately up front.

example.title: Order-processing pipeline for an e-commerce backend
example.body: You build an 'orders' topic with 6 partitions, keyed by customerId, feeding downstream services. The checkout service is a KafkaJS producer that emits OrderPlaced events; keying by customerId guarantees all events for one customer stay ordered on the same partition (so 'placed' is always processed before 'cancelled'). Two consumer groups read the same stream independently: a 'fulfillment' group (6 consumers, one per partition) that reserves inventory and ships, and an 'analytics' group (2 consumers) that aggregates metrics — each group keeps its own offsets, so a slow analytics consumer never blocks fulfillment. When you deploy a 7th fulfillment consumer during a sale, it sits idle because there are only 6 partitions, which teaches you to provision partitions for peak parallelism up front. During a rolling deploy you observe a rebalance pause, and you debug a hot partition when one wholesale customer's huge order volume hashes to a single partition — the classic key-skew problem.

qa.q: Why does Kafka use partitions, and what do they trade off?
qa.a: Partitions are the unit of parallelism and ordering. Splitting a topic into partitions lets many producers and consumers work concurrently and lets data exceed a single machine, since each partition is an independent log that can live on a different broker. The trade-off is that ordering is only guaranteed within a partition, so you sacrifice global ordering for throughput; you recover per-entity ordering by keying related messages to the same partition.

qa.q: How do consumer groups relate to partitions, and what happens if you have more consumers than partitions?
qa.a: Within a single consumer group, each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer, so the group's effective parallelism is min(consumers, partitions). If consumers exceed partitions, the surplus consumers get no assignment and stay idle as hot standbys. Different consumer groups are fully independent — each maintains its own offsets and receives the entire stream — which is how you fan the same data out to multiple applications.

qa.q: What delivery guarantees does Kafka provide, and how do you achieve exactly-once?
qa.a: By default Kafka gives at-least-once (consumer can reprocess after a crash before committing offsets) or at-most-once (commit before processing). Exactly-once within Kafka is achieved with the idempotent producer (dedupes retries via producer id + sequence number) plus transactions that atomically write messages and commit consumer offsets. End-to-end exactly-once to an external system still requires idempotent writes or the transactional outbox pattern, since Kafka can't make an arbitrary sink atomic.

qa.q: How does the default partitioner assign messages, and why can that cause a hot partition?
qa.a: With a key, the default partitioner uses hash(key) % partitionCount so the same key always lands on the same partition (preserving order); with no key it round-robins (or uses sticky batching in newer clients). A hot partition arises when key distribution is skewed — e.g. one tenant or one popular product generates most traffic — so its partition gets far more load than others, bottlenecking the single consumer that owns it. Fixes include a composite/salted key, more partitions, or a custom partitioner.

qa.q: What is a rebalance and why is it disruptive?
qa.a: A rebalance is the redistribution of partitions among a group's consumers when membership changes (join, leave, crash, or timeout). During a classic (eager) rebalance the group stops-the-world: all consumers revoke their partitions and pause processing until reassignment completes, which adds latency and can cause duplicate processing around the boundary. Cooperative/incremental rebalancing and static group membership reduce this by only moving the partitions that must move and avoiding churn on brief restarts.

qa.q: How does Kafka achieve durability and what do replicas, leader, and ISR mean?
qa.a: Each partition has replicas across brokers; one is the leader that handles all reads/writes and the others are followers that replicate it. The ISR (in-sync replica set) is the followers caught up with the leader. Durability is tuned via replication.factor, min.insync.replicas, and producer acks=all — with acks=all a write is acknowledged only after all in-sync replicas have it, so you can tolerate broker failures without data loss, at the cost of higher latency.
