qa.q: Why is choosing partition count up front important, and what makes it hard to change?
qa.a: Partition count caps consumer parallelism and is baked into the key-to-partition mapping. Increasing partitions is possible but changes hash(key) % N, so existing keys get remapped to different partitions and per-key ordering is broken for the transition; decreasing partitions is not supported at all. Because of this you size partitions for anticipated peak throughput early, accepting some over-provisioning rather than reshuffling a live keyed stream later.

qa.q: How does a consumer track progress, and what's the risk of auto-commit?
qa.a: A consumer commits offsets (per partition) to record how far it has read, stored in the internal __consumer_offsets topic. With auto-commit, offsets are committed on a timer regardless of whether processing finished, so a crash between commit and completing work causes message loss (at-most-once) or, depending on timing, reprocessing. For correctness you typically disable auto-commit and commit manually after the work is durably done, accepting at-least-once and making handlers idempotent.

bullets.point: Part 2 moves past raw pods/deployments into how traffic actually reaches your app and how state survives a pod restart: Services, Ingress, Namespaces, ConfigMaps/Secrets, TLS via cert-manager, and Volumes/PersistentVolumes. The through-line is decoupling — networking, config, and storage all get abstracted away from the pod so pods stay stateless and disposable.

Recap of Part 1: Pods are the smallest deployable unit; Deployments manage ReplicaSets which keep N pod replicas running and enable rolling updates/rollbacks. The key mental model to carry forward is that pods are ephemeral and get rescheduled with new IPs, which is precisely why Services and PersistentVolumes need to exist.

Local clusters via minikube or kind (Kubernetes-in-Docker) let you run a full control plane + node on your laptop. Interview angle: local clusters don't have a real cloud load balancer, so `type: LoadBalancer` stays Pending unless you use `minikube tunnel` or MetalLB — a common source of confusion when moving between local and cloud.

Managed offerings (EKS, GKE, AKS) run the control plane for you; you provision node pools (worker VMs) and the cloud controller manager wires `type: LoadBalancer` Services to real cloud LBs and PVCs to cloud disks. The trade-off is you give up control-plane tuning in exchange for not operating etcd/apiserver yourself.

A Service gives a stable virtual IP and DNS name in front of a set of pods selected by labels, load-balancing across them so clients never track individual pod IPs. Types: ClusterIP (internal only, default), NodePort (opens a port on every node), LoadBalancer (provisions a cloud LB). kube-proxy programs iptables/IPVS rules to route the VIP to backing pods.

`type: LoadBalancer` gives you one cloud LB (and one public IP + cost) per service, and Services operate at L4 — no host- or path-based routing, no shared TLS termination. Exposing 10 microservices this way means 10 load balancers; this pain is exactly what Ingress solves.

Ingress is just an API object holding L7 routing rules (host/path → Service). It does nothing on its own — you need an Ingress Controller running in the cluster to actually implement those rules. This separation of the declarative spec from the implementation is a recurring Kubernetes pattern worth naming in interviews.

The controller is the real reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, HAProxy, cloud-native ones) that watches Ingress resources and reconfigures itself to route traffic. It typically sits behind a single LoadBalancer Service, so one LB fans out to many backends — collapsing the per-service-LB cost from the previous step.

Namespaces logically partition one physical cluster into virtual sub-clusters for scoping names, applying RBAC, and enforcing ResourceQuotas/LimitRanges (e.g. dev/staging/prod or per-team). Note that namespaces isolate objects but not the network by default — pods across namespaces can still talk unless you add NetworkPolicies.

Install via Helm chart or manifest; it deploys the controller pods plus a LoadBalancer Service that becomes your cluster's single public entrypoint. Interview nuance: the ingress-nginx project (community) is distinct from NGINX Inc.'s controller — same proxy, different codebase and annotations.

Create an Ingress resource mapping hosts/paths to backend Services (e.g. `api.example.com/users` → users-svc). `pathType` (Prefix vs Exact) and `ingressClassName` (which controller handles it) are the fields people get wrong; rewrite/annotation behavior is controller-specific.

Traefik is an alternative controller with native dynamic config, dashboards, and its own CRDs (IngressRoute, Middleware) beyond the standard Ingress API. The takeaway is that swapping controllers is low-cost if you stick to the standard Ingress object, but you get locked in once you adopt a controller's proprietary CRDs.

Both externalize configuration out of the container image so the same image runs across environments — the 12-factor 'config in the environment' principle. They can be consumed as environment variables or mounted as files; volume mounts have the advantage of hot-reloading on update, whereas env vars are fixed at pod start.

Store non-sensitive key/value config (feature flags, URLs, whole config files) up to ~1MB. Injected via `envFrom`, individual `valueFrom.configMapKeyRef`, or a volume mount. Gotcha: pods do not automatically restart when a ConfigMap changes — env-var consumers need a rollout, and even mounted files can lag by the kubelet sync period.

Same shape as ConfigMaps but for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS keys); values are base64-encoded, which is encoding, NOT encryption. Anyone with `get secret` RBAC or etcd access can read them unless you enable encryption-at-rest (EncryptionConfiguration) or an external KMS/Vault provider — a frequent security-interview point.

Functionally similar APIs; the real differences are intent and handling — Secrets get base64 encoding, can be encrypted at rest, are held in tmpfs when mounted, and are treated more carefully by RBAC and tooling. Rule of thumb: anything you wouldn't paste into a Slack channel goes in a Secret.

cert-manager is a controller that automates X.509 certificate lifecycle — issuing and auto-renewing certs, typically from Let's Encrypt via the ACME protocol (HTTP-01 or DNS-01 challenge). You define an Issuer/ClusterIssuer, annotate the Ingress, and it provisions a TLS Secret the ingress controller uses to terminate HTTPS. Kills manual cert renewal toil.

Baseline for the k8s discussion: Docker offers bind mounts (host path mapped in) and named volumes (Docker-managed storage), both letting data outlive the container. This matters because a container's writable layer is destroyed on removal — the same problem k8s volumes solve at cluster scale.

A k8s Volume is attached to a Pod (not a single container) and is shareable across containers in that pod, with a lifecycle that depends on the volume type. The abstraction spans dozens of backends (emptyDir, hostPath, configMap, CSI cloud disks) behind one `volumes`/`volumeMounts` interface — CSI being the modern plugin standard.

emptyDir is created empty when the pod is scheduled and deleted when the pod is removed — great for scratch space, caches, or sharing files between sidecar containers. It survives container crashes/restarts within the pod but NOT rescheduling to another node, so never use it for data you need to keep.

PV/PVC decouples storage from pods: a PersistentVolume is a cluster storage resource, and a PersistentVolumeClaim is a pod's request for storage of a given size/access mode. The pod references the PVC, k8s binds it to a matching PV — so the pod can die and reschedule while the data (and its backing disk) persists independently.

An admin manually pre-provisions PV objects pointing at existing storage (an NFS export, a pre-created cloud disk); PVCs then bind to whichever PV matches their capacity/accessModes/storageClass. This is operationally heavy and doesn't scale — you must create disks ahead of demand — which motivates dynamic provisioning.

With a StorageClass, creating a PVC triggers a provisioner (CSI driver) to create the backing disk and PV on the fly — no admin pre-work. Key knobs: `reclaimPolicy` (Delete vs Retain — Delete will wipe the cloud disk when the PVC is deleted) and `volumeBindingMode` (Immediate vs WaitForFirstConsumer, which delays binding until a pod is scheduled so the disk lands in the right zone).

example.title: Exposing a 3-tier app on EKS with one load balancer, TLS, and a durable database
example.body: You're deploying a web app on EKS: a React frontend, a Node API, and Postgres, each in its own Deployment inside a `prod` namespace. Instead of three `type: LoadBalancer` Services (three cloud LBs, three IPs, three bills), you give each a ClusterIP Service and install the nginx ingress controller — it gets the single public ELB. One Ingress routes `app.example.com/` to the frontend and `app.example.com/api` to the API. cert-manager, wired to a Let's Encrypt ClusterIssuer, auto-issues the TLS cert into a Secret and nginx terminates HTTPS. The API's non-secret settings (log level, feature flags) live in a ConfigMap; the Postgres DSN and JWT signing key live in a Secret, both injected as env vars. Postgres is the stateful piece: it uses a PVC against a `gp3` StorageClass, so a CSI-provisioned EBS volume is created automatically and, thanks to `WaitForFirstConsumer`, lands in the same AZ as the pod. When you push a new API image and the pod reschedules to another node, the ClusterIP keeps its stable DNS, the Ingress routing is untouched, and Postgres re-attaches its EBS volume — no data loss. A classic debug during this build: your Ingress returns 404 because `pathType`/`ingressClassName` is wrong, or Postgres data 'disappears' because someone used an emptyDir instead of a PVC, or the PV's `reclaimPolicy: Delete` silently deleted the EBS volume when a PVC was recreated.

qa.q: What problem does an Ingress solve that a LoadBalancer Service doesn't, and what actually enforces the rules?
qa.a: A `type: LoadBalancer` Service is L4 and gives you one cloud load balancer (plus IP and cost) per service, with no host- or path-based routing or shared TLS termination. Ingress is an L7 API object that lets many services share a single entrypoint with host/path routing and centralized TLS. Critically, the Ingress object is inert — an Ingress Controller (nginx, Traefik) running in the cluster watches those objects and configures the actual reverse proxy that enforces them.

qa.q: Are Kubernetes Secrets encrypted? How would you actually secure them?
qa.a: No — by default Secret values are only base64-encoded and stored in plaintext in etcd, so anyone with `get secret` RBAC or etcd access can read them. To genuinely secure them you enable encryption-at-rest via an EncryptionConfiguration (ideally backed by a KMS provider), tighten RBAC so few subjects can read Secrets, and often use an external secret store (Vault, cloud Secrets Manager) synced in via a CSI driver or External Secrets Operator. Base64 is for binary-safe transport, not confidentiality.

qa.q: Walk through how a PVC ends up bound to real storage with dynamic provisioning, and the dangerous defaults.
qa.a: A pod references a PVC; the PVC names a StorageClass. With dynamic provisioning, that StorageClass's provisioner (a CSI driver) creates the backing disk and a matching PV on demand, then binds the PVC to it — no admin pre-provisioning. The two settings that bite people: `reclaimPolicy: Delete` (default for many classes) destroys the cloud disk and its data when the PVC is deleted, so use `Retain` for anything precious; and `volumeBindingMode` should be `WaitForFirstConsumer` so the disk is provisioned in the same zone the pod schedules to, avoiding cross-AZ attach failures.

qa.q: If a ConfigMap or Secret changes, do the consuming pods pick it up automatically?
qa.a: It depends on how it's consumed. Values injected as environment variables are read once at container start and never change until the pod is recreated (a rollout). Values mounted as a volume are updated by the kubelet within its sync period, so mounted files eventually reflect the new content — but only if your app watches/re-reads the file; most apps don't, so you still trigger a rollout. A common pattern is hashing the config into a pod annotation so any change forces a rolling restart.

qa.q: What's the difference between emptyDir and a PersistentVolume, and when would you reach for each?
qa.a: emptyDir is an ephemeral volume created when the pod is scheduled and deleted when the pod is removed; it survives container restarts within the pod but not rescheduling to another node — good for scratch space, caches, or sharing files between sidecars. A PersistentVolume (via PVC) is independent of any pod lifecycle: the pod can die and reschedule while the underlying disk and data persist and re-attach. Use PVs for databases, uploads, and anything that must outlive the pod; use emptyDir for disposable working data.

qa.q: Do Namespaces provide security isolation between teams?
qa.a: Only partially. Namespaces isolate the object/name scope and are the boundary for RBAC roles and ResourceQuotas/LimitRanges, so they're great for organizational and resource isolation. But by default they do NOT isolate the network — pods in one namespace can reach Services and pods in another. For real tenant isolation you add NetworkPolicies (and note that even those need a CNI that enforces them), plus separate ServiceAccounts and quotas; strong isolation often means separate clusters.

qa.q: How does cert-manager get and keep a TLS certificate valid, end to end?
qa.a: You define an Issuer or ClusterIssuer (e.g. a Let's Encrypt ACME issuer). When you create a Certificate — or annotate an Ingress with a TLS block — cert-manager requests a cert via ACME, proving domain ownership through an HTTP-01 challenge (served through the ingress) or DNS-01 challenge (creating a TXT record). On success it stores the signed cert and key in a Kubernetes Secret that the ingress controller uses to terminate HTTPS, and it auto-renews before expiry, eliminating manual cert rotation.

qa.q: How does a Service actually route traffic to pods, and how does it stay correct as pods churn?
qa.a: A Service selects pods by label; the endpoints controller continuously maintains an EndpointSlice listing the IPs of ready matching pods. kube-proxy on each node watches those and programs iptables or IPVS rules so the Service's stable ClusterIP is DNAT'd to a backing pod, load-balancing across them. Because the pod set is reconciled from labels and readiness, pods can be added, removed, or rescheduled with new IPs while the Service IP and DNS name stay constant — which is the whole point of the abstraction.

bullets.point: A marketplace that matches buyers and sellers of an asset and records the resulting trades; at its core it is a matching engine that maintains order books and enforces fair ordering (price-time priority). Interview angle: an exchange is not a CRUD app — the defining component is a deterministic in-memory matching engine, and everything else (API, DB, websockets) exists to feed and observe it.

The order book (sorted bids and asks), the best bid/ask, the spread, market depth, and the base/quote asset pair (e.g. SOL/USDC where SOL is base, USDC is quote). These are the shared vocabulary; knowing that a bid is a buy at-or-below a price and an ask is a sell at-or-above lets you reason about how a new order will match against the opposite side.

Market (execute now at best available price, no price guarantee), Limit (execute only at your price or better, no fill guarantee), plus modifiers/variants like Stop-Loss/Stop-Limit, IOC (immediate-or-cancel), FOK (fill-or-kill), and GTC (good-till-cancelled). The interview point is the trade-off axis: execution certainty vs price certainty — market orders take liquidity and risk slippage, limit orders provide liquidity but may never fill.

Maker vs taker (adding liquidity to the book vs removing it, usually with asymmetric fees), liquidity, slippage, spread, ticker, volume, and partial fills. These terms recur in fee models and matching logic; e.g. a market order is always a taker, and knowing maker/taker economics explains why exchanges incentivize resting limit orders.

Order lifecycle: client submits -> API validates and locks funds -> order enters the matching engine -> engine walks the opposite side of the book by price-time priority producing full/partial fills -> unmatched remainder rests on the book -> balances settle and fills/book updates are broadcast. The key insight is fund locking at placement time to prevent double-spend, and that matching is a deterministic sequential pass over sorted price levels.

REST endpoints for the request/response operations: place order, cancel order, get open orders, get order-book depth, get balances, and get trade/ticker history. These are stateless HTTP calls that mutate or read engine/DB state; place/cancel are writes routed to the engine (often via a queue), while depth/balances are reads that can be served from cache or the engine snapshot.

Real-time push channels for data that changes continuously — order-book depth diffs, trade prints, ticker, and per-user fill notifications — because REST polling can't deliver low-latency market updates at scale. Interview angle: clients subscribe to channels (e.g. depth@SOL_USDC), the server pushes deltas rather than full snapshots, and you typically bootstrap with a REST snapshot then apply websocket diffs by sequence number to avoid gaps.

Separation of concerns: stateless API servers accept orders and push them onto a queue (e.g. Redis); a single-threaded matching engine consumes the queue, mutates in-memory order books deterministically, and publishes results to a pub/sub channel; a websocket server fans those out to clients, while a persistence layer (DB + snapshots/event log) records fills and enables recovery. This decouples request ingress from matching, lets you scale API/websocket tiers horizontally, and keeps the engine authoritative and replayable.

Build the HTTP layer (Express/Node) that validates payloads, authenticates the user, serializes the order, and pushes it onto the Redis queue keyed for the engine, then awaits the engine's response (via a response queue or pub/sub) to return synchronously to the client. The practical lessons are request validation, idempotency, and treating the API server as a thin, stateless adapter that never mutates order-book state directly.

example.title: Placing a limit buy on SOL/USDC and watching it fill
example.body: You're an SDE-2 building a crypto exchange. A user submits a limit buy for 10 SOL at 150 USDC. The API server authenticates them, validates the payload, and locks 1,500 USDC of their balance to prevent double-spend, then pushes the order onto a Redis queue instead of touching any order book directly. The single-threaded matching engine pops the order, walks the ask side of the SOL/USDC book by price-time priority, and finds 4 SOL resting at 149 and 6 SOL at 150 — it produces two fills, fully matching the order, updates both users' locked/available balances, and emits the results to a pub/sub channel. The websocket server picks up those messages and pushes a depth diff and trade prints to every subscriber, plus a private fill notification to the buyer, while the persistence layer writes the trades to the DB. When you later debug a report that 'the order book on the frontend drifts from the engine,' the root cause is a client that applied websocket depth diffs without first anchoring to a REST snapshot by sequence number — so it missed an update and its book desynced.

qa.q: Why is the matching engine typically single-threaded and in-memory rather than a multi-threaded service backed by a database transaction per order?
qa.a: Matching must be deterministic and enforce strict price-time priority; a single thread over in-memory sorted books gives you sequential, lock-free, reproducible ordering with microsecond-level operations that a per-order DB transaction can't match. Concurrency would require locking each price level and risks reordering, and RDBMS round-trips add milliseconds. You get durability separately via an append-only event log plus periodic snapshots, so the engine can be rebuilt by replaying events — you trade horizontal scalability of the engine for correctness and speed, then scale by sharding per trading pair.

qa.q: Explain price-time priority and how a new limit order matches against the book.
qa.a: Orders are ranked first by best price (highest bid / lowest ask) and, within the same price level, by arrival time (FIFO). A new buy limit order matches against the lowest asks first, consuming resting quantity in time order until either the order is filled or the next ask price exceeds the order's limit price; any remainder rests on the bid side at its limit price. This guarantees the best-priced and earliest orders execute first, which is the fairness property exchanges are legally and reputationally expected to uphold.

qa.q: What's the difference between a maker and a taker, and why do exchanges charge them differently?
qa.a: A maker posts a resting order that adds liquidity to the book (a limit order that doesn't immediately match); a taker submits an order that removes liquidity by matching against resting orders (any market order, or a marketable limit). Exchanges usually charge takers a higher fee and makers a lower or negative (rebate) fee to incentivize deep, tight books, because liquidity is what attracts traders. This directly shapes order-type economics: a market order is always a taker and pays for immediacy plus slippage.

qa.q: How do you keep the API server and matching engine consistent given they're decoupled by a queue?
qa.a: Treat the engine as the single source of truth and the API server as a stateless adapter: the API locks funds and enqueues an order with a client-supplied idempotency key, then correlates the engine's async response (via a response queue or pub/sub keyed by request id) before replying. Idempotency keys make retries safe against duplicate submission, and because the engine processes the queue sequentially, ordering is well-defined. Balances shown to users should reflect engine-confirmed state, not the API's optimistic view, to avoid drift.

qa.q: Why use websockets for market data instead of REST polling, and how do clients stay in sync?
qa.a: Order books and trades change continuously; polling forces a latency-vs-load trade-off (poll fast and you hammer the server, poll slow and data is stale), while websockets let the server push deltas the instant they happen. Clients bootstrap correctly by fetching a REST depth snapshot with a sequence number, then applying websocket diffs whose sequence continues from it, discarding older diffs and reconnecting/re-snapshotting if they detect a gap. This snapshot-plus-diff pattern is what prevents the desynced-book bug that plagues naive clients.

qa.q: How do you prevent a user from spending the same balance twice across concurrent orders?
qa.a: Lock funds at order-placement time, not at fill time: when a buy limit for 10 SOL at 150 is accepted, move 1,500 USDC from available to locked so it can't back another order. Since the matching engine is the authoritative single-threaded owner of balances (or balance checks happen atomically before enqueue), there's no race — the second order sees reduced available funds and is rejected if insufficient. On cancel or partial fill, the unused locked amount is released back to available.

qa.q: How do you make the in-memory engine durable and recoverable after a crash?
qa.a: Use event sourcing: append every accepted order/cancel/fill to a durable, ordered log before or as it's applied, and periodically write a full snapshot of the order books and balances. On restart you load the latest snapshot and replay the log from that point to reconstruct exact state, because the engine is deterministic. This gives you crash recovery, an audit trail for compliance, and the ability to spin up a hot standby that consumes the same event stream.

qa.q: How would you scale this architecture as trading volume grows?
qa.a: Scale the stateless tiers horizontally — multiple API servers behind a load balancer and multiple websocket servers subscribed to pub/sub — since they hold no authoritative state. The engine is the bottleneck, so you shard by trading pair (one engine instance owns SOL/USDC, another owns BTC/USDC), which parallelizes across markets while keeping each book single-threaded and deterministic. Redis (or Kafka for higher throughput/durability) handles order ingress and result fan-out, and reads like depth/ticker are served from caches or snapshots to keep load off the engines.

bullets.step: Recap: what was built in Part 1
bullets.point: Part 1 built the client side — the Next.js UI, order book / depth view, and the shape of the REST + WebSocket contract the frontend expects. It matters because it fixes the API surface the backend must now satisfy; interviewers use this framing to check you can design a backend against an already-agreed contract rather than in a vacuum.

bullets.step: Scope for today: the backend services
bullets.point: Today's goal is the server side: an API server that accepts orders, an in-memory matching engine, a durable queue between them, a DB persistence path, and a real-time market-data fan-out. The interview angle is decomposition — being able to name the discrete services and their responsibilities before writing code.

The canonical design is: API server LPUSHes orders onto a Redis list, a single-process Engine BRPOPs them, mutates an in-memory order book, matches, then publishes results — to a per-request channel for the API's synchronous response, to a DB queue for persistence, and to market channels for the WS server. This separates the low-latency deterministic matching hot path from slow, scalable, best-effort concerns (DB writes, client fan-out); expect to defend why the engine is single-threaded and in-memory.

bullets.step: Pub/sub for request-reply and market-data fan-out
bullets.point: Redis pub/sub is used two ways: a request/response bridge where the API subscribes to a channel keyed by a unique order id and the engine publishes the result to it, and a fan-out where a WebSocket server subscribes to market channels (depth@MARKET, trade@MARKET) and pushes to browsers. Key point: pub/sub is fire-and-forget with no persistence, so it's right for ephemeral market data but orders must travel via the durable list, not pub/sub.

RedisManager (one connection for pushing/publishing), the Engine's order book, and the SubscriptionManager (channel → sockets map) are singletons so there is exactly one connection and one source of truth for in-memory state per process. Interview angle: know the pitfalls — hidden global state hurts testability, and 'singleton' is per-process, so it does not give you a cluster-wide single instance.

example.title: Placing a limit order and watching the book update in real time
example.body: A user submits a BUY 0.5 BTC @ 60,000 limit order. The Express API assigns a unique id, LPUSHes {order, id} onto the Redis `orders` list, and subscribes to the pub/sub channel `id`. The single Engine process BRPOPs the order, inserts it into the in-memory order book at the 60,000 price level (price-time FIFO), runs matching against the best asks, and produces fills. It publishes the execution result back to channel `id` (the API resolves the pending HTTP response), pushes trade + balance events onto a `db` queue for a DB processor to write to TimescaleDB asynchronously, and publishes the updated depth and trade ticks to `depth@BTC_USDC` / `trade@BTC_USDC`. A WebSocket server subscribed to those channels fans the update out to every connected browser, so the depth chart moves within milliseconds. When you build this, the bugs you actually hit are: the API request hanging because the engine crashed mid-order (needs a timeout + snapshot/replay recovery), and duplicate DB rows on engine restart (needs idempotent, sequenced writes).

qa.q: Why run the matching engine as a single-threaded, in-memory process instead of a multi-threaded service backed by a database?
qa.a: Matching is inherently sequential — price-time priority requires a total order of events — so a single thread eliminates lock contention and race conditions on the book and gives deterministic, replayable behavior. Keeping the book in memory keeps per-order latency in microseconds; a DB round-trip per match would be orders of magnitude slower. The trade-off is a throughput ceiling of one core and a durability problem, which you solve with snapshots plus an event log rather than by adding threads.

qa.q: If the order book lives in memory, how do you make the engine crash-recoverable?
qa.a: Combine periodic snapshots of the full book with a durable, ordered event source. Every order is on the Redis list (durable) before processing, and you snapshot the in-memory state to disk/Redis at intervals; on restart you load the latest snapshot and replay events since that snapshot's sequence number. The key invariants are that no accepted order is lost and that replay is deterministic, which is exactly why the engine is single-threaded and why writes downstream must be idempotent.

qa.q: Why send orders through a Redis list but market data through Redis pub/sub?
qa.a: A list with LPUSH/BRPOP is a durable, at-least-once work queue: messages persist until a consumer pops them, it provides natural backpressure, and it load-balances across consumers — orders must never be silently dropped. Pub/sub is fire-and-forget fan-out with no persistence; if no subscriber is connected the message is gone. That is unacceptable for orders but perfect for depth/trade updates, where subscribers are many, delivery is best-effort, and only the latest state matters.

qa.q: How does a synchronous REST call get a response from an asynchronous engine?
qa.a: The API generates a unique id, subscribes to a pub/sub channel named after that id, then pushes the order onto the queue and holds the HTTP response open (a pending Promise). The engine processes the order and publishes the result to that id-channel; the API's subscriber resolves the Promise and returns the response, then unsubscribes. You must add a timeout so a crashed or slow engine surfaces as a 5xx/timeout instead of a hung connection, and be careful to unsubscribe to avoid channel leaks.

qa.q: Why are RedisManager, the Engine, and SubscriptionManager implemented as singletons, and what are the downsides?
qa.a: They wrap resources and state that must be unique per process: one Redis connection avoids exhausting connections and interleaving issues, one Engine is the single source of truth for the in-memory book, and one SubscriptionManager owns the channel-to-socket map. The downsides are testability (global mutable state is hard to mock/reset) and a common misconception — a singleton is per-process, so running multiple API or WS instances gives you multiple singletons, which is fine for stateless managers but would be catastrophic if you accidentally ran two matching engines against the same market.

qa.q: How do you scale the WebSocket fan-out layer without duplicating or dropping updates?
qa.a: Run multiple stateless WS servers that each subscribe to the relevant Redis channels; Redis handles the fan-out, so horizontal scaling is cheap. Within a server, the SubscriptionManager should subscribe to each Redis channel once and ref-count the connected clients, adding sockets to an existing subscription rather than re-subscribing per user, and unsubscribing only when the last client for that channel disconnects. This avoids duplicate deliveries and leaked subscriptions as users open and close market views.

qa.q: Persisting trades to the database is slow — how do you keep it off the matching hot path safely?
qa.a: The engine emits trade, order, and balance events onto a separate DB queue and moves on; a dedicated DB processor consumes that queue and writes to the time-series/relational store asynchronously. This keeps matching latency independent of disk I/O and lets the persistence layer scale on its own. The costs are eventual consistency (reported balances can briefly lag the in-memory truth) and the need for ordered, idempotent writes — carry a monotonic sequence number so replays or retries after a crash don't double-apply.

bullets.point: Frames why async matters in JS: it's single-threaded with one call stack, so any long-running work must be pushed off the main thread or it freezes everything. The interview angle is understanding that 'async' in JS is about non-blocking scheduling, not multi-threading or parallelism.

A plain function call pushes a frame onto the call stack, runs to completion, and returns before the next line executes. This is the mental baseline: JS executes statements top-to-bottom on a single stack, and nothing else runs until the current function returns.

Synchronous code blocks the single thread — each operation must finish before the next starts, so a slow line stalls the entire program (including UI rendering in the browser). Interviewers probe whether you can identify blocking calls (e.g. fs.readFileSync, a tight loop) and explain their cost.

I/O (disk reads, network calls, DB queries, timers) is slow relative to CPU and is handled outside the JS engine by the OS/libuv, not by the JS thread itself. Doing them synchronously wastes the thread waiting; doing them async lets the thread keep working while the OS completes the I/O.

I/O-bound tasks spend most time waiting on external resources (network/disk) and benefit from async concurrency on one thread; CPU-bound tasks (hashing, image resize, big loops) saturate the core and async does NOT help — they still block the event loop. The trade-off: offload CPU work to worker threads / child processes / a queue, keep the main thread for I/O orchestration.

bullets.step: Doing I/O in the real world
bullets.point: In practice you use async APIs — fs.promises, fetch/axios, DB drivers — so the request thread isn't held while waiting, which is what lets a single Node process handle thousands of concurrent connections. The key insight: a Node server's throughput comes from not blocking on I/O, not from many threads.

Functions are values in JS: they can be passed as arguments, returned, and stored — this is the mechanism that makes callbacks possible. Know the distinction between passing a reference (setTimeout(fn, 100)) and invoking it (setTimeout(fn(), 100)), a classic bug source.

A callback is a function you hand to an async API to be invoked later when the operation completes; the outer call returns immediately and the callback runs on a future tick. Interview depth: Node's error-first callback convention (err, data), and 'callback hell' / inversion of control, which motivated Promises and async/await.

The runtime = call stack + Web/libuv APIs + callback (macrotask) queue + microtask queue, coordinated by the event loop, which only pushes a queued callback when the stack is empty. This is the single most-tested concept: microtasks (Promise .then, queueMicrotask) drain fully before the next macrotask (setTimeout, I/O), which explains ordering surprises.

example.title: An image-upload endpoint on a Node API server
example.body: You build a POST /upload endpoint on an Express/Node service. The handler must (1) read the uploaded file, (2) call an S3 API to store it, (3) write a row to Postgres, then (4) return 200. Steps 1-3 are I/O-bound, so you use async APIs (fs.promises, the AWS SDK, a pg driver) — while each awaits, the single Node thread is free to serve other users' requests, giving high concurrency from one process. Then a product requirement lands: generate a thumbnail. That resize is CPU-bound, and if you run it inline it pins the event loop and every other in-flight request stalls until it finishes. The fix is architectural: keep the I/O on the main thread but offload the CPU work — push a job to a queue (BullMQ) processed by a worker, or run it in a worker_thread — so the event loop stays responsive. Debugging this teaches the whole topic: sync vs async, I/O- vs CPU-bound, callbacks/Promises, and why the event loop is what makes or breaks throughput.

qa.q: JavaScript is single-threaded — so how does it do things concurrently?
qa.a: The JS engine runs your code on one thread with one call stack, but the runtime (browser Web APIs or Node's libuv) handles I/O, timers, and network on separate OS-level threads/mechanisms. When that work completes, its callback is queued and the event loop pushes it onto the stack only when the stack is empty. So concurrency comes from offloading waiting to the environment, not from running JS in parallel.

qa.q: Explain the difference between I/O-bound and CPU-bound work and why it matters in Node.
qa.a: I/O-bound work spends most of its time waiting on external systems (disk, network, DB); the CPU is idle during the wait, so async lets one thread handle many such tasks concurrently. CPU-bound work (hashing, compression, image processing) keeps the core busy and blocks the event loop no matter how you write it — async syntax doesn't help. The practical rule: async is the answer for I/O, but CPU work must be moved off the main thread via worker_threads, child processes, or an external job queue.

qa.q: Predict the output: console.log(1); setTimeout(()=>console.log(2),0); Promise.resolve().then(()=>console.log(3)); console.log(4);
qa.a: Output is 1, 4, 3, 2. Synchronous logs 1 and 4 run first. When the stack clears, the event loop drains the microtask queue fully before any macrotask, so the Promise callback (3) runs before the setTimeout callback (2), even with a 0ms delay. This demonstrates that microtasks always have priority over macrotasks.

qa.q: What is 'callback hell' and what problem beyond readability does it cause?
qa.a: Callback hell is deeply nested callbacks from sequencing dependent async operations, which hurts readability and makes error handling and early returns awkward. The deeper problem is inversion of control: you hand your continuation to someone else's code and must trust it to call your callback exactly once, at the right time, with correct arguments — a callback called twice, never, or synchronously causes subtle bugs. Promises and async/await restore control flow and give a single, composable error channel.

qa.q: What's the difference between setTimeout(fn, 0) and Promise.resolve().then(fn) in terms of when fn runs?
qa.a: Both defer fn, but they land in different queues. Promise.resolve().then(fn) schedules a microtask, which runs as soon as the current synchronous code finishes and before the next macrotask. setTimeout(fn, 0) schedules a macrotask, which runs only after the entire microtask queue is drained. So the Promise callback effectively runs sooner, and a flood of microtasks can starve timers.

qa.q: Why does fs.readFileSync hurt a Node web server but might be fine in a CLI script?
qa.a: readFileSync blocks the single event-loop thread until the disk read completes. In a web server serving concurrent requests, that blocks every other request for the duration, tanking throughput and latency. In a one-shot CLI script or during startup there's nothing else to serve, so blocking is acceptable and the simpler code is fine. The distinction is whether the thread has other work it should be doing.

qa.q: What's the difference between passing a callback as a reference versus invoking it, and why does it bite people?
qa.a: setTimeout(fn, 100) passes the function value to be called later; setTimeout(fn(), 100) calls fn immediately and passes its return value (often undefined) as the 'callback'. The second runs fn synchronously right now and schedules nothing useful. This is a common bug with event handlers and timers, and it stems from functions being first-class values — you must pass the reference, or wrap it in an arrow function if you need to bind arguments.

qa.q: When would you deliberately choose worker_threads or a job queue over async/await in Node?
qa.a: When the work is CPU-bound. async/await only helps when there's I/O waiting to overlap; a heavy computation still monopolizes the single thread and blocks the event loop regardless of async syntax. worker_threads give you true parallelism within the process for CPU work, while an external queue (e.g. BullMQ/SQS + workers) decouples the work entirely, adds durability and retries, and lets you scale processing independently of the request-serving tier.

bullets.point: A habit/motivation prompt, not a technical concept: the point is that fluency in JS comes from daily reps (small programs, dev-tools console experiments) rather than passive reading. Interview angle is indirect — consistent practice is what makes you fast at whiteboard/live-coding rounds where you can't lean on autocomplete.

JS is a high-level, dynamically-typed, single-threaded language executed by an engine (V8, SpiderMonkey) that JIT-compiles source to machine code rather than purely interpreting it. It runs in browsers and on servers (Node) via the same core language (ECMAScript) plus a host-provided runtime (DOM APIs vs Node APIs). Interviewers probe whether you understand the language is separate from its runtime, and that 'single-threaded' refers to the call stack while async I/O is offloaded.

Key properties: dynamic and weak typing (types resolved at runtime, implicit coercion), prototype-based inheritance (objects delegate to a prototype chain, not classes under the hood), first-class functions (functions are values — passed, returned, stored), and a single-threaded event-loop concurrency model. These properties explain most 'gotcha' questions: coercion bugs, closures, `this` binding, and why blocking the main thread freezes everything.

Covers declarations (`var` function-scoped/hoisted-as-undefined vs `let`/`const` block-scoped with temporal dead zone), operators (`===` strict vs `==` coercing, `??` vs `||`), control flow, and function forms (declarations, expressions, arrow functions). The load-bearing interview points are scoping rules, hoisting, and that arrow functions lexically bind `this` and lack their own `arguments`.

Objects, arrays, and functions are reference types: variables hold a pointer, so assignment and function arguments copy the reference, not the value — mutations are shared and `===` compares identity, not structure. Contrast with primitives (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint) which are copied by value and immutable. This is the root of shallow-vs-deep-copy questions, accidental shared-state mutation bugs, and why `{} === {}` is false.

example.title: Debugging a shared-state mutation in a shopping-cart module
example.body: You build a cart service where a default line-item template `const DEFAULT_ITEM = { qty: 1, options: {} }` is spread into each new item: `const item = { ...DEFAULT_ITEM, sku }`. QA reports that setting a gift-wrap option on one product silently applies it to every product added afterward. The bug ties the whole topic together: the spread does a shallow copy, so every item's `options` still points to the same nested object (reference type), and mutating `item.options.giftWrap = true` mutates the shared original. The fix requires understanding value-vs-reference semantics (create a fresh nested object per item, e.g. `structuredClone` or an explicit `{ ...DEFAULT_ITEM, options: {}, sku }`), plus knowing that `===` on the leaked objects would show they're the same identity — exactly the primitives-vs-objects distinction taught in this track.

qa.q: What does it mean that JavaScript is single-threaded, and how does it handle asynchronous work?
qa.a: There is one call stack, so only one piece of JS runs at a time — the language itself has no parallelism on the main thread. Async work (timers, network, I/O) is delegated to the host runtime (browser Web APIs or libuv in Node); when it completes, a callback is queued and the event loop pushes it onto the stack once the stack is empty. This is why a long synchronous loop freezes the UI, and why microtasks (Promises) drain fully before the next macrotask (setTimeout) runs.

qa.q: Explain the difference between == and === and why you'd prefer one.
qa.a: `===` is strict equality: it returns true only if type and value match, with no coercion. `==` performs type coercion before comparing, following non-obvious rules (e.g. `0 == ''` and `null == undefined` are true, but `null == 0` is false). Prefer `===` to avoid coercion surprises; the one common idiomatic use of `==` is `x == null` to check for both null and undefined in a single expression.

qa.q: Contrast var, let, and const in terms of scope and hoisting.
qa.a: `var` is function-scoped and hoisted such that the binding exists (initialized to `undefined`) from the top of the function, which enables buggy access before the assignment line. `let` and `const` are block-scoped and also hoisted, but sit in the temporal dead zone until their declaration executes, so accessing them early throws a ReferenceError. `const` additionally prevents reassignment of the binding — but it does not make the referenced object immutable, so `const obj = {}` still allows `obj.x = 1`.

qa.q: What are reference types vs primitives, and what breaks when engineers forget the difference?
qa.a: Primitives (number, string, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint) are copied by value and are immutable. Objects, arrays, and functions are reference types: the variable holds a pointer, so assignment or passing to a function copies the reference, and two variables can mutate the same underlying object. Forgetting this causes shared-state bugs (mutating a 'copy' that's actually the original), broken equality checks (`[1] !== [1]` because identity differs), and shallow-copy leaks where nested objects are still shared after a spread.

qa.q: How does prototypal inheritance actually work in JS?
qa.a: Every object has an internal link (`[[Prototype]]`, exposed via `Object.getPrototypeOf` / `__proto__`) to another object. Property lookups that miss on the object walk up this prototype chain until found or the chain ends at `null`. `class` syntax is sugar over this — methods live on `Constructor.prototype`, and instances delegate to it — so there's no classical class copying; it's live delegation, which is why adding a method to a prototype affects all existing instances.

qa.q: Why do arrow functions behave differently with `this`, and when is that a problem?
qa.a: Arrow functions don't bind their own `this`, `arguments`, or `super` — they capture `this` lexically from the enclosing scope at definition time. This is ideal for callbacks (e.g. inside `array.map` or `setTimeout`) where you want the surrounding `this`. It's wrong for object methods that rely on the call-site receiver, or for event handlers where you expect `this` to be the element, and arrow functions also can't be used as constructors.

qa.q: What is type coercion and give an example where implicit coercion causes a real bug.
qa.a: Coercion is JS automatically converting a value from one type to another in an operation. The classic bug is `+` doing string concatenation when one operand is a string: `1 + '1'` yields `'11'`, so summing values pulled from an input field or JSON-as-string produces `'0' + userInput` garbage instead of arithmetic. Another is truthiness — `if (arr.length)` is fine but `if (value)` treats `0`, `''`, and `NaN` as false, which is why `??` (nullish coalescing) exists to fall back only on null/undefined rather than any falsy value.

qa.q: Describe the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy in JS and how to produce each.
qa.a: A shallow copy (`{...obj}`, `Object.assign`, `array.slice`) duplicates the top level but nested objects remain shared references, so mutating a nested field affects both copies. A deep copy recursively clones nested structures so they're fully independent — `structuredClone(obj)` is the modern built-in, while `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj))` works only for JSON-safe data (it drops functions, undefined, symbols, and mangles Dates). Choosing wrong is a frequent source of state-mutation bugs in reducers and caches.

bullets.point: Node is the V8 engine plus libuv (the event loop and thread pool) packaged as a server-side runtime, installed via the official installer, a version manager (nvm/fnm/volta), or a package manager. Interview angle: know why a version manager beats a global install (per-project versions via .nvmrc, no sudo, painless upgrades) and the LTS-vs-Current distinction — pin LTS for production.

You execute scripts with `node file.js`, experiment in the REPL, and pin dependencies through npm/package.json; this is the shift from browser JS (DOM, no filesystem) to a runtime with `fs`, `process`, `require`/`import`, and CommonJS-vs-ESM module systems. Interview angle: explain the difference between the browser and Node globals (`window` vs `global`/`globalThis`), and how `type: module` toggles ESM.

Fundamentals drills: string/array manipulation, loops, and pure functions that take input and return output without side effects. Interview angle: these map directly to the warm-up round — demonstrate clean naming, edge-case handling (empty input, off-by-one), and choosing the right built-in (`map`/`filter`/`reduce`) over manual loops.

Composite problems combining object manipulation, higher-order functions, and small algorithms (counting, grouping, transforming datasets). Interview angle: this is where time/space complexity starts to matter — recognize when a `Map`/`Set` turns an O(n²) nested scan into O(n), and reason about mutation vs immutability.

Problems requiring data-structure design, recursion, or non-trivial state (e.g. an in-memory store, parsing, or a class-based abstraction like a calculator/todo engine). Interview angle: expect to defend your design — encapsulation, invariants, and how you'd extend it — plus handle tricky JS semantics like closures, `this` binding, and reference vs value.

First contact with asynchrony: reading a file or firing a timer, then handling the result with a callback, a Promise, or `async/await`. Interview angle: articulate why Node is single-threaded but non-blocking — the event loop offloads I/O to libuv so the main thread never waits — and why blocking calls (`fs.readFileSync`, tight CPU loops) are harmful in a server.

Coordinating multiple async operations: sequential vs parallel execution, `Promise.all`/`allSettled`/`race`, error propagation through `try/catch` around `await`, and avoiding the callback-pyramid/unhandled-rejection traps. Interview angle: know when to parallelize independent I/O (`Promise.all`) vs when order matters, and how one rejected promise in `all` fails the whole batch while `allSettled` does not.

example.title: Building a small file-processing CLI batch job
example.body: You're an SDE-2 asked to write a Node CLI that reads a directory of JSON order files, validates and transforms each record, enriches them by calling a pricing API, and writes a consolidated report. You start by pinning the runtime (an .nvmrc set to LTS via nvm so teammates and CI match). The synchronous JS core — parsing, validating, grouping orders by customer with a Map — is your 'easy/medium/hard' JS work. The async layer is where it gets real: you read files with fs/promises, fan out the API enrichment calls with Promise.all for the independent requests (parallel, fast), but keep the final write sequential. You wrap awaits in try/catch, switch to Promise.allSettled so one failed pricing lookup doesn't abort the entire batch, and confirm you never used a blocking readFileSync in the hot path so the event loop stays responsive. This one script exercises the entire topic: install, run, pure-JS logic, and both easy and coordinated async.

qa.q: Node is single-threaded — how does it handle thousands of concurrent I/O operations without blocking?
qa.a: The JavaScript executes on one main thread, but I/O is delegated to libuv, which uses the OS async facilities (epoll/kqueue/IOCP) and a small thread pool for operations like filesystem and DNS. When an operation completes, its callback is queued and the event loop picks it up when the call stack is clear. So the thread is never idle waiting on I/O; it's only blocked by synchronous CPU-bound work, which is why you offload heavy computation to worker threads or a separate service.

qa.q: What actually breaks if you call fs.readFileSync in a request handler on a busy server?
qa.a: The synchronous call blocks the single main thread until the disk read finishes, so every other in-flight request — even cheap ones — stalls behind it, tanking throughput and latency. The event loop cannot process queued callbacks during that window. Sync APIs are fine at startup (loading config once) but should never sit in a per-request hot path; use fs.promises or the callback form there.

qa.q: Compare Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, and Promise.race and when you'd pick each.
qa.a: Promise.all runs promises concurrently and resolves with all results, but rejects immediately if any one rejects — use it when you need every operation to succeed and want fail-fast. allSettled always waits for all and returns a status/value/reason per promise — use it for batch jobs where partial success is acceptable and you want to report failures. race settles as soon as the first promise settles (resolve or reject) — useful for timeouts (race a work promise against a delayed reject).

qa.q: CommonJS vs ES Modules in Node — what are the practical differences?
qa.a: CommonJS uses require/module.exports, loads synchronously, and resolves at runtime, so you can require conditionally. ESM uses import/export, is statically analyzable (enabling tree-shaking), loads asynchronously, and is the standard the ecosystem is moving to. You opt into ESM with "type": "module" in package.json or the .mjs extension; interop gotchas include no __dirname in ESM and the fact that you can't require() an ESM-only package — you need dynamic import().

qa.q: Why use a Node version manager like nvm/fnm instead of the system-wide installer?
qa.a: A version manager lets you run different Node versions per project (pinned via .nvmrc) without root, so upgrading one service doesn't break another and CI can match local exactly. It sidesteps EACCES permission issues from installing global packages under a system-owned prefix. The system installer is fine for a single-purpose box, but on a dev machine juggling multiple codebases, per-project pinning prevents 'works on my machine' drift.

qa.q: How does async/await relate to Promises, and what's the risk of awaiting sequentially?
qa.a: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises — an async function always returns a Promise, and await pauses the function until the awaited Promise settles without blocking the event loop. The common mistake is awaiting independent operations one after another (await a; await b), which serializes them needlessly; if they don't depend on each other, kick them off first and await Promise.all([a, b]) to run them concurrently. Also wrap awaits in try/catch, since a rejected awaited Promise throws.

qa.q: What is an unhandled promise rejection and how do you guard against it?
qa.a: It's a rejected Promise with no .catch or surrounding try/catch; in modern Node it emits an unhandledRejection event and by default crashes the process. You guard against it by always handling rejections at the boundary — try/catch around awaits, .catch() on floating promises, and allSettled for batches. A process-level process.on('unhandledRejection') handler is a last-resort safety net for logging, not a substitute for handling errors where they occur.

qa.q: When would you reach for worker threads or clustering in Node?
qa.a: Worker threads are for CPU-bound work (image processing, crypto, large parsing) that would otherwise block the event loop — they run JS in parallel on separate threads with message passing. Clustering (or a process manager like PM2) forks multiple processes to use all CPU cores for handling I/O-bound HTTP load, since one Node process only uses one core for JS. Rule of thumb: worker threads for heavy computation, cluster/multiple processes for scaling concurrent request handling.

bullets.point: The DOM is a live, in-memory tree representation of an HTML document that the browser builds during parsing; each tag, attribute, and text node becomes an object the browser exposes to JavaScript. It matters because it is the contract between JS and rendered UI — the DOM is not the HTML source string, it is a stateful object graph you mutate, and understanding that distinction explains why re-serializing (innerHTML) differs from reading the live tree.

Static HTML is markup shipped fully-formed from the server (or a file) and rendered as-is, with no client-side mutation after parse. The interview angle is understanding its trade-offs: fast first paint, SEO-friendly, cacheable at the CDN — but no interactivity without added JS, which motivates the move to dynamic manipulation.

Dynamic HTML is content the browser generates or mutates at runtime via JavaScript touching the DOM (in response to events, fetches, or timers) rather than being present in the initial payload. The key insight for an SDE-2 is that this shifts work to the client and affects SEO/first-contentful-paint, and that every mutation risks triggering layout/reflow, so batching and minimizing DOM writes matters for performance.

You obtain node references with querySelector/querySelectorAll (CSS-selector based) or the older getElementById/getElementsByClassName/getElementsByTagName. The interview nuance is the difference between a static NodeList (querySelectorAll — a snapshot) and a live HTMLCollection (getElementsBy* — auto-updates as the DOM changes), plus that getElementById is fastest and querySelector returns only the first match.

You change existing nodes by setting textContent/innerHTML, mutating attributes (setAttribute, or properties like .value/.className), or editing .style/classList. The critical distinction is textContent (safe, plain text, no parsing) vs innerHTML (parses markup and is an XSS vector with untrusted input), and that property assignment (el.className) differs from attribute assignment (setAttribute) for form/state properties like value and checked.

Remove nodes with element.remove() (modern) or parent.removeChild(child) (older, returns the detached node). The interview angle is memory/leak awareness: detaching a node does not automatically clear event listeners or references held elsewhere, so a removed subtree still reachable via a JS variable or a listener closure will not be garbage-collected.

Create nodes with document.createElement, populate them, then attach via appendChild/prepend/append or the more flexible insertBefore/insertAdjacentElement. The performance point is that inserting nodes one-by-one into the live tree forces repeated reflows, so you build in a DocumentFragment (or set innerHTML once) and insert in a single operation to reflow only once.

Building real components means composing subtrees — nested elements, attributes, data-* datasets, and event handlers wired before insertion — and often delegating events to a stable ancestor rather than binding per-child. The staff-level takeaway is event delegation (one listener on a container using event.target and closest()) for dynamic lists, plus preferring createElement/textContent over string concatenation to stay XSS-safe and avoid re-parsing existing markup.

example.title: Rendering and mutating a live comments feed
example.body: You are building the comments section of a product page. On load, static server-rendered HTML shows the first page of comments for fast FCP and SEO. Then JS fetches newer comments and appends them dynamically: you createElement each comment, set textContent (never innerHTML, since comment bodies are untrusted user input), and batch them into a DocumentFragment so the list reflows once instead of N times. A single delegated click listener on the <ul> container handles Edit/Delete for every row via event.target.closest('.comment') — so newly added comments work without rebinding. Edit updates the node's textContent in place; Delete calls comment.remove() and you also drop any cached reference to it so it can be garbage-collected. This one feature exercises the whole topic: static vs dynamic rendering, selecting, updating, deleting, adding, and composing complex nodes safely and performantly.

qa.q: What is the difference between the DOM and the HTML source, and between the DOM and what the browser paints?
qa.a: The HTML source is a text string; the browser parses it once to construct the DOM, a live object tree that then holds the document's state — subsequent JS mutations change the DOM, not the original source. The render tree (and pixels painted) is derived from the DOM plus CSSOM after layout, so the DOM can hold nodes that aren't visible (display:none) and the painted result can lag DOM changes until the next reflow/repaint. Practically, reading innerHTML re-serializes the current live DOM, which will differ from the bytes the server sent.

qa.q: querySelectorAll vs getElementsByClassName — what's the difference and when does it bite you?
qa.a: querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList — a snapshot taken at call time — while getElementsByClassName/getElementsByTagName return a live HTMLCollection that updates automatically as the DOM changes. The classic bug is looping over a live collection while removing matching nodes: the collection shrinks under you and you skip elements. Live collections can also be a subtle perf trap because re-reading .length forces the browser to re-query the DOM.

qa.q: textContent vs innerHTML vs innerText — trade-offs?
qa.a: textContent gets/sets raw text without parsing markup, is fastest, and is XSS-safe, but returns text of hidden nodes too. innerHTML parses its input as HTML — powerful for building markup but a direct XSS vector if the string contains untrusted data, and it destroys and recreates the subtree (dropping listeners). innerText is layout-aware (reflects visible, rendered text and triggers reflow to compute), so it's slower and should be avoided when you just want the text content.

qa.q: Why can naively adding many elements in a loop hurt performance, and how do you fix it?
qa.a: Each insertion into the live tree can invalidate layout, and interleaving reads (offsetHeight, getBoundingClientRect) with writes forces synchronous layout thrashing where the browser reflows repeatedly. The fix is to batch: build the subtree in a DocumentFragment or a detached parent and insert once, so only one reflow occurs; alternatively construct an innerHTML string and assign it in a single write. Also separate all DOM reads from writes to avoid forced synchronous reflow.

qa.q: What is event delegation and why is it important for dynamic DOM content?
qa.a: Event delegation attaches a single listener to a stable ancestor and uses event bubbling with event.target/closest() to handle events from many descendants. It matters because dynamically added children automatically work without rebinding listeners, it reduces memory from thousands of individual handlers, and it survives re-renders. The caveats are that it only works for events that bubble (focus/blur don't, though focusin/focusout do) and you must guard target matching so unrelated inner elements don't trigger the handler.

qa.q: Does removing an element from the DOM free its memory? What causes DOM-related memory leaks?
qa.a: remove()/removeChild detaches the node from the tree, but the memory is only reclaimed once nothing references it. Leaks happen when a JS variable, a closure inside an event listener, or an external structure (a Map/array cache) still holds the detached node — it stays reachable and uncollectable, along with its whole subtree. Detached-node leaks are common with SPAs; mitigate by nulling references, using event delegation instead of per-node listeners, and leveraging removeEventListener or AbortController for cleanup.

qa.q: Attribute vs property: what's the difference between setAttribute('value', x) and element.value = x?
qa.a: Attributes are the initial values parsed from HTML markup; properties are the live state on the DOM object. For form controls they diverge: the value attribute reflects the default/initial value (what a form reset restores), while the .value property holds the current user-edited value. So setAttribute('value',...) won't visibly update an input the user has typed into, and reading getAttribute('value') won't return the user's current text — you need the .value property. Similar divergence applies to checked, and some like id stay in sync.

qa.q: Why is directly injecting user input via innerHTML dangerous, and what are safer alternatives?
qa.a: innerHTML parses the string as HTML, so attacker-controlled input can inject

bullets.point: A bank is a centralized trusted intermediary that owns the ledger and can reverse or block entries; a blockchain is a decentralized append-only ledger where no single party is trusted and correctness is enforced by cryptography plus consensus. The interview angle is recognizing that public-key crypto is what replaces the institution: ownership and authorization become 'can you produce a valid signature' rather than 'is your name in the bank's database'.

A bit is a binary digit and a byte is 8 bits; keys, hashes, and addresses are just fixed-length byte arrays (e.g., a 256-bit private key is 32 bytes). This matters because entropy, key strength, and address length are all measured in bits/bytes, and getting sizes wrong (leading-zero bytes, endianness) is a classic source of wallet bugs.

Encoding maps raw bytes to a printable text representation and is fully reversible with no secret — hex, base64, Base58Check (Bitcoin), and 0x-hex (Ethereum) are common. The interview trap is conflating encoding with encryption or hashing: encoding provides zero security, and Base58/Base58Check exist mainly to drop visually ambiguous characters and add a checksum to catch typos.

Hashing (SHA-256, Keccak-256) is a one-way, deterministic, fixed-size digest used for integrity, commitments, and deriving addresses; encryption is reversible using a key and is used for confidentiality. Interviewers probe this because people say 'encrypt the password' when they mean 'hash it' — hashes can't be decrypted, and preimage/collision resistance is the property that matters.

Asymmetric crypto uses a mathematically linked keypair: one key does an operation the other reverses, so you can encrypt-to-public/decrypt-with-private, or sign-with-private/verify-with-public. It solves the key-distribution problem of symmetric crypto; note that blockchains use it almost entirely for digital signatures (authorization/authentication), not for encrypting transaction contents.

The private key is a large random number; the public key is derived by elliptic-curve point multiplication on secp256k1 (public = privateKey · G). This is a one-way function due to the elliptic-curve discrete log problem, so you can safely publish the public key/address, and interviewers expect you to know the derivation direction is irreversible and that address = hash(public key).

bullets.step: How transactions work on the blockchain
bullets.point: A sender constructs a transaction, signs its hash with their private key (ECDSA on secp256k1), and broadcasts it; nodes recover/verify the signature against the sender's public key/address and check the nonce and balance before including it in a block. The key insight is that the private key never leaves the wallet — the signature proves authorization, and the nonce prevents replay/double-spend.

An HD wallet (BIP32/39/44) generates an entire tree of keypairs deterministically from a single seed, itself represented as a human-readable mnemonic phrase, using derivation paths like m/44'/60'/0'/0/0. This means one backup restores every address, you can generate a fresh address per transaction for privacy, and hardened vs non-hardened derivation is a common deep-dive question.

In practice: gather secure entropy → encode as a BIP39 mnemonic → stretch to a seed (PBKDF2) → compute the BIP32 master key → derive child keys along a path → hash the public key into an address. Libraries like ethers.js or bitcoinjs do this, but SDE-2s should know the pipeline so they understand what a seed phrase actually protects and why entropy quality is critical.

Applied exercises to cement the pipeline — generate a keypair, derive an address, sign and verify a message, and reconstruct keys from a mnemonic. The point is to move from theory to being able to debug real key/signature/encoding mismatches.

example.title: Building 'Sign-in with Ethereum' (wallet-based auth) for a web app
example.body: Your team wants passwordless login: instead of storing password hashes, users authenticate by proving they control a wallet address. The flow ties the whole topic together. The backend issues a random nonce challenge; the frontend asks the user's HD wallet (a keypair derived from their BIP39 seed at path m/44'/60'/0'/0/0) to sign the message with its secp256k1 private key. The server receives the signature (hex-encoded), recovers the public key, hashes it to an address, and checks it matches the claimed account — no private key ever crosses the wire, and no shared secret is stored. Along the way you hit every concept: you debug an 'invalid signature' bug caused by encoding the message as UTF-8 vs hex bytes (encodings), you realize you must hash-then-sign not encrypt (hashing vs encryption), you add the nonce to prevent replay (transaction/signature semantics), and you explain to the team why compromising the DB leaks nothing because there are no secrets to steal — only public addresses. This is exactly the centralized-trust-to-cryptographic-trust shift from the Banks vs Blockchains framing, applied to plain auth.

qa.q: Why do blockchains use asymmetric cryptography for signatures rather than encrypting transactions?
qa.a: The problem being solved is authorization and integrity, not confidentiality — the network must publicly verify that the rightful owner approved a transaction. A digital signature lets anyone verify authenticity using the public key while only the private-key holder could have produced it, and the transaction data stays public so every node can validate balances and consensus. Encrypting contents would defeat the whole point of a transparent, independently verifiable ledger.

qa.q: Explain the relationship between a private key, public key, and address, and why you can't work backwards.
qa.a: The private key is a random 256-bit number; the public key is derived via elliptic-curve point multiplication (public = priv · G on secp256k1); the address is typically a hash of the public key (e.g., Keccak-256 then truncate for Ethereum). Working backwards is infeasible: recovering the private key from the public key requires solving the elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem, and recovering the public key from an address requires inverting a cryptographic hash. This one-way chain is why publishing your address or public key is safe.

qa.q: What is the difference between hashing and encryption, and where does each appear in a wallet?
qa.a: Hashing is a one-way deterministic function with fixed-size output used for integrity and derivation — it can't be reversed. Encryption is reversible using a key and provides confidentiality. In a wallet, hashing derives addresses from public keys, hashes transactions before signing, and stretches mnemonics into seeds (PBKDF2); encryption typically only appears when the keystore file itself is encrypted at rest with the user's password. Calling address derivation 'encryption' is a red flag in an interview.

qa.q: What does an HD wallet give you over generating independent keypairs, and what's the risk model?
qa.a: An HD wallet derives an unbounded tree of keys deterministically from one seed (BIP32/39/44), so a single mnemonic backs up every current and future address, and you can hand out a fresh receiving address per transaction to improve privacy. The trade-off is blast radius: the seed is a single point of failure — whoever has the mnemonic controls all derived accounts forever. A subtle risk is that leaking an extended public key (xpub) plus one non-hardened child private key can expose the parent, which is why the standard uses hardened derivation for account-level nodes.

qa.q: Walk through how a transaction is authorized and why a nonce is needed.
qa.a: The sender builds the transaction, computes its hash, and signs the hash with their private key using ECDSA; the signature and transaction are broadcast. Validating nodes recover the signer's public key from the signature, derive the address, and confirm it owns the funds and that the included nonce is the next expected value. The nonce (a per-account monotonically increasing counter) prevents replay and double-spending — without it, a valid signed transaction could be rebroadcast repeatedly.

qa.q: A signature verification is failing in your app even though the key is correct. How do you debug it?
qa.a: Almost always it's a byte/encoding or hashing mismatch, not the key. Check that both sides agree on the exact message bytes (raw bytes vs UTF-8 vs hex-decoded), that any prefix/domain-separation (like Ethereum's personal_sign prefix or EIP-712 typed-data hashing) is applied identically, and that the same hash function is used before signing. Also verify signature format (r,s,v vs DER), the recovery id/chain id, and that leading-zero bytes weren't stripped from the key or hash.

qa.q: Why is encoding (hex/base58) not a security mechanism, and why does Bitcoin use Base58Check?
qa.a: Encoding is a reversible, keyless representation of bytes as text — anyone can decode it, so it provides zero confidentiality or integrity by itself. Base58Check is chosen for addresses because it removes visually ambiguous characters (0/O, I/l) to reduce transcription errors and appends a checksum so a mistyped address fails validation instead of sending funds into a black hole. It's about human-safety and typo detection, not cryptographic protection.

qa.q: How much entropy does a private key or mnemonic need, and why does it matter?
qa.a: A secp256k1 private key has ~256 bits of entropy, and BIP39 mnemonics encode 128–256 bits (12–24 words) plus a checksum. High entropy is what makes brute-forcing or guessing keys computationally infeasible across the entire address space. The practical failure mode is weak randomness — using a non-CSPRNG, a predictable seed, or low entropy — which has led to real fund thefts; key generation must use a cryptographically secure random source.

bullets.point: Non-technical housekeeping and acknowledgements; no engineering content. Skip in revision — nothing here affects how you reason about async code.

ES6 `class` is syntactic sugar over JS's prototype-based inheritance: `constructor`, instance fields, and methods that actually live on `Class.prototype`. It matters here because `Promise` is itself a class you instantiate with `new`, and understanding `this`-binding pitfalls (methods lose `this` when passed as bare callbacks) is a recurring interview trap.

`extends` sets up the prototype chain and `super()` calls the parent constructor / `super.method()` calls the overridden parent method. Interview angle: explain that `extends` links prototypes (not a copy), method resolution walks the chain, and you must call `super()` before touching `this` in a subclass constructor.

Covers static methods (called on the class, not instances — e.g. `Promise.all`, `Promise.resolve`), getters/setters, and private fields (`#field`). Knowing static vs instance members clarifies why `Promise.resolve()` is static while `.then()` is on the instance.

A Promise is an object representing the eventual result of an async operation with three states — pending, fulfilled, rejected — that settle exactly once and are then immutable. You construct it with an executor `(resolve, reject) => {}` and consume it with `.then`/`.catch`/`.finally`; its callbacks run on the microtask queue, which drains before the next macrotask (e.g. `setTimeout`). This is the core abstraction the whole topic builds on.

Deeply nested callbacks ("pyramid of doom") for sequential async work — hard to read, hard to error-handle, and it cedes control to the callee (inversion of control). Promises fix this by flattening nesting into a linear `.then` chain with a single terminal `.catch`, and async/await flattens it further into straight-line code.

`await` suspends an `async` function until the awaited promise settles, returning its fulfilled value or throwing its rejection reason, so you write async logic as if it were synchronous and use normal `try/catch`. It's sugar over `.then` chains; the key interview point is that `await` in a loop serializes requests — use `Promise.all` when the operations are independent.

Any function marked `async` always returns a Promise (a returned value is auto-wrapped, a `throw` becomes a rejection). You also 'promisify' legacy callback APIs by wrapping them in `new Promise((resolve, reject) => …)` and calling resolve/reject inside the callback — the standard way to bridge old code into async/await.

bullets.step: Err-first callback vs rejects in promises
bullets.point: Node's classic convention is `callback(err, data)` where you must inspect `err` on every call, versus Promises which surface failures through the rejection channel handled by `.catch`/`try-catch`. The trade-off: err-first is easy to ignore silently and doesn't compose, whereas promise rejections propagate down the chain automatically and can be caught in one place.

Hands-on practice: promisify a callback API, chain vs parallelize with `Promise.all`, and add proper error handling. The point is to internalize sequencing, parallelism, and rejection propagation by writing them, not just reading.

example.title: Order-checkout service in a Node backend
example.body: You're building the `placeOrder(userId, cart)` handler for an e-commerce service. It must: (1) load the user and validate each cart item's inventory — independent reads, so you run them concurrently with `await Promise.all([...])` instead of awaiting them one-by-one in a loop (which would serialize the round-trips); (2) charge the payment provider and create the order row — these are sequential because the order needs the charge ID; (3) fire a confirmation SMS through a legacy library that only exposes an err-first callback `sms.send(to, body, (err, res) => …)`, which you wrap once in a `sendSms` helper that returns `new Promise((resolve, reject) => …)` so it slots into your async/await flow. The whole handler is one `async` function wrapped in `try/catch`: a rejected payment throws into the `catch`, where you roll back and return a 402, and the non-critical SMS is wrapped in its own `Promise.allSettled` (or an inner try/catch) so a failed text doesn't fail the paid order. This one function exercises classes (`new Promise`, `Promise.all`), rejection propagation, sequential-vs-parallel awaiting, and bridging an err-first callback into promises.
