qa.q: What are the three states of a Promise, and what does it mean that a promise is 'settled'?
qa.a: A promise is `pending` until it either fulfills (with a value) or rejects (with a reason). 'Settled' means it has moved to fulfilled or rejected; this transition happens exactly once and is immutable — calling `resolve` or `reject` again is a no-op. This is why you can safely attach `.then` handlers even after a promise has already settled: they'll still fire on the microtask queue with the stored result.

qa.q: Compare Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, Promise.race, and Promise.any.
qa.a: `all` resolves with an array of all values but rejects immediately (fail-fast) if any input rejects — good when you need every result and want to abort on the first failure. `allSettled` never rejects; it resolves with a `{status, value|reason}` array, ideal when you want every outcome regardless of failures (e.g. best-effort side jobs). `race` settles as soon as the first input settles, fulfilled or rejected — used for timeouts. `any` resolves with the first fulfillment and only rejects (with an `AggregateError`) if all inputs reject — used for redundancy/failover.

qa.q: Why does `console.log` after a resolved promise's `.then` sometimes run before the `.then` callback, but a `setTimeout(…,0)` callback runs even later?
qa.a: Promise callbacks are scheduled on the microtask queue, which the event loop drains completely after the current synchronous call stack finishes and before rendering or the next macrotask. `setTimeout` schedules a macrotask, which runs only after all pending microtasks are done. So synchronous code runs first, then all microtasks (promise handlers, including chained ones), then macrotasks like timers — a common ordering question to probe event-loop understanding.

qa.q: Does `await` make code slower, and how do you run independent async operations concurrently?
qa.a: `await` itself isn't slow, but awaiting operations one at a time serializes them — if you `await` inside a `for` loop, each iteration waits for the previous network call to finish. When the operations are independent, kick them all off first and await together: `await Promise.all(items.map(fetchItem))`. Only keep sequential awaits when a later call genuinely depends on an earlier result. This latency difference (sum vs max of durations) is a frequent real-world performance bug.

qa.q: How do you convert an err-first callback API into a promise, and why is rejection often better than err-first?
qa.a: Wrap it: `const readFileP = (p) => new Promise((resolve, reject) => fs.readFile(p, (err, data) => err ? reject(err) : resolve(data)))`, or use `util.promisify`. Rejection is generally better because errors propagate automatically down a `.then` chain to a single `.catch` (or are caught by `try/catch` with await), whereas err-first requires you to manually check `err` at every call site and is silently ignorable. Promises also compose with combinators like `Promise.all`, which raw callbacks don't.

qa.q: What does an async function return, and what happens if you return a value versus throw inside it?
qa.a: An async function always returns a Promise. A plain `return value` fulfills that promise with the value (a returned promise is adopted/flattened, not double-wrapped), and a `throw` inside it rejects the promise with the thrown reason. This is why callers must still `.then`/`await` the result and why you can use ordinary `try/catch` around `await` — the thrown rejection is turned back into a synchronous-looking exception at the await point.

qa.q: What is an unhandled promise rejection, and how does forgetting to await cause bugs?
qa.a: If a promise rejects and no `.catch` or surrounding `try/catch` handles it, Node emits an `unhandledRejection` (and crashes by default in modern versions). Forgetting `await` is a classic cause: a fire-and-forget async call whose rejection nobody catches, or a function that returns before its inner async work finishes, so errors surface after the request has already responded. Always await (or explicitly `.catch`) promises you start, and use `try/finally` to guarantee cleanup like releasing a DB connection.

qa.q: What's the difference between `return somePromise` and `return await somePromise` inside a try block?
qa.a: Functionally the resolved value is the same, but timing and error handling differ. `return await p` awaits the promise inside the function, so if `p` rejects the error is caught by the local `try/catch`; a bare `return p` hands the still-pending promise to the caller, so a rejection escapes the local `catch` entirely. Inside a `try/finally`, `return await` also ensures the `finally` runs only after the promise settles. Outside a try block the extra `await` is usually a redundant microtask tick.

bullets.point: An orientation to a program that deliberately couples web development (frontend + backend) with DevOps (deploy, CI/CD, infra). The point for an SDE-2 is the 'own it end-to-end' mindset: writing code is table stakes; being able to ship it, observe it in prod, and roll it back is what separates SDE-1 from SDE-2/staff.

Learning strategy: steady, spaced practice beats binge-then-burnout spikes. Skill retention follows spaced repetition and deliberate practice; a sawtooth effort curve loses most of the gains between sessions. The same principle maps to careers — sustainable throughput matters more than heroic sprints.

bullets.step: 'What would I do if I was you?' — build, don't just watch
bullets.point: Mentor advice to bias toward building projects and fundamentals over passively consuming tutorials or chasing every new framework. Interview angle: interviewers probe depth on core concepts (HTTP, the DOM, the event loop, data structures) that transfer across stacks, not framework trivia that expires.

Pick a beginner vs. intermediate track by honestly benchmarking your baseline. Meta-learning skill: entering material that's too easy wastes time and too hard causes churn; the productive zone is just past your current ability (the 'desirable difficulty').

bullets.step: Good things to hash out (expectations + prerequisites)
bullets.point: Align on ground rules up front: prerequisites, tooling, environment, time commitment, and how to ask for help. Analogous to a project kickoff or design-doc review — clarifying scope and constraints before writing code prevents expensive rework later.

Set up the editor plus the workflow around it: extensions (linter, formatter, language servers), integrated terminal, and debugger. Why it matters: consistent formatting/linting via config committed to the repo (e.g., ESLint + Prettier, EditorConfig) enforced in pre-commit hooks is the first link in a DevOps quality chain, keeping diffs clean and reviews focused on logic.

HTML defines the structure and semantics of a page via elements the browser parses into the DOM. Using semantic tags (header, nav, main, article, button) over div soup improves accessibility (screen readers, keyboard nav), SEO, and maintainability. Interview staple: the difference between semantic markup and presentational markup, and why native elements beat re-implementing behavior on divs.

bullets.step: CSS — styling, layout, and the cascade
bullets.point: CSS controls presentation via the cascade, specificity, and inheritance, plus the box model and layout engines (Flexbox for 1D, Grid for 2D). Core SDE-2 knowledge: how specificity resolves conflicts, why box-sizing: border-box simplifies sizing, and how layout choices affect rendering performance (reflow vs. repaint).

example.title: Ship a personal portfolio site with an automated deploy pipeline
example.body: You build a static portfolio page: semantic HTML for structure (header/nav/main/section/footer) and CSS with a Grid page layout plus Flexbox for the nav bar, using box-sizing: border-box and a small responsive breakpoint set. In VSCode you wire Prettier + a linter and commit the config to the repo so formatting is deterministic across machines. That's the 'web dev' half. The 'DevOps' half: you push to a Git branch, a CI job runs the linter and a build step, and on merge to main a CD job publishes the static assets to a host/CDN (e.g., GitHub Pages, Netlify, or an S3 + CloudFront setup) with cache headers. When a deploy makes the site look broken, you debug across the whole chain — is it a CSS specificity/cascade bug, a caching issue where the CDN served a stale stylesheet, or a build step that didn't emit the file? This single project exercises exactly what the orientation promises: writing the code, shipping it automatically, and reasoning about it in production.

qa.q: Why prefer semantic HTML over generic divs, concretely?
qa.a: Semantic elements carry built-in accessibility semantics and behavior: a <button> is focusable, keyboard-activatable, and announced as a button by screen readers, while a <div onclick> requires you to re-add role, tabindex, and key handlers by hand — and people usually forget. Semantics also aid SEO and give other developers (and browser features like reader mode) a machine-readable document outline. The rule of thumb: reach for the native element that already does the job before styling a div to imitate it.

qa.q: Explain the CSS box model and why box-sizing: border-box is the common default.
qa.a: Every element is a box of content + padding + border + margin. With the default content-box, width applies only to the content, so padding and border add to the rendered width, making layout math error-prone. border-box makes width include padding and border, so an element set to width: 200px is actually 200px wide regardless of its padding. Teams typically set box-sizing: border-box globally (often via *, *::before, *::after) to make sizing predictable.

qa.q: Flexbox vs. Grid — how do you choose?
qa.a: Flexbox is one-dimensional: it lays out items along a single axis (a row or a column) and is ideal for nav bars, toolbars, and distributing space among a set of items. Grid is two-dimensional: it controls rows and columns simultaneously and is ideal for page-level layouts or any structure where alignment in both axes matters. A common pattern is Grid for the overall page skeleton and Flexbox for the components inside each cell; they compose rather than compete.

qa.q: How does CSS resolve which rule wins when several target the same element?
qa.a: The cascade resolves in order: importance and origin first (an !important author rule beats a normal one; inline the trickiest), then specificity, then source order as the tiebreaker. Specificity is counted as (inline, IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements) — an ID selector outranks any number of classes. Because high-specificity selectors and !important are hard to override later, maintainable CSS keeps specificity low and flat (favoring classes) rather than winning fights with ever-more-specific selectors.

qa.q: What's the difference between reflow (layout) and repaint, and why care?
qa.a: Repaint happens when a change affects only appearance (e.g., color, background) — the browser redraws pixels without recomputing geometry. Reflow (layout) happens when geometry changes (width, adding/removing nodes, font size), forcing the browser to recompute positions and sizes, which can cascade to descendants and is more expensive. Performance-wise you avoid layout thrash (interleaving DOM reads and writes in a loop) and prefer animating transform/opacity, which can be composited on the GPU without triggering layout or paint.

qa.q: Compare CSS position values: static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky.
qa.a: static is the default, in normal flow, ignoring top/left. relative stays in flow but is offset from its normal spot and establishes a positioning context for descendants. absolute is removed from flow and positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor (else the initial containing block). fixed is positioned relative to the viewport and stays put on scroll. sticky is a hybrid — it behaves like relative until a scroll threshold, then sticks like fixed within its container.

qa.q: How does editor tooling like VSCode + linters/formatters fit into a DevOps workflow?
qa.a: The goal is that code quality is enforced by config, not by memory or reviewer nagging. You commit editor and tool config (EditorConfig, ESLint/Prettier settings) so every machine formats identically, run format-on-save locally, gate commits with a pre-commit hook (e.g., husky + lint-staged), and re-run the same lint/format/build checks in CI so nothing merges unformatted. This keeps diffs small and reviews focused on logic — the first, cheapest quality gate in the pipeline before build, test, and deploy stages.

qa.q: At a high level, what happens between typing a URL and seeing a styled page?
qa.a: DNS resolves the domain to an IP, a TCP (and for HTTPS, TLS) connection is established, and the browser sends an HTTP request. The server responds with HTML, which the browser parses into the DOM while fetching linked CSS (into the CSSOM) and JS. The DOM and CSSOM combine into a render tree, the browser computes layout (positions/sizes), paints pixels, and composites layers to the screen — and render-blocking CSS or synchronous JS in the <head> can delay first paint, which is why asset placement and loading strategy matter for perceived performance.

bullets.point: Recaps DOM Part 1 (selecting nodes, textContent, basic event listeners) and frames today's goal: building a full TODO app to graduate from ad-hoc DOM pokes to a repeatable rendering pattern. The interview angle is recognizing that manually mutating individual nodes doesn't scale, which motivates the state-driven model that React later formalizes.

Programmatically building nested element trees with document.createElement, element.append/appendChild, setAttribute, dataset, and classList, versus the string shortcut innerHTML. Key trade-off to articulate: createElement is verbose but safe and keeps node references/listeners intact; innerHTML is concise but re-parses/replaces the subtree (dropping existing listeners) and is an XSS vector when interpolating user input.

Wires the remaining pieces of the app end-to-end: capturing input, updating the in-memory list, and reflecting it in the DOM so the UI stays consistent. The point is a complete round-trip (event -> mutate data -> render) rather than isolated snippets, which is the mental model an interviewer probes.

The canonical CRUD-on-a-list exercise: an array of todo objects plus a render loop that paints each item with add/delete controls. It matters because it compresses selection, creation, events, and list rendering into one problem that mirrors real feature work (comment lists, cart items, notifications).

The paradigm shift: treat a plain JS data structure (e.g. an array of {id, text, done}) as the single source of truth, and make the DOM a pure projection of it — UI = f(state). This matters because it eliminates the class of bugs where the screen and the data disagree, and it's the exact mental model behind React/Vue/Svelte.

Implementing the projection: on every state change, run a render() that rebuilds the list from state (typically clear container + rebuild, or targeted updates). Discuss the trade-off — naive full re-render is simple and always correct but causes excess reflow/repaint and destroys focus/scroll/input state; this cost is precisely what React's virtual-DOM diffing and keyed reconciliation exist to avoid.

Read the input value, push a new item (ideally with a stable unique id, not just the array index) into state, clear the input, then call render(). The subtlety worth naming: mutate state first and let render be the only thing that touches the DOM, rather than appending a node imperatively AND updating the array (two sources of truth drift).

Remove the item from state (filter by stable id) and re-render, rather than removing the DOM node directly. Classic interview traps: using array index as the key breaks after other deletes, and attaching a per-row listener that closes over a loop variable declared with var captures the wrong id — solved with let/closured id or, better, event delegation from the parent container reading data-id.

example.title: Debugging a 'delete removes the wrong task' bug in a vanilla-JS task widget
example.body: You own an embeddable task-list widget (no framework — it ships on a marketing page) backed by an array of todos. A support ticket says clicking delete on the 2nd item sometimes removes the 3rd. You find the render loop attaches a delete handler per row using the array index as the identifier, so after any deletion the indices shift and stale handlers point at the wrong slot; a colleague's earlier attempt also used `var i` in the loop, so every handler closed over the final index. You refactor to the state-derived pattern: each todo gets a stable `id` (crypto.randomUUID()), the container uses a single delegated click listener that reads `e.target.closest('[data-id]').dataset.id`, delete does `todos = todos.filter(t => t.id !== id)` followed by one `render()`, and add does `todos.push(...)` then `render()`. You also swap an `innerHTML = userText` line for `textContent` after noticing a task titled `` executed — an XSS hole. The DOM is now a pure function of `todos`, the index bug is structurally impossible, and the input's focus loss on re-render becomes your next optimization (targeted updates or keys).

qa.q: What does 'state-derived rendering' mean, and why is it preferred over directly mutating the DOM on each user action?
qa.a: It means keeping application data in a JS structure as the single source of truth and rendering the DOM as a pure projection of that state (UI = f(state)); every interaction mutates state, then a render step reconciles the DOM. Directly mutating the DOM on each action creates two sources of truth (the data and the screen) that inevitably drift, producing bugs where the UI shows something the data doesn't reflect. Centralizing all DOM writes in one render function makes behavior predictable and is the same model frameworks like React are built on.

qa.q: A naive render() clears the container and rebuilds every item on each change. What are the downsides and how do frameworks address them?
qa.a: Full rebuilds are always correct but expensive: they trigger layout/reflow and repaint for the whole list, and they destroy transient DOM state like input focus, text selection, scroll position, and CSS transitions. They also detach and recreate event listeners each time. React and similar libraries avoid this with a virtual DOM diff plus keyed reconciliation, updating only the nodes that actually changed and preserving the rest — which is why stable keys matter.

qa.q: Why should you delete a todo by a stable id rather than by its array index?
qa.a: An index is positional, not identity: after any insertion or deletion the indices of other items shift, so a handler or key bound to index 2 now refers to a different item. Using a stable unique id (UUID, timestamp, or a monotonic counter) means `todos.filter(t => t.id !== id)` always targets the intended item regardless of list mutations. This is the same reason React warns against using array index as a `key` for dynamic lists.

qa.q: Explain the closure bug when attaching delete handlers in a loop, and how to fix it.
qa.a: If you loop with `var i` and write `btn.onclick = () => remove(i)`, all handlers share one function-scoped `i`, which after the loop equals its final value — so every button deletes the last item. Fixes: declare the loop variable with `let` (block-scoped, a fresh binding per iteration), capture the id in the closure explicitly, or avoid per-row handlers entirely by using event delegation on the parent and reading a `data-id` attribute. Delegation is usually best because it's one listener regardless of list size.

qa.q: When is innerHTML acceptable and when is it dangerous?
qa.a: innerHTML is fine for injecting trusted, static, or developer-controlled markup and is convenient for building a subtree in one string. It's dangerous whenever the string contains user-supplied content, because the browser parses it as HTML and will execute injected scripts/handlers (e.g. ``), an XSS vulnerability. For user text use textContent (or createElement + textContent), reserve innerHTML for sanitized/known-safe content, and note it also replaces the subtree, discarding existing nodes and their listeners.

qa.q: What is event delegation and why use it for a dynamic list?
qa.a: Event delegation attaches a single listener to a stable parent and relies on event bubbling; inside the handler you inspect e.target (often via closest()) to find which child was interacted with. For a dynamic list it means you don't add/remove a listener per row as items come and go — one listener covers all current and future children, reducing memory and avoiding the need to rebind after every re-render. You typically pair it with data-id attributes to map the clicked element back to a state item.

qa.q: How would you add basic performance and correctness improvements to a full-re-render TODO app without adopting a framework?
qa.a: Preserve identity with stable ids and reuse nodes: instead of clearing and rebuilding, diff the state against existing rows (add new, remove missing, update changed text) so focus/scroll survive. Batch DOM writes using a DocumentFragment when you do rebuild, so you touch the live DOM once and trigger a single reflow. Keep all mutations flowing through state -> render, debounce high-frequency inputs, and read layout properties before writing to avoid layout thrashing.

qa.q: Why does building this vanilla TODO app matter as preparation for React?
qa.a: It makes the framework's value concrete: you've felt the pain of manual DOM sync, index-based bugs, listener rebinding, and full re-render cost, so React's declarative render, keys, and reconciliation read as solutions rather than magic. The core loop — state change triggers re-render, UI is a function of state — is identical; React just automates the efficient DOM diffing and gives you components and hooks on top. Understanding the underlying imperative model also helps you debug when the abstraction leaks (stale closures in hooks, key warnings, unnecessary re-renders).

bullets.point: Course kickoff and orientation framing: what Web3/blockchain engineering covers and how the cohort is structured. Non-technical, but sets the mental model that the rest of the track builds a blockchain bottom-up from ledgers, hashing, and consensus rather than starting from a framework.

Logistics for a cohort hackathon — a chance to ship an end-to-end dApp/protocol under time pressure. Interview-relevant only in that portfolio projects (a working smart contract, a mini-chain, a wallet integration) are strong signals for Web3 SDE roles.

Motivates the core value proposition: a shared, append-only, tamper-evident state that multiple mutually-distrusting parties can agree on without a trusted intermediary. The key interview point is knowing the trade-off — blockchains buy decentralization, censorship resistance, and immutability at a large cost in throughput/latency/complexity, so they're justified only when no trusted central party exists.

bullets.step: How to create a new currency? (building a ledger)
bullets.point: Currency is derived first-principles as a ledger of balances/transactions rather than physical coins, exposing the two hard problems: authorization (proving you own funds) and the double-spend problem (preventing the same funds being spent twice). This frames why you need digital signatures for authorization and a decentralized ordering mechanism (consensus) to prevent double-spends — the setup for everything after.

Cryptographic hash functions (e.g. SHA-256): deterministic, fixed-size output, preimage/second-preimage/collision resistant, with an avalanche effect where one bit flips ~half the output. They're the primitive behind block linking (each header embeds the previous block's hash), Merkle trees for efficient integrity proofs, and Proof of Work — expect to be asked how these properties make tampering detectable.

PoW is a Sybil-resistant consensus mechanism: miners brute-force a nonce until the block-header hash falls below a difficulty target, which is expensive to produce but trivial to verify. Difficulty auto-retargets to keep block time roughly constant, and the cost of hashing is what makes rewriting history economically infeasible — the classic interview angle is how this replaces identity-based voting with work-based voting.

Bitcoin ties the pieces together: a UTXO-based ledger, ECDSA signatures for authorization, hash-linked blocks, PoW mining with the heaviest/longest-chain rule (Nakamoto consensus), and a fixed 21M supply with halvings. Know the UTXO model vs account model, why deeper confirmations mean stronger finality, and what a 51% attack can and cannot do.

example.title: Building a tamper-evident audit log with a mini-blockchain
example.body: An SDE-2 on a fintech team is asked to build an append-only audit log for a compliance system that regulators must trust even if an internal admin turns malicious. They model each batch of events as a block whose header stores the SHA-256 hash of the previous block plus a Merkle root of that batch, so editing any past record changes its hash and breaks every downstream prev-hash pointer — making tampering instantly detectable. To make silent rewrites expensive rather than instant, they bolt on a lightweight proof-of-work (find a nonce so the block hash has N leading zeros) and sign each block with the service's private key so authorship is provable. This exercise mirrors Bitcoin's core design and forces the real architectural decision: does this need full decentralization and public consensus, or is a signed, hash-chained log periodically anchored to a public chain sufficient — which is exactly the 'why blockchains?' trade-off in production form.

qa.q: What properties must a cryptographic hash function have, and why do they matter for a blockchain?
qa.a: It must be deterministic, produce fixed-size output, be fast to compute, and be preimage-, second-preimage-, and collision-resistant, with an avalanche effect where flipping one input bit changes ~half the output. In a blockchain these guarantee that block linking is tamper-evident (you can't find a different block with the same hash) and that Proof of Work is hard to satisfy but cheap to verify. If collision resistance broke, an attacker could substitute a fraudulent block that hashes to the same value and slip it into the chain undetected.

qa.q: What is the double-spend problem and how does Bitcoin solve it?
qa.a: Digital data is trivially copyable, so a naive coin could be spent twice by broadcasting two conflicting transactions. Bitcoin solves it with a globally-ordered ledger: each transaction consumes specific prior UTXOs, and nodes reject any transaction spending an already-spent output. Ordering is agreed via PoW plus the heaviest-chain rule, so only one of two conflicting transactions can ever be confirmed, and waiting for more confirmations (block depth) makes a reversal exponentially less likely.

qa.q: How does Proof of Work provide Sybil resistance and consensus?
qa.a: PoW ties influence to computational work rather than to identities or IP addresses, so an attacker cannot cheaply create many nodes to sway the network — they would need a majority of global hashrate. Miners vary a nonce until the header hash is below the difficulty target; the network retargets difficulty to hold block time roughly constant as hashrate changes. Because valid blocks cost real electricity and hardware, rewriting confirmed history means out-mining the honest majority, which is economically prohibitive.

qa.q: When would you deliberately NOT use a blockchain, and what would you use instead?
qa.a: If a trusted central party exists, if you don't need censorship resistance, or if you need high throughput and low latency, a replicated database (Postgres, Kafka, a signed audit log) is far cheaper, faster, and easier to evolve. Blockchains trade several orders of magnitude of performance for decentralized trust and immutability. They're justified only when multiple mutually-distrusting parties must share tamper-evident state with no acceptable intermediary; otherwise it's over-engineering.

qa.q: How are blocks linked, and why does that make the chain tamper-evident?
qa.a: Each block header contains the hash of the previous block's header, forming a hash chain (often with a Merkle root summarizing that block's transactions). Altering any historical transaction changes that block's hash, which invalidates the prev-hash pointer of every subsequent block and their PoW. To rewrite one old block, an attacker must redo PoW for it and all blocks after it, faster than the honest network keeps extending the tip — which is why depth equals security.

qa.q: Explain the difficulty target and nonce in Proof of Work.
qa.a: A block is valid only if its header hash, interpreted as a number, is below the current target; a lower target means fewer valid hashes and thus more work. Miners search by incrementing the nonce (and other malleable fields) and rehashing until they find a qualifying value. Difficulty is proportional to max_target/current_target, and Bitcoin retargets every 2016 blocks (~two weeks) to keep the average block time near 10 minutes as total hashrate rises or falls.

qa.q: How does Bitcoin authorize spending, and what is the UTXO model?
qa.a: Funds are locked to a public key (address); to spend, the owner signs the transaction with the corresponding private key using ECDSA on secp256k1, and every node verifies the signature — proving authorization without exposing the key. Bitcoin tracks state as UTXOs (unspent transaction outputs) rather than account balances: a transaction fully consumes input UTXOs and creates new ones, with the difference (minus change) as the fee. This differs from Ethereum's account model and enables straightforward parallel validation and clear ownership provenance.

qa.q: What is the longest-chain rule, and what can a 51% attacker actually do?
qa.a: Nodes treat the chain with the most cumulative Proof of Work (the 'heaviest', loosely 'longest') as canonical, which deterministically resolves forks. An entity with majority hashrate can out-mine the honest network to reorganize recent blocks and double-spend their own coins, and can censor transactions. Crucially they cannot forge signatures to steal others' funds, mint coins out of protocol rules, or cheaply rewrite deeply-buried history, since re-mining all subsequent blocks against the honest chain is infeasible for old blocks.

bullets.step: Context: what a wallet actually is
bullets.point: A blockchain wallet stores keypairs, not coins; the network holds balances as on-chain state, and the private key is just the authority to sign state transitions from your address. This framing matters in interviews because it explains why 'losing a wallet' means losing keys, and why a wallet is fundamentally a key-management + transaction-signing app rather than a database of funds.

Ethereum's core hash function, used to derive addresses (last 20 bytes of keccak256 of the uncompressed public key) and 4-byte function selectors. The interview gotcha: Ethereum uses the original Keccak padding (0x01), NOT the finalized NIST SHA3-256 (0x06), so keccak256 and sha3-256 produce different digests despite the confusing naming.

bullets.step: Frontend vs backend and the trust boundary
bullets.point: For a web wallet, key generation and signing must run entirely client-side (browser/JS); the private key or seed must never be transmitted to a server. The 'backend' is essentially an RPC node you read from and broadcast to, so the security-critical logic lives in the frontend and the network boundary only ever sees already-signed transactions and public data.

Blockchains expose their state through Remote Procedure Calls using the JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol: a POST body of {jsonrpc, method, params, id} returning {jsonrpc, result|error, id}. It matters because every wallet interaction (read balance, send tx, estimate gas) is a JSON-RPC request; interviewers probe whether you know it's stateless request/response over HTTP, with WebSockets used for subscriptions and arrays used for batching.

Chains denominate value in integer base units to avoid floating-point error: 1 ETH = 10^18 wei, 1 SOL = 10^9 lamports. The practical point is that all balance and fee math must use BigInt/BN, never JS Number (which loses precision beyond 2^53), and you only convert to human-readable decimals at the display layer.

The RPC server is the node (or managed gateway like Alchemy, Infura, QuickNode, Helius) that accepts JSON-RPC and answers from chain state. Interview angle: full node vs archive node (historical state), self-hosted vs managed trade-offs, and awareness of rate limits, request quotas, and eventual consistency across load-balanced nodes.

Core methods: getBalance, getAccountInfo, getLatestBlockhash, sendTransaction, getSignatureStatuses/confirmTransaction, and requestAirdrop (devnet only). Key concept: a Solana transaction must embed a recent blockhash that expires after ~150 slots, which provides replay protection and a natural expiry window instead of an ETH-style nonce.

Core methods: eth_getBalance, eth_getTransactionCount (the account nonce), eth_estimateGas / eth_gasPrice (or EIP-1559 fee fields), eth_sendRawTransaction (broadcast a signed tx), eth_call (read-only contract execution), and eth_getTransactionReceipt (confirmation + logs). The recurring theme is per-account sequential nonces and locally signing before broadcasting the raw hex.

Tie it together: generate a BIP39 mnemonic to a seed, derive HD keypairs via BIP32/BIP44 paths (m/44'/60'/... for ETH, m/44'/501'/... for Solana), render balances from RPC reads, and build/sign/broadcast transactions client-side. This is the capstone that combines cryptography, unit handling, HD derivation, and the RPC read/write cycle into one product.

example.title: A minimal multi-chain web wallet (lightweight MetaMask/Phantom)
example.body: You're an SDE-2 building a browser wallet that supports both Ethereum and Solana from a single seed phrase. On first run you generate a BIP39 mnemonic in the browser, derive an ETH keypair at m/44'/60'/0'/0/0 and a Solana keypair at m/44'/501'/0'/0' from the same seed; the mnemonic is encrypted with a user password and kept in local storage, never sent anywhere. The dashboard reads balances via JSON-RPC against Alchemy endpoints: eth_getBalance for ETH (rendered by dividing wei by 10^18 using BigInt) and getBalance for SOL (lamports / 10^9). To send ETH, you fetch the nonce with eth_getTransactionCount(address,'pending'), estimate fees with eth_estimateGas plus EIP-1559 fields, sign the transaction locally, and broadcast with eth_sendRawTransaction, then poll eth_getTransactionReceipt. To send SOL, you fetch getLatestBlockhash, attach it to the transaction, sign, call sendTransaction, and confirm via getSignatureStatuses. A bug you'd realistically debug: two sends in quick succession both grab nonce N and one gets dropped as a replacement, or a Solana tx fails with 'blockhash not found' because it was signed too long after fetching the blockhash and expired past the ~150-slot window.

qa.q: Why must a web wallet generate keys and sign transactions client-side, and what is the trust boundary?
qa.a: The private key (or seed) is the sole authority over funds, so if it ever reaches a server that server can drain the wallet and becomes a catastrophic single point of compromise. In a non-custodial web wallet the browser is the trust boundary: it holds and uses the key locally, and the only things that cross the network are public reads and already-signed transactions. The RPC node therefore never needs the key and can be fully untrusted for confidentiality (though it can still censor or lie about reads, which is why some clients verify against multiple providers).

qa.q: What's the difference between keccak-256 and SHA3-256, and where does it bite in Ethereum?
qa.a: They use the same Keccak sponge construction but different padding: Ethereum adopted the original Keccak submission (0x01 domain padding) before NIST finalized SHA-3 with different padding (0x06), so the two produce different digests for the same input. This matters because Ethereum addresses, function selectors, and event topics all depend on keccak-256; using a library's 'sha3' that actually implements NIST SHA3-256 will silently produce wrong addresses and selectors. Always confirm the library returns Ethereum-style keccak256.

qa.q: Walk through how an Ethereum transaction gets from a wallet to the chain.
qa.a: You read the sender's next nonce with eth_getTransactionCount, determine fees (EIP-1559 maxFeePerGas/maxPriorityFeePerGas or legacy gasPrice) and a gas limit via eth_estimateGas, then construct and RLP-encode the transaction and sign it locally with the private key (ECDSA over secp256k1). You broadcast the signed hex with eth_sendRawTransaction, which returns the tx hash immediately, and then poll eth_getTransactionReceipt until it's mined to get status and logs. The nonce is the tricky part: it must be strictly sequential per account, so concurrent sends or a stuck pending tx can block or replace later transactions.

qa.q: Why do base units like wei and lamports exist, and how do you handle them safely in JavaScript?
qa.a: Chains store value as unsigned integers to make arithmetic deterministic and avoid floating-point rounding, so 1 ETH is 10^18 wei and 1 SOL is 10^9 lamports. JavaScript's Number is an IEEE-754 double that only holds integers exactly up to 2^53-1, far below 10^18, so doing balance or fee math with Number will silently corrupt values. You must use BigInt (or a big-number library) for all on-chain quantities and only convert to a decimal string at the presentation layer.

qa.q: What is a recent/latest blockhash on Solana and why is it required in a transaction?
qa.a: Every Solana transaction must reference a recent blockhash (from getLatestBlockhash), which the runtime uses both to prevent replay of old transactions and to give each transaction a short expiry window of roughly 150 slots (about a minute). If you sign a transaction and submit it too late, validators reject it as expired ('blockhash not found'), which is a common source of failed sends in a wallet. This differs from Ethereum's model, where sequential per-account nonces provide replay protection instead of a time-boxed blockhash.

qa.q: Describe the JSON-RPC 2.0 request/response shape and when you'd use batching or WebSockets.
qa.a: A request is a JSON object with jsonrpc:'2.0', a method name, a params array, and an id; the response echoes the id and returns either result or an error object with code and message. It's normally sent as an HTTP POST and is stateless, so each call is independent. You can batch by POSTing an array of request objects to cut round-trips, and you switch to a WebSocket connection for push-style subscriptions (eth_subscribe / logsNotification or Solana's accountSubscribe) when you need live updates instead of polling.

qa.q: How does HD wallet derivation (BIP39/32/44) let one phrase control many chains and accounts?
qa.a: BIP39 turns entropy into a human-readable mnemonic and then into a 512-bit seed; BIP32 defines hierarchical deterministic derivation so a single seed produces a tree of child keys via a derivation path; BIP44 standardizes that path as m/44'/coin'/account'/change/index, where the coin type distinguishes chains (60 for ETH, 501 for Solana). This means one backed-up phrase deterministically regenerates every account across chains, and hardened derivation (the apostrophe) prevents a leaked child key plus parent public key from exposing siblings. The trade-off is that the mnemonic is the master secret, so its storage and encryption are the entire security model.

qa.q: When would you run your own RPC node versus using a managed provider like Alchemy or Infura?
qa.a: Managed providers give you instant, highly-available JSON-RPC with archive data and no ops burden, which is right for most wallets, but you pay per request, hit rate limits, and trust them not to censor or return stale/incorrect reads. Running your own node removes that trust and cost-per-call and can lower latency, but it's expensive to operate, needs an archive node for historical state, and you must handle sync, storage, and uptime yourself. A common middle ground is using multiple providers with failover and cross-checking critical reads to avoid a single point of trust.

bullets.point: Frames HTTP as the application-layer protocol that lets a frontend (or any client) talk to a backend over the network. The interview angle: HTTP sits on top of TCP (and TLS for HTTPS) at L7 — knowing where it lives in the stack signals you understand what HTTP does vs. what the transport layer guarantees.

A protocol is an agreed-upon contract for message format so heterogeneous clients and servers (any language, any OS) can interoperate. HTTP standardizes how requests/responses are structured so a Python server and a JS browser can communicate without custom wire formats. Interviewers probe whether you can distinguish a 'protocol' (rules) from an 'implementation' (a specific server).

HTTP is stateless and client-driven: the client always initiates a request, the server replies with exactly one response, and the server never speaks unprompted. Statelessness is the key point — each request carries all context it needs (auth token, cookies), which is why servers scale horizontally but also why you need tokens/sessions to simulate state. Contrast with WebSockets/SSE when full-duplex or server-push is needed.

Humans use domain names; machines route on IP addresses, so DNS resolves the name to an IP before any HTTP request is sent. The request itself travels to that IP:port. Common interview follow-up: DNS resolution happens before the TCP handshake, is cached at multiple layers (browser, OS, resolver), and one domain can map to many IPs (load balancing / CDN).

A port disambiguates which process on a host receives the traffic — HTTP defaults to 80, HTTPS to 443. An IP identifies the machine; the port identifies the service on it, so one server can run many services on distinct ports. Know the well-known defaults and that the browser hides :80/:443 in the URL bar.

HTTP verbs express intent: GET (read), POST (create/action), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove). The properties that matter: GET is safe (no side effects) and cacheable; GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent while POST is not. Interviewers care that you choose verbs by semantics (idempotency, safety, caching) rather than treating everything as POST.

Every response has a status line (protocol version + status code), headers, and an optional body. It mirrors the request structure. The point to internalize: the client branches its logic on the status code first, then parses the body — a 200 with an error-shaped body is a common API design smell worth calling out.

Grouped by class: 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. Specifics matter — 200 vs 201 (created) vs 204 (no content); 301 vs 302 (permanent vs temporary redirect); 400 vs 401 (unauthenticated) vs 403 (unauthorized) vs 404; 429 rate limit; 500 vs 502/503/504 (gateway/unavailable/timeout). Using the right code drives client retry logic, caching, and monitoring.

The payload carrying the actual data — JSON is the common format, indicated via the Content-Type header. GET requests conventionally have no body (data goes in query params); POST/PUT/PATCH carry it. Key nuance: the server must be told how to parse the body (Content-Type), which is why body-parser/express.json() middleware exists and why a mismatched header yields a parse failure.

The path portion of the URL (e.g., /users/42/orders) that maps a request to a specific handler on the server. Combined with the method, (method + route) forms the addressable endpoint. REST design uses nouns and hierarchy in routes and verbs for actions; interviewers look for clean resource modeling and awareness of path params vs query params.

Key-value metadata sent on both request and response — Content-Type, Authorization, Accept, Cookie, Cache-Control, CORS headers, User-Agent. They carry cross-cutting concerns (auth, content negotiation, caching, CORS) separate from the body. Strong answers cite specific headers for a scenario, e.g., Authorization: Bearer <token> for auth or Access-Control-Allow-Origin for CORS.

A consolidation step: given a full URL like https://api.example.com:443/users?id=5, you should be able to decompose it into protocol, domain (→IP via DNS), port, route, and query — plus the method, headers, and body that accompany the request. This end-to-end decomposition is exactly the whiteboard exercise interviewers use to check depth.

Anything that can send an HTTP request is a client — the browser (GET on navigation, fetch/XHR for the rest), curl for scripting/CI, Postman for manual API exploration. The point: the server is client-agnostic, so you can test/debug an endpoint with curl before any frontend exists. Being fluent in curl flags (-X, -H, -d) is a practical expectation.

Implement a server with Node's http module or Express: define routes by (method + path), read headers/body, and send back a status code + body. On the client side, use fetch. Interview value: you can articulate the request lifecycle in code — parsing the body, setting Content-Type, returning correct status codes — rather than just describing HTTP abstractly.

example.title: Debugging a broken 'Add to Cart' call in an e-commerce app
example.body: Your React frontend calls POST https://api.shop.com/carts/123/items with a JSON body {"sku":"ABC","qty":2}, but items silently fail to add. Walking the HTTP path: the browser resolves api.shop.com via DNS to an IP, opens a TLS connection on port 443, and sends the request with headers Authorization: Bearer <jwt> and Content-Type: application/json. You reproduce it outside the UI with `curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Authorization: Bearer …' -d '{"sku":"ABC","qty":2}' https://api.shop.com/carts/123/items` and get back 415 Unsupported Media Type. The root cause: the Express server was missing app.use(express.json()), so the body was never parsed and Content-Type validation rejected it. After adding the middleware, the endpoint returns 201 Created with the updated cart in the body. This single bug touches nearly every concept in the topic — DNS/IP, port, method, route, headers, body, status codes, and the client tools used to isolate frontend vs backend fault.

qa.q: HTTP is described as stateless. What does that mean and how do real apps maintain user sessions?
qa.a: Stateless means the server retains no memory of prior requests; each request must carry everything needed to be understood, and the server can handle any request on any instance. Apps simulate state by having the client resend identity on every request — typically a session cookie (server-side session store) or a self-contained JWT in the Authorization header. This is what lets you load-balance across many stateless server instances, at the cost of moving state into tokens or an external store like Redis.

qa.q: Which HTTP methods are idempotent and safe, and why does it matter in practice?
qa.a: Safe methods (GET, HEAD) have no side effects. Idempotent methods (GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD) produce the same server state whether called once or N times; POST is neither safe nor idempotent. It matters because idempotency lets clients, proxies, and load balancers safely retry on network failures or timeouts without duplicating effects — retrying a POST could create duplicate orders, which is why real APIs add idempotency keys to make POST safely retryable.

qa.q: A client gets a 401 and a 403 in different cases. What's the distinction, and how would you handle each?
qa.a: 401 Unauthorized means the request is not authenticated — no valid credentials — so the client should (re)authenticate, e.g., refresh the token or redirect to login. 403 Forbidden means the client is authenticated but not permitted to access that resource, so re-auth won't help; it's an authorization/permissions problem. Conflating them leads to bad UX like infinite login loops on a 403.

qa.q: Walk me through what happens between typing a URL and the server receiving the HTTP request.
qa.a: The browser parses the URL into protocol, host, port, path, and query. It resolves the host to an IP via DNS (checking browser/OS/resolver caches first). It opens a TCP connection to that IP on the port (80 or 443), and for HTTPS performs a TLS handshake. Only then does it send the HTTP request line (method + path + version), headers, and optional body to the server process listening on that port.

qa.q: When should you use query parameters vs a request body vs path parameters?
qa.a: Path params identify a resource and belong in the route hierarchy (/users/42). Query params are for optional, non-hierarchical modifiers like filtering, sorting, and pagination (?status=active&page=2) and travel on GET requests, which have no body. The request body carries the payload for create/update operations (POST/PUT/PATCH) and can hold large, structured, or sensitive data — unlike the URL, it isn't logged in access logs or browser history by default.

qa.q: What role do Content-Type and Accept headers play, and what breaks if they're wrong?
qa.a: Content-Type tells the receiver how to parse the body being sent (e.g., application/json vs multipart/form-data); Accept is content negotiation — the client stating which response formats it can handle. If Content-Type is missing or wrong, the server may fail to parse the body or reject it with 415, which is a classic cause of 'my POST body is empty' bugs. These headers decouple the wire format from the data and let one endpoint serve multiple representations.

qa.q: Why would you reach for curl or Postman instead of just testing through the browser UI?
qa.a: Because the server is client-agnostic, you can exercise an endpoint in isolation — before the frontend exists or to determine whether a bug is frontend or backend. curl and Postman let you control the method, headers, and body precisely, inspect raw status codes and response headers, and script/automate calls in CI. The browser mainly issues GETs on navigation and applies CORS and caching that can mask the true server behavior.

qa.q: HTTP runs on top of TCP. What does TCP give HTTP, and when would you not want HTTP's request-response model?
qa.a: TCP provides a reliable, ordered, connection-oriented byte stream, so HTTP doesn't handle packet loss, retransmission, or ordering itself. HTTP's strict one-request/one-response, client-initiated model is a poor fit when the server needs to push data or you need low-latency bidirectional communication — e.g., live chat, notifications, or collaborative editing. There you'd use WebSockets (full-duplex) or Server-Sent Events (server→client stream), which is a common follow-up interviewers use to test protocol trade-offs.

bullets.point: A server-side JavaScript runtime that embeds Google's V8 engine and pairs it with libuv, which provides the event loop and a thread pool for async I/O. It is not a language or framework: the key mental model is a single-threaded event loop that offloads blocking work (file, network, DNS, crypto) to libuv/OS so JS code stays non-blocking. Interview angle: explain why CPU-bound work stalls the loop while I/O-bound work scales well.

An alternative JS runtime built on Apple's JavaScriptCore (not V8) and written in Zig, bundling a runtime, package manager, bundler, and test runner into one binary. Its selling points are fast cold start and much faster installs, plus first-class TypeScript/JSX execution without a build step. Interview angle: know the trade-off — speed and DX vs Node's larger ecosystem maturity and npm-compatibility edge cases.

`npm init` (or `-y`) scaffolds a package.json, the manifest that declares name, version, entry point (`main`), module system (`type`), `scripts`, and dependency ranges. It is the contract that tooling and the resolver read. Interview angle: know what `type: module` vs default CommonJS changes, and that `scripts` run in a shell with node_modules/.bin on PATH.

Node's default package manager: a CLI plus the public registry plus semver-based dependency resolution that materializes a node_modules tree. Understand `install` vs `--save-dev`, semver ranges, and that install runs lifecycle scripts (postinstall) — a common supply-chain risk. Interview angle: contrast npm with pnpm (content-addressed store, symlinks) and yarn on disk layout and speed.

Node's built-in core modules (fs, path, http, crypto, os, stream, events) shipped with the runtime — no install, no node_modules entry. Best practice is the `node:` prefix (e.g. `node:fs`) to disambiguate from userland packages and avoid shadowing. Interview angle: know which are sync vs async APIs and that most core modules expose both callback and promise (`fs/promises`) forms.

Third-party libraries pulled from the npm registry into node_modules and located at runtime by Node's resolution algorithm (walk up node_modules directories, honor package.json `exports`/`main`). These carry transitive dependency trees, so the real concern is bloat, versioning, and supply-chain trust. Interview angle: explain how the resolver finds a module and why duplicate versions can coexist in the tree.

The lockfile that pins the exact resolved version, source URL, and integrity hash of every package in the full tree, making installs deterministic across machines and CI. Commit it. Interview angle: `npm install` may mutate the lock to satisfy manifest ranges, while `npm ci` installs strictly from the lock (failing on drift) and is the correct choice for CI/reproducible builds.

Build an executable Node script: add a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang, parse `process.argv` (index 2 onward for user args), and expose it via the package.json `bin` field so `npm link`/`npx`/global install create a PATH shim. Interview angle: know how `npx` resolves and runs a bin, and why the shebang + executable bit matter on POSIX.

A follow-on exercise applying the same runtime primitives — typically reading/writing files with `node:fs`, doing async work, or calling an API with `fetch` (global since Node 18) and printing formatted output. The point is composing core modules, argument parsing, and proper exit codes/error handling into a small real tool. Interview angle: use `process.exitCode`/non-zero exits and handle rejections rather than letting the process crash silently.

example.title: Shipping an internal `deploy-notes` CLI to the team via npm
example.body: Your team needs a small CLI that reads a service's changelog, calls an internal release API, and posts a summary. You start with `npm init -y`, set `type: module`, and add a `bin` entry pointing at `cli.js` with a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang. Core work uses internal packages — `node:fs/promises` to read the changelog and `node:path` to resolve paths — while `process.argv` parsing selects the target environment. For HTTP you use the built-in global `fetch` (Node 18+) instead of adding axios, keeping the dependency tree lean; the one external package you do add (say `chalk` for colored output) lands in `dependencies`, and `vitest` goes in `devDependencies`. You commit `package-lock.json` so CI runs `npm ci` for byte-identical, reproducible installs, and teammates run the tool via `npx` after `npm link`. When someone reports the tool runs slowly on cold start in a scratch container, you evaluate running it under bun for faster startup and installs — and confirm your reliance only on standard APIs keeps it portable across both runtimes.

qa.q: Node.js is single-threaded, so how does it handle thousands of concurrent connections?
qa.a: JavaScript executes on one thread via the event loop, but I/O is delegated to libuv, which uses OS async primitives (epoll/kqueue/IOCP) and a small worker thread pool for operations that can't be async at the OS level (file I/O, DNS, crypto). While an I/O request is outstanding the loop is free to process other callbacks, so I/O-bound concurrency scales cheaply. The catch is CPU-bound work: a heavy synchronous computation blocks the single thread and stalls all connections, which is why you offload to worker_threads or a separate process.

qa.q: What's the difference between dependencies, devDependencies, and peerDependencies?
qa.a: `dependencies` are needed at runtime and are installed for anyone who installs your package. `devDependencies` are only needed during development/build/test (bundlers, linters, test runners) and are skipped for consumers and with `npm install --production`. `peerDependencies` declare a package your code expects the host project to provide (e.g. a React plugin expecting `react`), avoiding duplicate/mismatched copies; npm v7+ auto-installs them by default, which can surface version conflicts.

qa.q: Why commit package-lock.json, and when do you use `npm ci` vs `npm install`?
qa.a: The lockfile records exact versions and integrity hashes for the entire transitive tree, so committing it guarantees every machine and CI run resolves an identical node_modules. `npm install` reads the manifest ranges and may update the lockfile to satisfy them, so it can drift. `npm ci` ignores the manifest for resolution, installs strictly from the lockfile, errors if the two are out of sync, and wipes node_modules first — making it the deterministic, faster choice for CI and reproducible builds.

qa.q: Explain semver ranges: what do `^1.2.3` and `~1.2.3` allow?
qa.a: `^1.2.3` allows any version compatible under the same major — `>=1.2.3 <2.0.0` — so it picks up minor and patch updates. `~1.2.3` is narrower, allowing patch updates only — `>=1.2.3 <1.3.0`. The premise is that maintainers follow semver (breaking changes bump major), but that trust plus floating ranges is exactly why the lockfile exists to pin the actual installed version. For `0.x` versions the caret behaves more conservatively, treating the minor as breaking.

qa.q: CommonJS vs ES Modules in Node — what actually differs?
qa.a: CommonJS uses `require`/`module.exports`, loads synchronously, and resolves at runtime, so you get dynamic requires and a live `__dirname`. ESM uses `import`/`export`, is statically analyzable (enabling tree-shaking), loads asynchronously, and top-level `await` is allowed; `__dirname` is gone in favor of `import.meta.url`. Node selects the system via the file extension (`.mjs`/`.cjs`) or the package.json `type` field, and interop is asymmetric — ESM can import CJS, but CJS can only reach ESM via dynamic `import()`.

qa.q: How does Node resolve `require('lodash')` or an import of a bare specifier?
qa.a: For a bare specifier (no `./` or `/`), Node walks up from the current file's directory checking each `node_modules` folder until it finds a matching package. Inside that package it consults the `exports` map first (which can gate and remap entry points), falling back to `main` or `index.js`. Because each dependency can have its own nested node_modules, multiple versions of the same package can legitimately coexist in one tree, which explains duplicate installs and version-specific bugs.

qa.q: When would you choose bun over Node.js, and what are the risks?
qa.a: Bun is attractive when startup latency, install speed, and zero-config TypeScript/JSX matter — short-lived CLIs, scripts, CI, and fast local dev — and it consolidates runtime, package manager, bundler, and test runner into one tool. The risks are ecosystem maturity: it runs on JavaScriptCore rather than V8, and some npm packages relying on Node-specific internals or native addons may behave differently or break. For long-lived production services the safer default is still Node, given its operational track record and broader tooling/observability support.

qa.q: How do you turn a Node script into an installable CLI command?
qa.a: Add a `bin` field in package.json mapping a command name to your script, and start that script with a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang so the OS runs it with Node. On install (global, `npm link`, or via `npx`) npm creates a shim on PATH that points at your file. Inside, read arguments from `process.argv` (real args start at index 2), and set a non-zero `process.exitCode` on failure so shells and CI can detect errors.

bullets.point: Quick review of the core model the client sits on: accounts, lamports (1 SOL = 1e9 lamports), ed25519 keypairs, RPC endpoints (devnet/testnet/mainnet-beta), and programs. Interview angle: a browser dApp never holds a private key — it only holds a public key and delegates all signing to the wallet, so you must be fluent in which operations are on-chain (cost fees, need a blockhash) vs off-chain (message signing).

Bootstrap with Vite (or CRA) and install the adapter stack: @solana/web3.js, @solana/wallet-adapter-react, @solana/wallet-adapter-react-ui, and @solana/wallet-adapter-wallets. Why it matters: the packages split responsibilities — web3.js is the RPC/transaction layer, the adapter packages abstract dozens of wallets (Phantom, Solflare, Backpack) behind one interface so you don't integrate each wallet by hand.

The 'things to do' is wrapping the app in three nested providers: ConnectionProvider (holds the RPC Connection + endpoint), WalletProvider (manages selected wallet, autoConnect, event listeners), and WalletModalProvider (the connect-wallet UI). Interview angle: order and nesting matter — useConnection and useWallet are context hooks, so any component reading them must live inside these providers, and the endpoint is where you pick devnet vs mainnet.

Use connection.requestAirdrop(publicKey, amountInLamports) to fund a wallet on devnet/testnet for testing, then confirm the returned signature. Why it matters: airdrops only work on non-mainnet clusters and are aggressively rate-limited (often ~1-2 SOL per request), so production funding flows can't rely on this — it's purely a test faucet, and you should handle 429/'airdrop limit reached' errors gracefully.

Read the connected wallet's SOL via connection.getBalance(publicKey), which returns an integer number of lamports; divide by LAMPORTS_PER_SOL for display. Interview angle: this is an async RPC call tied to publicKey — re-fetch in a useEffect keyed on the key (and after transactions), keep amounts in lamports internally to avoid floating-point drift, and consider commitment level since balances differ across processed/confirmed/finalized.

Use the signMessage function from useWallet: encode the string with new TextEncoder().encode(...), get back an ed25519 signature, and verify it server-side. Why it matters: this is authentication (Sign-In With Solana / proof of key ownership) with no transaction, no fee, and no on-chain footprint — the classic use case is logging a user in by proving they control the wallet, and the interviewer wants you to distinguish this from signing a transaction.

Construct a Transaction with SystemProgram.transfer({fromPubkey, toPubkey, lamports}), then call sendTransaction(tx, connection) from useWallet, which asks the wallet to sign and broadcasts it. Interview angle: know the moving parts — the wallet (connected pubkey) is the fee payer, the transaction needs a recentBlockhash and feePayer (the adapter/wallet fills these), and you must await confirmation on the returned signature rather than assuming instant finality.

Ties it together: build a working dApp that connects a wallet, shows balance, airdrops on devnet, signs a message, and transfers SOL to another address. The goal is demonstrating the end-to-end pattern — provider setup, the useConnection/useWallet hooks, error/loading states, and confirmation handling — which is exactly the skeleton every real Solana frontend reuses.

example.title: A devnet-first token-tipping dApp for a creator platform
example.body: You're an SDE-2 building the tipping widget for a creator platform. The React app wraps everything in ConnectionProvider (pointed at devnet during dev, mainnet-beta in prod), WalletProvider with autoConnect, and WalletModalProvider so users click one 'Connect Wallet' button and pick Phantom or Backpack. On connect you read connection.getBalance(publicKey) to show their SOL balance, and during QA you expose a 'Fund test wallet' button that calls requestAirdrop so testers aren't blocked on real funds. To gate access to a creator's private page, you require the user to signMessage a nonce your backend issued — no fee, no transaction — and your server verifies the ed25519 signature against their public key to establish a session. When a fan tips, you build a SystemProgram.transfer transaction, hand it to sendTransaction, and only show 'Tip sent!' after you confirm the signature. The bugs you actually hit are instructive: airdrop 429s on devnet, a stale balance because you forgot to re-fetch after the transfer, and treating sendTransaction's returned signature as final before confirmation — the transaction was still only 'processed', not 'finalized'.

qa.q: What is the responsibility split between ConnectionProvider, WalletProvider, and WalletModalProvider?
qa.a: ConnectionProvider owns the web3.js Connection object and the RPC endpoint (which cluster you talk to), exposed via useConnection. WalletProvider manages wallet state — the list of supported wallets, the currently selected/connected wallet, autoConnect, and connect/disconnect events — exposed via useWallet. WalletModalProvider is purely the UI layer providing the wallet-selection modal. They're nested (Connection outermost) because the hooks are React context, so any component using useConnection/useWallet must render inside the relevant provider.

qa.q: Why can't you use requestAirdrop on mainnet, and what should a production funding flow do instead?
qa.a: Airdrops are a faucet feature of devnet/testnet validators that mint test SOL out of thin air; mainnet has real economic value and no faucet, so the call fails there. Faucets are also rate-limited (typically a small amount per IP/address per window), so even on devnet you must handle 429/'airdrop limit reached' errors and back off. In production, users fund wallets by buying/transferring SOL from an exchange or on-ramp — the app never mints tokens for them.

qa.q: getBalance returns what, and why keep amounts in lamports internally?
qa.a: It returns an integer number of lamports (1 SOL = 1,000,000,000 lamports), not SOL. You convert for display by dividing by LAMPORTS_PER_SOL, but you should keep and compute with lamports as integers because SOL amounts as JS floats introduce rounding errors — 0.1 + 0.2 style drift is unacceptable for money. It's also an async RPC call, so it's affected by the commitment level and needs re-fetching after state-changing operations.

qa.q: When would you use signMessage versus sendTransaction, and how do they differ?
qa.a: signMessage is off-chain: it produces an ed25519 signature over arbitrary bytes, costs no fee, touches no ledger, and is used to prove key ownership — authentication, Sign-In With Solana, or authorizing an API call. sendTransaction is on-chain: it signs and broadcasts an actual transaction that mutates state (e.g., transferring SOL), requires a recent blockhash and a fee payer, and must be confirmed. Confusing the two is a common mistake — you don't 'send a transaction' just to log someone in.

qa.q: Walk through sending SOL with the wallet adapter. Who pays the fee and what's the blockhash for?
qa.a: You build a Transaction containing a SystemProgram.transfer instruction with fromPubkey (the connected wallet), toPubkey, and lamports, then call sendTransaction(tx, connection) from useWallet, which prompts the wallet to sign and then broadcasts. The connected wallet is the fee payer by default. The transaction needs a recentBlockhash — a recent slot's hash that bounds the transaction's validity window and prevents replay; the adapter/wallet typically fetches and sets it. After sending you get a signature and must await confirmation rather than assuming success.

qa.q: How does the wallet adapter protect private keys, and what's the trust model?
qa.a: The dApp never sees or handles the private key. The adapter communicates with the wallet extension/app over a defined interface; the wallet holds the secret key, shows a confirmation prompt, signs internally, and returns only the public key and signatures. The security model is that the user trusts the wallet (not the dApp) with their key, and they review each signing request — which is why phishing that tricks users into approving malicious transactions, not key theft, is the realistic attack surface.

qa.q: How do you verify a signed message on the backend?
qa.a: The client sends the original message bytes, the signature, and the public key. The server re-encodes the message the same way (TextEncoder) and verifies the ed25519 signature using a library like tweetnacl (nacl.sign.detached.verify(messageBytes, signature, publicKeyBytes)) or @noble/ed25519. To prevent replay, the message should embed a server-issued nonce and/or timestamp that the backend tracks and expires, so a captured signature can't be reused.

qa.q: What do the commitment levels processed, confirmed, and finalized mean, and why do they matter here?
qa.a: They describe how much of the cluster has acknowledged a slot. 'processed' means a single validator has processed it (fastest, can be rolled back), 'confirmed' means a supermajority of the cluster has voted on it (a good default for UX), and 'finalized' means it's rooted and effectively irreversible (~31+ confirmations). They matter because getBalance and transaction confirmation depend on the level — showing 'success' at 'processed' can lie to the user if the block is dropped, so money-moving flows should wait for at least 'confirmed', ideally 'finalized' for high-value actions.

bullets.point: HTTP is a stateless, text-based, request/response protocol over TCP: a client sends a method (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) + path + headers + optional body, and the server replies with a status line, headers, and body. Interview angle: statelessness is why we need cookies/tokens for sessions, and knowing status code families (2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error) plus idempotency (GET/PUT/DELETE idempotent, POST not) is table stakes.

Headers carry metadata separate from the body — Content-Type/Content-Length describe the payload, Authorization carries credentials, and Cache-Control/ETag/If-None-Match drive caching and conditional requests. Interview angle: be able to explain content negotiation (Accept), the difference between Authorization vs Cookie auth, and how caching validators (ETag + 304 Not Modified) save bandwidth.

bullets.step: Fetch API: making HTTP calls from the client
bullets.point: fetch() is the promise-based browser (and modern Node) HTTP client; it resolves to a Response object and does NOT reject on 4xx/5xx — you must check response.ok / response.status yourself. Interview angle: know that the body is a stream consumed once (await res.json()), that fetch only rejects on network failures/CORS, and how to attach headers, method, body, credentials, and an AbortController for timeouts/cancellation.

A server binds to a port and handles incoming requests; Node's core http.createServer((req,res)=>…) is the primitive, while Express wraps it with routing and middleware ergonomics. Interview angle: understand that req is a readable stream (body arrives in chunks), res needs an explicit status + headers + end(), and that a single Node process handles concurrency via the event loop, not threads.

Middleware are functions (req, res, next) that run in order for each request, forming a pipeline where each can read/mutate req/res, short-circuit by sending a response, or call next() to pass control (or next(err) to jump to error handlers). Interview angle: explain ordering matters, that forgetting next() hangs the request, and that this is the composition pattern behind auth, logging, body parsing, and error handling.

Middleware can be scoped: app.use(mw) runs globally, app.use('/admin', mw) runs for a path prefix, and app.get('/x', mw, handler) runs only for that route/method. Interview angle: use this to apply auth or validation only where needed (e.g. protect /admin routes) instead of gating every request, and know that error-handling middleware has a distinct 4-arg signature (err, req, res, next).

Practice building real middleware: a request logger, an auth/JWT verifier that attaches req.user, a rate limiter using an in-memory or Redis counter, and input validation. Interview angle: the takeaway is reusable cross-cutting concerns — interviewers probe whether you can write one correctly, including calling next() on the happy path and responding with the right status (401 vs 403, 429 for rate limits) on rejection.

Production apps compose well-known middleware: express.json()/urlencoded() to parse bodies, cors for cross-origin access, helmet for security headers, morgan for request logging, cookie-parser, and compression. Interview angle: know why express.json() is needed (raw body is a stream, req.body is undefined without it) and that order matters — body parsers before handlers, error handler last.

CORS is a browser-enforced mechanism where the server opts in to cross-origin requests via Access-Control-Allow-Origin/Methods/Headers; the browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request for non-simple requests and blocks the response if the headers don't permit it. Interview angle: CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server (curl/Postman ignore it), Allow-Origin '*' cannot be combined with credentials, and preflight caching via Access-Control-Max-Age reduces overhead.

example.title: Building and debugging a React SPA + Node/Express API split across two origins
example.body: You build a Node/Express JSON API on api.myapp.com and a React SPA served from app.myapp.com. The SPA uses fetch() with credentials:'include' to hit the API, sending a JWT in an Authorization header. On the server you compose a middleware pipeline: express.json() to parse bodies, morgan for logging, a global auth middleware that verifies the token and sets req.user, and route-specific middleware that gates /admin behind a role check — plus a final (err,req,res,next) error handler that maps failures to 401/403/500. The first thing that breaks in the browser is a CORS error: the preflight OPTIONS request fails because the server never sent Access-Control-Allow-Origin/Allow-Headers, so you add the cors middleware configured with the specific origin (not '*', since you use credentials) and allow the Authorization header. Then you notice fetch isn't throwing on a 401, so you fix the client to check res.ok before calling res.json(). Finally you add ETag/Cache-Control on a GET endpoint so repeat requests return 304 and save bandwidth. This single flow exercises headers, fetch semantics, the server, the middleware chain, route-scoped auth, common middleware, and CORS end to end.

qa.q: Why doesn't fetch() reject the promise on a 404 or 500, and how do you handle it correctly?
qa.a: fetch only rejects on network-level failures (DNS, connection refused, CORS block, aborted request); any completed HTTP response — including 4xx/5xx — is considered a success at the transport level, so the promise resolves. You must inspect response.ok (true for 200–299) or response.status and throw/branch yourself. This trips people up because axios throws on 4xx/5xx by default, so migrating code between them changes error-handling flow.

qa.q: Walk through what happens in an Express middleware chain when a request comes in, and what breaks if you forget next().
qa.a: Middleware run in registration order; each gets (req, res, next) and can mutate req/res, end the response, or call next() to pass control to the next matching middleware/handler. If you neither send a response nor call next(), the request hangs until the client times out — a common bug. Calling next(err) skips remaining normal middleware and jumps to the error-handling middleware (the 4-arg signature), which should be registered last.

qa.q: Explain CORS and the preflight request. Why can a request work in Postman but fail in the browser?
qa.a: CORS is a browser security mechanism that restricts cross-origin requests unless the server explicitly opts in via Access-Control-Allow-* response headers. For non-simple requests (custom headers, methods like PUT/DELETE, or certain content types) the browser first sends a preflight OPTIONS request and only proceeds if the response permits the origin, method, and headers. Postman/curl aren't browsers and don't enforce the same-origin policy, so they bypass CORS entirely — meaning a CORS failure is a client-browser enforcement issue, not something the server 'blocks.'

qa.q: Why can't you use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentialed requests, and what's the fix?
qa.a: When a request includes credentials (cookies or Authorization via credentials:'include'), the browser forbids the wildcard '*' for Allow-Origin and also requires Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. You must echo back the specific requesting origin instead of '*'. Practically this means reflecting an allowlisted origin dynamically, which is exactly what the cors middleware does when you pass it a concrete origin and credentials:true.

qa.q: What does express.json() actually do, and what happens if you omit it?
qa.a: The raw HTTP request body arrives as a readable stream of chunks; Express does not buffer or parse it by default, so req.body is undefined. express.json() is middleware that reads the stream, parses it as JSON based on Content-Type, and populates req.body. If you omit it (or the client omits Content-Type: application/json), req.body stays undefined and you get 'cannot read property of undefined' errors in handlers. It also must be registered before the routes that need it.

qa.q: How do you implement a request timeout / cancellation with fetch?
qa.a: fetch has no built-in timeout, so you use an AbortController: pass controller.signal in the fetch options and call controller.abort() after a setTimeout; the fetch promise then rejects with an AbortError. Modern runtimes also offer AbortSignal.timeout(ms) as shorthand. This is important for preventing hung requests and for cancelling stale in-flight requests (e.g. type-ahead search) to avoid race conditions where an older response overwrites a newer one.

qa.q: When would you use route-specific middleware versus global (app.use) middleware?
qa.a: Use global middleware for concerns every request needs — body parsing, logging, security headers, CORS. Use route- or path-scoped middleware for concerns that apply to a subset, like requiring authentication only on /api or a role check only on /admin, applied via app.use('/admin', authz) or per-route app.get('/x', authz, handler). Scoping avoids running expensive or restrictive logic on public routes (like a healthcheck or login endpoint) and keeps the pipeline efficient and correct.

qa.q: How do ETag / If-None-Match enable caching, and what status code signals a cache hit?
qa.a: The server sends an ETag header (a hash/version of the resource) with a response; on the next request the client sends If-None-Match with that ETag.
