If the resource is unchanged, the server responds 304 Not Modified with no body, letting the client reuse its cached copy and saving bandwidth. This is a validation/conditional-request model (freshness confirmed with the server), distinct from Cache-Control max-age which lets the client skip the server entirely until the TTL expires.

bullets.point: Authentication answers "who are you" (proving identity via credentials); authorization answers "what can you do" (permissions/roles). Interviewers probe this distinction constantly — auth failures are 401, authz failures are 403, and conflating them is a common design smell.

The bank teller checks your ID (login/credentials), then hands you a token/passbook that proves you're allowed to transact without re-proving identity each time. The mental model: prove identity once, then carry a bearer credential for subsequent stateless requests. It frames why HTTP (stateless) needs a token passed on every call.

Signup stores a hashed password (bcrypt/argon2, never plaintext); login verifies the hash and issues a credential (session id or token); every protected request presents that credential. Key interview point: passwords are salted+hashed, and the login response is where the trust boundary is established.

Bootstrap an Express server with express.json() body-parsing, route handlers, and a listen port — the substrate for auth endpoints. The interview-relevant detail is middleware ordering: body parsers and CORS must run before route handlers, and auth middleware sits between the two.

An endpoint that returns data only when the caller presents a valid credential, initially tracked with an in-memory/opaque token map. This exposes the core problem: the server must look up the token to know who's calling, which motivates the stateful-vs-stateless tradeoff next.

Opaque tokens are random strings the server must store and look up (stateful — easy to revoke, needs a store/DB per request); JWTs are self-contained signed payloads the server verifies with a secret (stateless — no lookup, but revocation is hard). The classic tradeoff: server-side session store vs. stateless scalability, and revocation is the JWT weakness.

A JWT is base64url(header).base64url(payload).signature, where the signature is HMAC/RSA over header+payload using a server secret (HS256) or private key (RS256). Verification recomputes the signature to guarantee integrity — the payload can't be tampered without invalidating it. Know the difference between HS256 (shared secret) and RS256 (asymmetric, good for multi-service verification).

Swap the in-memory token map for jwt.sign(payload, secret) on login and jwt.verify(token, secret) on protected routes — the server no longer stores per-user session state. This is the concrete refactor from stateful to stateless auth and demonstrates why JWTs scale horizontally without a shared session store.

bullets.step: JWTs can be decoded by anyone
bullets.point: The payload is base64-encoded, NOT encrypted — anyone with the token can read its claims at jwt.io. The signature protects integrity (tamper detection), not confidentiality, so never put secrets/passwords in a JWT; if you need confidentiality use JWE or keep sensitive data server-side. This is a frequent gotcha question.

A reusable Express middleware that extracts the token from the Authorization: Bearer header, verifies it, attaches req.userId, and calls next() or returns 401. This is the DRY pattern for protecting many routes and the standard place interviewers expect verification and error handling to live.

On login the client stores the JWT (localStorage/sessionStorage or cookie) and sends it on subsequent requests via the Authorization header. Interview angle: localStorage is vulnerable to XSS, httpOnly cookies are vulnerable to CSRF — the storage choice is a security tradeoff, not a default.

A full-stack build tying signup/login/JWT/middleware together with per-user data isolation — every TODO query is scoped to req.userId from the verified token. It forces the authorization step: authenticated is not enough, you must ensure users only access their own resources (prevents IDOR/broken object-level authorization).

example.title: A multi-tenant TODO/notes API for a small SaaS
example.body: You're building the backend for a notes app used by thousands of users. On POST /signup you bcrypt-hash the password and store it; on POST /login you compare the hash and return a signed JWT containing { userId } (HS256, 24h expiry). The React frontend saves the token and sends it as Authorization: Bearer <token> on every call. An auth middleware verifies the signature, rejects expired/tampered tokens with 401, and sets req.userId. Every notes query is then scoped by req.userId so user A can never read user B's notes. In production you hit the classic issues: a leaked token can't be revoked before expiry (so you add short TTLs plus a refresh-token flow and a small server-side denylist), you realize you accidentally put the user's email in the JWT payload which is publicly decodable, and you debug a 403-vs-401 mixup where a valid token was hitting an admin-only route. This scenario exercises hashing, stateless verification, per-user authorization, and the real operational tradeoffs of JWTs.

qa.q: What's the difference between authentication and authorization, and which HTTP status codes map to each?
qa.a: Authentication verifies identity — proving you are who you claim (login, credentials, token validity); its failure is 401 Unauthorized. Authorization verifies permissions — whether an authenticated principal may perform an action or access a resource; its failure is 403 Forbidden. A request can be authenticated (valid token) yet unauthorized (a normal user hitting an admin route).

qa.q: Opaque tokens vs JWTs — when would you choose each?
qa.a: Opaque (random) tokens are stateful: the server stores them and looks each one up, which makes revocation trivial (delete the row) but requires a shared session store and a DB hit per request. JWTs are stateless and self-verifying via a signature, so they scale horizontally with no lookup, but you can't easily revoke one before expiry. Choose JWTs for scalable, short-lived access with a refresh flow; choose opaque tokens when instant revocation and central session control matter more than avoiding lookups.

qa.q: If a JWT can be decoded by anyone, what actually makes it secure?
qa.a: The payload is base64url-encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read the claims — security comes from the signature, not secrecy. The server signs header+payload with a secret (HS256) or private key (RS256); verification recomputes the signature, so any tampering with the claims invalidates it. It guarantees integrity and authenticity, not confidentiality — so never store secrets in the payload.

qa.q: How do you handle JWT revocation and logout given tokens are stateless?
qa.a: Since a valid JWT stays valid until it expires, you can't truly "log out" server-side without adding state. Common approaches: keep access tokens short-lived (minutes) plus a long-lived refresh token that can be revoked in the DB; maintain a denylist/blocklist of revoked token IDs (jti) checked on each request; or bump a per-user token version that's embedded as a claim and compared. Each reintroduces some server state, which is the fundamental tradeoff against stateless auth.

qa.q: Where should the frontend store a JWT, and what are the security tradeoffs?
qa.a: localStorage/sessionStorage is simple and immune to CSRF but readable by any injected script, so it's exposed to XSS. An httpOnly cookie is invisible to JavaScript (mitigates XSS token theft) but is sent automatically, exposing you to CSRF unless you add SameSite and/or CSRF tokens. There's no free lunch — pick based on your threat model; httpOnly + SameSite=strict/lax cookies is often the safer default for browser apps.

qa.q: How do you securely store passwords, and why not just hash with SHA-256?
qa.a: Use a slow, salted, adaptive hash like bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 with a per-user salt and a tunable work factor. Fast hashes like SHA-256 are the problem: they let attackers try billions of guesses per second and enable rainbow-table/precomputation attacks. Adaptive functions are deliberately slow and memory-hard, and the per-user salt ensures identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed tables.

qa.q: What is broken object-level authorization (IDOR) and how does it apply to the TODO app?
qa.a: IDOR happens when an endpoint authenticates the user but doesn't check that the requested resource belongs to them — e.g. GET /todos/123 returns todo 123 regardless of owner. Being authenticated is not being authorized; every data access must be scoped to req.userId derived from the verified token (WHERE userId = req.userId), not just to "any logged-in user." It's OWASP's top API risk and a very common interview/design bug.

qa.q: Why put auth logic in Express middleware instead of each route handler?
qa.a: A single middleware extracts and verifies the token, handles 401s, and attaches req.userId in one place, so protected routes stay DRY and can't accidentally skip verification. It centralizes the trust boundary — one audited code path for token parsing and error handling — and lets you compose it (e.g. authMiddleware then adminMiddleware). Ordering matters: it must run after body/CORS parsing and before the protected handlers.

bullets.point: Solana is a high-throughput L1 combining Proof of History (a verifiable clock that orders events before consensus), Tower BFT PoS, the Sealevel parallel runtime, and Gulf Stream tx forwarding, giving ~400ms slots and sub-cent fees. Interview angle: the parallel runtime is why transactions must declare every account they read/write upfront - non-overlapping accounts execute concurrently, unlike Ethereum's sequential EVM.

Everything on Solana is an account: a record with a lamports balance, an owner (a program), a data byte-array, and an executable flag. Programs are stateless executable accounts; their state lives in separate data accounts they own. Interview angle: this code/state separation contrasts with EVM contracts that bundle logic and storage, and it is what enables account-level parallelism.

Set up solana-cli plus spl-token CLI, configure the cluster (solana config set --url devnet), generate a keypair (solana-keygen), and airdrop devnet SOL. Foundational tooling for creating mints and inspecting accounts before touching client SDKs.

In Web2 a trusted server owns a central database; rows are keyed by a user id, freely mutable, and access is gated by app-layer auth. Set up as the contrast baseline: state ownership and mutation authority sit entirely with the operator.

State is spread across many accounts owned by programs rather than rows in one DB; there is no ambient trust, only account ownership and signatures. Token balances live in per-holder token accounts, not in the mint - the mint holds supply/decimals/authorities while balances are separate accounts. Interview angle: understand mint account vs token account (ATA) separation.

Use the SPL Token program: create a mint account specifying decimals, mint authority, and optional freeze authority, then create associated token accounts and mint_to them. The spl-token CLI wraps these instructions; the mint defines the asset, ATAs hold balances.

With @solana/web3.js + @solana/spl-token you call createMint, getOrCreateAssociatedTokenAccount, and mintTo, which assemble instructions into a transaction you sign and send. Interview angle: these helpers are just instruction builders - a transaction is an ordered list of instructions targeting program ids with explicit account metas and data.

The same mint/ATA/mint_to instructions can be built from Rust, Python (solana-py/solders), or Go SDKs because the on-chain program is language-agnostic. Interview angle: clients only serialize the same instruction data and accounts; the SDK language never changes on-chain behavior.

Program Derived Addresses are addresses deliberately pushed off the ed25519 curve, derived deterministically from seeds + program id + a bump byte, so they have no private key. The owning program 'signs' for a PDA via invoke_signed, making PDAs ideal for deterministic state accounts and program-controlled authorities - Associated Token Accounts are themselves PDAs. High-frequency interview topic.

Token-2022 (Token Extensions) is a newer SPL token program at a distinct program id that is a superset of the original, adding opt-in extensions like transfer fees, transfer hooks, confidential transfers, non-transferable/soulbound, and interest-bearing tokens. Interview angle: it is not automatically backward compatible - the program id differs, so wallets/clients must explicitly support it, and extensions change account sizing and transfer semantics.

The metadata (and metadata-pointer) extension stores name/symbol/uri directly on the mint via the Token Metadata interface, avoiding a separate Metaplex metadata PDA. Interview angle: contrast on-mint metadata (self-contained, resizable account) with the legacy Metaplex approach where metadata lives in a separate program-owned account.

example.title: Launching an on-chain rewards token for a consumer app
example.body: You are the SDE-2 shipping 'GRAV points', a loyalty token users earn in-app on Solana. You create a mint on Token-2022 with 6 decimals, set the mint authority to a PDA derived from your program (so only your program can mint, not a leaked keypair), and attach the metadata extension so the name, symbol, and logo URI live directly on the mint. When a user earns points, your backend derives their Associated Token Account (itself a PDA), creates it if missing via getOrCreateAssociatedTokenAccount, and calls mintTo with the program signing for the mint-authority PDA through invoke_signed. Two bugs you will actually debug: (1) 'incorrect program id for instruction' because you passed the classic Token program id to a Token-2022 mint, and (2) transfers failing because you added a transfer-fee extension and the recipient's ATA was sized for the base token layout. When finance later asks to freeze compromised balances, you use the freeze authority you set at mint creation; when they ask to cap supply, you set the mint authority to null to make it immutable.

qa.q: What is the difference between a mint account and a token account, and where does a user's balance live?
qa.a: The mint account is the asset definition - it stores decimals, total supply, and the mint/freeze authorities, but no per-user balances. Each holder has a separate token account (typically an Associated Token Account, a PDA) that references the mint and stores that holder's amount. So one mint has many token accounts; balances live in the token accounts, never in the mint itself.

qa.q: What is a PDA and how can a program authorize actions from an address with no private key?
qa.a: A Program Derived Address is derived from seeds + the program id + a bump byte, chosen so the result falls off the ed25519 curve - meaning no private key can exist for it. A program signs on the PDA's behalf using invoke_signed, passing the same seeds and bump; the runtime verifies the derivation and treats the PDA as a signer only within that program's CPI. This gives you deterministic, program-controlled accounts and authorities without managing secret keys.

qa.q: Why must a Solana transaction list every account it will read or write, and what does that enable?
qa.a: The Sealevel runtime executes transactions in parallel by inspecting their declared account access lists: transactions with no overlapping writable accounts run concurrently. Declaring accounts upfront lets the scheduler detect conflicts statically instead of discovering them mid-execution. The trade-off is developer friction - you must know and pass all accounts, including PDAs, in advance.

qa.q: How does Solana's account model differ from Ethereum's contract storage model?
qa.a: On Ethereum a contract bundles its bytecode and its storage in one address, and state is mutated internally. On Solana programs are stateless executable accounts; all mutable state lives in separate data accounts the program owns. This separation enables the same program to operate over many independent state accounts in parallel and makes upgrade/state boundaries explicit, but requires passing state accounts into every instruction.

qa.q: When would you choose Token-2022 over the original SPL Token program, and what is the main risk?
qa.a: Choose Token-2022 when you need capabilities the base program lacks - transfer fees, transfer hooks, confidential transfers, non-transferable tokens, interest-bearing, or on-mint metadata. The main risk is ecosystem compatibility: it lives at a different program id, so wallets, DEXs, and clients must explicitly support it, and extensions like transfer hooks can inject custom logic (or fees) into every transfer, which is both a feature and an attack surface you must audit.

qa.q: What is rent and rent-exemption on Solana, and why does it matter for token accounts?
qa.a: Every account consumes on-chain storage, so it must hold a minimum lamport balance proportional to its data size to be rent-exempt; below that it would be purged. Practically all accounts today are created rent-exempt by funding that minimum at creation. It matters because creating a mint or an ATA costs SOL, and Token-2022 extensions increase account size, raising the rent-exempt minimum - a common source of 'insufficient funds' errors when creating extended accounts.

qa.q: What do the mint authority and freeze authority control, and how do you create a fixed-supply token?
qa.a: The mint authority is the only signer allowed to mint new tokens; the freeze authority can freeze/thaw individual token accounts, blocking transfers. To make supply permanently fixed, set the mint authority to null (SetAuthority to None) after minting the desired amount - after that no more tokens can ever be created. Leaving the freeze authority set is a centralization signal holders often check, so many projects disable it too.

qa.q: A createMint call in @solana/web3.js succeeds but mintTo fails with an owner/program mismatch. How do you diagnose it?
qa.a: This almost always means the mint was created under one token program but the ATA or mintTo call used the other. Token-2022 and the classic Token program have distinct program ids, and helpers default to the classic one unless you pass the TOKEN_2022_PROGRAM_ID. Verify the mint's owner field matches the program id you pass to getOrCreateAssociatedTokenAccount and mintTo; aligning all three (mint owner, ATA program, mintTo program) resolves it.

bullets.point: In-memory arrays/globals reset on every process restart and can't be shared across horizontally-scaled server instances. A database gives durable, concurrent, queryable state — this framing is the interview-standard justification for introducing a DB tier instead of storing data in the app.

A database is a system for durable storage, concurrent access, and efficient querying/indexing, with guarantees (ACID for relational, tunable consistency for many NoSQL). Interview angle: know the axes that differentiate them — data model (relational/document/key-value/graph), consistency model, and scaling story (vertical vs horizontal sharding).

MongoDB is a document database storing BSON (binary JSON) documents in collections, with a flexible/implicit schema instead of rigid tables. It suits denormalized, read-heavy, evolving-shape data; the key trade-off vs SQL is no cross-document joins by default and app-enforced schema, in exchange for easier horizontal sharding and developer velocity.

MongoDB Atlas gives a free-tier managed cluster; you create a DB user, allow-list IPs, and get a SRV connection string. Interview-relevant hygiene: the connection string holds credentials so it belongs in an env var/secret, never committed, and IP allow-listing plus least-privilege DB users are baseline security.

Seeding pre-populates the DB with baseline/reference or test data via a script (insertMany or a seed file) so dev and CI environments start in a known state. It matters for reproducible local setup and integration tests, and the script should be idempotent so re-running doesn't create duplicates.

Create/Read/Update/Delete map to insertOne/find/updateOne/deleteOne (native driver) or Mongoose model methods. SDE-2 depth: understand query filters, projection, atomic update operators ($set/$inc/$push) that avoid read-modify-write races, and that update/delete take a filter — a missing filter or a typo can touch far more documents than intended.

A REST backend wires Express routes to Mongoose models, connecting once at startup and mapping HTTP verbs to CRUD. Interview angle: define a schema/model, keep the DB connection as a singleton (don't reconnect per request), and structure routes/controllers so validation and error handling can be layered cleanly.

Exercise each endpoint (Postman/curl or automated tests) covering happy path and failure cases — bad payloads, missing auth, not-found IDs. The point is to verify status codes and response shapes, not just that the DB write happened; for real tests use a dedicated/ephemeral test DB (or mongodb-memory-server) so tests are isolated and repeatable.

Refactor the naive app toward production shape: authentication (JWT), middleware for cross-cutting concerns, separation of routes/controllers/models, and config via env vars. This step is where you introduce the concerns that the next steps formalize — secure credentials, robust errors, and validated input.

Never store plaintext passwords — hash with a slow, salted adaptive algorithm like bcrypt (salt is generated per-password and embedded in the hash; a cost/rounds factor makes brute-force expensive). Interview must-knows: hashing is one-way (vs reversible encryption), per-user salts defeat rainbow tables, and you compare via bcrypt.compare rather than re-hashing and string-matching.

Wrap async route logic so failures surface as proper HTTP responses instead of unhandled rejections/500s, ideally via a centralized Express error-handling middleware (the 4-arg (err,req,res,next) signature). SDE-2 depth: distinguish operational errors (bad input, not found → 4xx) from programmer errors, avoid leaking stack traces/internals to clients, and remember async errors must be forwarded to next() or caught by a wrapper.

Zod defines a schema and validates/parses untrusted request input at the boundary, giving both runtime safety and static TypeScript types via z.infer. Why it matters: never trust client input (injection, malformed data, type confusion); use safeParse to return structured 400 errors, and validation belongs before any DB write, typically as middleware.

Model relationships either by embedding subdocuments (denormalized, single-read, good for contained one-to-few data) or by referencing ObjectIds and joining with populate/$lookup (normalized, good for shared or unbounded data). The core trade-off is read performance and atomicity (embedding) vs data duplication and update complexity (referencing) — choose based on access patterns and cardinality.

example.title: Multi-tenant Todo/Task API backed by MongoDB Atlas
example.body: You're building the backend for a task-management app: users sign up, log in, and manage their own todos. You provision a free Atlas cluster and keep the SRV connection string in an env var. Mongoose models define User and Todo, with each Todo referencing its owner via an ObjectId (userId) rather than embedding, since a user's todo list is unbounded and frequently updated. On signup you hash the password with bcrypt before saving; on login you bcrypt.compare and issue a JWT. Every mutating route runs Zod validation middleware first (rejecting malformed bodies with a 400 and a structured error list), then performs the CRUD operation scoped to the authenticated user's id so one user can never read or delete another's todos. All async handlers are wrapped so thrown errors — a duplicate email, an invalid ObjectId, a DB timeout — flow into a central error-handling middleware that maps them to clean 4xx/5xx responses without leaking stack traces. When you later add a 'fetch todos with owner name' endpoint, you use populate to resolve the referenced user. This single app touches every step: Atlas setup, schema/CRUD, hashing, validation, error handling, and reference-based relationships.

qa.q: When would you embed documents versus reference them with ObjectIds in MongoDB?
qa.a: Embed when the related data is owned by and read together with the parent, is bounded in size, and doesn't need to be queried independently — e.g. a user's address or a post's small comment list — because it gives single-read access and atomic updates on the whole document. Reference when the data is shared across parents, grows unboundedly, or is updated independently — e.g. users↔orders — to avoid duplication and the 16MB document limit. The deciding factors are access patterns, cardinality, and how often the sub-data changes; reference reads require a populate/$lookup join, which is the cost you pay for normalization.

qa.q: Why hash passwords with bcrypt instead of a fast hash like SHA-256, and what does the salt do?
qa.a: Fast hashes are designed for speed, which is exactly wrong for passwords — an attacker with the hash dump can compute billions of guesses per second. bcrypt is deliberately slow with a configurable cost factor, so you can tune it to stay expensive as hardware improves. The salt is a unique random value per password (bcrypt generates and stores it inside the hash string) that ensures identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow tables and making each hash independently attackable. Verification uses bcrypt.compare, which re-derives the hash using the embedded salt.

qa.q: How does error handling work in Express for async route handlers, and why is a centralized error middleware preferred?
qa.a: Express's default error handling only catches synchronous throws; an error thrown or a rejected promise inside an async handler must be passed to next(err) (or caught by an async-wrapper utility) or it becomes an unhandled rejection and the request hangs or 500s. A centralized error-handling middleware — the special four-argument (err, req, res, next) signature registered last — is preferred because it puts all response formatting, logging, and status-code mapping in one place instead of duplicating try/catch response logic in every route. It also lets you distinguish operational errors (map to 4xx) from unexpected ones (500) and ensures you never leak stack traces to clients.

qa.q: Why validate input with Zod at the API boundary when Mongoose already has schema validation?
qa.a: They operate at different layers and serve different goals. Zod validates untrusted request input before it reaches business logic or the DB, gives you structured, client-friendly 400 errors, coerces/transforms shapes, and — crucially in TypeScript — produces a static type via z.infer so your validated data and your types can't drift. Mongoose validation is a last-line DB-schema guard but runs later, couples validation to the persistence layer, and produces less friendly errors. Validating early at the edge follows 'never trust the client' and prevents malformed data from ever touching your handlers.

qa.q: What's the difference between Zod's parse and safeParse, and which belongs in a request handler?
qa.a: parse throws a ZodError on invalid input, while safeParse returns a discriminated result object ({ success: true, data } or { success: false, error }) without throwing. In a request handler, safeParse is usually cleaner because you branch on success and return a 400 with the formatted error, avoiding try/catch for expected validation failures. parse is fine when you've centralized error handling and want a ZodError to bubble up to your error middleware, which then formats it — either works, but the choice should be consistent with how the rest of your errors flow.

qa.q: How do you keep atomic, race-free updates in MongoDB?
qa.a: Use atomic update operators like $set, $inc, and $push in updateOne/findOneAndUpdate rather than reading a document into the app, mutating it, and writing it back — the read-modify-write pattern has a lost-update race under concurrency. Operators like $inc apply server-side atomically per document. For invariants that span the read and the write (e.g. 'decrement stock only if > 0'), express the condition in the query filter itself, or use findOneAndUpdate with the condition so the check and update are one atomic operation. Multi-document atomicity requires transactions on a replica set.

qa.q: Why should the MongoDB connection be established once at startup rather than per request?
qa.a: Opening a connection is expensive (TCP + TLS handshake + auth), and the driver maintains an internal connection pool that's meant to be reused across requests. Connecting per request exhausts the pool, adds latency to every call, and can crash the DB with connection storms under load. The correct pattern is to connect once at boot (awaiting it before serving traffic) and let the driver's pool multiplex concurrent queries; in serverless environments you additionally cache the connection across warm invocations to avoid reconnecting on every cold-ish call.

qa.q: How would you test the todo backend without polluting or depending on production data?
qa.a: Point tests at an isolated database — either a dedicated test cluster, a per-run ephemeral DB, or an in-memory instance like mongodb-memory-server — and seed a known state before each test, cleaning up after so tests don't leak into each other. Cover both happy paths and failure modes: invalid payloads (expect 400 from validation), unauthenticated/unauthorized access (401/403 and correct user-scoping), and missing resources (404). Assert on status codes and response shape, not just that a write occurred, so the API contract itself is under test.

bullets.point: Context lets you pass shared state down the tree without prop-drilling via a Provider/Consumer (or useContext). The interview angle: Context is a dependency-injection mechanism, not a state manager — any change to the Provider's value re-renders every consuming component, which becomes the core motivation for Recoil.

Recoil is a state-management library from Meta that models state as a graph of atoms (units of state) and selectors (derived state), consumed via hooks like useRecoilState. It matters because it gives you fine-grained, subscription-based reactivity with a React-idiomatic API and minimal boilerplate compared to Redux.

With Context, one value object drives all consumers, so an update forces every consumer to re-render regardless of which field changed; Recoil subscribes each component only to the specific atoms/selectors it reads, so unrelated components don't re-render. The trade-off: Context is built-in and zero-dependency (great for low-frequency, truly shared config like theme/auth), while Recoil adds a dependency but scales far better for frequently-changing, granular state.

An atom is the smallest unit of shared, writable state, defined with a unique key and default, and read/written via useRecoilState (or useRecoilValue / useSetRecoilState). Any component subscribing to an atom re-renders only when that atom changes — the key mechanism behind Recoil's performance story; the unique key also enables persistence and debugging.

React.memo memoizes a component so it skips re-rendering when its props are shallow-equal to the previous render. In the Recoil discussion it matters because atom-level subscriptions plus memo let you contain re-renders to exactly the components whose data changed — and it illustrates the difference between preventing re-renders via props (memo) versus via granular subscriptions (atoms).

Instead of useRecoilState, use useSetRecoilState in components that only mutate state and useRecoilValue where you only read it. This avoids subscribing a write-only component to value changes, so a button that sets state never re-renders when the value updates — a common Recoil optimization pattern.

A selector is derived state: a pure function (get) that computes a value from atoms/other selectors, with optional set to make it writable, and it memoizes and recomputes only when its dependencies change. It matters because it moves computed/aggregated logic out of components into the data graph, supports async data (returning Promises that integrate with Suspense), and keeps derived values consistent and cached.

example.title: A dashboard navbar counter that re-renders the whole page
example.body: You build an admin dashboard where a top navbar shows a live 'pending notifications' count while a large table, sidebar, and charts render below. Initially you keep the count in a Context Provider wrapping the app. Every time the count polls and updates, the entire subtree under the Provider re-renders — the heavy table and charts flicker and the page janks, because Context has no per-field subscriptions. You migrate the count to a Recoil atom: the navbar badge reads it with useRecoilValue and re-renders on every tick, while the table and charts (which never read that atom) stay untouched. You then add a notificationSummarySelector that derives 'unread vs total' from the atom so the badge label logic lives in the data graph, and the polling component uses useSetRecoilState so it can write the count without itself subscribing to it. React.memo on the table row component guarantees rows don't re-render from unrelated parent updates. The result: updates that once re-rendered the whole dashboard now touch only the small badge.

qa.q: Why does the Context API cause unnecessary re-renders, and how does Recoil avoid them?
qa.a: When a Context Provider's value changes, React re-renders every component that calls useContext on it, because the subscription is to the whole value object with no field-level granularity — memoization on the consumer doesn't help since the context value itself changed. Recoil instead has each component subscribe to specific atoms/selectors it reads, so only components whose exact piece of state changed re-render. That fine-grained subscription model is the primary reason to reach for Recoil over Context for frequently-updating state.

qa.q: When would you still use Context over Recoil?
qa.a: Use Context for low-frequency, broadly-shared values that rarely change — theme, locale, current user/auth, or a DI container for services — where the re-render cost is negligible and you want zero extra dependencies. Context is also fine when the shared value is essentially static after load. Recoil earns its keep when state changes often and is read by many components that should update independently, or when you need derived/async state.

qa.q: What is an atom and how do useRecoilState, useRecoilValue, and useSetRecoilState differ?
qa.a: An atom is a unit of shared writable state identified by a unique key with a default value. useRecoilState returns [value, setter] and subscribes the component to changes; useRecoilValue returns only the value and subscribes; useSetRecoilState returns only the setter and does NOT subscribe. Choosing the read-only or write-only hook prevents unnecessary subscriptions — a write-only button won't re-render when the value changes, which is a standard optimization.

qa.q: What is a selector and how is it different from an atom?
qa.a: A selector is derived state defined by a pure get function that computes a value from atoms or other selectors; an atom is a source of truth you write to directly. Selectors are memoized and only recompute when their tracked dependencies change, and they can be made writable by adding a set function. They keep computed/aggregated logic out of components and guarantee derived values stay consistent with their inputs.

qa.q: How does Recoil handle asynchronous data?
qa.a: A selector's get can return a Promise, and Recoil integrates that with React Suspense — components reading the selector suspend until it resolves and show a fallback, with errors surfaced to error boundaries. Recoil caches the result by dependency values, so it acts as a lightweight async data cache. This lets you model server-fetched, derived data declaratively in the same graph as synchronous state, though for heavy server-cache needs many teams pair it with or prefer React Query.

qa.q: Why is every atom and selector required to have a unique key?
qa.a: The key is a stable string identifier Recoil uses to track the node in its internal state graph, deduplicate definitions, and support features like persistence and time-travel debugging. Duplicate keys throw (or warn), and hot-reload can surface key collisions. It's essentially the node's identity across the app and across serialization boundaries.

qa.q: How do atoms plus React.memo work together, and what problem does each solve?
qa.a: They address re-renders at different layers: React.memo skips a component's render when its incoming props are shallow-equal, protecting it from parent-driven re-renders; atom subscriptions ensure a component only re-renders when the specific state it reads changes. Atoms cut re-renders caused by shared state, memo cuts re-renders caused by prop propagation from parents. Used together you can constrain updates to precisely the components whose data actually changed.

qa.q: What are the downsides or risks of adopting Recoil?
qa.a: Recoil was experimental for a long time and its maintenance/activity has been uncertain, so betting a large codebase on it carries ecosystem risk versus more established options like Redux Toolkit, Zustand, or Jotai (which shares the atomic model). It requires a RecoilRoot wrapper, global unique keys can collide, and its testing/devtools story is thinner than Redux's. For simple apps the granular API can also be overkill compared to Context or a small store.

bullets.point: A WebSocket is a persistent, full-duplex, bidirectional channel over a single TCP connection, established by 'upgrading' an ordinary HTTP request (client sends Upgrade: websocket + Sec-WebSocket-Key, server replies 101 Switching Protocols). After the handshake, both sides push framed messages at any time with no per-message HTTP header overhead. Interview angle: know when to reach for it vs HTTP polling, long-polling, or SSE — WebSockets win when you need low-latency server-to-client push AND client-to-server messages (chat, live cursors, trading, multiplayer).

Node's single-threaded, non-blocking event loop is well suited to holding many idle-but-open socket connections cheaply, since each connection is mostly waiting on I/O rather than burning CPU. Native Node has no browser-style WebSocket server API, so you use the `ws` library, which piggybacks on an existing http.Server and handles the framing/handshake. Interview angle: the constraint isn't CPU per message, it's file descriptors and memory per open connection, plus the fact that CPU-heavy message handling blocks all connections on that event loop.

You instantiate `new WebSocketServer({ server })` (or `{ port }`), then handle the `connection` event to get a per-client socket, and on that socket handle `message`, `close`, and `error`; you send with `ws.send()` and broadcast by iterating `wss.clients`. Interview angle: be ready to discuss tracking connection state (rooms/user maps), heartbeat/ping-pong to detect dead peers (`ws.isAlive` + `terminate()`), and backpressure via `ws.bufferedAmount` for slow consumers.

The browser exposes the WebSocket API: `new WebSocket(url)` with `onopen`, `onmessage`, `onclose`, `onerror` handlers and a `send()` method; `wss://` is the TLS variant. Production clients must add reconnection with exponential backoff + jitter, app-level heartbeats, and re-subscription/state resync after reconnect. Interview angle: the network can drop a connection silently, so you can't trust `onclose` alone — heartbeats and idempotent resync are what separate a toy from a real client.

WebSockets are stateful — a client is pinned to one server instance for the life of the connection — so a naive multi-instance deploy breaks broadcasts because user A on server 1 can't reach user B on server 2. Solve fan-out with a shared pub/sub backplane (Redis Pub/Sub, Kafka, or NATS): each node subscribes to channels and relays published messages to its locally connected clients; use sticky sessions / connection-aware load balancing at the LB. Interview angle: discuss the connection ceiling per node (fds, memory), horizontal scaling limits, and why the pub/sub layer (not the ws nodes) usually becomes the bottleneck.

example.title: Multi-instance live chat / collaboration service
example.body: You're building the real-time layer for a collaboration app (think shared docs presence + chat). A browser client opens a `wss://` connection and joins room-123. On the server, `ws` accepts the upgrade, stores the socket in an in-memory map keyed by room, and runs a 30s ping/pong heartbeat to evict dead connections. When a user sends a message, the node persists it and needs to broadcast to everyone in room-123 — but you run 4 Node instances behind an ALB, and the room's members are spread across all of them. So instead of only iterating local `wss.clients`, each node publishes the message to a Redis channel `room:123`; every node is subscribed and relays it to its own locally-connected room members. The LB uses sticky sessions so a client's HTTP upgrade and subsequent frames land on the same node. On the client you wrap the socket in a reconnect-with-backoff helper that re-joins the room and refetches missed messages after any drop. Classic debugging story: messages work with one instance but silently fail to fan out after you scale to two — the fix is the pub/sub backplane.

qa.q: How does a WebSocket connection get established, and how does it relate to HTTP?
qa.a: It starts as a normal HTTP/1.1 GET with `Upgrade: websocket`, `Connection: Upgrade`, and a base64 `Sec-WebSocket-Key`. The server validates it, echoes back a hashed `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`, and returns `101 Switching Protocols`; from that point the same TCP connection is used for full-duplex WebSocket frames instead of HTTP. This reuse of the HTTP handshake is why WebSocket servers can share a port/server with HTTP and why WebSockets traverse most proxies and firewalls.

qa.q: When would you choose WebSockets over SSE or long-polling?
qa.a: Use WebSockets when you need low-latency, bidirectional traffic — the client sends frequently and the server pushes frequently (chat, multiplayer, collaborative editing, live trading). SSE is simpler and fine when you only need server-to-client push over HTTP and are okay with text/one-way (dashboards, notifications), and it auto-reconnects. Long-polling is a fallback for environments that block upgrades, but it has higher latency and overhead; WebSockets are the right default when true duplex is the requirement.

qa.q: Why are WebSockets harder to scale horizontally than stateless HTTP?
qa.a: A WebSocket is a long-lived, stateful connection pinned to a single server instance, so the usual 'any request to any node' model breaks. Broadcasting to a logical group (a room) requires reaching clients that may be connected to different nodes, so you need a shared pub/sub backplane (Redis/Kafka/NATS) for fan-out and sticky routing at the load balancer. You also cap out on per-node resources — open file descriptors and memory per connection — long before CPU, so scaling is about connection count, not request throughput.

qa.q: How do you detect and handle dead or half-open WebSocket connections?
qa.a: TCP can silently drop a peer (network partition, laptop sleep) without a clean `close` frame, so you can't rely on the `close` event. Implement application-level heartbeats: the server sends periodic pings, marks each socket `isAlive=false` before sending, sets it true on `pong`, and `terminate()`s any socket that didn't respond by the next interval. On the client, run a matching heartbeat plus reconnect-with-backoff, because `onclose`/`onerror` may fire late or not at all.

qa.q: What is backpressure in a WebSocket server and how do you handle a slow consumer?
qa.a: If you push messages faster than a client can receive them, they queue in the server's send buffer (`ws.bufferedAmount` grows), consuming memory and eventually risking OOM across many slow clients. Handle it by checking `bufferedAmount` before sending and dropping/coalescing messages, applying per-connection rate limits, or disconnecting clients that fall too far behind. This matters most for fan-out heavy workloads like market data where one stalled client shouldn't degrade the whole node.

qa.q: Walk through how a broadcast to a chat room works across multiple server instances.
qa.a: Each instance keeps only its locally-connected sockets, grouped by room, in memory. When a message arrives, the receiving node publishes it to a shared channel (e.g. Redis `PUBLISH room:123 payload`); every instance is subscribed to that channel and, on receipt, relays the payload to its own local members of room-123. This decouples senders from receivers so any node can originate a broadcast, and the load balancer uses sticky sessions so a given client's frames consistently hit the node that holds its socket. The pub/sub layer, not the ws nodes, typically becomes the scaling bottleneck.

qa.q: What are the main security concerns with WebSockets?
qa.a: First, authentication/authorization: the browser WebSocket API can't set custom headers, so you typically auth via a token in the query string or a cookie during the upgrade, then validate it before accepting the connection — and re-check authz per message for sensitive actions. Second, CSWSH (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking): WebSockets aren't bound by the same-origin policy, so you must validate the `Origin` header server-side. Also enforce `wss://` (TLS), input validation on messages, and per-connection rate limiting to prevent abuse.

qa.q: What happens to WebSocket connections during a deploy or restart, and how do you minimize disruption?
qa.a: A restart drops every open connection on that instance simultaneously, causing a reconnect storm that can hammer the new instances and any auth/pub-sub dependencies. Mitigate with graceful shutdown (stop accepting new connections, send `close` frames, drain over a window), client reconnect with exponential backoff + jitter to spread the herd, and idempotent state resync so a reconnected client refetches anything missed. Rolling deploys plus a connection-draining-aware load balancer keep only a fraction of connections churning at once.

bullets.point: Scaffold with Vite (`npm create vite@latest`) rather than CRA — Vite uses esbuild for dev and Rollup for prod, giving near-instant cold start and true HMR. Interview angle: know that JSX is transpiled by the bundler (esbuild/Babel/SWC) into `React.createElement`/`jsx()` calls, and that the dev server is not what ships to prod.

A component is a pure function of props (and state) that returns JSX describing UI; composition replaces inheritance. Interview angle: distinguish a component (the function) from an element (the object it returns) and know rendering should be side-effect-free so React can call it multiple times safely.

`useState` gives a component local state; calling the setter schedules a re-render and state is preserved across renders by call order. Key points: updates are asynchronous and batched, you must treat state as immutable, and use the functional updater `setX(x => x+1)` when the new value depends on the previous to avoid stale reads.

A component re-renders when its own state changes, its parent re-renders, or its context value changes — not simply because a prop 'looks' different. Interview angle: use the React DevTools Profiler / 'highlight updates' to see wasted renders, and understand a re-render recomputes JSX but only commits actual DOM diffs.

Runs side effects (data fetching, subscriptions, manual DOM) after the render is committed and painted; the dependency array controls when it re-runs and the returned function is the cleanup. Interview angle: missing deps cause stale closures, empty deps `[]` means run-once, and StrictMode double-invokes effects in dev to surface missing cleanup.

Props are the read-only inputs passed parent-to-child, enforcing one-way (top-down) data flow. Interview angle: props are immutable from the child's view; to communicate upward you pass callbacks down, and mutating props directly is a bug.

Render branches with ternaries, `&&`, or early returns; returning `null` renders nothing. Interview angle: the classic footgun is `count && <X/>` rendering a literal `0` when count is 0 — guard with an explicit boolean (`count > 0 && ...`).

`children` is the implicit prop holding whatever JSX is nested inside a component's tags, enabling generic wrapper/slot components (cards, modals, layouts). Interview angle: it powers composition and inversion of control, letting you build reusable containers without them knowing their contents.

Render collections with `.map()` and give each element a stable, unique `key`. Interview angle: keys let React match elements across renders during reconciliation — using the array index as key corrupts component state and causes subtle bugs when items are inserted, removed, or reordered.

The `style` prop takes a JS object with camelCased properties (`backgroundColor`) rather than a CSS string. Interview angle: inline styles are convenient for dynamic values but can't use pseudo-classes/media queries and create a new object every render; prefer `className`/CSS modules for static styling.

Class components use `this.state`/lifecycle methods; functional components use hooks and are now the standard. Interview angle: hooks won because they let you reuse stateful logic without HOC/render-prop wrapper hell, avoid `this`-binding bugs, and colocate related logic instead of scattering it across lifecycle methods.

The three phases are mount, update, and unmount; in classes these are `componentDidMount`/`componentDidUpdate`/`componentWillUnmount`. Interview angle: be able to map them to hooks — mount+update is `useEffect(fn)`, mount-only is `useEffect(fn, [])`, and unmount cleanup is the effect's returned function.

A class component implementing `getDerivedStateFromError`/`componentDidCatch` that catches render-phase errors in its subtree and shows fallback UI instead of crashing the whole app. Interview angle: they do NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code, or SSR — those need try/catch; there is still no hook equivalent, so boundaries remain class-based.

`<React.Fragment>` / the shorthand `<>...</>` groups siblings without adding an extra DOM wrapper node. Interview angle: it satisfies the single-root-element rule while keeping the DOM (and flex/grid layouts) clean; use the explicit `<Fragment key=...>` form when a fragment is a list item.

An SPA loads one HTML shell and swaps views on the client; React Router intercepts navigation via the History API so there's no full page reload. Interview angle: understand client-side vs server-side routing trade-offs (fast transitions and shared state vs SEO/initial-load cost) and that deep links require server fallback to index.html.

Shared chrome (nav, sidebar, footer) is expressed as a layout route whose child renders into an `<Outlet/>`, using nested routes. Interview angle: layout routes avoid re-mounting/re-fetching the shell on every navigation and keep persistent UI mounted across route changes.

Integration step: assemble components, routing, layouts, state, and data fetching into a working multi-page app. Interview angle: demonstrates folder structure, component boundaries, and where state should live (colocated vs lifted) in a realistic app.

Returns a mutable `{ current }` container that persists across renders WITHOUT triggering a re-render; used for DOM node access and for holding mutable values (timers, previous values). Interview angle: contrast with state — writing `ref.current` is a side effect that doesn't re-render, so never store rendered UI data in a ref.

A function prefixed with `use` that composes built-in hooks to extract and share stateful logic (e.g. `useFetch`, `useDebounce`). Interview angle: they share logic, not state — each call gets its own isolated state — and they must obey the Rules of Hooks (top-level, from React functions only).

When two components need the same state you lift it to their nearest common ancestor and pass it down — but that ancestor's re-render now cascades to its whole subtree. Interview angle: this is the core tension — centralizing state creates broad re-renders, mitigated by state colocation, `React.memo`, and splitting components.

Passing a prop through many intermediate components that don't use it, just to reach a deep consumer. Interview angle: it couples the tree and is noisy, which motivates Context (or a state library) — but note drilling one or two levels is often clearer than reaching for context prematurely.

`createContext` + `Provider` broadcasts a value to any descendant via `useContext`, removing prop drilling for cross-cutting data (theme, auth, locale). Interview angle: the caveat is that EVERY consumer re-renders whenever the context value's identity changes, so memoize the provider value and split contexts by update frequency.

Verifying context behavior means asserting that consumers receive the right value and re-render only when expected. Interview angle: demonstrate that an unmemoized provider `value={{...}}` object re-renders all consumers every parent render, and that Context is a dependency-injection/broadcast tool, not an optimized state manager.

Recoil models state as atoms (units of state) and selectors (derived state); components subscribe to specific atoms via `useRecoilState`, so only components reading a changed atom re-render. Interview angle: this granular subscription model solves Context's all-consumers-re-render problem and adds async/derived state — the same problem Redux, Zustand, and Jotai target with different trade-offs.

example.title: A multi-tenant analytics dashboard SPA
example.body: You're building the front end for a SaaS analytics dashboard. You scaffold with Vite and set up React Router with a persistent layout route (sidebar + top nav) whose pages render through an <Outlet/>. Auth state and the light/dark theme live in Context so any page can read them without prop drilling, and data-fetching per page is done in useEffect with cleanup to cancel stale requests on route change. Report tables render lists keyed by stable record IDs (never array index) and use conditional rendering for loading/empty/error states, wrapped in an Error Boundary so one failing widget doesn't blank the whole dashboard. You extract repeated logic into custom hooks like useFetch and useDebouncedSearch, and use useRef to focus the filter input and hold interval IDs for the live-refresh timer. Trouble starts when you put the frequently-updating 'live metrics' object into the same Context as auth: every ticking update re-renders the entire tree. You first mitigate with React.memo and by splitting the fast-changing context from the slow-changing one, then migrate the metrics to Recoil atoms so only the specific tiles that read a given metric re-render — turning a re-render storm into surgical updates.

qa.q: Why does a React component re-render, and how do you eliminate unnecessary re-renders?
qa.a: A component re-renders when its own state changes, its parent re-renders, or a context it consumes changes value — not because a prop's value 'differs'. To cut waste: colocate state as low as possible, wrap pure children in React.memo, stabilize callback/object props with useCallback/useMemo, and split large contexts. Crucially, memoization only helps if the props' referential identity is actually stable, so fixing the source of new object/function identities matters more than sprinkling memo everywhere.

qa.q: Walk through useEffect's dependency array and cleanup, and the common bugs.
qa.a: The effect runs after commit/paint; the dependency array decides re-runs (`[]` = mount only, omitted = every render) and the returned function runs cleanup before the next run and on unmount. The classic bugs are stale closures from omitting dependencies (the effect captures old state/props) and leaks from missing cleanup on subscriptions/timers. In dev StrictMode, effects mount-unmount-mount to surface missing cleanup, so effects must be idempotent.

qa.q: What is the re-render problem with Context, and how does Recoil address it?
qa.a: Every component calling useContext re-renders whenever the provider's value identity changes, even if it only cares about one field — so a fast-changing value in a broad context re-renders the whole subtree. Mitigations are memoizing the value object and splitting contexts by update cadence. Recoil instead exposes atoms that components subscribe to individually, so changing one atom re-renders only the components reading that atom; Zustand and Jotai solve the same granularity problem differently.

qa.q: Why is using the array index as a list key dangerous?
qa.a: Keys tell React's reconciler which element is which across renders so it can preserve component state and DOM. With index keys, inserting/removing/reordering shifts every index, so React mismatches old state to new items — you get wrong input values, lost focus, or animation glitches. A stable domain ID (e.g. record.id) fixes identity to the data, not the position; index keys are only safe for static, never-reordered lists.

qa.q: What do Error Boundaries catch and not catch, and how are they implemented?
qa.a: They catch errors thrown during rendering, in lifecycle methods, and in constructors of the components below them, showing fallback UI via getDerivedStateFromError and logging via componentDidCatch. They do NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code (setTimeout/promises), server-side rendering, or errors thrown in the boundary itself — those need ordinary try/catch. There's still no hook version, so boundaries remain class components (or you use react-error-boundary).

qa.q: When would you use useRef instead of useState?
qa.a: Use a ref for a mutable value that should persist across renders but must NOT trigger a re-render when it changes — DOM element references, interval/timeout IDs, previous-value tracking, or a mutable flag. Use state when the value affects the rendered output. The rule of thumb: if changing it should update the UI, it's state; if it's out-of-band bookkeeping, it's a ref, and you should never read/write refs during render.

qa.q: Lifting state up, prop drilling, and Context all move state around — how do you choose?
qa.a: Lift state to the nearest common ancestor only when multiple components genuinely need to share it; over-lifting broadens re-renders and couples the tree. Prop drilling is fine for one or two levels and keeps data flow explicit. Reach for Context when a value is truly cross-cutting (theme, auth, locale) and read by many distant components — but if that value updates frequently or you need derived/granular subscriptions, a dedicated state library (Recoil/Zustand/Redux) is the better tool.

bullets.point: A token launchpad is a client-only React app that lets a user create their own fungible SPL token on Solana without deploying any on-chain program. The interview angle is the Solana mental model: unlike Ethereum where each ERC-20 is its own deployed contract, on Solana you create a Mint account owned by the single pre-deployed Token (or Token-2022) program, so 'making a token' is really just creating and initializing an account.

An instruction is the atomic unit of work (program id + ordered accounts + opaque data byte array); a transaction is an ordered list of instructions signed by all required signers and executed atomically all-or-nothing. This matters because creating a token requires several steps (create account, init mint, init metadata) that must land together in one transaction so the mint is never left half-initialized; also worth knowing the ~1232-byte size cap and the single recent blockhash used for expiry/dedup.

Scaffold a React app, in this era with Vite rather than the deprecated Create React App. The relevant detail for later: Vite serves native browser ESM and, unlike CRA's webpack 4, does not auto-polyfill Node core modules, which is exactly why step 7 is needed.

Wrap the app in ConnectionProvider (RPC endpoint), WalletProvider (supported wallets like Phantom/Backpack), and WalletModalProvider (connect UI), then consume useWallet()/useConnection() for publicKey and sendTransaction. The key point is security separation: the app never sees a private key — it builds unsigned transactions and delegates signing to the wallet extension.

Add @solana/web3.js (Connection, Transaction, Keypair, SystemProgram), @solana/spl-token (mint helper instructions and program constants), and the wallet-adapter packages. Interview-relevant nuance: spl-token ships both high-level helpers (createMint) and low-level instruction builders (createInitializeMintInstruction) — the launchpad needs the low-level ones.

The convenient spl-token createMint() helper takes a payer Keypair and internally calls sendAndConfirmTransaction, so it only works server-side / in a script. In a browser you have a wallet that signs but never exposes its secret key, so you must decompose createMint into its underlying instructions and send them via wallet.sendTransaction — this is the central 'why the tutorial is built this way' moment.

web3.js and spl-token depend on Node globals (Buffer, process, crypto, stream) that don't exist in the browser; under Vite this surfaces as a runtime 'Buffer is not defined'. Fix it with vite-plugin-node-polyfills (or manually alias the buffer package and define global). The interview point is understanding bundler differences: CRA/webpack 4 auto-polyfilled these, Vite and webpack 5 do not.

Manually build the transaction: generate a fresh mint Keypair, compute rent via getMinimumBalanceForRentExemption(getMintLen(...)), SystemProgram.createAccount for the mint address, then createInitializeMintInstruction with decimals, mint authority and freeze authority. Crucially the transaction needs two signers — the wallet (fee payer) and the new mint Keypair — so you partialSign with the mint keypair before wallet.sendTransaction, because SystemProgram.createAccount requires the new account to sign for itself.

With Token-2022 you store metadata inline on the mint using the MetadataPointer extension (pointing the mint at itself) plus the TokenMetadata extension carrying name/symbol/uri, rather than a separate Metaplex PDA. Ordering and sizing are exam-worthy: size the account with getMintLen([ExtensionType.MetadataPointer]) + pack(metadata) length + TYPE_SIZE/LENGTH_SIZE, initialize the pointer before initializeMint, and initialize the metadata after initializeMint.

Creating the mint yields zero supply; to mint you derive the user's Associated Token Account (getAssociatedTokenAddressSync), add createAssociatedTokenAccountInstruction, then createMintToInstruction for amount * 10^decimals signed by the mint authority. The distinction to articulate: the mint account defines the token, the ATA holds a specific owner's balance, and base-unit math (decimals) is applied at the boundary between UI and chain.

example.title: Shipping a self-serve memecoin/creator-token launchpad for a client
example.body: You're an SDE-2 building a self-serve page where a non-technical creator connects Phantom, types a name, symbol, image URI, decimals and initial supply, clicks 'Launch', and gets a live Token-2022 token on devnet/mainnet — no backend key custody. You wire up the wallet adapter providers, then discover the app crashes with 'Buffer is not defined' the moment you import spl-token, so you add vite-plugin-node-polyfills. Next, your first attempt reuses createMint() and fails because there's no payer Keypair in the browser, forcing you to hand-assemble the transaction: SystemProgram.createAccount for a new mint keypair, initialize the MetadataPointer extension, initializeMint, initialize the on-chain TokenMetadata, then create the creator's ATA and mintTo the full supply. The bug you spend the most time on is a 'signature verification failed' error — you forgot to partialSign with the mint keypair before calling sendTransaction — and a rent error because you sized the account with MINT_SIZE instead of getMintLen plus the packed metadata length. This scenario exercises every step: provider setup, polyfills, instruction decomposition, multi-signer transactions, extension sizing, and the mint-vs-ATA split.

qa.q: Why can't you use spl-token's createMint() helper directly in a browser token launchpad?
qa.a: createMint() expects a payer Keypair and a mint-authority Keypair and internally calls sendAndConfirmTransaction, meaning it must hold and sign with raw secret keys. In a browser the wallet adapter only signs transactions and never exposes the private key, so there's no Keypair to pass. The fix is to decompose it into SystemProgram.createAccount + createInitializeMintInstruction and submit via wallet.sendTransaction, letting the wallet supply the fee-payer signature.

qa.q: Explain the difference between an instruction and a transaction, and why atomicity matters here.
qa.a: An instruction is a single program invocation carrying a program id, the list of accounts it touches, and a data payload. A transaction bundles one or more instructions, is signed by every required signer, and executes atomically — if any instruction fails, the entire transaction reverts. That atomicity is why create-account, initialize-mint and initialize-metadata are packed into one transaction: the mint can never end up created-but-uninitialized. Transactions are also capped near 1232 bytes and carry one recent blockhash for expiry and replay protection.

qa.q: Why do you need a Node polyfill in a Vite React app, and what changed from CRA?
qa.a: @solana/web3.js and @solana/spl-token rely on Node built-ins like Buffer, process, crypto and stream. Create React App used webpack 4, which shimmed these automatically, whereas Vite (and webpack 5) intentionally do not, so you hit runtime errors such as 'Buffer is not defined'. You resolve it with vite-plugin-node-polyfills, or by aliasing the buffer package and defining a global shim in the Vite config.

qa.q: What is the difference between a mint account and an associated token account (ATA)?
qa.a: A mint account holds token-wide state: total supply, decimals, mint authority and freeze authority — it defines the token. A token account holds one owner's balance of one specific mint, and the ATA is the canonical, deterministically derived token account for a given (owner, mint, token program) so each owner has exactly one per mint. Creating the mint produces zero supply; you must create an ATA and mintTo it to give someone a balance.

qa.q: Which signers does the token-creation transaction require, and why the mint keypair?
qa.a: It needs two: the connected wallet as fee payer/mint authority, and the newly generated mint Keypair. The mint keypair must sign because SystemProgram.createAccount is creating a brand-new account at that public key, and Solana requires a new account to authorize its own creation. In practice you partialSign the transaction with the mint keypair, then call wallet.sendTransaction so the wallet adds the fee-payer signature.

qa.q: How does Token-2022 metadata differ from the older Metaplex approach?
qa.a: Token-2022 stores metadata inline on the mint via extensions: a MetadataPointer extension (typically pointing the mint at itself) plus a TokenMetadata extension holding name, symbol and URI — no separate Metaplex metadata PDA or program is required. You must pre-size the account for those extensions and respect ordering: initialize the metadata pointer before initializeMint and initialize the metadata after. The trade-off is that Token-2022 is newer with historically thinner wallet/explorer support, while Metaplex metadata is more universally recognized.

qa.q: Why must you compute rent with getMintLen and getMinimumBalanceForRentExemption instead of a fixed size?
qa.a: Solana accounts must be rent-exempt, holding a minimum lamport balance proportional to their byte size, or they get garbage-collected. A plain mint is MINT_SIZE bytes, but enabling extensions makes the account larger, so you compute getMintLen([ExtensionType.MetadataPointer]) and add the packed metadata length plus the type/length headers, then pass that to getMinimumBalanceForRentExemption. Using the fixed MINT_SIZE underfunds the account and the transaction fails or the account can't hold its extension data.

qa.q: How are decimals handled when minting a supply, and who must sign?
qa.a: On-chain amounts are unsigned integers denominated in the token's smallest unit, so a token with decimals=9 and a desired display supply of 100 requires minting 100 * 10^9 base units; the UI applies this scaling at the read/write boundary. The mintTo instruction must be signed by the mint authority set during initializeMint — in the launchpad that's the connected wallet — and it deposits the base-unit amount into the target ATA.

bullets.point: On Solana everything is an account — a blob of bytes with metadata — and the runtime cleanly separates code (stateless, executable program accounts) from state (data accounts a program operates on). This is the opposite of Ethereum's contract-holds-its-own-storage model, and it's what enables Sealevel's parallel execution (transactions declaring non-overlapping accounts run concurrently). Interview angle: understanding that state lives in separate accounts is the prerequisite for every ownership/PDA question.

bullets.step: Accounts: fields and the owner rule
bullets.point: Every account has lamports (balance; 1 SOL = 1e9 lamports), a data byte array, an owner (a program's pubkey), an executable flag, and rent metadata, all keyed by a 32-byte pubkey. The hard runtime invariant is: only the owner program may mutate an account's data or debit its lamports — no one else can, even the fee payer. Accounts must also be rent-exempt (hold a minimum balance for their size) or they get purged, which is a common footgun when sizing accounts.

The System Program (address all-ones, 111...111) owns every ordinary wallet/SOL account and is the only program that can create accounts, allocate their space, assign their ownership to another program, and transfer lamports out of system-owned accounts. To give your program a data account you CPI into System Program's CreateAccount (fund + allocate + assign) — your program can't conjure an account itself because it doesn't own the source lamports or the new address. Interview angle: 'who creates the account and how does ownership get transferred' is a classic setup question.

bullets.step: BPF Loader: how programs are stored and executed
bullets.point: A deployed program is just an account whose data is SBF/eBPF bytecode, marked executable=true, and owned by a BPF loader (today the BPFLoaderUpgradeable program). The loader is what the runtime actually invokes to run your code, and the upgradeable variant splits the program into a Program account plus a ProgramData account holding the bytes. Key point: programs aren't special magic — they're loader-owned executable accounts, and the loader is what governs upgradeability.

bullets.step: Authority: program-enforced privilege vs runtime ownership
bullets.point: An 'authority' is a convention/application-level pubkey (upgrade authority, mint/freeze authority, update authority) that a program's logic — or the loader — requires to be a transaction signer before allowing a privileged action. It is NOT the same as owner: owner is the runtime rule for who can write bytes, while authority is who the program decides can do X. The upgrade authority is the sharpest example: whoever holds it can redeploy new bytecode, and setting it to None makes the program permanently immutable — a security/trust decision teams make deliberately.

A PDA is an address derived deterministically from seeds + the program ID that is intentionally pushed OFF the Ed25519 curve, so it has no private key and no one can produce a normal signature for it. findProgramAddress hashes the seeds, program ID, and a bump byte, decrementing the bump from 255 until the result is off-curve (the first success is the 'canonical bump'). This gives programs deterministic, discoverable addresses for state (e.g. per-user accounts) AND lets a program 'sign' for its own accounts via invoke_signed by supplying the seeds — the foundation of vaults, escrows, and program-owned token accounts.

example.title: A token-staking vault where a PDA custodies user deposits
example.body: You're building a staking program. A user deposits SPL tokens and the program derives a PDA from seeds ["vault", user_pubkey, program_id] whose canonical bump you store in the account. During deposit, your program CPIs into the System Program to create the vault data account (fund for rent-exemption, allocate space, assign ownership to your program), then initializes it — now your program is the runtime owner and the only thing that can mutate the balance record. When the user withdraws, the PDA itself must authorize moving tokens out of the program-owned token account, so you call invoke_signed passing the same seeds + bump; the runtime re-derives the PDA, sees it matches, and treats it as a valid signer even though no private key exists. Separately, 'authority' shows up twice: your program logic enforces that only the original depositor's signature can trigger a withdrawal, and at the deployment level your team holds the BPF upgrade authority so you can patch bugs — which you later revoke (set to None) to make the vault immutable and trustless. A real debugging session here usually ends at one of three things: a 'ConstraintSeeds'/wrong-bump error (you didn't use the canonical bump), a rent-exemption failure (account underfunded for its size), or a missing-signer error (forgot to pass seeds to invoke_signed, so the PDA wasn't recognized as signer).

qa.q: What's the difference between an account's `owner` and an `authority`?
qa.a: `owner` is a runtime primitive: it's the program pubkey that is the only entity allowed to mutate the account's data and debit its lamports, enforced by the Solana runtime itself. `authority` is an application/convention concept — a pubkey that a program's own logic (or the loader) requires as a transaction signer before permitting a privileged operation like upgrading, minting, or withdrawing. In short: owner = who can write the bytes; authority = who the program decides is allowed to do a specific action. A single account can be owned by one program yet reference several distinct authorities.

qa.q: What is a PDA and why must it be off the Ed25519 curve?
qa.a: A Program Derived Address is deterministically derived from seeds plus the program ID and is deliberately chosen to lie off the Ed25519 curve, which means no corresponding private key exists. That's the whole point: because no one can hold a key for it, no external party can forge a normal signature for the address, so the program alone can authorize actions on it via invoke_signed. This lets a program own and 'sign' for accounts without managing secrets, giving you secure, deterministic addresses for state and program-controlled funds.

qa.q: Explain bump seeds and why the canonical bump matters for security.
qa.a: Since roughly half of candidate addresses land on the curve, findProgramAddress appends a 1-byte 'bump' and decrements it from 255 until the derived address is off-curve; the first (highest) value that works is the canonical bump. Security matters because if your program accepts any bump the caller supplies, an attacker can grind a different valid bump to derive an alternate PDA for the same logical seeds and bypass your account checks. The fix is to always compute or store the canonical bump and validate against it — Anchor's `bump` constraint does this for you.

qa.q: How does a program actually 'sign' for a PDA if it has no private key?
qa.a: It uses invoke_signed for the cross-program invocation, passing the exact seeds (including the bump) used to derive the PDA. The runtime re-derives the address from those seeds plus the calling program's ID, confirms it matches the PDA in the instruction, and then treats that PDA as an authorized signer for the CPI. This works only for PDAs derived from the caller's own program ID — a program can never sign for a PDA belonging to a different program.

qa.q: Why must you go through the System Program to create an account, and what does it do?
qa.a: The System Program owns all system accounts and SOL and is the only program permitted to create accounts, allocate their data space, assign their ownership to another program, and move lamports out of system-owned accounts. To set up a data account for your program you CPI CreateAccount, which funds it (typically to rent-exemption), allocates bytes, and assigns ownership to your program in one step. Your program can't do this itself because the source lamports live in a system-owned account that only the System Program can debit.

qa.q: How are programs stored and executed on Solana, and how do upgrades work?
qa.a: A program is an account whose data is compiled SBF/eBPF bytecode, flagged executable, and owned by a BPF loader — the loader is what the runtime invokes to run it. With the upgradeable loader, the deployment is split into a Program account and a ProgramData account holding the bytes, plus an upgrade authority. Whoever holds the upgrade authority can redeploy new bytecode; setting that authority to None makes the program permanently immutable, which is the standard way to make a program trustless.

qa.q: What's the difference between findProgramAddress and createProgramAddress?
qa.a: createProgramAddress (create_program_address) takes an exact set of seeds — including a specific bump — and returns the derived address, erroring if the result happens to be on-curve; it does no searching. findProgramAddress wraps it in a loop, decrementing the bump from 255 until it finds an off-curve address, and returns both the address and that canonical bump. You use find off-chain or once to discover the bump, then store it and validate on-chain with the cheaper create call to avoid re-running the search every instruction (it costs compute).

qa.q: Where does rent-exemption fit into ownership and PDAs, and what breaks if you get it wrong?
qa.a: Every account must hold a minimum lamport balance proportional to its data size to be rent-exempt, otherwise the runtime can purge it. When you create a PDA or data account via the System Program you must fund it to that threshold up front, and if you later reallocate to a larger size you must top it up. Getting this wrong shows up as account-creation failures or, worse, silent data loss when an underfunded account is garbage-collected — so account sizing and funding are part of correctly designing any program-owned state.

bullets.point: Consolidation checkpoint anchoring Part 3 on top of App Router fundamentals: file-based routing, Server vs Client Components, layouts, loading/error boundaries, and data fetching.
