Interview angle: be able to state where the App Router draws the server/client boundary (`"use client"`) and why RSCs are the default.

A folder wrapped in parentheses like `(marketing)` organizes routes and lets you scope layouts without adding a segment to the URL. It matters for giving distinct sections their own root layout (e.g. `(auth)` vs `(dashboard)`) or splitting one layout tree into several; interviewers probe that it is purely organizational and does not change the path.

A `[id]` folder maps a single path segment to a param exposed via `params` (e.g. `/blog/[slug]`). Core to any detail page; the interview angle is that params arrive as strings, are available in the page/layout/route-handler, and that you pre-render them at build via `generateStaticParams`.

`[...slug]` matches one OR MORE trailing segments and delivers them as an array (`/docs/a/b/c` -> `['a','b','c']`). Useful for docs trees, nested category paths, or proxy-style routes; the key distinction interviewers want is that it requires at least one segment, so the bare parent path does NOT match.

Double brackets make the catch-all optional so the parent route matches too — `/shop` and `/shop/a/b` both hit `[[...slug]]`, with `params.slug` being `undefined` for the base case. It matters when one route must serve both an index and arbitrary nesting; the interview point is precisely this: `[...x]` needs a segment, `[[...x]]` does not.

Pages are rendered to HTML at build time (or via ISR revalidation) rather than per request, giving CDN-cacheable, fast responses. In the App Router this is the default when a route has no dynamic APIs; dynamic segments are enumerated with `generateStaticParams`, and `revalidate`/`dynamicParams` control ISR and on-demand fallback. Interview angle: know SSG vs SSR vs ISR trade-offs and what silently opts a route into dynamic rendering (cookies, headers, `no-store`).

Hydration is React attaching event listeners and reconciling client state onto the server-rendered HTML so a static page becomes interactive. It matters because mismatches between server and client render (dates, random values, `window` access) throw hydration errors, and heavy client trees delay interactivity — the interview angle is reducing hydration cost by keeping components as Server Components and minimizing `"use client"` boundaries.

`middleware.ts` at the project root runs on the Edge runtime BEFORE a request is completed, ideal for auth gating, redirects/rewrites, A/B routing, i18n, and setting headers/cookies. Scoped via a `matcher` config; the interview angle is its constraints — Edge runtime only (no Node APIs), must be fast, cannot read a request body, and returns a `NextResponse` rather than rendering.

Applied build combining route groups, dynamic + catch-all segments, SSG with `generateStaticParams`, and middleware-based auth into one app. The takeaway is wiring these primitives together correctly — e.g. a statically generated content site with a protected dashboard — which is exactly the system-design shape an SDE-2 is expected to reason about.

example.title: A statically generated docs + blog site with a gated dashboard
example.body: You build a company site on the App Router. Marketing and legal pages live under a `(marketing)` route group so they share a clean root layout without polluting URLs, while the app itself lives under `(dashboard)` with its own layout. Docs use `app/docs/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` so `/docs` renders an index and `/docs/guides/routing` renders a deep page from the same route, with all known paths pre-rendered at build via `generateStaticParams` and ISR (`revalidate = 3600`) so CDN-cached HTML stays fresh. The blog uses `app/blog/[slug]` for individual posts. A `[...path]` catch-all under `/legacy` rewrites old CMS URLs. Root `middleware.ts` runs on the Edge, checks a session cookie, and redirects unauthenticated users away from `(dashboard)` routes via a `matcher`. When a 'text content does not match' hydration error appears on a post page, you trace it to a `new Date().toLocaleString()` rendered in a Client Component and fix it by formatting on the server or gating on `useEffect`, keeping the interactive island small so hydration stays cheap.

qa.q: What is the difference between `[...slug]` and `[[...slug]]`, and when would you choose each?
qa.a: `[...slug]` is a catch-all that matches one or more segments and requires at least one, so the bare parent path (e.g. `/docs`) is a 404. `[[...slug]]` is an optional catch-all: the parent path also matches and `params.slug` is `undefined` in that case. Choose the optional form when a single route must serve both an index page and arbitrary nested paths — a docs section where `/docs` and `/docs/a/b` share one component.

qa.q: Do route groups affect the URL, and why use them?
qa.a: No — a folder like `(marketing)` is stripped from the URL entirely; it is purely for organization. The main reasons to use them are to give different sections of the app their own layout hierarchy (e.g. an auth layout vs a dashboard layout) without a shared parent segment, to split one large layout tree into multiple root layouts, and to co-locate related routes. A gotcha: you cannot have two routes resolve to the same final path across different groups, or you get a collision.

qa.q: How does SSG work in the App Router, and what silently turns a route dynamic?
qa.a: A route is statically rendered by default at build time when it uses no request-time (dynamic) APIs; dynamic segments are enumerated with `generateStaticParams`. Using `cookies()`, `headers()`, the `searchParams` prop, `fetch` with `cache: 'no-store'`, or setting `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` opts the route into per-request server rendering. ISR sits in between — `export const revalidate = N` regenerates the static page in the background after N seconds, and `dynamicParams` controls whether params not returned by `generateStaticParams` are rendered on demand or 404.

qa.q: What causes a hydration mismatch and how do you fix it?
qa.a: It happens when the HTML React renders on the client during hydration does not match what the server produced — common causes are `Date.now()`/`new Date()`, `Math.random()`, locale-dependent formatting, reading `window`/`localStorage` during render, or invalid HTML nesting. Fixes: compute non-deterministic values on the server and pass them down, defer client-only logic to `useEffect` (so it runs after hydration), or use `suppressHydrationWarning` for intentionally divergent nodes like timestamps. The deeper point is that hydration replays the server render, so render output must be deterministic across environments.

qa.q: What are the constraints of Next.js middleware and what is it good for?
qa.a: Middleware runs on the Edge runtime before the request is completed, so it must be fast and cannot use Node-only APIs (fs, native modules) or read the request body; it returns a `NextResponse` (next, redirect, rewrite, or header/cookie mutation) rather than rendering UI. It is ideal for cross-cutting concerns: auth/session gating, redirects and rewrites, i18n locale routing, A/B bucketing, and bot/geo handling. Scope it with a `matcher` in the config export so it does not run on every asset request, and keep heavy logic (DB calls, verification) minimal or push it to the route itself.

qa.q: How do you statically pre-render a dynamic route like `/blog/[slug]`?
qa.a: Export an async `generateStaticParams` from the segment that returns an array of param objects, e.g. `[{ slug: 'hello' }, { slug: 'world' }]`; Next.js pre-renders one HTML file per entry at build time. Combine it with `revalidate` for ISR and with `dynamicParams` (default `true`) to decide whether slugs not in the list are rendered on demand and cached, or return a 404 (`false`). This is the App Router replacement for the old `getStaticPaths` + `getStaticProps` pairing.

qa.q: Why are Server Components the default, and how does that relate to hydration cost?
qa.a: Server Components render on the server and ship zero JS for their own logic, so only their serialized output crosses the wire — this shrinks the client bundle and reduces the amount of JavaScript that must be downloaded, parsed, and hydrated. Only components marked `"use client"` (and their subtree) hydrate. The practical rule is to keep `"use client"` boundaries as low and small as possible — pass server-rendered content into client components as children/props rather than pulling large trees into the client — which directly lowers time-to-interactive.

qa.q: Middleware vs a layout vs a route handler for auth — which and why?
qa.a: Middleware is best for coarse, fast gate-keeping (redirect unauthenticated users away from a section) because it runs before rendering and can short-circuit with a redirect at the Edge. A layout/page can enforce auth too via server-side session checks and is the right place when you need data-backed decisions or to render a partial UI, but it runs later and after routing. Route handlers protect API endpoints specifically. In practice you layer them: middleware for the cheap redirect, plus a server-side check in the protected route so security does not depend on middleware alone (defense in depth).

bullets.point: Framing lesson: Web3 apps (dApps) put business logic in smart contracts deployed to a blockchain (e.g. Ethereum), with a JS/TS frontend talking to them via a provider (MetaMask/RPC) and a library like ethers.js/viem. The interview angle is understanding the architecture split: on-chain code is immutable, costs gas, and is the source of truth; the frontend is just a thin client that reads state and submits signed transactions.

Solidity is a statically-typed, contract-oriented, compiled language for the EVM; a contract is like a class whose fields are persisted in on-chain storage and whose public functions are the callable interface. It matters because its execution model is unusual: code runs deterministically across all nodes, every state write costs gas, there is no floating point, and reverting undoes all state changes in the transaction. Expect questions on storage vs memory vs calldata, visibility (public/external/internal/private), and view/pure vs state-changing functions.

The compiler emits bytecode (the EVM instructions deployed on-chain) and the ABI (a JSON description of functions/events/types used to encode calls and decode results off-chain). This is the single most common integration gotcha: the frontend needs the ABI plus the contract address to build a typed interface, function calls are dispatched by a 4-byte selector (first 4 bytes of keccak256 of the signature), and events are read from indexed logs — knowing this explains 'why can't my JS see this function' and 'why is my decoded value garbage.'

Contrast lesson: despite similar syntax, Solidity is statically typed, deterministic, and gas-metered with no runtime GC, no async/promises, no floating point, and integer overflow that reverts (since 0.8.0). The interview point is the mental model shift — you optimize for gas and safety rather than developer ergonomics, all 'I/O' is via transactions/calls, msg.sender/msg.value replace request context, and there is no console/network access from within a contract.

example.title: Wiring a React dApp to an ERC-20 token contract
example.body: You are building the frontend for a token-gated feature. A teammate deploys an ERC-20 contract in Solidity; you receive its deployed address and its compiled ABI (the artifact JSON from the build). In your React app you instantiate a contract object with ethers.js using address + ABI + a provider, call the view function balanceOf(user) (a free read, no gas, no signature) to gate access, and for a transfer you build a transaction that the user signs in MetaMask — which costs gas and only mutates state if it doesn't revert. When balances come back wrong, you debug the classic pitfalls: the token has 18 decimals so you must scale with parseUnits/formatUnits (Solidity has no floats), the ABI you bundled is stale after a redeploy so the function selector doesn't match, and you listen for the Transfer event via indexed logs to update the UI rather than polling. This one flow exercises the whole topic: contracts as the on-chain source of truth, the ABI/bytecode split, and how Solidity's execution model differs from the JS you write around it.

qa.q: What is the difference between the ABI and the bytecode, and which does the frontend need?
qa.a: Bytecode is the compiled EVM instructions that get deployed and executed on-chain; the ABI is a JSON schema describing the contract's functions, parameters, return types, and events. The frontend needs the ABI (plus the deployed address) to ABI-encode calls and decode responses — it never touches raw bytecode. Bytecode matters at deploy time and for verification; the ABI matters at every interaction, which is why a stale ABI after a redeploy is a frequent bug.

qa.q: Explain storage, memory, and calldata in Solidity and their cost implications.
qa.a: Storage is persistent per-contract state that lives on-chain and is the most expensive to write (SSTORE), memory is a temporary, mutable, zero-initialized scratch space that exists only for the duration of a call, and calldata is a read-only region holding function arguments. You use calldata for external function parameters to avoid copy costs, memory for local temporary data, and storage only when you must persist. Getting the data location wrong on reference types (arrays/structs/strings) is both a common compile error and a gas footgun.

qa.q: How does the EVM dispatch a function call, and what is a function selector?
qa.a: A call's calldata begins with a 4-byte function selector, which is the first 4 bytes of keccak256 of the canonical function signature (e.g. transfer(address,uint256)), followed by ABI-encoded arguments. The contract's dispatcher compares this selector to its known functions and routes accordingly, falling back to receive/fallback if none match. This is why the exact signature (types, no parameter names) matters and why two functions can't share a selector — collisions are a real concern in proxy patterns.

qa.q: Why does Solidity have no floating-point numbers, and how do you represent fractional values like token amounts or percentages?
qa.a: Floating point is non-deterministic across platforms, and the EVM requires bit-for-bit deterministic execution across all nodes, so Solidity only has integers. You represent fractions using fixed-point scaling: tokens use decimals (commonly 18, so 1 token = 1e18 base units) and percentages/rates use basis points (e.g. 250 = 2.5%). The rule of thumb is multiply before you divide to preserve precision, and be explicit about the scaling factor in your interfaces.

qa.q: What are the function visibility levels and the meaning of view/pure?
qa.a: Visibility is public (callable internally and externally, generates a getter for state vars), external (only via transactions/other contracts, cheaper for large args via calldata), internal (this contract and derivatives), and private (this contract only). Orthogonally, view functions read but don't modify state and pure functions neither read nor write state — both are free when called off-chain (eth_call, no gas) but still cost gas if invoked inside a state-changing transaction. Note that visibility is not a security boundary for confidentiality: all on-chain data is publicly readable regardless of 'private'.

qa.q: How is error handling in Solidity different from exceptions in JavaScript?
qa.a: Solidity has no try/catch around your own logic in the JS sense; instead require/revert/assert abort execution and revert all state changes made in the transaction, refunding remaining gas (assert consumes all gas and signals an invariant violation). Since 0.8.0 you get custom errors (cheaper than string messages) and automatic overflow/underflow reverts. You can catch reverts of external calls with try/catch on that specific call, but there is no general-purpose exception propagation, and a revert is all-or-nothing rather than a recoverable throw.

qa.q: Since syntax looks JS-like, what execution-model differences actually bite you when moving from JS to Solidity?
qa.a: There is no event loop or async — everything is synchronous within a single transaction, and cross-contract calls are synchronous message calls, not promises. Every state write costs gas so you optimize storage access aggressively, integers are fixed-width and revert on overflow, and there is no runtime environment: no filesystem, network, timers, or console — 'time' is block.timestamp and 'randomness' must come from an oracle. Context that a JS server gets from a request comes instead from msg.sender, msg.value, and tx metadata, and code is immutable once deployed unless you design an upgrade proxy.

qa.q: What does 'the frontend is a thin client' mean in a dApp, and where does trust live?
qa.a: The smart contract on-chain is the authoritative source of truth and enforces all rules; the frontend just reads state via RPC and constructs transactions the user signs in their wallet. You can never trust client-side validation for correctness because anyone can call the contract directly, bypassing your UI. That means all access control, invariants, and value checks must be enforced in Solidity (modifiers, require), and the JS layer is purely for UX — a critical distinction from traditional web apps where the server you control mediates every request.

bullets.point: Framing step for the Web3 module: the goal is to build a browser dApp frontend that reads chain state and lets users sign/send transactions. The mental model to lock in early is that your React app is just a client to Ethereum, not a custodian of funds or keys.

bullets.step: Context: how a frontend talks to a chain
bullets.point: A web app never holds private keys or runs a node; it communicates with Ethereum over JSON-RPC (via an RPC endpoint for reads) and delegates all signing to the user's wallet. Interview angle: separate the two channels clearly, reads go through an RPC transport (public client), writes get signed by the wallet (wallet client) and broadcast.

A wallet (MetaMask, Rabby, Coinbase Wallet) manages the user's private keys and exposes an injected provider on window.ethereum following the EIP-1193 standard (request/on events). Know the difference between an EOA (externally owned account, key-controlled) and a smart-contract wallet, and that the frontend only ever asks the wallet to sign — it never sees the key.

Wagmi is a React hooks library that turns wallet connection, account state, reads, and writes into declarative, reactive hooks (useAccount, useConnect, useBalance, useSendTransaction). It matters because it eliminates the boilerplate of manually wiring window.ethereum, managing connection state, and handling reconnection/chain-change events.

Viem is the low-level, tree-shakeable, type-safe TS library that actually talks to the chain (replacing ethers/web3.js); TanStack Query provides async caching, deduplication, and refetching; Wagmi composes the two into React hooks. Interview angle: this is clean separation of concerns — viem = transport/encoding, TanStack = async state, wagmi = React glue.

Wagmi wraps viem's imperative actions (getBalance, readContract, sendTransaction) into hooks backed by TanStack Query, so each call gets isLoading/isError/data/refetch and automatic caching keyed by args. This replaces the error-prone pattern of calling viem inside a useEffect with manual loading flags and no cache invalidation.

You configure a WagmiProvider with chains, transports, and connectors, then use useConnect to trigger connection and useAccount to read address/isConnected/chainId reactively. Key details to discuss: connector selection, persisted reconnection on reload, and reacting to the user switching accounts or networks in the wallet.

useBalance fetches the account's native (or token) balance, which comes back as a BigInt in wei; you format it for display with viem's formatEther/formatUnits using the correct decimals. Interview angle: never use floats for balances — arithmetic stays in BigInt and formatting is only a display concern to avoid precision loss.

useSendTransaction (or useWriteContract) prompts the wallet to sign and broadcasts; you build value with parseEther and then wait for inclusion with useWaitForTransactionReceipt. Cover the full lifecycle: hash returned immediately vs. mined receipt, waiting for N confirmations, and handling user rejection (code 4001) and gas/revert errors distinctly.

Consolidate the flow end-to-end: connect, display balance, send ETH, and ideally extend to an ERC-20 transfer via useWriteContract with an ABI. The practical goal is fluency in the read-vs-write distinction, unit conversion, and transaction-state UX (pending/confirmed/failed).

example.title: A donation/tip widget on a creator dashboard
example.body: You are an SDE-2 adding a 'Tip in ETH' widget to a creator's public page. The user clicks Connect, wagmi's useConnect opens MetaMask and useAccount exposes their address and chainId. You render their balance with useBalance, formatting the wei BigInt via formatEther so it doesn't show 18 zeros. When they enter an amount and hit Send, useSendTransaction calls parseEther(amount) and asks the wallet to sign; you show a spinner keyed off useWaitForTransactionReceipt until it mines, then a success toast with the tx hash linking to Etherscan. Debugging you would actually hit: the send silently fails because the user is on Sepolia instead of mainnet (guard on chainId and prompt a switchChain), a rejection throws with code 4001 that you must catch and show as 'cancelled' rather than a scary error, and the balance appearing stale after a tip because you forgot that TanStack caches it — so you invalidate/refetch the balance query on receipt.

qa.q: Why use viem over ethers.js or web3.js?
qa.a: Viem is designed for a modern TS toolchain: strong end-to-end type inference from ABIs, a tree-shakeable modular API that keeps bundles small, and clear separation of public client (reads over RPC) vs. wallet client (writes/signing). It also has predictable BigInt-based number handling and is the native substrate wagmi is built on, so you get consistent behavior between low-level calls and hooks. Ethers is still fine, but viem's typing and DX are the reason the ecosystem shifted.

qa.q: How does wagmi leverage TanStack Query, and why does that matter?
qa.a: Every wagmi read hook is a TanStack Query under the hood, with a query key derived from the call's arguments (address, chainId, args). That gives you caching, request deduplication across components, background refetching, and stale-while-revalidate for free, so ten components reading the same balance make one request. It also means you invalidate or refetch chain data through the query client — e.g., refreshing a balance after a transaction confirms — instead of manual state juggling.

qa.q: How can a frontend send transactions without ever holding the private key?
qa.a: The wallet injects an EIP-1193 provider (window.ethereum) that exposes a request interface. Reads go through your own RPC transport via a public client, but for writes the app builds an unsigned transaction and calls the wallet to sign it; the private key never leaves the wallet's process. The wallet returns a signed tx or broadcasts it, and the app only ever receives a hash — this is the core security boundary of a dApp.

qa.q: Why are balances and amounts handled as BigInt, and how do you display them?
qa.a: Ether has 18 decimals and token amounts routinely exceed JavaScript's safe integer range, so representing them as Number risks silent precision loss. Viem/wagmi keep all on-chain values as BigInt in the smallest unit (wei), and you only convert at the display boundary with formatUnits/formatEther, going the other way with parseEther/parseUnits for input. Rule of thumb: do all math in BigInt with the token's decimals, and treat formatting purely as a view concern.

qa.q: Walk through the full lifecycle of sending a transaction with wagmi.
qa.a: useSendTransaction (or useWriteContract for a contract call) prompts the wallet to sign and broadcasts, returning a transaction hash almost immediately — that does not mean it's mined. You then pass that hash to useWaitForTransactionReceipt to await inclusion in a block and get success/revert status, optionally waiting for extra confirmations for finality. UX-wise you distinguish three states — signing (can be rejected, error code 4001), pending (broadcast, not mined), and confirmed/failed — and only mutate app state or refetch balances on the receipt.

qa.q: How do you handle the user being on the wrong network?
qa.a: Read chainId from useAccount and compare it to the chain your contracts are deployed on; if it mismatches, disable actions and offer useSwitchChain to request a network switch (which prompts the wallet, and may prompt to add the chain if unknown). Never assume the connected chain — users can switch networks in the wallet at any time, and wagmi will reactively update chainId, so your guard should be reactive too. Sending to the wrong chain is a common cause of 'my transaction did nothing' bugs.

qa.q: What's the difference between viem's public client and wallet client?
qa.a: A public client is read-only and talks to an RPC endpoint you configure (transport) to fetch balances, call view functions, read receipts, and estimate gas — no signer required. A wallet client wraps the user's account/provider and handles anything requiring a signature: sending transactions, signing messages, writing to contracts. Wagmi maps this split onto hooks — read hooks use the public client, write hooks use the wallet client — so mixing them up (e.g., trying to write with only an RPC transport) is a frequent conceptual error.

qa.q: Why not just call window.ethereum directly inside a useEffect?
qa.a: You can, but you'd re-implement connector management, persisted reconnection, account- and chain-change event subscriptions, request deduplication, caching, and loading/error state by hand — and get it subtly wrong (stale closures, race conditions, no cache invalidation). Wagmi + TanStack give you all of that declaratively and reactively, plus viem's typed encoding/decoding. Direct provider access is reserved for edge cases the hooks don't cover, not the default path.

bullets.point: Framing step positioning DEXs and token funding as the culmination of the DeFi curriculum. Interview-wise it's a no-op, but it signals the mental model: on-chain markets replace centralized order-matching and custody, and the rest of the topic builds toward launching and funding a token via a DEX.

A DEX (decentralized exchange) is a set of smart contracts that let users swap assets peer-to-contract with no custodian or KYC; 'funding' here means projects raise capital by minting a token and bootstrapping a liquidity pool rather than a traditional raise. The key insight is that listing = creating a pool, and the pool's math (not a market maker or order book) sets the price.

Ethereum is EVM/Solidity, account-based, ~12s slots, higher gas, but deepest liquidity, composability, and battle-tested security; Solana is Rust/accounts-model with Proof of History + parallel execution (Sealevel), ~400ms blocks, thousands of TPS, and cent-level fees. The trade-off interviewers probe: Solana's throughput/cost enables order-book DEXs (Serum/OpenBook) and consumer UX, while Ethereum wins on decentralization, tooling maturity, and TVL.

An Automated Market Maker prices trades from a formula over pooled reserves instead of matching orders — Uniswap v2's constant product x*y=k is canonical, so price = y/x and every trade moves along the curve, producing slippage/price impact that grows with trade size relative to pool depth. Liquidity providers deposit both assets and earn swap fees; this is the mechanism a token launch uses to become tradable without a counterparty.

IL is the shortfall an LP suffers versus simply holding the two assets, caused by the AMM rebalancing reserves as prices diverge (arbitrageurs sell you the appreciating asset). It's 'impermanent' because it fully reverses if the price ratio returns to entry, and only realized on withdrawal; magnitude is fixed by the price ratio (~5.7% at 2x, ~25% at 10x) and is offset only by accrued trading fees — the core LP profitability question.

On Solana an SPL token is not a bespoke contract: you create a Mint account (decimals, supply, mint/freeze authority) owned by the shared Token Program, and balances live in per-user Associated Token Accounts (ATAs). On Ethereum an ERC-20 is its own contract implementing a standard interface (balanceOf, transfer, approve/transferFrom). Interview angle: revoking mint and freeze authority (Solana) or making supply immutable (ERC-20) is the trust signal that supply can't be inflated or accounts frozen.

example.title: Launching a project token and seeding a DEX liquidity pool
example.body: Your team wants to fund development by launching a utility token instead of doing a traditional raise. You mint an SPL token on Solana — a Mint account with 9 decimals and a capped supply — then revoke the mint authority so holders can trust supply is fixed. To make it tradable you seed an initial liquidity pool on an AMM DEX (e.g. Raydium/Orca) pairing your token with USDC; the constant-product curve, not a market maker, now sets the price, and the ratio you deposit is effectively the opening price. Weeks later early liquidity providers complain they'd have done better just holding the token, and you diagnose classic impermanent loss: as your token appreciated against USDC, arbitrageurs rebalanced the pool, leaving LPs underweight the winner — profitable only if accumulated swap fees exceed that divergence. You also had to defend choosing Solana over Ethereum in review: sub-second finality and cent-level fees suit a consumer-facing launch, accepting Ethereum's deeper liquidity and richer composability as the cost.

qa.q: Explain the constant product formula and how it determines price and slippage.
qa.a: An x*y=k AMM keeps the product of the two reserves constant, so the spot price is simply reserve_y/reserve_x. A trade moves reserves along the hyperbola: buying token X shrinks its reserve and raises its price, and the executed (average) price is worse than the spot price — that gap is slippage/price impact. Impact scales with trade size relative to pool depth, which is why large swaps in shallow pools are expensive and why deep liquidity matters.

qa.q: What is impermanent loss, when does it become permanent, and how large is it?
qa.a: IL is the value gap between providing liquidity and just holding the same assets, arising because the AMM sells the appreciating asset and buys the depreciating one as arbitrage keeps the pool priced to market. It's 'impermanent' because it depends only on the current price ratio versus entry and fully disappears if the ratio returns — it becomes permanent only when you withdraw at a diverged ratio. For a 50/50 constant-product pool it's about 5.7% at a 2x move and ~25% at 10x, and LPs are net-positive only when collected fees exceed IL.

qa.q: Compare Solana and Ethereum for building or hosting a DEX.
qa.a: Ethereum offers the deepest liquidity, strongest decentralization, mature tooling, and composability, but ~12s finality and volatile gas make high-frequency, order-book-style trading impractical, pushing complex DEXs onto L2s. Solana's parallel execution and ~400ms blocks with sub-cent fees make on-chain central-limit order books and high-throughput AMMs viable (Serum/OpenBook, Raydium), at the cost of a smaller validator set, more historical downtime, and a steeper Rust/accounts programming model. The choice comes down to liquidity depth and security assurances (Ethereum) versus latency, throughput, and cost (Solana).

qa.q: How does creating a token differ on Solana versus Ethereum?
qa.a: On Ethereum every token is its own smart contract implementing the ERC-20 interface, so each deployment ships and maintains code. On Solana tokens share one audited Token Program: you create a Mint account holding metadata (decimals, supply, mint/freeze authorities) and balances live in per-owner Associated Token Accounts, so no custom code is needed for a standard token. This means Solana tokens are cheaper and safer to issue but less customizable, whereas ERC-20s can embed arbitrary logic (fees-on-transfer, hooks) at the cost of bespoke, exploit-prone code.

qa.q: If impermanent loss is unavoidable, how do liquidity providers actually make money?
qa.a: LPs earn a cut of every swap's trading fee (e.g. 0.3% on Uniswap v2) proportional to their pool share, plus any liquidity-mining/token incentives. Providing liquidity is profitable only when fee revenue plus incentives exceeds IL over the holding period, which favors high-volume pools and low-volatility/correlated pairs (like stablecoin pairs) where price divergence — and thus IL — stays small. It's effectively a bet that volume-driven fees beat the divergence cost.

qa.q: What is the difference between an AMM and an order-book (CLOB) DEX, and why did order books appear on Solana?
qa.a: An AMM prices trades algorithmically from pooled reserves with passive LPs and guaranteed liquidity at every price, whereas a CLOB matches discrete bid/ask limit orders posted by active makers, giving tighter spreads and price control but needing constant order updates. Order updates are cheap and fast only where fees and latency are low, so CLOB DEXs like Serum/OpenBook emerged on Solana, while Ethereum mainnet's gas costs made AMMs the dominant design. AMMs also enable permissionless listing — anyone can create a pool — which is why token launches use them.

qa.q: What is concentrated liquidity (Uniswap v3) and how does it change the LP trade-off?
qa.a: Instead of spreading liquidity across the entire 0-to-infinity price range like v2, v3 lets LPs concentrate capital within a chosen price band, so within that band the same capital provides far deeper liquidity and earns proportionally more fees (capital efficiency). The catch is amplified impermanent loss and the risk of the price leaving your range, at which point you hold 100% of one asset and earn zero fees. It effectively turns passive LPing into an active position-management problem resembling a range order.

qa.q: Why do launches revoke mint and freeze authority, and what's the equivalent concern on Ethereum?
qa.a: On Solana the mint authority can create new tokens (inflate supply) and the freeze authority can lock user token accounts, so revoking both is the on-chain proof that supply is fixed and balances can't be frozen — a key trust and rug-pull check for buyers. The Ethereum equivalent is verifying the ERC-20 has no owner-only mint() or blacklist/pause functions and that ownership is renounced or supply is immutable. In both cases due diligence is reading the authority/permission surface, not just the total supply number.

bullets.point: Web3 is the shift from Web2's centralized read/write servers to user-owned, decentralized state living on public blockchains. The mental model to keep for interviews: Web1 = read, Web2 = read/write (platform owns your data), Web3 = read/write/own (assets and identity are cryptographically owned by the user, not a company).

Ethereum is a decentralized, programmable blockchain — a globally-replicated 'world computer' that runs Turing-complete smart contracts; ETH (Ether) is its native asset used to pay for computation (gas) and secure the network via staking. Interview angle: contrast with Bitcoin, which is primarily a value-transfer ledger (UTXO, script is intentionally limited), whereas Ethereum uses an account model and general-purpose compute.

A blockchain is an append-only, tamper-evident ledger where each block commits to the previous block's hash, so altering old history invalidates every subsequent block. Consensus (Ethereum uses Proof-of-Stake post-Merge) lets thousands of untrusting nodes agree on one canonical state without a central authority. The core trade-off to articulate: decentralization + security come at the cost of throughput and latency (every node re-executes every transaction).

Two account types share an address, balance, and nonce: Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs), controlled by a private key, and Contract Accounts, controlled by their deployed code and owning persistent storage. Key interview fact: only an EOA can originate a transaction — contract code executes only when called, and contract-to-contract calls all trace back to an initiating EOA (until account-abstraction / EIP-4337 blurs this).

The EVM is an abstract, stack-based virtual machine specified by the protocol; it runs identically on top of whatever physical/OS hardware each node uses. Virtualization is what guarantees deterministic, sandboxed, platform-independent execution so that every node computes the exact same post-transaction state — a hard requirement for consensus that raw native execution couldn't provide.

The EVM processes transactions sequentially — no threads, no concurrency in the global state machine — because every node must reproduce byte-identical results. This is why sources of nondeterminism (wall-clock time, real randomness, floating point) are banned or restricted, and it's the fundamental reason base-layer Ethereum has limited TPS, motivating rollups/L2s for scale.

The EVM has distinct data locations with very different cost/lifetime profiles: the stack (1024 x 32-byte words, cheapest), memory (volatile, cleared after the call, linear byte array), calldata (read-only input), and storage (persistent 32-byte key→value slots written to chain). Interview angle: storage is orders of magnitude more expensive because every node persists it forever, so gas optimization is largely about minimizing SSTORE/SLOAD.

Gas meters computational and storage work; the gas limit caps how much a transaction (and a block) may consume, which solves the halting problem — an infinite loop simply runs out of gas and halts. Know the distinction between the per-transaction gas limit set by the sender and the per-block gas limit set by the protocol, plus that out-of-gas still charges for work done.

Solidity compiles to EVM bytecode, a sequence of ~140 low-level opcodes (PUSH, ADD, MLOAD, SSTORE, CALL, JUMP, etc.) executed against the stack. Understanding that contracts are ultimately opcodes explains debugging with disassembly/traces, why some Solidity patterns are cheaper than others, and how tools like the ABI and function selectors map onto raw bytecode.

Each opcode has a gas price reflecting its real resource cost: arithmetic like ADD is 3 gas, while state-changing SSTORE can be up to ~20,000 gas and cold SLOAD is 2,100 (EIP-2929 warm/cold access). This pricing is the lever for gas optimization — batching storage writes, using memory over storage, and packing variables into single slots directly reduce transaction cost.

example.title: Debugging an out-of-gas revert while optimizing an on-chain reward distributor
example.body: You're an SDE-2 shipping a smart contract that pays out staking rewards to a list of users. In testing, calling distribute() with 50 addresses works, but on mainnet it reverts with 'out of gas' for 500 addresses. Tracing the transaction, you see the loop performs one SSTORE per user to update each balance in persistent storage — at up to ~20,000 gas each, the loop blows past the block gas limit, so the whole transaction reverts and consumes the gas without doing the work. Because the EVM is single-threaded and deterministic, you can't parallelize the loop; instead you redesign around its cost model: switch to a pull-based Merkle-claim pattern so each user pays their own SLOAD/SSTORE at claim time, cache the reward-per-token accumulator in a memory variable inside the loop instead of re-reading storage, and pack related struct fields into one 32-byte slot to turn multiple SSTOREs into one. This exercise touches every concept in the topic: EOA-initiated transactions, contract storage vs memory, opcodes and their gas prices, and the gas limit as the hard ceiling that forces the redesign.

qa.q: What is the difference between an EOA and a contract account, and why does it matter that only EOAs can initiate transactions?
qa.a: An EOA is controlled by a private key and has a balance and nonce but no code; a contract account is controlled by its deployed bytecode and additionally owns persistent storage. Only an EOA can originate a transaction — contract code is purely reactive and runs only when invoked. This matters because every execution trace on-chain, including deep contract-to-contract call chains, ultimately roots at an externally-signed transaction from an EOA (account abstraction / EIP-4337 introduces smart-contract wallets that relax this UX-wise but still settle via the protocol's transaction rules).

qa.q: Why is the EVM single-threaded and deterministic, and what are the consequences?
qa.a: Consensus requires that every independent node re-executing a block arrives at the exact same resulting state, so execution must be fully deterministic — which rules out threads, real randomness, floating point, and wall-clock dependence. Sequential single-threaded execution is the simplest way to guarantee identical ordering and results everywhere. The consequence is limited base-layer throughput, which is why scaling is pushed to L2 rollups that execute off-chain and post compressed proofs/data back to L1.

qa.q: Compare memory, storage, and calldata in terms of lifetime and gas cost.
qa.a: Storage is persistent key→value state written to the chain and replicated by all nodes forever, making it by far the most expensive (SSTORE up to ~20k gas). Memory is a volatile linear byte array that exists only for the duration of a single call and is comparatively cheap (though it grows quadratically in cost as it expands). Calldata is the read-only, immutable input to a call — cheapest to read and ideal for function arguments you don't need to mutate. Good contract design keeps hot data in memory/calldata and touches storage as little as possible.

qa.q: What problem does the gas limit solve, and what happens when a transaction runs out of gas?
qa.a: Gas metering with a hard limit solves the halting problem on a public VM: since you can't statically prove arbitrary contract code terminates, you cap execution so a runaway or malicious infinite loop simply exhausts gas and stops, preventing network DoS. When a transaction runs out of gas it reverts all state changes it made (as if it never ran), but the sender is still charged for the gas consumed up to that point, since nodes already did the work. There are two limits to know: the per-transaction limit set by the sender and the per-block limit set by the protocol.

qa.q: Explain cold vs warm storage access (EIP-2929) and why it affects gas optimization.
qa.a: EIP-2929 made the first (cold) access to a given storage slot or account within a transaction more expensive than subsequent (warm) accesses — a cold SLOAD is 2,100 gas versus 100 for a warm one. The intent is to price in the disk I/O and state-access cost that a cold read actually incurs on nodes, and to mitigate certain DoS vectors. For optimization it means repeatedly reading the same slot is cheap after the first touch, so caching a storage value in a local (stack/memory) variable and reusing it, rather than re-reading storage in a loop, meaningfully lowers cost.

qa.q: How does the EIP-1559 fee model work, and what happens to the base fee?
qa.a: Under EIP-1559 each block has a protocol-computed base fee per gas that adjusts up or down based on how full the previous block was relative to a target, plus a user-specified priority fee (tip) that incentivizes validators to include the transaction. The total paid is (base fee + priority fee) x gas used, capped by the max fee the sender sets. Critically, the base fee is burned rather than paid to the validator, which removes an incentive to manipulate fees and makes ETH partially deflationary under high demand.

qa.q: How does Solidity source relate to opcodes, and why should an SDE-2 care about the bytecode level?
qa.a: The Solidity compiler lowers your source into EVM bytecode — a linear sequence of stack-based opcodes (PUSH, ADD, SSTORE, CALL, JUMP, etc.) — and the ABI/function selectors are just conventions layered on top of that bytecode. Caring about this level pays off when debugging reverts via execution traces, reasoning about why one code pattern costs more gas than another, and auditing for security issues like unexpected delegatecall or reentrancy that are clearer in the opcode flow than in high-level source.

qa.q: At a high level, how does Ethereum's model differ from Bitcoin's, beyond 'smart contracts'?
qa.a: Bitcoin uses a UTXO model where the ledger is a set of unspent outputs and script is intentionally limited (not Turing-complete) to keep validation simple and safe. Ethereum uses an account/balance model with global mutable state and a Turing-complete VM, so contracts can hold arbitrary state and logic — at the cost of needing gas metering to bound execution. That design choice is exactly what enables programmable applications (tokens, DeFi, NFTs) but also introduces the attack surface and gas-cost concerns Bitcoin largely avoids.

bullets.point: Sets the mental model for the whole track: Solidity source compiles to EVM bytecode, gets deployed on-chain, and is interacted with off-chain via JSON-RPC using the contract's ABI. Interview angle: be able to describe this pipeline (source -> bytecode + ABI -> deployed address -> RPC calls) because every tool below just automates one part of it.

You never iterate against mainnet; you run a local EVM node (Ganache, Anvil, or Hardhat Network) that mines instantly, pre-funds deterministic accounts, and can fork live mainnet state at a block. Why it matters: fast, gas-free, reproducible tests, plus the ability to fork and impersonate accounts to integration-test against real protocols (Uniswap, Aave) without touching real funds.

The original JS toolchain: Ganache is a local blockchain (GUI/CLI) with prefunded accounts and configurable block times; Truffle handled compilation, migrations, and Mocha/Chai tests. Interview angle: ConsenSys sunset both in 2023, so treat them as legacy you may inherit in old repos; the modern answer is Hardhat or Foundry.

A JS/TS development environment built around Hardhat Network, which adds Solidity console.log, readable revert stack traces, and mainnet forking. Strength: deep JS/npm ecosystem and plugins (ethers, typechain) that suit full-stack teams; weakness: tests run in JS so they are slower and force a context switch between JS test logic and Solidity contract logic.

A Rust-based toolkit where you write tests in Solidity itself, giving very fast compile/run cycles plus first-class fuzzing and invariant testing. Four binaries: forge (build/test), cast (CLI for RPC and tx encoding), anvil (local node), and chisel (Solidity REPL). Interview angle: preferred today for speed and Solidity-native tests using cheatcodes via the vm object.

Install via the foundryup manager, which fetches/updates forge, cast, anvil, and chisel; foundryup lets you pin nightly or a specific version. `forge init` scaffolds a project with foundry.toml (compiler/config), src/, test/, and lib/ for dependencies.

Write Solidity in src/ with an SPDX license identifier and pragma, then `forge build` compiles it into out/ (bytecode + ABI). Tests live in test/ as contracts inheriting forge-std's Test, with functions prefixed test... that forge auto-discovers and runs.

`forge install <org>/<repo>` vendors dependencies as git submodules under lib/ (pinned by commit), and remappings in foundry.toml/remappings.txt map import prefixes to those paths, e.g. `@openzeppelin/=lib/openzeppelin-contracts/`. Interview angle: contrast the git-submodule model with Hardhat's npm node_modules approach, and know that remappings are how the compiler resolves imports.

Inherit OpenZeppelin's audited ERC20, pass name/symbol to its constructor, and _mint an initial supply. Interview angle: know the ERC-20 surface (totalSupply, balanceOf, transfer, approve, allowance, transferFrom), the Transfer/Approval events, and the two-step approve + transferFrom pattern that lets contracts pull tokens on your behalf.

Use `vm.expectEmit(checkTopic1, checkTopic2, checkTopic3, checkData)` immediately before the call, emit the exact event you expect, then invoke the function; forge asserts the indexed topics and/or data byte-for-byte match. Why it matters: events are the contract's observable log output that off-chain indexers and frontends depend on, so testing them protects that implicit interface.

forge-std provides Test.sol (assertions like assertEq plus the Vm cheatcode interface), console/console2 for logging during runs, and helpers like StdCheats (deal, prank, hoax) and StdStorage. Cheatcodes such as vm.prank (spoof msg.sender), vm.warp (set timestamp), vm.expectRevert, and vm.deal (set balance) are what make Solidity-native testing expressive.

Use `forge create` for a single contract or, preferably, `forge script` with a Script contract wrapping the deploy in vm.startBroadcast()/stopBroadcast(), run with --rpc-url and --broadcast. Interview angle: never hardcode private keys - use an encrypted keystore or env var, keep scripts versioned in Solidity for reproducible deploys, and pass --verify to publish source to the block explorer.

example.title: Shipping and integration-testing a rewards token with Foundry
example.body: You are an SDE-2 building an ERC-20 rewards token for a dApp. You scaffold with `forge init`, `forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts`, add a remapping, and write RewardToken by inheriting OpenZeppelin's ERC20 and minting an initial supply to the treasury. In test/ you write Solidity tests using forge-std: vm.prank to act as different users, assertEq on balances, vm.expectEmit to assert the Transfer event fires with the right from/to/amount, and vm.expectRevert to prove a transfer beyond balance fails. To be sure rewards can actually be swapped, you run against a mainnet fork via anvil (or --fork-url), impersonate a whale with vm.deal, and route a swap through the real Uniswap router to catch decimals/allowance bugs that a bare local chain would hide. Finally you write a Deploy.s.sol script that broadcasts the deployment, dry-run it against a testnet with an env-loaded key, then `forge script --broadcast --verify` to deploy and publish the source. If you had inherited an older repo, the same flow would have run through Ganache + Truffle migrations and JS/Mocha tests instead.

qa.q: Foundry vs Hardhat - when would you pick each?
qa.a: Foundry writes tests in Solidity, compiles/runs far faster, and gives native fuzzing and invariant testing, which makes it strong for protocol/contract-heavy work and tight test loops. Hardhat is JS/TS-based with a rich npm plugin ecosystem (ethers, typechain) and Solidity console.log/stack traces, which suits full-stack teams whose deploy scripts, frontend, and tests all live in TypeScript. Many teams now use Foundry for contract testing and keep Hardhat or a JS layer for tooling/integration; the two can coexist.

qa.q: Why develop against a local node, and what does mainnet forking add?
qa.a: A local EVM node (Anvil/Hardhat Network/Ganache) mines instantly, pre-funds accounts, and costs no gas, giving deterministic, fast, repeatable tests. Forking spins up that local node seeded with real mainnet state at a chosen block, so you can integration-test against deployed protocols like Uniswap or Aave and impersonate any account. This catches real-world issues (token decimals, existing allowances, oracle values) that a blank local chain never exposes.

qa.q: How does vm.expectEmit work and why test events at all?
qa.a: You call vm.expectEmit with booleans for which indexed topics and the data to check, then emit the exact expected event, then make the real call; forge asserts the next emitted log matches on the fields you enabled. Events are the contract's canonical off-chain log output that indexers, subgraphs, and frontends consume, so they are effectively part of the public interface. Testing them prevents silently breaking downstream consumers even when core state logic is correct.

qa.q: Explain the approve/transferFrom pattern and the allowance front-running risk.
qa.a: transfer moves your own tokens, but to let a contract (e.g. a DEX) pull tokens you first approve it for an allowance, and it later calls transferFrom up to that limit. The classic risk: changing an allowance from N to M is two transactions, and a spender watching the mempool can spend the old N before the new M lands, then spend M too. Mitigations are setting allowance to 0 first, or using increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance / EIP-2612 permit.

qa.q: How do you deploy safely with forge script, and how are keys handled?
qa.a: You write a Script contract that wraps the deployment between vm.startBroadcast() and vm.stopBroadcast(), then run it with --rpc-url and --broadcast so forge builds, signs, and sends the real transactions. Keys should come from an encrypted keystore (cast wallet) or an env var, never hardcoded in source, and you dry-run without --broadcast first. Keeping the script in Solidity makes deploys reproducible and reviewable, and --verify publishes source to the explorer.

qa.q: How does Foundry manage dependencies and imports compared to Hardhat?
qa.a: forge install vendors dependencies as git submodules under lib/, pinned to a specific commit, and remappings (in foundry.toml or remappings.txt) map an import prefix like @openzeppelin/ to that lib path so the compiler resolves imports. Hardhat instead uses npm/node_modules with semver ranges. The submodule model gives exact commit reproducibility without a package registry, at the cost of git submodule ergonomics.

qa.q: What cheatcodes from forge-std do you use most and what do they do?
qa.a: vm.prank/startPrank spoof msg.sender for the next (or a span of) calls; vm.deal sets an address's ETH balance; vm.warp and vm.roll set block.timestamp and block.number; vm.expectRevert asserts a call reverts with a given reason; vm.expectEmit asserts event emission. These let you simulate access control, time-based logic, and failure paths entirely in Solidity without deploying helper mocks.

qa.q: What is fuzz and invariant testing in Foundry and why does it matter?
qa.a: A fuzz test takes typed parameters and forge runs it hundreds of times with randomized inputs, surfacing edge cases (overflows, boundary amounts) that hand-picked cases miss. Invariant testing declares a property that must always hold (e.g. sum of balances equals totalSupply) and forge drives random sequences of calls trying to break it. Both push you past example-based testing toward property-based guarantees, which is critical for value-holding contracts where a single edge case can be an exploit.

bullets.point: Sets the mental model that a dApp is a normal frontend/backend plus a connection to a blockchain node; the 'client side' half is just your app translating user intent into RPC calls. Interview angle: be able to draw the boundary between off-chain code (your JS) and on-chain code (the deployed contract) — most confusion comes from blurring the two.

Consolidates the primitives everything downstream depends on: accounts (EOA vs contract), private keys/addresses, transactions vs calls, gas, and that contracts are just bytecode at an address with a defined ABI. Interview angle: an SDE-2 should crisply distinguish an EOA (controlled by a private key) from a contract account (controlled by code) and know that only EOAs can originate transactions.

All node communication is JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP or WebSocket — methods like eth_call, eth_sendRawTransaction, eth_getBalance, eth_estimateGas, eth_getLogs, eth_chainId. Libraries (ethers/viem/web3) are thin ergonomic wrappers over these methods; the node is usually a hosted provider (Alchemy/Infura) or your own geth/reth. Interview angle: know which operations are pure node queries vs which require a signed payload, and that eth_getLogs is how you read past events for indexing.

bullets.step: Connecting to an existing contract from the browser
bullets.point: To talk to a deployed contract you need three things: its address, its ABI (the interface describing function selectors and argument/return types), and a provider or signer. In the browser the provider comes from an injected EIP-1193 object (window.ethereum from MetaMask), which you wrap with ethers/viem to build a typed Contract instance. Interview angle: the ABI is off-chain metadata — the chain stores only bytecode — so a wrong or partial ABI silently breaks encoding/decoding.

view/pure functions are executed via eth_call, which runs the function against a node's local copy of state and returns the ABI-decoded result without creating a transaction — so it is free, needs no wallet signature, and is synchronous from the user's view. You can pin the query to a block tag (latest, a number, or 'pending') to read historical state. Interview angle: eth_call costs no gas because nothing is broadcast or mined; it never mutates state and only needs a provider, not a signer.

bullets.step: Writing to a contract from the browser
bullets.point: State-changing functions require a signed transaction: the app builds the call, the wallet (MetaMask) prompts the user to sign, and it is broadcast via eth_sendRawTransaction, costing gas and returning a tx hash you must then wait on for confirmation. You deal with nonces, EIP-1559 fees (maxFeePerGas/maxPriorityFeePerGas), gas estimation, and the fact that success is asynchronous and can revert. Interview angle: the returned hash means 'accepted into the mempool', not 'succeeded' — you must await the receipt and check status to know it mined and didn't revert.

Server-side there is no injected wallet, so you construct a signer directly from a private key (loaded from a secret manager/env) and connect it to an RPC provider — used for backends, relayers, bots, and indexers. Read paths are identical to the browser; write paths mean you now own key security and nonce management for concurrent transactions. Interview angle: the big new risks are leaking the private key and nonce collisions when firing multiple txs in parallel from one account.

example.title: ERC-20 token dashboard with a server-side airdrop distributor
example.body: You build a dApp for an ERC-20 token. The React frontend connects to the user's MetaMask via window.ethereum, wraps it in an ethers Signer, and instantiates the token Contract from its address + ABI. Balances and allowances render through read-only eth_call queries (free, no signature). When a user clicks 'Transfer', you call the state-changing transfer(), MetaMask prompts them to sign, you broadcast it, then await the receipt to flip the UI to 'confirmed' or surface a revert. Separately, a Node.js backend runs a scheduled airdrop: it loads a hot-wallet private key from a secret manager, builds a signer against an Alchemy RPC provider, and sends batched transfer transactions — here you must manage the nonce yourself (incrementing per tx rather than trusting 'pending') so parallel sends don't collide, set EIP-1559 fees sanely, and retry stuck transactions. An indexer process on the same backend uses eth_getLogs to read Transfer events and reconcile off-chain balances. Debugging this system means knowing exactly which failures are RPC-layer (rate limits, wrong chainId), encoding-layer (stale ABI), or on-chain (reverts, insufficient gas, nonce gaps).

qa.q: What is the difference between eth_call and eth_sendTransaction, and when do you use each?
qa.a: eth_call executes a function against a node's local state view and returns the decoded result without broadcasting anything — it is free, needs only a provider, and is used for view/pure reads. eth_sendTransaction (really eth_sendRawTransaction with a pre-signed payload) broadcasts a signed transaction into the mempool to change state, costs gas, requires a signer, and only returns a tx hash. You use eth_call for reads and simulations (including dry-running a write to catch reverts cheaply) and eth_sendTransaction when you actually need to mutate on-chain state.

qa.q: Why don't read-only calls cost gas, given that gas exists to pay for computation?
qa.a: Gas pays for computation that every node in the network must redundantly execute and for the state changes they must persist. An eth_call runs only on the single node you queried, against its local state, and its result is discarded — it is never mined into a block or replicated. Since nothing is broadcast or committed, there is no consensus cost to compensate, so the caller pays nothing (the node operator absorbs the trivial CPU cost, which is why hosted providers rate-limit).

qa.q: How does a browser dApp get a provider, and what is EIP-1193?
qa.a: A wallet extension like MetaMask injects an EIP-1193 provider object at window.ethereum — a standardized interface exposing a request({method, params}) function plus events like accountsChanged and chainChanged. Your library (ethers BrowserProvider / viem custom transport) wraps this object so RPC methods and account access route through the wallet, which mediates permissions and signing. Standardizing on EIP-1193 is what lets one frontend work across MetaMask, Rabby, Coinbase Wallet, etc., instead of coding to each wallet's proprietary API.

qa.q: What is an ABI and why is it required off-chain when the contract is already deployed?
qa.a: The ABI (Application Binary Interface) is a JSON description of a contract's functions and events — names, argument types, return types, and mutability. The chain stores only compiled bytecode, which has no self-describing type information, so your client needs the ABI to encode calls (compute the 4-byte function selector and pack arguments) and to decode return values and event logs. A stale or incomplete ABI causes silent encoding/decoding failures or missing functions even though the contract itself is fine.

qa.q: A user's write transaction returns a hash immediately, but the token balance hasn't changed. What's happening and how do you handle it?
qa.a: The hash only means the signed transaction was accepted into the mempool, not that it mined or succeeded. You must await the transaction receipt (e.g., tx.wait()) and check receipt.status — a successful mine is status 1, while a mined-but-reverted tx is status 0 and still consumed gas. Properly you wait for a chosen number of confirmations to guard against reorgs, and you handle the tx being dropped or stuck (underpriced) by re-broadcasting or replacing it with the same nonce and higher fee.

qa.q: How does signing differ between the browser and a Node.js server, and what new risks does the server introduce?
qa.a: In the browser the private key never touches your code — the wallet holds it and the user manually approves each signature via EIP-1193. On a server you instantiate a signer directly from a raw private key you control, so there is no human in the loop and signing is automatic. The new risks are key custody (a leaked key drains the account, so keys belong in a KMS/secret manager or HSM, never in code or logs) and nonce management, since your process is now solely responsible for issuing correct sequential nonces.

qa.q: Why do concurrent server-side transactions from one account collide, and how do you fix it?
qa.a: Every transaction from an EOA carries a nonce that must increment by exactly one; nodes reject duplicates and queue gaps. If you fire several transactions in parallel and each fetches the 'pending' nonce independently, they all read the same value and collide, so only one lands. The fix is to serialize nonce assignment in your own process — maintain a local counter (seeded from getTransactionCount) and hand out incrementing nonces under a lock/queue — plus handle replacement and gap recovery when a tx gets dropped.

qa.q: In ethers.js, what is the practical difference between a Provider and a Signer?
qa.a: A Provider is a read-only connection to the chain: it can query state and call view functions but cannot authorize anything. A Signer wraps a Provider with the ability to produce signatures, so it can send transactions and sign messages on behalf of a specific address. In the browser the Signer is backed by the wallet (user approves); on the server it is backed by a private key you hold. A Contract built with a Provider can only read; one built with a Signer can also write.

bullets.step: Web3 Cohort: the full-stack dApp loop
bullets.point: Sets the frame for the whole topic: a dApp is a smart contract (on-chain state + logic) plus a frontend that reads/writes it through a node RPC and a wallet. The interview angle is being able to trace the request path end to end: FE -> library (ethers/viem) -> JSON-RPC -> node -> EVM -> contract, and back.

Author the Solidity contract: state variables, functions marked view/pure vs state-changing, events, and access modifiers (onlyOwner, require checks). Compiling it (Hardhat/Foundry/Remix) produces two artifacts you must remember for interviews: the bytecode (deployed to the chain) and the ABI (the JSON interface the FE needs to encode calls); deploying returns the contract address.

Build the React frontend that talks to the deployed contract: connect a wallet (window.ethereum / MetaMask), instantiate a provider for reads and a signer for writes, and construct a Contract object from address + ABI. The key point is the read/write split: view functions go through a provider via eth_call (free, synchronous-ish), while state changes require a signer, cost gas, and are asynchronous transactions.

bullets.step: Add the ABI + address wiring (glue layer)
bullets.point: Wire the compiled ABI JSON and the per-network contract address into the FE (typically imported from artifacts or an env/config keyed by chainId). This is the load-bearing glue: a stale ABI silently breaks call encoding/decoding, and a wrong address or chain means you're calling the wrong (or nonexistent) contract. Interviewers probe how you keep ABI and address in sync across redeploys and networks.

Hands-on task: implement, deploy to a testnet, and drive a contract from the UI to internalize the full loop (connect wallet -> read state -> send tx -> await receipt -> reflect updated state). It matters because the failure modes only show up when you actually deal with pending transactions, reverts, user rejection, and network switching.

example.title: A decentralized crowdfunding dApp (contract -> React) on a testnet
example.body: You build a Crowdfund contract in Solidity: it stores a goal and totalRaised, exposes a payable contribute() that increments state and emits Contributed(address,uint256), and a view getStatus() returning progress. You compile with Hardhat, deploy to Sepolia, and note the address. On the frontend, users click Connect Wallet (MetaMask); you create a provider for reads (rendering totalRaised via getStatus through eth_call) and, once connected, a signer for writes. contribute() is called through the signer, returns a tx hash immediately, and you await tx.wait() to get the receipt before showing a success state and refreshing the total. You subscribe to the Contributed event (or poll) so other users' contributions update the progress bar live. Debugging this surfaces the real end-to-end concerns: a redeploy changes the address and sometimes the ABI (both must be re-wired), the user may reject the signature, the tx may revert if the goal is already met, and a wallet on the wrong chainId must be prompted to switch to Sepolia before any call succeeds.

qa.q: What exactly is the ABI, and why can't the frontend talk to a contract without it?
qa.a: The ABI (Application Binary Interface) is a JSON description of the contract's functions, their input/output types, and events. Libraries like ethers/viem use it to ABI-encode a function call into the calldata bytes the EVM expects, and to decode the raw return bytes and event logs back into JS values. Without the ABI the client has no idea how to serialize arguments or interpret results, so the address alone is useless; a stale ABI (after changing a signature) causes silent decode failures or reverts.

qa.q: Explain the difference between a provider and a signer, and how it maps to reads vs writes.
qa.a: A provider is a read-only connection to a node over JSON-RPC; it executes view/pure functions via eth_call, which run locally on the node, cost no gas, and return a value synchronously. A signer wraps a wallet/private key and can sign transactions, which is required for any state-changing function because that produces an on-chain transaction that costs gas and must be mined. In practice you read with a provider even when disconnected, but you can only write after the user connects a wallet and you obtain a signer.

qa.q: A state-changing call returns a tx hash, not the new value. How do you handle that in the UI?
qa.a: State changes are asynchronous: the send resolves with a transaction hash once it's in the mempool, but the state isn't updated until the tx is mined. You await tx.wait() (or wait for N confirmations) to get the receipt, then re-read state or trust an emitted event to update the UI. You must also handle the intermediate pending state, user rejection of the signature, reverts (out-of-gas or failed require), and reorg risk for low-confirmation counts; optimistic UI is possible but must roll back if the tx fails.

qa.q: How do you keep the contract address and ABI correct across multiple networks and redeploys?
qa.a: Key the address by chainId in a config/env map (e.g. one address for Sepolia, another for mainnet) and read the wallet's current chainId at runtime to pick the right one, prompting a network switch on mismatch. The ABI should come straight from the build artifacts and be regenerated/re-imported on every redeploy, ideally as part of the build pipeline, so it never drifts from the deployed bytecode. Hardcoding a single address or committing a hand-edited ABI is the classic source of 'works on testnet, broken on mainnet' bugs.

qa.q: Why should you never trust the frontend for validation in a dApp, and where do checks belong?
qa.a: The frontend and even the RPC layer are fully controllable by an attacker who can craft raw transactions bypassing your UI entirely, so any invariant (ownership, limits, balances, deadlines) must be enforced on-chain with require/revert in the contract. FE validation is only a UX convenience to fail fast and save gas. This is why interviewers care about on-chain access control (onlyOwner), reentrancy guards on functions that send ETH, and checks-effects-interactions ordering.

qa.q: How do you reflect contract state changes in the UI in near real time?
qa.a: You subscribe to contract events via contract.on(...) over a WebSocket provider (or filter/query logs), which pushes updates when transactions emit events, and update local state accordingly. Alternatives are polling read functions on an interval, or for richer/historical queries using an indexer like a subgraph. Events are cheaper and more scalable than polling, but you should still reconcile on load by reading current state, since events can be missed during disconnects or reorgs.

qa.q: ethers.js vs wagmi/viem — when would you reach for each in the frontend?
qa.a: ethers/viem are the low-level libraries that handle providers, signers, ABI encoding, and transaction sending; viem is the newer, more typesafe and tree-shakeable option. wagmi is a React layer on top (usually of viem) that gives you hooks like useAccount, useReadContract, and useWriteContract plus wallet-connection state, caching, and re-render handling. For a plain script or non-React context you'd use ethers/viem directly; for a React dApp, wagmi removes a lot of boilerplate around connection lifecycle and query invalidation.

bullets.point: Sets the EVM + Solidity foundation: accounts, gas, transactions, and tooling (Hardhat or Foundry, ethers/viem, testnets). Interview angle: be able to explain that a 'bridge' is not magic — chains are isolated state machines that cannot read each other, so any cross-chain movement is really a lock/burn on one chain plus an off-chain actor triggering a mint/unlock on the other.

A source-chain vault that pulls the user's ERC-20 into custody via approve + transferFrom and emits a Deposit/Locked event carrying (sender, amount, destination address, nonce). It matters because the locked balance is the collateral backing whatever gets minted on the other side — the vault is the single source of truth and the prime hack target.

Deploy the vault to a testnet (e.g. Sepolia) using a deploy script, a funded key in .env, and Etherscan verification so the ABI/source is public. Interview angle: never commit private keys, understand nonce/gas at deploy time, and know that a deployed address is immutable — upgradeability requires a proxy pattern chosen up front.

A bridge moves value across chains using lock-and-mint (lock native token on source, mint a wrapped/pegged version on destination) or burn-and-mint. The core insight: total supply is conserved because minted destination tokens are always 1:1 backed by locked source tokens — if that invariant ever breaks (bad mint, double-spend), the peg collapses.

Architecture is three parts: a source lock contract, a destination mint contract (an ERC-20 with a restricted mint), and an off-chain relayer/validator that watches source events and submits mint transactions. The relayer is the trust boundary — this is a trusted/centralized bridge, and the design question is who is authorized to call mint.

A deliberate simplification: value flows only source→destination (lock→mint), with no burn-and-unlock path back. It matters because it halves the attack surface and state you must track, and it forces you to reason clearly about the single direction before adding the harder reverse leg (which needs its own events, nonces, and unlock authorization).

Solidity for both sides: the vault does transferFrom + emits an event with a monotonically increasing nonce; the destination token exposes mint(to, amount, nonce) guarded by onlyRelayer/onlyOwner access control. Interview angle: use SafeERC20 for non-standard tokens, follow checks-effects-interactions to avoid reentrancy, and emit events for every state change since the relayer relies on them.

Each deposit gets a unique nonce, and the destination stores mapping(uint256 => bool) processed to reject any nonce it has already minted for. Without this, a relayer (or attacker replaying a signed message) could submit the same mint twice and print unbacked tokens — nonces enforce exactly-once processing across an inherently at-least-once messaging layer.

With Hardhat/Foundry, deploy a mock ERC-20 + both contracts, then simulate the full flow in one test env: approve, lock, assert the Deposit event and vault balance, then call mint as the relayer and assert the destination balance. Critically test the failure paths: replaying a used nonce reverts, and a non-relayer calling mint reverts.

example.title: Bridging a game token from Ethereum to an L2
example.body: You run a game whose reward token GEM lives as an ERC-20 on Ethereum, but gameplay happens on a cheaper L2. You build a one-way lock-and-mint bridge: a Vault contract on L1 where a player calls approve(vault, amount) then deposit(amount, l2Address), which runs transferFrom to custody the GEM and emits Locked(player, amount, l2Address, nonce). An off-chain relayer subscribes to that event, waits for enough confirmations to survive a reorg, and calls mint(l2Address, amount, nonce) on the wGEM contract on the L2. wGEM's mint is onlyRelayer and checks a processed[nonce] mapping so a crashed-and-restarted relayer that re-submits the same event cannot double-mint. The invariant you monitor in prod: total wGEM minted on L2 must always equal GEM locked in the L1 vault — a divergence means a bug or a compromised relayer key, and you'd pause minting immediately.

qa.q: Why can't a smart contract on Ethereum just read state from another chain to bridge tokens?
qa.a: Each chain is an isolated, deterministic state machine; a node validating Ethereum has no access to another chain's state, and allowing external network calls would break determinism and consensus. So cross-chain communication is always mediated by an off-chain actor (a relayer/oracle/validator set) that observes an event or proof on the source chain and submits a corresponding transaction on the destination. That off-chain component is why bridges introduce a trust assumption that pure on-chain logic doesn't have.

qa.q: Walk me through lock-and-mint and why supply stays conserved.
qa.a: The user locks N tokens in a source-chain vault, and a relayer mints N wrapped tokens on the destination chain to the user's address. Total economic supply is unchanged because every minted wrapped token is 1:1 collateralized by a token frozen in the vault; the wrapped token is a claim on that locked collateral. The whole system's safety rests on this invariant — if you ever mint without a matching lock, the wrapped token becomes under-collateralized and its peg breaks.

qa.q: Why approve + transferFrom instead of having users just transfer tokens to the vault?
qa.a: A plain transfer to the contract address moves tokens but the contract gets no execution hook and no reliable record tying the deposit to a user intent, so it can't emit the structured Deposit event the relayer needs (and funds can be stranded). The approve/transferFrom pattern lets the vault pull a specific amount inside its own deposit() call, so it can atomically record the sender, destination address, and nonce and emit the event in the same transaction. The trade-off is the two-transaction UX (approve then deposit), which permit/EIP-2612 can collapse into one.

qa.q: What exactly do the nonces protect against, and how?
qa.a: They enforce exactly-once minting over a messaging layer that is only at-least-once — relayers can crash and re-send, and signed mint messages can be replayed by anyone who sees them. Each deposit carries a unique, monotonically increasing nonce, and the destination keeps a mapping(uint256 => bool) processed that it sets on first mint and checks on every mint, reverting on a repeat. Without it, a single legitimate deposit could be minted multiple times, creating unbacked supply — a classic double-mint bridge exploit.

qa.q: This is a trusted bridge. Who can mint, and what are the main attack vectors?
qa.a: Minting is gated by access control (onlyRelayer/onlyOwner), so whoever holds that key can mint arbitrarily — that key is the crown jewel. The dominant vectors are: compromise of the relayer/owner key (Ronin, ~$625M, was stolen validator keys), a bug in mint validation or signature verification (Wormhole, ~$325M), and missing replay/nonce checks. This is why bridges are the single largest category of DeFi losses, and why production designs move toward multisig/MSC validator sets, rate limits, and a pausable circuit breaker.

qa.q: The relayer sees a Deposit event and mints immediately — what could go wrong?
qa.a: Block reorganizations: if the relayer mints on the strength of an event that later gets reorged out, tokens are minted on the destination with no backing lock remaining on the source.
